title: An Intention-Driven Lane Change Framework Considering Heterogeneous Dynamic Cooperation in Mixed-Traffic Environments
authors: Xiaoyun Qiu, Haichao Liu, Yue Pan, Jun Ma, Xinhu Zheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: In mixed-traffic environments, autonomous vehicles (AVs) must interact with heterogeneous human-driven vehicles (HVs) whose intentions and driving styles vary across individuals and scenarios. Such variability introduces uncertainty into lane change interactions, where safety and efficiency critically depend on accurately anticipating surrounding drivers’ cooperative responses. Existing methods often oversimplify these interactions by assuming uniform or fixed behavioral patterns. To address this limitation, we propose an intention-driven lane change framework that integrates driving-style recognition with cooperation-aware decision-making and motion-planning. A deep learning-based classifier identifies distinct human driving styles in real time. We then introduce a dual-perspective cooperation score composed of intrinsic style-dependent tendencies and interactive dynamic components, enabling interpretable and adaptive intention prediction and quantitative inference. A decision-making module combines behavior cloning (BC) and inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) to determine lane change feasibility. Later, a coordinated motion-planning architecture integrating IRL-based intention inference with model predictive control (MPC) is established to generate collision-free and socially compliant trajectories. Experiments on the NGSIM dataset show that the proposed decision-making model outperforms representative rule-based and learning-based baselines, achieving 96.98% accuracy in lane change classification. Motion-planning evaluations further demonstrate improved maneuver success and execution stability in mixed-traffic environments. These results validate the effectiveness of structured cooperation modeling for intention-driven autonomous lane changes.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Hierarchical MHE–EKF for Joint Estimation of Vehicle Lateral Velocity and Road Friction
authors: Jiaxing Lu, Wei Pan, Weiheng Chen, Lin Zhang, Chunlai Zhao, Hong Chen
abstract: Accurate joint estimation of vehicle lateral velocity and tire-road friction coefficient (TRFC) is critical for active safety systems. However, under near-limit conditions, strong nonlinear coupling and mutual error propagation severely degrade estimation accuracy. To address these challenges, this article proposes a novel uncertainty-aware hierarchical moving horizon estimation and extended Kalman filter (MHE–EKF) framework. This two-stage architecture employs a dual EKF (DEKF) to provide robust coarse priors, followed by a bidirectionally coupled MHE–EKF for high-precision joint estimation. The key novelty of this MHE–EKF structure lies in an embedded dual-innovation mechanism explicitly designed to manage uncertainty: first, an error-propagation model translates state errors into a high-confidence TRFC constraint interval, strictly bounding the MHE search space; second, a flag-driven adaptive arrival-cost strategy integrates multistage priors to accelerate mutual convergence. Vehicle experiments validate the algorithm. Compared to the conventional DEKF, the proposed framework reduces the lateral velocity mean absolute error to 28.8<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> and 42.1<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> of the DEKF baseline in dry-asphalt slalom and double-lane-change maneuvers, respectively, while concurrently decreasing the corresponding TRFC estimation errors from 5.73<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> to 0.11<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> and from 11.61<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> to 1.38<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Extended evaluations on low-adhesion snow roads further confirm the algorithm's exceptional robustness and consistent accuracy across variable friction conditions.

title: A Wide-Gain Isolated Single-Output Bipolar DC–DC Converter With Improved Buck-Boost Operation
authors: Hafiz Furqan Ahmed, Alireza Lahooti Eshkevari, Iman Abdoli
abstract: This article introduces a new isolated buck–boost DC–DC converter with a single bipolar output port, along with its switching strategy. The proposed converter is designed by combining the standard single-ended primary inductance converter (SEPIC) and Ćuk topologies, thereby inheriting the advantages of both. As a result, it provides continuous input current as well as continuous output current during negative output voltage operation. Unlike previous designs, the proposed circuit delivers symmetric bipolar output voltages over a wide range of duty cycles and a broad buck–boost operation range. Furthermore, compared with counterpart topologies, this two-quadrant converter incorporates a high-frequency isolation transformer. The turns ratio of the high-frequency transformer provides another degree of control freedom, along with the switch duty ratio, and can extend both buck and boost voltage gains without limiting the overall buck–boost factor, thereby ensuring a wide output voltage range. Compared with existing designs, the proposed bipolar output converter improves efficiency while maintaining symmetric operation. A comprehensive analysis of the converter and its switching method is presented, and the design concept is validated through a laboratory-built prototype.

title: CFR-Net: A Coarse-to-Fine Concatenated Dual Decoder Based on Frequency-Space-Structure Fusion for Robust Remote Sensing Change Detection
authors: Yuanzhu Wang, Yukun Zhang, Liguo Weng, Haifeng Lin, Min Xia
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Current state-of-the-art change detection methods face a critical dilemma: achieving robustness against non-stationary pseudo-changes (e.g., seasonal shifts and vegetation phenology) while preserving fine-grained boundary details often requires computationally expensive models. Existing lightweight approaches usually sacrifice one aspect for the other, leading to either excessive false alarms or blurred object boundaries. To address this challenge, we propose a novel coarse-to-fine refinement network, termed CFR-Net. CFR-Net adopts a dual-time Mamba-based encoder to suppress non-stationary variations and capture robust spatiotemporal dependencies, followed by a cascaded dual-decoder architecture that progressively refines change boundaries from coarse localization to fine delineation. Extensive experiments on three benchmarks (LEVIR-CD, GZ-CD, and SYSU-CD) demonstrate that CFR-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Specifically, on the LEVIR-CD dataset, our model achieves an F1-score of 97.46% and requires only 6.54 G FLOPs, striking an optimal balance between accuracy and efficiency. These results confirm the effectiveness of CFR-Net for robust and efficient high-resolution remote sensing change detection. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/wyyyz-web/CFR-Net.git</uri>

title: Surviving (and Thriving) as Organizational Newcomers: Technical Communicators and Proactive Socialization
authors: Kim Sydow Campbell, Samantha Jo Cosgrove, Maia Pacini
abstract: Background: Socialization challenges for employee onboarding are well-established in management studies. This study investigates awareness of proactive socialization tactics for newly hired technical communicators. Literature review: The review examines organizational socialization within management studies and technical communication. Literature reveals the relevance and some application of this theory to technical communicators and encourages further research. Research questions: Do experienced professionals recognize proactive socialization tactics as a means of successful onboarding? What proactive socialization tactics do experienced professionals mention as appropriate for new technical writers? Methodology: Semistructured interviews were conducted with technical writers and their managers (n = 28), and transcripts were coded for instances of proactive socialization tactics (i.e., sensemaking, relationship building, and positive framing) mentioned as challenges faced by newly hired technical writers. Results and conclusion: Participants overwhelmingly recognized proactive behaviors as valuable, mentioning sensemaking most frequently (n = 25) followed by relationship building (n = 10) and positive framing (n = 5). Results offer four actionable insights for new technical writers: 1. be proactive to move more quickly through stages of onboarding; 2. seek information and feedback by independent learning, asking questions, and volunteering; 3. build relationships through social activities; and 4. aim to frame new or less-desirable tasks positively. Research limitations included sample size, interview methodology, deductive coding, and the prominence of remote/hybrid participants. Future research could explicitly address proactive socialization tactics through surveys for a larger sample size and more generalizable findings and explore differences in tactics specific to onsite versus distributed work arrangements.

title: ST-CoSense: Spatio-Temporal Cooperative Sensing for Resource-Constrained Urban Applications
authors: Yaxin Mei, Huiling Qin, Guangxue Zhang, Xiaolong Xu, Zhanchuan Cai, Yu Zheng, Tian Wang
abstract: Urban sensing is a critical infrastructure for smart cities, yet dense sensor deployments remain prohibitively costly, and sensor data quality degrades severely under real-world interference (e.g., signal occlusion, extreme weather) that results in data gaps. Existing approaches treat sampling and imputation as two independent stages: sampling strategies select locations based on heuristics or optimization criteria without considering the downstream reconstruction objective, while imputation models passively adapt to whatever observations are provided, with no mechanism to inform the sampler which locations would most effectively reduce reconstruction error. We propose ST-CoSense, a unified framework that bridges this gap through end-to-end joint optimization of active sparse sampling and spatio-temporal imputation. A mask-guided encoder dynamically selects informative sensing locations by integrating historical sampling patterns with learned spatio-temporal representations, while an attention-based imputation module reconstructs complete urban data from sparse observations. Imputed results in turn refine subsequent sampling decisions, allowing continuous adaptation under sampling budget constraints. Experimental results show that ST-CoSense outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving up to 9.07% MAE and 10.53% RMSE improvement at a 50% sampling rate. Furthermore, ST-CoSense demonstrates superior robustness in large-scale urban networks, achieving a 4.95% RMSE improvement over the best-performing baseline. These results underscore ST-CoSense's effectiveness and its practical potential in resource-constrained urban sensing applications.

title: Self-Expressive High-Order Tensor Unrolling Network for Unsupervised Hyperspectral and Multispectral Image Fusion_supp1-3695389.pdf
authors: Yang Xu
abstract: Hyperspectral and multispectral image fusion (HMF) enhances spatial-spectral quality by fusing low-resolution hyperspectral images (LR-HSI) with high-resolution multispectral images (HR-MSI). Although recent fusion methods have shown promise in preserving the multi-mode structure of high-dimensional data, existing fusion methods still face some challenges. For tensor-based approaches, conventional mode-wise decomposition, such as order-3 CP or Tucker decomposition, may disrupt intrinsic spatial consistency. Furthermore, although deep learning exhibits powerful feature representation ability, existing deep fusion methods either rely on ‘black-boxed’ deep fusion networks remain insufficiently interpretability with large training data. To address these issues, a novel Self-Expressive High-Order Tensor Unrolling Network (SHOTUN) is proposed for unsupervised HSI-MSI fusion. Within the sparse core tensor decomposition framework, we introduce the intrinsic self-expressive relationships among overlapping image patches as a form of high-order mode representation to preserve spatial structure of the fusion model. During optimization, we adopt an alternative optimizing strategy and design dedicated modules for each sub-problem, yielding an interpretable end-to-end training pipeline. Furthermore, to improve generalization across different sensors, we introduce a pre-training strategy into the unsupervised training for the more accurate estimation of unknown degraded parameters. Extensive experimental results on simulated and real datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method. The source code will be publicly available at https://github.com/Shawn-H-Wang/SHOTUN.

title: Leveraging Feature Alignment in Grassmannian Manifold for Multi-output Regression Tasks
authors: Lingping Kong, Jan Zdrazil, Nuria De Diego, Francisco Ignacio Jasso Robles, Václav Snášel, Swagatam Das, Jeng-Shyang Pan
abstract: Despite notable progress in domain adaptation for classification, regression-based domain adaptation remains challenging, particularly in terms of handling complex data structures, ensuring cross-domain generalization, and maintaining the precision and mathematical rigor required to validate model effectiveness. Unlike classification tasks, which are more resilient to variations in feature scaling, regression tasks are notably more sensitive, making their performance more vulnerable in domain adaptation scenarios. In this paper, we propose a generalized regularization technique grounded in the Grassmannian manifold to address the feature alignment problem. This approach leverages the underlying manifold structure of the data while preserving mathematical bounds, thereby enhancing the precision and efficiency of problem-solving. To demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm, we apply it to estimate multi-output parameters in two distinct domains: 1) the Arabidopsis thaliana plant dataset, collected from a high-throughput phenotyping platform at Palacký University, and 2) the publicly available dSprites shape recognition with six adaptation tasks. These tasks are critical to advance agricultural research and address generalization challenges in multi-output regression. Accurate predictions provide deeper insights into plant growth and health, thereby supporting more effective crop management strategies. We evaluate the effectiveness of our framework by comparing it with state-of-the-art regression alignment techniques that are independent of the underlying backbone and adaptable to transfer learning tasks. Experimental results show that our framework consistently outperforms existing methods,results description. The source code will be made publicly available upon acceptance at https://github.com/lingping-fuzzy.

title: Coherent Radar Networks: Terminology, Synchronization, and Applications
authors: David Werbunat, Lukas Paulus, Julian Aguilar, Vinzenz Janoudi, Julian Kanz, Max Basler, Martin Vossiek, Christian Waldschmidt
abstract: In coherent radar networks, multiple radar sensors not only operate cooperatively but also facilitate coherent evaluation of signals from other spacially distant radar units. This overcomes the limitations of single radars, enabling extended apertures and new multistatic sensing geometries. Applications are manifold, ranging from network-based direction of arrival (DoA) estimation to bi-/multistatic synthetic aperture radar (SAR) and joint communication and sensing (JCAS). In recent years, this has led to significant research interest and the design of a wide range of radar networks and synchronization concepts. This paper introduces a unified terminology and a general signal model applicable to radar networks and bistatic radars. Building on this model, this work provides a detailed review of the main sources of synchronization errors and their impact on radar signal processing. The paper further surveys and categorizes synchronization strategies proposed in the literature to address these errors. It thereby enables both a structured comparison of current radar networks and the design of novel network and synchronization concepts for emerging applications. To illustrate the potential of these approaches, three applications are presented in detail: a novel DoA estimation strategy for distributed radar networks, UAV-borne bistatic SAR, and JCAS.

title: Masthead
authors: N/A

title: Optimized Digital Filtering of EEG Signals for ERP Preservation and Noise Suppression
authors: Md. Mostafa Goni, Amir KhosruTalha, Yaseen Rahat, Razia Sultana Rimu, Rakayet Rafi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Accurate filtering of EEG signals is essential for reliable brain activity interpretation, particularly in Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) and neurodiagnostic applications where noise can distort event-related potentials (ERPs). This study compares the performance of four widely used digital filters applied to real EEG data from a rapid serial visual presentation (RSVP) task (PhysioNet, 2048 Hz sampling rate): FIR bandpass filters with Hamming, Hanning, and Blackman windows, IIR Butterworth, IIR Chebyshev Type II, and FIR Parks-McClellan notch filters. Filter performance was assessed using SNR, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\triangle$</tex> SNR, MSE, RMSE, PSNR, and frequency-domain metrics including stopband attenuation, passband ripple, and group delay. Results show that the IIR Butterworth filter achieved the highest realistic SNR improvement (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\triangle$</tex> SNR = <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$+12.89 ~\text{dB}$</tex>), while FIR bandpass filters offered moderate denoising (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\triangle \text{SNR}=+5.94 ~\text{dB}$</tex>) and preserved waveform fidelity due to linear phase characteristics. Among FIR windows, the Blackman design achieved the lowest MSE (0.7226) and highest PSNR (1.411 dB), indicating superior signal fidelity. The Chebyshev II filter, however, introduced artifacts, limiting its practical use. Overall, Butterworth filters are recommended for real-time EEG denoising, whereas Blackmanwindowed FIR filters are best suited for offline ERP analysis where phase preservation is critical.

title: Blood Cancer Classification from Microscopic Images via ViTResNetEnsemble with XAI
authors: Tasnim Sultana Sintheia, Mahafozur Rahman, Badhon Sutrudhar, Saif Mostasir Rohan, Mian Mohammad Rassel, Md. Sayem Kabir
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Blood cancers, particularly leukemia, remain a major cause of cancer-related mortality worldwide, placing significant pressure on healthcare systems due to their rapid progression and the need for early, accurate detection. Ensuring timely and reliable identification of leukemia subtypes is therefore critical, yet manual microscopic examination is both laborintensive and susceptible to observer variability. To address this challenge, this study introduces ViTResNetEnsemble, a hybrid deep learning model designed to capture both global structural patterns and fine-grained cellular details in leukocyte images. The model combines Vision Transformer embeddings with ResNet50-v2 feature representations, forming a complementary architecture that enhances discrimination among visually similar cancer subtypes. A balanced dataset of 8,000 images was created through targeted augmentation to reduce class imbalance and improve generalization. Trained with optimized hyperparameters and evaluated through extensive metrics, the proposed model achieves 99.17% accuracy with strong reliability metrics (Cohen's Kappa = 0.989, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{MCC}=0.989$</tex>, Macro <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{F} 1=0.992$</tex>), outperforming several CNN and Transformer baselines. Ablation studies confirm the contribution of each model component, while calibration curves and XAI techniques (LIME, Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++) provide insights into prediction reliability and clinical interpretability. These results highlight the potential of hybrid Transformer-CNN models to deliver robust and transparent assistance in hematological diagnosis.

title: Intelligent Heat Stress Management: Economic Impacts and Policy Strategies for Informal Women Workers
authors: Md. Sakib Zubayer, Siam Muhammad Shakil, Lamia Ferdous, Md. Taraquzzaman Tonmay
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This study examines the economic impact of heat exposure on informal women workers in Rajshahi City, Bangladesh, focusing on income losses and out-of-pocket (OOP) expenditures. Using a cross-sectional survey, it evaluates how income from cooling affects the unpaid care work that generates heat-related economic burdens. The results suggest that cooling-related income loss reductions benefited street vendors, synopses, and auxiliary construction workers the most, who were the greatest beneficiaries of improved cooling resources. On the other hand, unpaid care work increases income loss among home-based pieceworkers. The average heat stress-related income loss is estimated at 19%, which is nearly BDT 1,950 per worker per month. The total economic loss, when extended to the informal workforce of Rajshahi, is estimated to be between BDT 94 million and BDT 146 million per heat event. To complement these empirical findings and address reviewer recommendations, the paper introduces a concise intelligent heat-stress management framework integrating low-cost IoT microclimate sensing, lightweight predictive modeling, and mobile alerting to support early risk detection and worker protection. The study calls for policy interventions to enhance cooling access and adaptation for vulnerable informal workers facing climate change-induced heat stress.

title: RXV: Interpretable RF-Stacked CNNs for Brain Tumor MRI Classification
authors: Abu Tahim Habiby, Anika Bushra, Shaikh Ahmed Fayaz, Md. Esrat Hossan, Md. Mahim Khan, Md. Asaduzzaman Asaduzzaman
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Brain tumors cause significant deaths across the world with, early and correct diagnosis being crucial in enhancing patient survival. Although deep learning has shown impressive results in medical imaging, single convolutional neural networks (CNNs) can experience poor generalization because of inter-class similarity, small size of lesions and variations in image quality of MRI. The proposed study, RXV, aims to deal with these issues by combining three highest-performing CNNs ResNet50V2, VGG16 and Xception with a 100-tree Random Forest meta-learner. An equal sample of 6,004 brain MRI images of four categories (glioma, meningioma, pituitary tumor, and normal) was used, and CLAHE preprocessed, and augmented extensively to increase robustness. RXV obtained an accuracy of 99.56% on the test, which was more than the individual CNNs (maximum error was ResNet50V2 with a 99.33 percent), and minimized the error to 4 out of 901 tests. Grad-CAM was used to ensure interpretability, demonstrating the areas of tumor relevance. These results make RXV a consistent, understandable, and deployable CAD system.

title: Voltage-Dependent Dark Current Modeling in Thin-Film Solar Cells: Effects of Surface Recombination and Device Parameters
authors: Mesbahus Saleheen, A. Arefin, Chamak Ganguli, Shahana Akter
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: In thin-film solar cells, dark current serves as a key indicator of diode quality and recombination dynamics. Its dependence on applied voltage provides valuable information about both interfacial recombination process and bulk transport limitations. In this work, we present a comprehensive analytical model that captures the voltage-dependent behavior of dark current in thin-film devices. The model explicitly incorporates the influence of surface recombination velocity, carrier mobilities, and layer thicknesses on the dark current profile across a range of applied voltages. The model is validated using numerical simulations perfomed in SCAPS-1D for a simplified <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{n}^{+} \mathrm{p}$</tex> solar cell structure. Our results reveal that surface recombination at interfaces significantly alters the ideality factor and leakage current, especially in low-voltage regimes. Parameter variation studies highlight the importance of surface passivation and optimized layer design to minimize recombination losses and improve charge collection. The proposed framework offers a reproducible tool for diagnosing dark current behavior and guiding material and interface engineering in next-generation thin-film photovoltaics.

title: A Deep Learning and Explainable Ai Framework to Rank Solar Irradiance Zones in Southern Bangladesh
authors: Wazia Haque Onti, Safiul Haque Chowdhury, Mohammed Ibrahim Hussain, Muhammad Minoar Hossain, Mohammad Mamun
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Bangladesh currently faces a shortage of natural gas, inefficient power plants, and significant system losses, which prevent the country from meeting its electricity demand. In this case, solar energy utilization in Bangladesh has great potential, especially in southern Bangladesh. This study presents a data-driven framework for assessing and modeling multivariate environmental and solar energy data in the context of renewable energy research. The workflow starts with collecting and preprocessing the datasets to guarantee data quality and uniformity. Exploratory data visualization: Heatmaps and KDE plot analyses are a refinement of the classics, primarily centered around understanding feature distributions and relationships. In the following phases, the model is trained, and predictions are made from the extracted feature representations using Autoencoder-based modeling for unsupervised feature extraction and performance analysis with standard metrics evaluating reconstruction quality. Then, the reduced feature space is used for K-means clustering and validation using clustering validity indices such as Silhouette Score, Calinski-Harabasz Index, and Davies-Bouldin Index. Lastly, classification modeling and SHAP are executed to explain our model's outcome interpretation through the CatBoost classifier. Finally, solar zones are ranked utilizing the clustering score, and a visualization map determines geographically advantageous areas to locate solar energy.

title: Enhancing Bangla Spam SMS Detection with BERT: A Deep Learning Perspective
authors: Jobaida Abedin, Yasha Fatema, Farzana Tasnim, Fariba Tasnia Khan, Tanjim Mahmud, Rakhimjon Rajapboyevich Rakhimov, Valisher Sapayev Odilbek Uglu, Abubokor Hanip, Mohammad Shahadat Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Spam messages pose a persistent problem in digital communication, particularly for low-resource languages like Bangla. The complex grammatical structure of Bangla, frequent code-mixing with English, and informal writing styles make spam detection far more challenging than in high-resource languages such as English. This study explores a comparative deep learning approach for classifying Bangla spam SMS messages. The models evaluated include Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM), Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT), BanglaBERT, XLM-BERT, and a hybrid BiLSTM + CNN model. A balanced dataset of 2,004 Bangla SMS messages- 1,030 spam and 971 ham-has been prepared for training and evaluation. Among the tested models, BanglaBERT has achieved the highest accuracy of 98.5 %. This outcome has demonstrated the strength of language-specific transformer models in capturing the contextual and structural nuances of Bangla text. Explainable AI (XAI) methods, including LIME and SHAP, have been applied to interpret the model's internal decision-making process and ensure transparency. The analysis has revealed that the words “ফ্রি”, “অফার”, and “জয়” are the most influential indicators for identifying spam messages. The proposed framework has achieved an effective balance between performance and interpretability, setting a new benchmark for Bangla spam SMS detection. This work contributes to the growing field of Bangla Natural Language Processing (NLP) and illustrates that combining sequential learning with contextual modeling can effectively address the challenges of spam detection in low-resource languages.

title: Noninvasive Assessment of Lung Fluid Accumulation Using Tetrapolar Impedance Measurement
authors: Mahjabin Mobarak, Habiba Begum, Md. Moniruzzaman, Ruhul Amin, Abdullah Al Mahfazur Rahman
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Electrical impedance technique is a noninvasive and low-cost technique which plays a very significant role in bioimpedance measurement. Lung being dynamic organ, where the impedance changes during respiration, and the changes of impedance is an indicator of lungs condition. This study investigates the performance of a six-electrode Tetrapolar Impedance Measurement (TPIM) configuration in isolating and monitoring the left and right lungs individually, with a focus on the impact of fluid accumulation in the lower part of the right lung. Simulations were conducted using a phantom with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$663 \text{cm}^{3}$</tex> of fluid introduced into the lower segment of the right lung. 6electrode TPIM configurations were implemented by placing electrode on the right side (RS) and left side (LS) of the thorax to assess sensitivity to each lung. Results indicated that in the absence of fluid, percentage changes in total impedance during respiration were nearly identical for RS and LS placements (∼ 34%). However, with fluid in the right lung, the RS placement showed a significant drop in percentage change (from 34.65% to 9.76%), while the LS placement remained largely unaffected (35.24%). This validates the TPIM's ability to distinguish between lung conditions. Additionally, simulations at various vertical electrode placements showed that the percentage change increased gradually with elevation, reaching a peak about 15 cm, indicating sensitivity to fluid location.

title: Fundamental-Frequency Switching Strategy for a Five-Level Cascaded H-Bridge Inverter Using Selective Harmonic Elimination
authors: Sadikur Rahman, Fahim Shaharia, Md Zunaid Hossen, Samia Sultana Tuly, Ziam Chowdhury Noun
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This paper presents the design, simulation, and experimental validation of a single-phase five-level Cascaded Hbridge (CHB) inverter employing Selective Harmonic Elimination (SHE) with Newton-Raphson-based switching-angle computation. The topology comprises two H-bridge cells fed by isolated DC sources, synthesizing a five-level stepped output <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left\{-2 V_{d c},-V_{d c}, 0, V_{d c}, 2 V_{d c}\right\}$</tex>. A Fourier model is derived and SHE equations with two switching angles per quarter-cycle <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\alpha_{1}, \alpha_{2})$</tex> are formulated to set the fundamental while canceling dominant low-order harmonics <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(5^{{th }}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7^{{th }})$</tex>. Angles are solved iteratively via Newton-Raphson and executed on a controller that drives the CHB power stage at fundamental frequency. A MATLAB/Simulink model is used to verify waveform synthesis, RMS voltage, and harmonic spectra. Hardware measurements using a Fluke power analyzer show close agreement with theory; for a representative case with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha_{1} \approx 17.9^{\circ}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha_{2} \approx 57.4^{\circ}$</tex>, the analyzer reported <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{\text{rms}} \approx 16.87 \mathbf{V}$</tex> at 49.2 Hz and Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 20.4 \%$</tex>. The results demonstrate that five-level SHE with fundamental-frequency switching achieves effective harmonic control without high-frequency PWM, making the approach well-suited to cost-sensitive UPS, renewable energy interfaces, and low-frequency motor drives.

title: Bengali Fake Shield: Developing an Efficient Transformer-based Fake News Detection System for Bengali Language
authors: Ranjana Karmaker, Fariya Islam, Tasnia Ishrat Khan, Promi Madhu, Sayma Alam Suha
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Fake news is a huge threat to social trust and cohesion whichhave little resources,particularly in languages such as Bengali. But fake news is hard to find in these languages: There are not many annotated datasets and the grammar structurescan be complicated. In this work we introduce Bengali FakeShield, a strong detecting system offake news in Bengali based on transformer models. We used theBanFakeNews dataset with 48,000 real news and 1,000 fake news. It also addresses class imbalanced problemby applying SMOTE and upsampling. A exhaustive analysis of diverse models (including traditional machine learning and deep-learning approaches) was carriedout. Bangla-BERT performed the best among them, interms of accuracy by 99.97% and recall by 100 %. The final model was then integrated intoa Flutter app optimised for real-time inference using TensorFlow Lite. This work provides a scalable method for addressing misinformation in Bengali and demonstrates the transformers effectiveness for low-resourcelanguage settings.

title: Integrating HTS Technology in Wind Energy Systems: A Case for Sustainable Power in Bangladesh
authors: Nuzhat Ahmed Rudhy, Sabrina Alam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Bangladesh's energy consumption is increasing while its natural gas reserves are declining making it necessary for the country to explore renewable energy sources. Wind power holds great promise but traditional turbines are limited by high costs and can be inefficient. This study is a feasibility survey that investigates the integration of High-Temperature Superconducting (HTS) generators into wind turbines for the coastal regions of Bangladesh. Using wind resource data and MATLAB simulations, HTS machines were compared with Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generators (PMSGs). Results show that HTS turbines yield <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 - 1 2 \%}$</tex> more energy at mean wind speeds of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5-6 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$</tex>, while reducing nacelle weight and improving efficiency. Economic analysis indicates higher onshore costs but <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim \mathbf{5} \boldsymbol{\%}$</tex> lower Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) in offshore fixed and floating applications. This study validates the positive impacts on the power sector of Bangladesh of incorporating HTS technology into the next generation wind turbines by performing simulative studies.

title: Towards Real-Time Tea Leaf Disease Management: A Deep Learning Solution for Resource-Constrained Environments
authors: Zakia Sultana Simin, Md. Raisul Islam, Saiba Aabira, Arnob Ghosh, Arshean Subah, Kazi Newaj Faisal
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: As one of the most consumed beverages worldwide, tea holds substantial economic importance in Bangladesh, contributing significantly to both production and employment. Nevertheless, tea plants are susceptible to numerous diseases that can adversely affect crop yield and quality. This research introduces a deep learning methodology for the automated detection of tea leaf diseases, utilizing the teaLeafBD dataset. This dataset comprises <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5, 2 7 8}$</tex> high-resolution images of tea leaves sourced from various tea estates in Bangladesh, encompassing six distinct tea leaf diseases and one category for healthy leaves. An efficient and lightweight convolutional neural pretrained network (EfficientNetV2B0), was utilized for the proposed model. The proposed model was successful in classifying diseases, achieving a comprehensive accuracy of 95.45 %. The proposed model demonstrated superior performance compared to other lightweight baseline models and previously developed models, exhibiting high classification accuracy alongside computational efficiency. The efficacy of the model was also assessed through metrics such as precision, recall, F1-score, and Cohen's Kappa, underscoring its robustness, particularly in addressing class imbalance. Additionally, the study highlights the model's potential for real-time use in devices like mobile phones and drones, giving tea farmers in Bangladesh a practical way to manage diseases and boost productivity.

title: Fabrication Defect Detection and Characterization of Semiconductor Devices from SEM Images Using Morphological Image Processing
authors: Sakhawat Hossen Rakib, A. S. M. Bakibillah, Tazin Hossain, Muhammad Towfiqur Rahman, Md Shafiul Alam, Md. Shofiqul Islam, Tasnia Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This paper presents defect detection and characterization methods of semiconductor devices using morphological image processing. The proposed algorithm can detect various processing and fabrication defects, such as cracks, delamination, EOS, and ESD damage, from SEM images of the devices. It can also characterize the defects based on properties of the defected region. Like other image processing computational methods of defect detection; this algorithm does not need any reference image to compare with the inspected image. The algorithm involves different steps like filtering, global thresholding, segmentation, edge detection, feature extraction and defect characterization. The algorithm is implemented using MATLAB R2018b Software. The proposed method is cost-effective and less time-consuming for defect detection of semiconductor devices.

title: Secrecy Analysis of Pinching-Antenna Systems
authors: Osamah S. Badarneh, Hugerles S. Silva, Meysam Ghanbari, Yazan H. Al-Badarneh, Tamer M. Khattab, Mazen O. Hasna, Khalid A. Qaraqe
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: In this paper, we investigate the performance of physical-layer security of a pinching-antenna system on a lossless dielectric waveguide. In particular, the system uses a single pinching-antenna to convey confidential information from a base station to a legitimate destination equipped with a single antenna, while an eavesdropper, also equipped with a single antenna, attempts to decode the transmitted information. As such, the performance of the pinching-antenna system is evaluated in terms of average secrecy capacity, strictly positive secrecy capacity, and secrecy outage probability. To this end, accurate mathematical expressions for the aforementioned performance metrics are provided. To validate the analysis, the analytical results are numerically evaluated and further validated through MonteCarlo simulations. The results demonstrate that secrecy capacity between the base station and the legitimate destination improves when the height of the pinching-antenna placed closer to the destination. Additionally, the performance can be improved when the eavesdropper's location over a rectangular area increases.

title: Corrections to “Semi-Classical Monte Carlo Simulation of Quantum Confinement Effects in Si Nanosheet and Fin FETs in Industry Standard Orientation”
authors: Uiho Lee, Jaeyeon Kim, Byoung Hak Hong, Wookhyun Kwon, Branch T. Archer, Leonard F. Register, Sanjay K. Banerjee, Jiwon Chang
abstract: Presents corrections to the paper, (“Semi-Classical Monte Carlo Simulation of Quantum Confinement Effects in Si Nanosheet and Fin FETs in Industry Standard Orientation”).

title: Knowledge-Driven Memetic Algorithm with Forgetting Mechanism for the Energy-Aware Flexible Job-Shop Scheduling Problem with Variable Machining Speeds
authors: Hongquan Qu, Peng Shi, Maolin Cai, Yibo Li, Xiaomeng Tong
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Against the backdrop of global energy conservation, emission reduction, and sustainable development, energy-aware scheduling in manufacturing workshops has become a growing focus. However, existing research on the energy-aware flexible job-shop scheduling problem (EFJSP) with variable machining speeds primarily considers energy consumption at the job level, neglecting the energy heterogeneity of individual operations under different constraints, a factor of key environmental significance. To address this problem, this paper innovatively incorporates processing quality constraints to define upper limits on variable speeds. The study develops a mathematical model for the speed-adjustable flexible job shop scheduling problem considering operation-level energy consumption. Considering limitations in current multi-neighborhood structure selection, we propose a knowledge-driven memetic algorithm with a forgetting mechanism (KDMA-FM). The model's effectiveness is validated through comparisons with the exact solver OR-Tools on a simplified system. Furthermore, KDMA-FM's performance is evaluated against existing algorithms across instances of varying scales. Results demonstrate that KDMA-FM outperforms benchmark algorithms in over 80% of instances and achieves notable energy savings.

title: CAOA - Completion-Assisted Object-CAD Alignment
authors: Hiranya Garbha Kumar, Minhas Kamal, Balakrishnan Prabhakaran
abstract: Accurately aligning CAD models to their corresponding objects in indoor RGB-D scans is a central challenge in 3D semantic reconstruction. The task requires estimating a 9-Degree-of-Freedom (DoF) pose-position, rotation, and scale along three axes-but is hindered by noisy and incomplete scans, as well as segmentation errors that cause geometric distortions. We present Completion-Assisted Ob-ject-CAD Alignment (CAOA), a method that integrates a semantically and contextually aware point cloud completion module with a symmetry-aware relative pose estimation algorithm, enabling precise alignment of CAD models to scanned objects. Existing completion methods are typically trained and evaluated on synthetic datasets, which often fail to generalize to real-world scans. To bridge this gap, we introduce a synthetic data generation strategy tailored to indoor scenes, significantly reducing the synthetic-to-real domain gap-validated through quantitative comparisons with widely used completion datasets. In addition, we release S 2 C-Completion, an expert-annotated dataset of over 8,500 object-CAD pairs from Scan2CAD, created for real-world indoor single-object completion and intended as a new benchmark for this task. For object-CAD alignment, we incorporate symmetry information via a symmetry-aware loss, improving robustness to symmetric ambiguities. On the Scan2CAD benchmark, CAOA achieves a 17 % accuracy improvement over state-of-the-art methods. All code, datasets, and annotation tools will be publicly available on GitHub.

title: Simulation-Based Modeling and Standing Stabilization of a Bird-Inspired Bipedal Robot with Multi-Joint Dynamics with Contact
authors: Alejandra Paola Ticlla Gonzalez, Ricardo Sebastian Arce Calero, Eddie Angel Sobrado Malpartida, Jose Luis Becerra Felipe, Ayrton Krickst Nieves Acosta, Nain Maximo Ramos Alvarez
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: This work presents the modeling and simulation of a bipedal robot that is structurally humanoid while adopting locomotion principles inspired by avian gait. Rather than replicating bird anatomy, the proposed robot employs a simplified lowerlimb configuration composed of femur, tibia, and ankle joints, emphasizing heel-less support and a forward-leaning posture characteristic of bird locomotion. The system is implemented in a physics-based simulation environment using the MuJoCo engine to support the development and validation of baseline standing balance control strategies over short time horizons. A joint-level proportional-derivative (PD) control framework, augmented with hierarchical stabilization of the floating base, is employed to achieve and maintain a stable initial upright posture under gravity and foot-ground contact interactions. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed model can maintain a consistent standing configuration with bounded joint motion and physically plausible contact behavior over short durations, validating the mechanical model and control structure. The presented framework emphasizes mechanical realism, structured control design, and reproducible simulation setup, providing a baseline platform for future extensions toward dynamic locomotion control.

title: Are Pose Estimators Ready for the Open World? STAGE: A GenAI Toolkit for Auditing 3D Human Pose Estimators
authors: Nikita Kister, István Sárándi, Jiayi Wang, Anna Khoreva, Gerard Pons-Moll
abstract: For safety-critical applications, it is crucial to audit 3D human pose estimators before deployment. Will the system break down if the weather or the clothing changes? Is it robust regarding gender and age? To answer these questions and more, we need controlled studies with images that differ in a single attribute, but real benchmarks cannot provide such pairs. We thus present STAGE, a GenAI data toolkit for auditing 3D human pose estimators. For STAGE, we develop the first GenAI image creator with accurate 3D pose control and propose a novel evaluation strategy to isolate and quantify the effects of single factors such as gender, ethnicity, age, clothing, location, and weather. Enabled by STAGE, we generate a series of benchmarks to audit, for the first time, the sensitivity of popular pose estimators towards such factors. Our results show that natural variations can severely degrade pose estimator performance, raising doubts about their readiness for open-world deployment. We aim to highlight these robustness issues and establish STAGE as a benchmark to quantify them.

title: Learning High-Fidelity Garment Deformation via Skinning-Free Image Transfer
authors: Rong Wang, Wei Mao, Changsheng Lu, Hongdong Li
abstract: We present a novel method for generating 3D garment deformations from underlying body poses, which is key to a wide range of applications, including virtual try-on and extended reality. To simplify the cloth dynamics, existing methods mostly rely on linear blend skinning to obtain low-frequency posed garment shape and only regress highfrequency wrinkles. However, due to the lack of explicit skinning supervision, such skinning-based approach often produces misaligned shapes when posing the garment, consequently corrupts the high-frequency signals and fails to recover high-fidelity wrinkles. To tackle this issue, we propose a skinning-free approach by independently estimating posed (i) vertex position for low-frequency posed garment shape, and (ii) vertex normal for high-frequency local wrinkle details. In this way, each frequency modality can be effectively decoupled and directly supervised by the geometry of the deformed garment. To further improve the visual quality of deformation, we propose to encode both vertex attributes as rendered texture images, so that 3D garment deformation can be equivalently achieved via 2D image transfer. This enables us to leverage powerful pretrained image models to recover fine-grained visual details in wrinkles, while maintaining superior scalability for garments of diverse topologies without relying on manual UV partition. Finally, we propose a multimodal fusion to incorporate constraints from both frequency modalities and robustly recover deformed 3D garments from transferred images. Extensive experiments show that our method significantly improves animation quality on various garment types and recovers finer wrinkles than state-of-the-art methods.

title: PASTA: Controllable Part-Aware Shape Generation with Autoregressive Transformers
authors: Songlin Li, Despoina Paschalidou, Leonidas Guibas
abstract: The increased demand for tools that automate the 3D content creation process led to tremendous progress in deep generative models that can generate diverse 3D objects of high fidelity. In this paper, we present PASTA, an autoregressive transformer architecture for generating high quality 3D shapes. PASTA comprises two main components: An autoregressive transformer that generates objects as a sequence of cuboidal primitives and a blending network, implemented with a transformer decoder that composes the sequences of cuboids and synthesizes high quality meshes for each object. Our model is trained in two stages: First we train our autoregressive generative model using only annotated cuboidal parts as supervision and next, we train our blending network using explicit 3D supervision, in the form of watertight meshes. Evaluations on various ShapeNet objects showcase the ability of our model to perform shape generation from diverse inputs e.g. from scratch, from a partial object, from text and images, as well size-guided generation, by explicitly conditioning on a bounding box that defines the object's boundaries. Moreover, as our model considers the underlying part-based structure of a 3D object, we are able to select a specific part and produce shapes with meaningful variations of this part. As evidenced by our experiments, our model generates 3D shapes that are both more realistic and diverse than existing part-based and non part-based methods, while at the same time is simpler to implement and train.

title: Implicit Preference Learning for Adaptive Human-Robot Collaboration Using Task Graphs
authors: Liliana Antão, João Reis, Gil Gonçalves
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: As human-robot collaboration (HRC) becomes increasingly essential in industrial settings, ensuring flexible and adaptive interaction is critical for enhancing productivity and worker safety. This paper presents a framework designed to enable true HRC by using task graphs, real-time human motion prediction, and online preference learning to adapt robot actions based on operator preferences and behaviors. The framework was implemented in a car axle assembly task. The system dynamically adjusted its behavior using task graphs that represented the task as granular operations such as grabbing, placing, and dropping. Human movements were predicted and actions classified using an LSTM network to anticipate and learn operator preferences within the task graph. Additionally, voice control allowed operators to command the robot during critical actions such as dropping heavy components. Experimental results with six operators demonstrated that the system achieved high adaptability, with workflow interruptions reducing by 77.6% over 15 trials and task completion time improving by 27.5%. The ability to adapt in real-time to individual operator preferences represents a significant improvement over traditional fixed-sequence workflows.
