title: A Rapid Pipeline for Training and Deploying ML Models on WeBe Band
authors: Ehsan Kourkchi, Asmita Asmita, Houman Homayoun, Mahdi Eslamimehr
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Developing optimized machine-learning algorithms for edge devices with limited computational and memory resources is challenging, time-consuming, and highly dependent on device-specific constraints. In this work, we streamline an edge ML workflow to enable rapid development, optimization, and deployment of machine-learning (ML) models directly on the WeBe Band, a wrist-worn wearable device designed for multimodal physiological data monitoring. The proposed system automatically generates hardware-efficient ML models that can be easily integrated into the WeBe core firmware, supporting AutoML, hardware-aware quantization, and performance profiling to build models that meet desired latency targets while remaining compatible with device memory and power limitations.The proposed framework tightly integrates the open source Piccolo AI ecosystem with an automated pipeline that generates deployable firmware artifacts, performs hardware-aware model compilation, and supports over-the-air (OTA) deployment. The system supports multiple lightweight model classes, including classical machine-learning algorithms and neural networks, and provides built-in on-device profiling tools to evaluate inference latency and memory footprint under realistic execution conditions. Experimental results demonstrate clear trade-offs between model complexity and deployability on a microcontroller, showing that classical models offer strong real-time performance while lightweight neural networks require careful resource management.Rather than proposing new learning architectures, the current work mainly focuses on system-level automation, deployability, and enabling researchers and developers to rapidly iterate on models and evaluate them directly on target hardware. Although demonstrated on the WeBe Band platform, the workflow is designed to be extensible to other ML-powered edge devices.

title: Active Interposers for High Bandwidth Memory
authors: Andres Ayes, Eby G. Friedman
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Integrating the functionality of the base die supporting high bandwidth memory stacks into an active interposer is an effective method to increase physical density while lowering cost. Yield and cost models are presented to compare conventional architectures that use a dedicated base die per stack with configurations that place the necessary logic within a shared active interposer. Yield, latency, and cost comparisons across several technology nodes, interposer areas, and memory stack configurations are discussed. Active interposers with mature technology nodes (e.g., 65 nm) can offer meaningful savings, while more advanced nodes (e.g., 11 nm) are beneficial in configurations composed of multiple memory stacks and smaller area per stack. Memory fetch latencies of passive and active interposers are compared. Integrating base die functionality into an active interposer is shown to be most effective in systems composed of multiple memory stacks and several DRAM layers (e.g., four layers), achieving cost reductions of approximately $20 \%$. The cost advantage diminishes with increasing number of DRAM layers.

title: Handle-based Mesh Deformation Guided By Vision Language Model_supp1-3695016.pdf
authors: Xingpeng Sun
abstract: Mesh deformation is a fundamental tool in 3D content manipulation. Despite extensive prior research, existing approaches often suffer from low output quality, require significant manual tuning, or depend on data-intensive training. To address these limitations, we introduce a training-free, handle-based mesh deformation method. Our core idea is to leverage a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to interpret and manipulate a handle-based interface. We begin by applying cone singularity detection to identify a sparse set of potential handles. The VLM is then prompted to select both the deformable sub-parts of the mesh and the handles that best align with user instructions. Subsequently, we query the desired deformed positions of the selected handles in screen space. To reduce uncertainty inherent in VLM predictions, we aggregate the results from multiple camera views using a novel multi-view voting scheme. Across a suite of benchmarks, our method produces deformations that align more closely with user intent, as measured by CLIP and GPTEval3D scores, while introducing low distortion—quantified via membrane energy. In summary, our approach is highly automated and consistently delivers high-quality mesh deformations.

title: Multi-Module Cooperative Triggering Strategy for Pulse Power Supply Based on Online Prediction
authors: Xin Su, Tao Ma, Xiao Zhang, Yufeng Dai, Junyong Lu
abstract: This article proposes an online prediction-based cooperative triggering strategy with dynamic adjustment to address the challenges of current waveform control in multi-module pulse power supply (PPS) systems. A nonlinear system model incorporating time-varying load impedance is established. This model enables the real-time prediction of current transients based on active current feedback and the dynamic adjustment of discharge timing for each pulse-forming unit (PFU). This approach achieves the precise synthesis of a flat-top current waveform under highly nonlinear and time-varying load conditions. Simulation results demonstrate that, compared to the offline iterative calculation method, the proposed method significantly reduces current ripple while maintaining a constant target muzzle velocity. Specifically, it reduces the peak overload by 12.6% and the peak jerk (the time derivative of acceleration) by 13.6%. Consequently, this study provides a practical and efficient solution for achieving high-precision, low-impulse control in modern PPS applications.

title: A Dynamic-Static Transition Loss Minimization Control Strategy for IPMSM Drives with Improved Model Predictive Current Control
authors: Cheng Song, Jinhua Du, Guoqiang Zhang, Boyan Zhang, Han Wu, Geping Xu
abstract: This paper proposes a dynamic-static transition control (DSTC) strategy for interior permanent magnet synchronous motors (IPMSM) to achieve real-time optimization of both static-state efficiency and dynamic performance. Based on the IPMSM loss model considering iron loss, a fourth-order nonlinear total loss function with respect to current is derived. A hybrid approach combining the simplified method and the Newton downhill algorithm realizes the loss model-based control (LMC) during static state, obtaining the optimal current within one 100-μs control period using a few iterations. To compensate the torque reduction caused by the flux-weakened effect of the LMC, a compensation current method is analytically formulated. Moreover, an accurate dynamic activation scheme is developed to ensure that the compensation current only works during dynamic process. In addition, an improved model predictive current control incorporating the iron loss model further enhances dynamic response and current tracking. Finally, experiments are conducted on a 1.5-kW IPMSM platform to validate that the proposed DSTC-MPCC strategy achieves both loss minimization in static state and high dynamic performance in dynamic state compared with conventional strategies.

title: Multi-source Fusion Positioning Revisited by Drawing on Human Thinking Process
authors: Jianfan Chen, Qingquan Li, Ke Dong, Jianghai Liao, Dejin Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Reliable and long-term positioning is a fundamental requirement for deploying robots across various real-world scenes. Although multi-source fusion has been widely used to enhance positioning robustness, most existing methods are designed for specific scenes and lack generalizability. This reveals that the limitations of current approaches do not stem from a lack of technical tools, but rather from the robot's inability to adaptively select suitable positioning strategies under different spatiotemporal conditions. Inspired by human thinking process—such as information filtering, memory reliability evaluation, and knowledge-based reasoning when faced with multiple potential positioning cues—this paper systematically rethinks the design of multi-source fusion positioning. We propose a novel framework with self-assessment capabilities, aiming to make real-time decisions on the optimal source combination scheme to provide continuous and robust positioning results. Experimental results further verify the robustness of the proposed method under environmental uncertainty, demonstrating its potential in unifying the source selection process in multi-source fusion positioning.

title: Automated and Near-Flawless Micropipette PIH Assembly to MEMS Nanoforce Sensor With A Robotic Microscopy System
authors: Wendi Gao, Yujie Yang, Wenji Cui, Yang Lv, Libo Zhao, Zhuangde Jiang, Dong Sun
abstract: Peg-in-hole (PIH) assembly of micropipettes to micro-electromechanical system (MEMS) nanoforce sensors can be performed to improve spatial probing resolution and operation space, but their spatial attitude angle deviations and <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off</small>-axial distances lead to unstable assembly, sensor performance deterioration, and even structural damage. In this article, an automated and near-flawless micropipette PIH assembly method was proposed based on the built robotic microscopy system, to the best of our knowledge, for the first time. Utilizing an ISPR-based method with a mechanical equilibrium model and a sliding mode controller, the planar deflection angle was adjusted below 0.07<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> without being pushed out of FOV. The out-of-plane inclination angle was then reduced to below 0.05<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> by the proposed GTC-based method, whose depth is obtained from a feasible FDTBR inclination metrology and mechanical contact model. The <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off</small>-axial distance was actively adjusted by an AIHM-based recenter method during sensor probe insertion, leading to a high assembly successful rate of 100% with negligible contact forces of 1.64 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\boldsymbol{\mu}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>N. The methodology effectiveness was validated through calibration tests, wherein no structural damage and performance deterioration were produced on the processed sensor for a long-term usage, indicating its great potential in nanoforce measurement.

title: Predictive Verification of Reliability Evaluation for Multiple Interconnected Microgrids
authors: Mohamed Abdelghany, Adnan Rashid, Sofiène Tahar
abstract: Modern power systems increasingly rely on interconnected microgrids (MGs) integrating a high proportion of renewable energy sources (RES). However, the intermittent nature of RES introduces significant reliability challenges, particularly in island-mode operation. Traditional Monte Carlo (MC) sampling-based simulations, while widely used, suffer from randomness-induced variability and lack provable guarantees of accuracy. Recent Artificial Intelligence (AI)-based approaches have shown potential in handling complex operational data but often rely on large training datasets and lack mathematical interpretability. This article introduces a novel formal verification-based approach that enables a more precise and comprehensive reliability assessment of interconnected smart MGs at the subsystem level. We illustrate the capability of our proposed formal analysis method by evaluating important MG reliability and energy indices, such as average service availability index, average service unavailability index, average system curtailment index, energy not supplied index, loss of energy expectation, loss of load expectation, and energy index of reliability. Subsequently, we compare our obtained results with those of MATLAB-based MC simulations and mathematical manual paper-and-pencil analysis.

title: Robust Jamming Detection for Weak and Strong Signal Powers Using Pilot Subspace Projection
authors: Amir Mehrabian, Georges Kaddoum
abstract: Reliable jamming detection is essential for ensuring the security ofwireless communication.Multi-antenna receivers can effectively detect a jammer’s signal fromthat of the legitimate node, and several studies have used singular value (SV)-basedmethods for this task.However, these methods frequently suffer degraded performance when the legitimate transmitting node (TN) has a weak signal. In such cases, their detection probability remains low and fixed even under strong jamming, demonstrating a critical sensitivity to the TN’s signal power that was not addressed in previous research. To address this gap, we propose a jamming detection framework based on pilot subspace projection. Accordingly, two detectors are derived: the rank-one (R1) detector and its constant false alarm rate (R1-CFAR) variant. Simulation results confirm that the proposed methods achieve consistently higher detection probability and robustness as compared to existing SV-based detectors, with at least 40% improvement in detection probability under weak TN signal scenarios.

title: High-Isolation Free-Space Optical Circulator for High-Capacity Bidirectional Satellite Links
authors: Takashi Kan, Kensuke Murakami, Akihiko Nishitani, Akira Akasaka, Shota Ishimura, Tomonori Yazaki, Shin Fukuhara, Hidenori Takahashi, Takehiro Tsuritani
abstract: High-capacity satellite free-space optical (FSO) communication systems require effective transmit–receive (Tx Rx) separation to enable bidirectional operation while handling large power disparities between Tx and Rx signals. Conventional Tx–Rx separation schemes based on spatial, polarization, wavelength, or temporal domains provide high isolation but inherently limit achievable capacity by consuming degrees of freedom that could otherwise be used for signal multiplexing. Optical circulators (CIRs) represent a promising alternative; however, the Tx–Rx isolation of existing devices is insufficient for satellite FSO applications. In this paper, we present the development of a high-isolation free-space optical circulator (FSO CIR) achieving 90 dB isolation over the 1535–1567 nm wavelength range. We show that Tx–Rx crosstalk (XT) in conventional CIRs originates from back-reflected light generated by internal reflection and scattering, and we provide a quantitative evaluation of this back-reflected light based on both theoretical analysis and experimental measurements. By systematically suppressing these reflections, the proposed FSO-CIR achieves significantly enhanced isolation. Furthermore, we demonstrate a 1-Tb/s bidirectional optical communication experiment using the proposed FSO-CIR and show that the improved isolation enhances demodulation performance in the forward direction under bidirectional operation compared with a conventional CIR. These results indicate the potential of high-isolation FSO-CIRs for future satellite FSO communication systems.

title: Large-Scale and High-Sensitivity Quasi-Distributed Vibration Sensing Based on Time-Division Multiplexed Dual CFBGs
authors: Xin Gui, Zhe Wang, Changjia Wang, Yiming Wang, Jiaqi Wang, Zhengying Li
abstract: To meet the technical requirements for high sensitivity quasi-distributed vibration sensing in fields such as structural health monitoring (SHM), energy exploration, and underwater acoustic surveillance, this study proposes an innovative quasi-distributed point-like vibration sensing scheme based on dual chirped fiber Bragg gratings (CFBGs). By utilizing pairs of CFBGs with a 20-mm spacing as point-like sensing units and leveraging the low temporal coherence of the Amplified Spontaneous Emission (ASE) source, the proposed scheme effectively avoids self-interference of the reflected light without altering the optical pulse modulation width. Incorporating path matched interferometry, the system realizes high-sensitivity phase detection for single-point vibration, achieving a sensitivity of 33.82 dB re rad/g and an acceleration resolution of 11.4 μg/√Hz at 100Hz. By combining ultra-short gauge length sensing with time-division multiplexing (TDM), the system successfully realizes the large-scale multiplexing of 240 sensing units at a sampling rate of 10 kHz. This scheme overcomes the technical bottleneck of traditional methods in balancing high sensitivity with large-scale multiplexing capacity in quasi-distributed vibration detection, providing a novel solution for applications requiring large-scale, high-sensitivity, and precise point-based vibration monitoring.

title: Sparsity Aware Pre-processing for Systolic Array Dataflow Acceleration
authors: Tadikonda Venkata Sai Chaitanya, D V Bhargav, Madhav Rao
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Structured sparsity is key to reducing the computational cost of neural networks. However, conventional systolic array (SA) architectures fail to leverage sparse weights, performing redundant multiply-accumulate operations on zero elements. This paper proposes a novel pre-processing block that reorders rows and columns of the sparse weight matrix prior to systolic array execution. The experimental analysis of this reordering enhances data locality and computational alignment, thereby significantly reducing the number of clock cycles required during matrix multiplication. Our approach enables more efficient utilization of the processing elements (PEs). This work designs $4 \times 4$ Output Stationary systolic array to validate its effectiveness on several neural networks, including LeNet - 5, VGG - 11, and ResNet - 50, across varying sparsity levels, demonstrating substantial cycle savings and performance gains. The hardware efficiency is evaluated using the ASAP $7 n m$ Regular $V_{t} P D K$, yielding a $18 \%$ and $36.22 \%$ increase in silicon footprint and static power due to the integration of a preprocessing block, yet leading to significant dynamic power savings of $50.18 \%$. The pre-processed sparsity reordering strategy achieves overall performance gain for ResNet - 50 model by $9 \%$ to $34 \%$, and for VGG - 11 model by $15 \%$ to $28 \%$. This lightweight optimization provides a practical path towards sparsity-aware acceleration in systolic array based architectures.

title: S-OPTQ: Rapid Quantization for Spiking Vision Transformers
authors: Zaidao Mei, Qinru Qiu
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have achieved state-of-the-art performance across a wide range of computer vision tasks. Their adaptation to spiking neural networks (SNNs) has also shown strong potential in applications such as classification, object detection, and transfer learning. However, deploying these models on neuromorphic hardware like Intel Loihi requires fixed-point implementations, making model quantization essential for maintaining energy efficiency. Current state-of-the-art quantization approaches for spiking ViTs rely on quantizationaware training (QAT), which is computationally expensive due to the $\mathcal{O}\left(T^{2}\right)$ time complexity of backpropagation through time (BPTT). To address this challenge, we propose S-OPTQ, a fast and accurate post-training quantization method based on OPTQ, tailored for spiking neural networks. S-OPTQ significantly reduces computational overhead while maintaining competitive accuracy with minimal performance degradation.

title: Multi-Level Cell Memory Driven Efficient Cache Organization
authors: Binu Christopher, Rwicheek Sarker, Vivek M. Bhasi, Sumitha George
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Emerging non-volatile multilevel cell (MLC) memories offer high density and exhibit ultra-low leakage power, making them ideal candidates over SRAM and eDRAM for larger caches in modern processors. However, the asymmetric access latency/energy among the states in an MLC memory cell presents opportunities for memory design optimisations. This paper leverages this MLC state asymmetry to propose cache schemes that optimize the tag/data access latency and energy efficiency. We propose to map all tag bits to the MSB to enable the All Tag in One Read (ATOR) scheme, which reduces hit latency and miss penalty by consolidating tag lookup into a single step MSB read. To further improve hit latency and energy efficiency, we propose to eliminate the data latency by having a priority way data block and reading the data along with all tag bits in the ATOR scheme. On top of that, we enhance cache capacity by utilising unused LSB bits in the tag array to store an extra data block. Our evaluation shows that the proposed ATOR-S/ATOR-P/ATOR-P-E achieves an average ${5. 9 \%} {/} {9. 2 \%} {/} {9. 4 \%}$ performance improvement (IPC) over the baseline design, respectively. Our evaluations also showcase further average performance (IPC) improvement of $12.7 \%$ due to the most recently used data’s placement in the priority way over the baseline design. ATORS achieves $11.7 \%$ reduction in average energy delay product compared to the baseline design on SPEC 2017 benchmarks.

title: Hybrid GCN-CNN Framework for Fast Timing-Driven Layer Assignment in Global Routing
authors: Sai Harika Julakanti, Vidya A. Chhabria
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Timing-driven layer assignment during global routing significantly impacts delay and congestion. Traditional methods rely on iterative heuristics with repeated timing and congestion analysis under the hood, leading to high runtime and limited scalability for modern designs. We present a hybrid machine learning (ML) framework that combines graph convolutional networks (GCNs) and convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for fast, timing- and congestion-driven layer assignment. GCNs model netlist connectivity and timing-critical paths, while CNNs extract spatial features such as capacity and utilization maps across the metal layer stack from a placed layout. Formulated as a multi-class classification problem, our model predicts the layer for every Steiner tree edge of a net. The ML model is trained on timing and congestion-driven global-routed layout data to predict optimized layer assignments rapidly in a single pass. Experiments on benchmark designs demonstrate that our approach improves worst-case and total negative slack post detailed route while achieving a speedup compared to traditional congestion and timing-driven methods.

title: Consumer Electronics in Healthcare and Medicine: a report from the EMBS Young Professional Roundtable
authors: Declan O’Loughlin, Ilaria Facchi, Neelesh Kumar, Patrycja Lebiecka-Johansen
publish: IEEE Journal of Translational Engineering in Health and Medicine
abstract: Wearable consumer electronics such as smart watches, rings and hearables are increasingly used to collect real-time physiological and activity data for health monitoring and disease prevention. As researchers and practitioners at the forefront of wearable technologies in health and medicine, the Young Professionals of EMBS organized a Roundtable Discussion on the 17<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</sup> of July 2025 at the 47<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</sup> Annual International Conference of the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society (EMBC’25) to identify key challenges and recommendations for the field and we would like to highlight some key recommendations and insights from our discussions for the broader community.

title: A Novel Bipolar AC–AC DVR Topology
authors: Ancheng Liu, Hyeong-Seung Moon, Sung-Jun Park
abstract: Voltage sags and swells can significantly degrade the performance of sensitive loads, requiring fast and reliable compensation solutions. This paper proposes a novel bipolar AC–AC dynamic voltage restorer (DVR) topology based on a direct AC–AC chopper structure. The key innovation lies in the integration of an energy-dissipation snubber circuit, which provides a continuous current path during dead-time and effectively eliminates current interruption without increasing control complexity. In addition, a peak-value-based control strategy is developed to achieve seamless bipolar voltage compensation without requiring coordinate transformation or complex modulation schemes. Compared with conventional AC–AC DVR structures, the proposed topology enables both inverting and non-inverting operation with simplified control and improved energy continuity. Experimental results validate the improved energy continuity. Experimental results validate the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed method, demonstrating enhanced dynamic performance, improved waveform quality, and strong robustness under various grid disturbances.

title: 5GLoc: Graph-Based Learning for Robust 5G Indoor Localization under Dynamic Anchor Visibility
authors: Teng Wang, Guoliang Chen, Jingxue Bi, Jiaqi Dong, Zitao Lin, Meng Sun, Jie Liu
abstract: With the evolution of Fifth-Generation (5G) wireless systems, high-resolution channel observations enabled by large bandwidths and massive Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) have opened new opportunities for indoor positioning. However, while existing fingerprinting-based learning methods excel in complex multipath environments, they often rely on fixed anchor configurations. This makes them vulnerable to dynamic anchor visibility caused by signal blockages, user mobility, or hardware failures in practical deployments. To address this, we propose 5G Graph-based Localization (5GLoc), a learning-driven framework designed for robust indoor positioning under dynamic anchor availability. By modeling localization instances as graphs, 5GLoc utilizes a Dynamic Graph Attention Network (DGAT) to flexibly accommodate varying numbers and combinations of anchors, ensuring robust inference under heterogeneous observations. We further introduce a Combinatorial Dynamic Node Masking (CDNM) strategy for data augmentation, as well as a composite loss function with learnable weights for balancing accuracy and stability. Finally, a map-constrained Particle Filter (PF) is integrated to refine trajectories for spatial feasibility and trajectory consistency. Experimental results based on the IPIN 2025 dataset demonstrate that 5GLoc achieves superior performance in complex warehouse environments, with a 75th percentile error of 0.857 m. Compared to several representative positioning methods, 5GLoc reduces the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) by 42.2%–80.2% and 50.6%–80.8%, respectively, maintaining high reliability even under extreme conditions with reduced anchor availability. This framework provides an effective solution for 5G localization in dynamic environments, meeting both precision and real-time requirements.

title: Cinematic Memories: EEG-Based Decoding of Recall Using Naturalistic Stimuli
authors: Ana Matran-Fernandez, Sebastian Halder
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive and Developmental Systems
abstract: Identifying neural correlates of long-term memory retrieval has been an area of considerable research. Previous studies used custom-built, static stimuli to elicit and study memory processes, limiting the insights into brain activity in a natural environment. Instead, we used movie clips to identify neural correlates of memory retrieval. We extracted features from the time-frequency domain from electroencephalography (EEG) signals to classify whether a 10-second-long video clip had been remembered, obtaining greater-than-chance memory decoding (median area under the receiver operating characteristic curve = 0.64) in a naturalistic movie-watching task with greater real-world validity than word-list or image recall paradigms. Feature analysis revealed that remembered clips, compared to not recognised clips, showed increased event-related desynchronisation (ERD) in the theta and low alpha bands early after stimulus onset, followed by stronger ERD in the alpha bands. While beta-band differences were observed at the group level, high within-class variability precluded their use for reliable single-trial classification, with theta and alpha activity emerging as the primary discriminative features. Our ERD/S analysis generalises observations previously demonstrated in the literature using simpler stimuli and more controlled experimental designs. Finally, we observed a significant correlation between classifier performance and retrospective memory ratings of participants (ρ = 0.49) demonstrating that individual differences in memory ability are reflected in neural activity patterns. This work demonstrates the potential of naturalistic stimuli to advance memory research using EEG, revealing neural dynamics that may not be detectable with simpler stimuli, with possible implications for the development of clinical tools in memory assessment.

title: Fine-Grained Detection and Analysis of Unknown Encrypted Malicious Traffic From Mixed Noisy Labels
authors: Qianwei Meng, Qingjun Yuan, Meng Shen, Siqi Lu, Guangsong Li, Jing Tao, Yong Yu, Yongjuan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: The efficacy of deep learning-based Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) is critically constrained by the availability of high-quality labeled data. In real-world environments, datasets often suffer from mixed label noise—consisting of both closed-set and open-set noise—which significantly distorts decision boundaries and leads to critical security misses for previously unknown attacks. Achieving robust classification of known traffic while accurately detecting unknown threats in the presence of such mixed noise remains a key challenge. In this paper, we introduce Sieve, a robust framework designed for the fine-grained detection and analysis of unknown encrypted malicious traffic in mixed noise conditions. Sieve consists of three collaborative modules: (i) a noise-resilient label correction module that filters out mixed noise using neighbor consistency metrics and confidence-based subset expansion; (ii) a post-hoc detection module that employs Mahalanobis distance in a purified, compact feature space to identify unknown traffic; and (iii) an unknown traffic labeling module that utilizes semi-supervised clustering to facilitate efficient updates of the dataset. Empirical evaluations across four public datasets demonstrate that Sieve significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both known-class classification and unknown-threat detection. Notably, on the Mal_TLS2023 dataset under 50% noise conditions, Sieve maintains robust performance, with accuracy and F1 scores exceeding 94%. Further theoretical analyses corroborate the superiority of the Sieve framework. The code is available at: https://github.com/niebikong/Sieve.

title: DefectSynth: Few-Shot Defective Image Generation by Modeling Shape and Appearance
authors: Dexu Zhao, Xukun Qin, Xinghui Dong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Since the acquisition of a large amount of defect data is expensive and time-consuming, the limited availability of defective samples impairs the accuracy and generalizability of defect detection methods. Although defect generation approaches have been explored for data augmentation, they usually suffer from either a lack of realism or limited diversity. To address these challenges, we propose a two-stage controllable few-shot<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup> defective image generation network, namely, DefectSynth<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, which models both the shape and appearance of defects. The first stage aims to generate continuous defect masks even with a few real masks. To this end, we propose a Hybrid Mask Interpolation (HMI) module, which performs interpolation in the image or latent space. The second stage is used to synthesize the defective appearance. We first fine-tune the pre-trained ControlNet, which is then used together with a pre-trained stable diffusion model to synthesize defective images. Given a mask generated in the first stage and a text prompt, they are integrated with a defect-free image to synthesize a high-fidelity defective image. To alleviate the issue of generation of indistinct defects with existing methods, we propose a Selective Attention Enhancement (SAE) mechanism that highlights the details of defects. We also design a Similarity-Based Feature Fusion (SFF) module to merge different local features, thereby further enriching the appearance diversity of defects. Using the defect data generated by DefectSynth, the classification accuracy on MVTec-AD has been improved from 44.03% to 66.70% compared with the baseline without synthetic data augmentation, while the F1-Score values computed on GDXray and DeepCrack for small-defect localization have been increased from 70.48% to 76.64% and from 70.08% to 83.27%, respectively. These performance gains should be due to the fact that our method is able to generate realistic and diverse defective images by modeling both the shape and appearance of defects.

title: ConvNeXt-Based Classifier for Early Detection of Autism Spectrum Disorder(ASD) From a Generalized Facial Image Dataset
authors: Aishwarya Mazumder, Jannat Tohfa, Md Mahbubur Rahman
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is a condition of neurodevelopment disorder, which is associated with failure of communication, socialization and behavior. Early diagnosis is very important to allow timely interventions and ease the longterm outcomes. The current paper introduces an ASD detection system based on deep learning models and analysis of face images, where ConvNeXt is the main part of the system. The vast, heterogeneous, and highly processed body of publiclyavailable facial images was created, divided into autistic and nonautistic groups, and filtered to guarantee the generalization to various sources. ConvNeXt was fine-tuned and tested on standard performance metrics, such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and area under the curve (AUC) and reached a 98 % classification rate. Other state-of-the-art transfer learning models such as ResNet50, DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, and MobileNetV2 were also tested to compare them with the model. Our findings suggest that ConvNeXt has better predictive performance, as well as generalization, which provides a reliable, non-invasive, and scalable AI-assisted screening of ASD. The given methodology will be able to help clinicians with proper diagnosis and planning of individual intervention.

title: Lightweight and Explainable CNN for Accurate Multi-Species Medicinal Leaf Disease Detection
authors: Jannatul Ferdous Riva, Bibi Aysha Raha, Md Asif Shahriar Arpon, Foujia Afrose Tanha, Farzana Anjum Oishy
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Medicinal plants are very important in terms of therapeutic uses, and their yield is often at risk due to diseases. These diseases are hard to identify accurately and automatically because they have subtle differences in the textures, colors, and shapes of leaves. This study presents Light-MedLeafNet, a lightweight CNN with only 0.07 M parameters, developed from scratch for multi-species medicinal leaf disease classification. The model was trained and tested on the publicly available AIMedLeafX dataset, which included four species (Cinnamomum camphora, Terminalia chebula, Moringa oleifera, and Azadirachta indica) with 13 classes. Light-MedLeafNet reached 96-97% accuracy, matching that of DenseNet201 using much less computing power. Initial tests using confusion matrices showed correct predictions on a class-wise basis with few misclassifications, even between similar disease classes. Also, SHAP and LIME analysis revealed that the model was based on disease-related areas, which offered quality interpretability and transparency. In general, Light-MedLeafNet provides a computationally efficient, robust, and understandable way to detect diseases in medicinal plants, showing that lightweight architectures can be both highly accurate and also without impacting performance or reliability.

title: Implementation of an Uninterrupted Real-Time Wireless Thermal Pixel Data Transfer Using HTTP Protocol via Node-MCU
authors: Tofayel Islam Shohag, Siam Sufi MD Abu, Shaira Tashnub Torsa, Nahiun Lubaba Tasnim, Anasul Haque, MD. Nayeem Hossain, Sadia Nahar, Muhammad Kabir Hussain, S.M. Sojiba Jaman, Md. Abdul Muttalib Moon, Sanjoy Kumar Paul
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Thermal imaging is a technology which allows the detection of heat radiation emitted by humans, animals and objects. It is becoming increasingly popular due to its unique applications across various fields, including medical diagnostics, building automation and industrial monitoring. Even though thermal imaging has great promise, the devices available today are costly and occasionally lack sophisticated communication features. In this study, we proposed a wireless system for real-time thermal pixel data transmission by using Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) over a Wi-Fi network. The system utilizes ESP-32 and ESP-8266 microcontrollers as transmitter and receiver respectively, with an AMG8833 thermal camera that is connected to the ESP-32 for data acquisition and processing. Performance of the HTTP is compared to Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) in terms of latency and packet loss. Results showed that HTTP achieved significantly lower latencies (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.588-2.354$</tex> seconds) compared to TCP (2.328-2.497 seconds). While connections established within 5-6 seconds over 100 meters, longer distances introduced potential packet loss. At one meter range, HTTP experienced zero packet loss. On the other hand, TCP experienced only 20 lost packets out of 30. The system accurately detected and classified objects based on temperature, recording consistent readings across client and server: 29.0°C and 20.0°C (normal object), 31.75°C and 5.25°C (cold object), and 392.00°C and 45.50°C (hot object). Data logging and visualization are implemented by utilizing ArduSpreadsheet and Excel.

Since then, 3DV has become a leading venue for advances in 3D vision and graphics, encompassing sensing, modeling, analysis, rendering, and visualization.

title: Short-Term Forecasting of Dissolved Gas in Transformer Oil via Bidirectional Markov Imputation and EWT-Driven GBRBFNN
authors: Chunjia Gao, Deshen Li, Peng Zhang, Hanlin Li, Ruoyu Zhang, Bo Qi, Chengrong Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Accurately predicting dissolved gas concentration in transformer oil is crucial for condition monitoring and early fault warning. However, online dissolved gas analysis (DGA) data often suffer from incompleteness, high noise, and nonlinear multi-scale dynamics. This paper proposes a two-stage framework to improve data quality and enable short-term prediction. First, a bidirectional Markov transition model with an adaptive weight adjustment mechanism reconstructs missing values, preserving both local temporal evolution and global statistical features. Then, the completed time series is decomposed via empirical wavelet transform (EWT), and each component is predicted using a gradient-boosted radial basis function neural network (GBRBFNN). The final prediction is obtained by reconstructing all predicted components. Validation on field data shows an average relative error of 1.76% for data reconstruction while retaining statistical characteristics. Under normal operating conditions (400 hydrogen samples), the proposed EWT-GBRBFNN model achieves a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 1.70%, reducing prediction error by 17.1% to 51% compared with other five benchmarks. Under abnormal fluctuation conditions (168 ethylene samples), the model attains a MAPE of 2.9%, with error reductions of 64.8% to 78% and a coefficient of variation below 4.5%. The framework provides reliable data completion and accurate predictions for transformer condition monitoring.

title: GaussianArt: Unified Modeling of Geometry and Motion for Articulated Objects
authors: Licheng Shen, Saining Zhang, Honghan Li, Peilin Yang, Zihao Huang, Zongzheng Zhang, Hao Zhao
abstract: Reconstructing articulated objects is essential for building digital twins of interactive environments. However, prior methods typically decouple geometry and motion by first reconstructing object shape in distinct states and then estimating articulation through post-hoc alignment. This separation complicates the reconstruction pipeline and restricts scalability, especially for objects with complex, multi-part articulation. We introduce a unified representation that jointly models geometry and motion using articulated 3D Gaussians. This formulation improves robustness in motion decomposition and supports articulated objects with up to 20 parts, significantly outperforming prior approaches that often struggle beyond <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2-3$</tex> parts due to brittle initialization. To systematically assess scalability and generalization, we propose MPArt-90, a new benchmark consisting of 90 articulated objects across 20 categories, each with diverse part counts and motion configurations. Extensive experiments show that our method consistently achieves superior accuracy in part-level geometry reconstruction and motion estimation across a broad range of object types. We further demonstrate applicability to downstream tasks such as robotic simulation and human-scene interaction modeling, highlighting the potential of unified articulated representations in scalable physical modeling. Project Page.

title: GRADRobot: Geometry-Aware Rendering with Articulation and Diffusion for Robot Modeling
authors: Yunlong Li, Boyuan Chen, Chongjie Ye, Bohan Li, Zhaoxi Chen, Shaocong Xu, Hao Tang, Hao Zhao
abstract: Gaussian fields are a promising representation for robot body modeling due to their differentiability and inherently low sim-to-real gap. However, existing methods like Dr-Robot overlook explicit geometric constraints, leading to artifacts under novel poses or views. Directly enforcing depth and normal supervision on articulated Gaussians is unstable due to entanglement between pose deformation and 3D appearance learning. To address this, we propose a two-stage training strategy: we first learn a canonical Gaussian field in a canonical pose using dense RGB, depth, and normal supervision, establishing a geometryaware reconstruction. We then fine-tune the Gaussian parameters jointly with a deformation network conditioned on joint angles using only RGB losses, ensuring consistent geometry and appearance across poses. To further mitigate rendering artifacts in novel poses and viewpoints, we integrate a diffusion-based refinement module. This module conditions on both the initial Gaussian renderings and the target robot skeletons, and significantly enhances visual fidelity while preserving pose accuracy. Experiments across multiple robotic platforms show that GRADRobot outperforms DrRobot by a large margin in both rendering quality (PSNR) and geometric accuracy (Chamfer Distance). https://github.com/liyunlooong/GRADRobot

title: Reliable Hardware Trojan Detection for RISC-V Processors using Formal Verification and Automation
authors: Czea Sie Chuah, Christian Appold, Tim Leinmueller
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Due to cost and time reasons, hardware development is currently often done using IP of external vendors and outsourcing of fabrication to third parties. This leads to a globalization of the supply chain, which highly increases the risk of inserted Hardware Trojans (HTs). HTs are malicious modifications of hardware to change hardware functionality or to leak secret data. Especially in safety- and security-critical areas like autonomous driving, Hardware Trojans can cause severe consequences and endanger even human lives. For this reason, we present in this work how model checking based formal verification can be used in a systematic way to detect HTs reliably. We are the first additionally using signal connection properties on top of design functionality properties for reliable HT detection and full HT detection coverage. To reduce verification time and strongly increase HT detection coverage, we developed a tool to generate a high number of properties automatically. Additionally, we intend to publish a large set of specification and microarchitecture intent derived properties important for HT detection in processors and show how these properties have to be combined with connection properties for reliable HT detection. We finished our work on identifying a complete property set for detecting each inserted Hardware Trojan modifying the Program Counter at an arbitrary position in a RISC-V processor. In this paper we present this property set and HT detection properties writing guidelines we identified during this work. Experimental results confirm we get the full proofs needed for reliable Hardware Trojan detection for all properties in below 24 hours. Our work will result in a guideline for reliable HT detection in processors using formal verification.

title: Tiny Changes, Big Drops: Unveiling Security Vulnerabilities in ML Accelerators
authors: Cassandra Westergrenv, Filip Grimsholm, Mahdi Fazeli, Ahmad Patooghy
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: The multi-party fabrication of semiconductor products has exposed Machine Learning (ML) hardware accelerators to significant security threats, particularly malicious hardware modifications aiming at service denials. While traditional adversaries typically create specific backdoors, this paper investigates a more insidious class of attacks, i.e., gradual accuracy-degrading attacks. We propose a novel sensitivity analysis method based on partial derivatives, which quantitatively assesses the security implications of weights in a hardware-implemented ML model. This analysis not only identifies the most critical parameters to target but also provides a foundation for protecting ML accelerators. Leveraging this method, we design, implement, and evaluate six novel hardware Trojans (HTs) that tamper with model parameters to gradually degrade accuracy over time. The proposed HTs are demonstrated on a LeNet-5 CNN accelerator deployed on a Xilinx Zybo Z7-20 FPGA. Our hardware evaluation shows that these stealthy HTs can drive final accuracy down to $\mathbf{1 5 . 2 \%}$ on hardware (up to $\mathbf{8 3 . 6}$ percentagepoint drop) while adding at most 136 LUTs ($\approx 0.30 \%$), and $\leq 0.025 \mathrm{~W}$ power overhead. These results demonstrate both the practical feasibility of highly effective and stealthy HTs capable of evading conventional testing, and the dual role of the proposed sensitivity analysis as a tool for exposing vulnerabilities and enabling targeted defense mechanisms.

title: Constacyclic codes with best-known parameters
authors: Zekai Chen, Min Sha
abstract: In this paper, we construct several infinite families of q-ary constacyclic codes over a finite field Fq with length n, dimension around n/2, and minimum distance at least cn/ logq n for some positive constant c. They contain many constacyclic codes with optimal, or almost-optimal, or best-known parameters. We also consider constacyclic codes of various lengths.

title: Nonvolatile RF Switch Using FOM-Enhanced Resistive Memristive Device With Multifinger and Gradient-Line Configuration
authors: Guangxu Shen, Xinyu Chen, Yang Song, Wanchen Yang, Xiufeng Song, Gangyi Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article proposes a novel device structure for resistive random access memory (ReRAM)-based radio frequency (RF) switches to overcome the inherent trade-off between <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</small>-state resistance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$R_{on}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) and <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off</small>-state capacitance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$C_{of\!\!f}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). Unlike prior studies that primarily focus on material and fabrication processes, this work focus on the design of device structure to mitigate high-frequency parasitic effects. By leveraging the nonlinear area-decoupling resistive property of ReRAM device, a multifinger structure is developed to effectively reduce <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$R_{on}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> without proportional <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$C_{of\!\!f}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> degradation, thereby improving the figure of merit [(FOM), <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$R_{on}{}^{\ast } C_{of\!\!f}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>]. In addition, a gradient-line coplanar waveguide (CPW) structure is introduced while a constant <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$50~\Omega $</tex-math> </inline-formula> characteristic impedance, which can mitigate the gap capacitive effects and improve the high-frequency impedance matching. For demonstration, two single-pole single-throw (SPST) switches have been fabricated based on metal-oxide ReRAM device. The proposed dual-finger switch achieves nearly a 50% reduction in FOM compared to single-finger designs. The DC characteristics remain stable over multiplecycles, and RF measurements confirm good insertion loss and isolation up to 30GHz. These results demonstrate the potential of the proposed multifinger and gradient-line CPW architecture for next-generation high-performance ReRAM RF switches.

title: Accurate Wi-Fi RTT indoor positioning with NLOS mitigation by floor map: a multi-wall modeling approach
authors: Qing Liang, Guohao Zhang, Li-Ta Hsu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper first proposes a novel empirical non-line-of-sight (NLOS) ranging model, called a multi-wall model (MWM), for accurate Wi-Fi round-trip-time (RTT) indoor positioning under NLOS conditions. The model is designed to account for NLOS ranging biases due to through-wall signal propagation in complex indoor environments. It requires a 2D geometry floor map and learns model parameters from a few on-site training data. We present a model-fitting technique based on least-squares optimization to ease this process. Using the multi-wall ranging model, we also develop a particle-filter-based RTT positioning method that integrates odometry, RTT ranges, and a floor map. The positioning method exploits map information twofold via map matching and NLOS ranging. The modeland method are evaluated extensively by experiments on a public RTT dataset and our private data collected in NLOS scenarios. Results show that MWM hasoutperformed the naive ranging and polynomial fitting models in NLOS scenario.Compared to a data-driven Gaussian processes regression model, it has comparable ranging performance with rich training data and much better performance with few training data. Under bad NLOS conditions and with sparse AP deployment, our positioning method, with and without map matching, has outperformed the existing particle filter methods (without using MWM). This is due to the explicit modeling of through-wall ranging biases by MWM during positioning.

title: Flow-Kansformer: Predicting Gas-Liquid Flow Rate with Hybrid KAN-Enhanced Transformer
authors: Haoxiang Tang, Yan Zhang, Lin Chen, Heping Chen, Yi Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Accurate, real-time prediction of gas and liquid two-phase flow rates is crucial for effective production monitoring and reservoir management. Conventional separation-based metering requires cumbersome devices and cannot support timely process control. Existing data-driven models often construct separate networks for each phase, overlooking complex flow regimes and demonstrating limited robustness to noise or well-to-well variation. We present Flow-Kansformer, a multimodal deep learning framework that integrates multistage Venturi pressure signals with electrical resistance tomography sequences. We first extract temporal features using a bidirectional long short-term memory network. Next, we refine these features with Transformer encoders, where we replace traditional feed-forward networks with Kolmogorov–Arnold networks to enhance nonlinear modeling capacity. Finally, we employ a multi-task KAN-based head to output gas and liquid flow rates simultaneously. By replacing conventional multilayer perceptrons with adaptive spline-based KANs, our architecture gains enhanced representational power through flexible, learnable activation functions. Flow-Kansformer achieves mean absolute percentage errors of 4.28% for gas and 3.75% for liquid on nine months of field data from Well A. We successfully adapt the model to Well B within days through incremental learning. These results demonstrate a robust, accurate, and efficient solution for industrial multiphase flow rate measurement.

title: SkyDSafe: Federated Learning Driven Security for Drone Data_supp1-3694885.docx
authors: Amit Kumar Singh
abstract: With the rapid development of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), such as drones, data can be gathered from multiple sources across various sectors. Compared with other media, images are the most vital carriers of information and are more lifelike. As illegal access to these images can result in serious privacy breaches, ensuring the authenticity of digital data remains a challenging task for drones. This paper proposes a method, called SkyDSafe, for securing image data based on federated learning and advanced encryption techniques. To address data poisoning attacks, this paper employs a cryptographic Merkle tree commitment over the training dataset, allowing auditors to detect data poisoning via Merkle proofs and each client’s training transcript. Using a multi-scale deep neural network, tamper-proof image features are effectively divided into abstract representations and secret hierarchical tensors. Subsequently, only the abstract representations of the image are encrypted through a cryptographic embedder to balance the efficiency and visual security. At the receiver end, the encrypted abstract image is decrypted and combined with the corresponding hierarchical tensors retrieved from the central server to reconstruct the original image. A comparative analysis with existing techniques highlights the advantages of SkyDSafe, including tamper-proof learning and reduced time and cost, as demonstrated by the experimental results. This aspect makes SkyDSafe a robust security solution for protecting drone data and other critical applications.

title: DisenTS: Disentangled Channel Evolving Pattern Modeling for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
authors: Zhiding Liu, Jiqian Yang, Qingyang Mao, Yuze Zhao, Mingyue Cheng, Zhi Li, Qi Liu, Enhong Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Multivariate time series forecasting plays a crucial role in various real-world applications. Significant efforts have been made to integrate advanced network architectures and training strategies that enhance the capture of temporal dependencies, thereby improving forecasting accuracy. On the other hand, mainstream approaches typically utilize a single unified model with simplistic channel-mixing embedding or crosschannel attention operations to account for the critical intricate inter-channel dependencies. Moreover, some methods even trade capacity for robust prediction based on the channel-independent assumption. Nonetheless, as time series data may display distinct evolving patterns due to the unique characteristics of each channel (including multiple strong seasonalities and trend changes), the unified modeling methods could yield suboptimal results. To this end, we propose DisenTS, a tailored framework for modeling disentangled channel evolving patterns in general multivariate time series forecasting. The central idea of DisenTS is to model the potential diverse patterns within the multivariate time series data in a decoupled manner. Technically, the framework employs multiple distinct forecasting models, each tasked with uncovering a unique evolving pattern. To guide the learning process without supervision of pattern partition, we introduce a novel Forecaster Aware Gate (FAG) module that generates the routing signals adaptively according to both the forecasters' states and input series' characteristics. The forecasters' states are derived from the Linear Weight Approximation (LWA) strategy, which quantizes the complex deep neural networks into compact matrices. Additionally, the Similarity Constraint (SC) is further proposed to guide each model to specialize in an underlying pattern by minimizing the mutual information between the representations. We conduct extensive experiments on a wide range of forecasting settings with diverse state-of-theart forecasting models, and the results validate the effectiveness and generalizability of the proposed DisenTS framework.

title: Multimodal Fusion Network for Unbalanced Spatiotemporal Data Prediction
authors: Yuan Liang, Yunyou Huang
abstract: Spatiotemporal data prediction plays a crucial role in many real-world applications, such as urban planning, environmental monitoring, and traffic flow forecasting. However, spatiotemporal data are often unbalanced, with uneven distributions across both spatial and temporal dimensions, presenting significant challenges for accurate prediction. This paper proposes the Multimodal Fusion Network for Unbalanced Spatiotemporal data prediction (MFNUS), an innovative framework designed to address these challenges. First, MFNUS employs hierarchical feature fusion, which aggregates features from sparse and dense modalities by fusing information at multiple levels, enabling more effective combination of the features. Second, we integrate two plug-and-play modules: the Unified Feature Interaction Module (UFIM) and the Self-Correcting Transformer Module (SCTM). UFIM enables fine-grained interactions between different modalities through an orthogonal attention mechanism, enhancing the complementarity between modalities, allowing sparse data to gain more valuable information from other modalities; SCTM leverages self-attention mechanisms to suppress redundant features, optimizing the fused representations. Finally, we conduct extensive experiments on four real-world datasets—New York and Beijing housing prices, U.S. crop yield data, and PEMS-BAY traffic flow data. The experimental results show that MFNUS significantly outperforms state-of-the-art models on all datasets, achieving substantial improvements in prediction accuracy. Specifically, MFNUS reduces RMSE by 20.1% and 18.5% on the New York and Beijing housing price datasets, respectively, and demonstrates superior predictive capability on the PEMS-BAY traffic flow dataset, showcasing its effectiveness in spatiotemporal prediction tasks.

title: Safety and Performance Co-Optimization in Human–Robot Interaction: An Adaptive Energy Tank Design With Dynamic Control Barrier Functions
authors: Jiawen Yu, Jianing Liu, Weichao Sun
abstract: Theco-optimization of safety and task performance is a central challenge in physical human–robot interaction. Passivity ensures safety by limiting system energy, and impedance control enables compliant execution. However, conventional energy-tank methods often rely on fixed power constraints and produce discontinuous control signals, severely limiting adaptability in dynamic environments. To overcome these limitations, this article introduces a novel safety-aware passive control framework that unifies high-order dynamic control barrier functions (HD-CBFs) with an energy tank. Our contributions are twofold: an HD-CBF-based energy tank that continuously enforces time-varying power bounds, and an adaptive iterative learning scheme that autonomously optimizes power profiles for complex tasks without compromising safety. Extensive experiments on a 7-DOF Franka Emika Panda manipulator validate the framework’s robustness across key scenarios, including task interruption, unstructured environmental variations, and clinically relevant robotic ultrasound scanning. The system consistently maintains real-time power constraints and reduces force-tracking root-mean-square error by 53.9% compared to baselines. This demonstrates an effective balance between safety and performance, making it highly suitable for real-world human–robot collaboration.

title: Task-tailored Pre-processing: Fair Downstream Supervised Learning
authors: Jinwon Sohn, Qifan Song, Guang Lin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Fairness-aware machine learning has recently attracted various communities to mitigate discrimination against certain societal groups in data-driven tasks. For fair supervised learning, particularly in pre-processing, there have been two main categories: data fairness and task-tailored fairness. The former directly finds an intermediate distribution among the groups, independent of the type of the downstream model, so a learned downstream classification/regression model returns similar predictive scores to individuals inputting the same covariates irrespective of their sensitive attributes. The latter explicitly takes the supervised learning task into account when constructing the pre-processing map. In this work, we study algorithmic fairness for supervised learning and argue that the data fairness approaches impose overly strong regularization from the perspective of the Hirschfeld-Gebelein-Rényi correlation. This motivates us to devise a novel pre-processing approach tailored to supervised learning. We account for the trade-off between fairness and utility in obtaining the pre-processing map. Then we study the behavior of arbitrary downstream supervised models learned on the transformed data to find sufficient conditions to guarantee their fairness improvement and utility preservation. To our knowledge, no prior work in the branch of task-tailored methods has theoretically investigated downstream guarantees when using pre-processed data. We further evaluate our framework through comparison studies based on tabular and image data sets, showing the superiority of our framework which preserves consistent trade-offs among multiple downstream models compared to recent competing models. Particularly for computer vision data, we see our method alters only necessary semantic features related to the central machine learning task to achieve fairness.

title: Privacy-preserving Online Federated Learning for Massive Infinite Streams_supp1-3697332.pdf
authors: Shusen Yang
abstract: Online federated learning (OFL) is essential for privacy-preserving collaborative online analytics over decentralized streams. Different from batch-based FL, OFL faces new challenges including longitudinal privacy leakage, and accumulated utility loss and communication costs, caused by the infinite data streams. This paper first extends the definition of traditional differential privacy (DP) to OFL, to provide window-based privacy protection with a tunable granularity for infinite streams. By analyzing baseline methods, a generic sampling-based solution framework is then proposed for designing a DP-enhanced OFL algorithm. We prove that despite the DP constraint, the sampling solution framework can achieve an asymptotic optimality when time tends to infinity. Finally, we present Sampling<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{3}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-OFL, an adaptive triple-sampling strategy driven by deep reinforcement learning, which can dynamically determine a near-optimal sampling strategy with significant gains in both utility and efficiency. Extensive experiments on six real-world datasets demonstrate that Sampling<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{3}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-OFL can scale to millions of streams, and achieves utility improvements of 0.74%-15.84% and communication cost reductions of 33.33%-95.24% across these datasets compared to state-of-the-art methods.

title: Machine Learning and Cultural Heritage: An Italian Perspective
authors: Giovanna Maria Dimitri
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Cultural heritage plays a crucial role for societal identity, allowing a deep connection between individuals and their historical roots. In this context the advent of deep learning technologies has significantly influenced the preservation and analysis of cultural heritage, offering innovative and technological solutions for heritage documentation, classification, and restoration. This article presents a short review of existing deep learning methodologies applied to cultural heritage preservation in Italy. By leveraging a systematic literature search, we investigate technological solutions deployed across various Italian regions and analyze their impact on the dissemination of artificial intelligence in this field. Furthermore, we perform a geolocation-based assessment of these approaches to uncover potential correlations between technological novel solutions and regional characteristics. Our findings indicate the critical role of deep learning in modernizing heritage conservation efforts, moreover, highlighting the necessity for continued research and interdisciplinary collaboration in this domain.

title: A Mechanism and Data Hybrid-Driven Method for Diagnosing Main Circuit Ground Faults under Different Working Conditions in Traction Drive Systems
authors: Zhenglin Cheng, Xueming Li, Kan Liu, Zhiwen Chen, Chuanbo Liu, Fengbing Jiang

title: Continuous-Wave THz Imaging of Moisture Distribution During Ceramic Drying
authors: Emil John Y. Magaway, Aleksander M. Cendrowicz, Zhenyu J. Zhang, Miguel Navarro-Cía
publish: IEEE Transactions on Terahertz Science and Technology
abstract: The lack of a non-contact methodology to monitor the ceramic drying process impedes the optimization of this critical production stage. Resorting to continuous-wave THz imaging, we are able to generate images of the water dis tribution within samples and, when paired with single-point dielectric property data, produce absorption coefficient maps of two ceramic samples as a function of drying time and water mass fraction of the whole system. This highlights the spatially-dependent drying time of ceramic samples due to local variation of material properties (e.g., porosity) that cannot be captured with the contact gravimetry industry standard. The THz continuous-wave system enables contactless, spatially-resolved visualisation and tracking of moisture distribution during drying, however, for quantitative interpretation of absorption coefficients complementary THz-TDS measurements are needed to provide the dielectric properties required for the transmission model. We report variations up to 1400% in absorption coefficient for different pixels at the same water mass fraction of the overall system. This work provides a proof-of-concept demonstration of a cost-effective non-contact inline monitoring system based on THz technology for use in the ceramic production process.

title: Physics-Aware Semantic Graph Classifier for Remote Sensing Images
authors: Lingling Li, Jiadong Lin, Licheng Jiao, Fang Liu, Xu Liu, Yangyang Li, Wenping Ma
abstract: Remote sensing scenes face the challenges of high inter class similarity and high intra class diversity in classification. Methods based on deep learning have advanced the field by learning discriminative features, but these methods have two key limitations: 1) the scope of intra-image feature extraction is limited to the planar spatial domain, mainly capturing visual features such as color and shape, while neglecting other important physical information like the vertical structure of scene inference and its frequency domain features; and 2) the lack of inter-image relationship modeling, which prevents the capture of latent semantic correlations among different scene categories. To address these issues, we propose a physics-aware semantic graph classifier (PASG) for remote sensing image classification. Firstly, we propose a height-aware spatial-frequency feature extraction module (HASF), which represents key physical attributes by integrating the vertical structure, spatial features, and frequency domain information of scenes. Secondly, we design a semantic-aware global-local encoder (SGLE), modeling the dataset as a graph structure where the physical features of images serve as graph nodes. SGLE refines the representation of each node by capturing local neighborhood structures and global contextual dependencies, leading to a robust cross-image contextual semantic representation. Experiments on four publicly available remote sensing datasets (AID, UCM, WHU-RS19, and NWPU-RESISC45) achieve optimal overall accuracies of 98.60%, 99.81%, 99.42%, and 97.55%, respectively. These results outperform state-of-the-art methods and validate the effectiveness of the proposed PASG classifier.

title: A Root Prediction System for Single-Variable Equations with Existing Taylor Polynomials
authors: Seokjun Jeong, Yoosoo Oh
abstract: Root finding is a fundamental problem in numerical analysis with applications across scientific computing, control systems, and optimization. Classical iterative algorithms such as Newton-Raphson, bisection, and secant methods are highly sensitive to initial values and may fail to converge reliably for nonlinear equations, particularly when encountering zero derivatives, ill-conditioned Jacobians, or complex nonlinear structures. To address these limitations, we propose a direct root prediction framework that reformulates root finding as a structured prediction problem. The proposed system consists of four stages: (1) a Transformer-based interval predictor identifies the top-25 candidate intervals likely to contain real roots, (2) variable shifting and 25th-order Maclaurin expansion generate local polynomial representations, (3) coefficient-based neural models generate multiple candidate roots from Taylor coefficients, and (4) residual-based selection and validation identify the final numerically reliable root. Experiments on 10,000 test equations spanning 12 function families show that the proposed framework achieves strong performance under a strict residual threshold of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−10</sup>. Additional operating-point analysis indicates that using a 25th-order local expansion together with top-25 candidate intervals provides the most stable overall trade-off between success rate and computational cost across the evaluated model variants. Under the limited-prior-information evaluation setting adopted in this study, all proposed variants show competitive performance relative to the baseline numerical solver while producing validated residuals on the order of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−12</sup> to 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−11</sup> under the adopted evaluation protocol. Further analysis indicates that the observed performance ceiling is primarily governed by representation-level limitations of truncated Taylor expansions rather than by deficiencies of a specific prediction model.

title: Development of a Traffic Fingerprinting-based Hidden Service Access Identification System for Dark Web Drug Investigations
authors: Sol Gyu Park, Jiyeon Kim
abstract: Advances in cryptographic technologies for information security have led to the emergence of anonymous communication systems such as Tor. While the Tor network contributes to privacy protection and freedom of expression, it is also exploited for illicit activities, including drug trafficking, child sexual exploitation, and illegal financial or weapons transactions. Consequently, investigative agencies require new technical approaches capable of identifying user access behaviors within anonymous networks. This study proposes a system for identifying connections to dark web drug marketplaces based on Tor network traffic analysis. The proposed system leverages raw traffic obtained from network bridge segments accessible to investigators and incorporates a realistic data preprocessing pipeline consisting of Tor Metrics–based filtering, user–operator distinction, and guard-node–level traffic separation. From the refined traffic, we extract core features—Burst-Cycle, Distribution-Entropy, and TCP/TLS sequence patterns—and transform them into fixed-length vectors suitable for machine learning. We incrementally increased the number of identifiable dark web sites to compare machine learning and deep learning models. Among the evaluated models, the CNN classifier achieved the highest performance, maintaining an accuracy of approximately 0.90 in the seven-class classification setting. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework under the evaluated experimental setting and suggest its potential usefulness for inferring a suspect’s access to monitored dark web drug sites.
