authors: Martina Ali, Ummi Raba’ah Hashim, Kasturi Kanchymalay, Lizawati Salahuddin, Teo Hong Chun, Andri Pranolo

title: Estimating Hemoglobin Changes in Body Tissue Using Multispectral Imaging, Spectral Unmixing and Machine Learning
authors: Diego A. Polanco-Díaz, Liliana Granados-Castro, Omar Gutierrez-Navarro, Aldo R. Mejia-Rodriguez, Daniel Ulises Campos-Delgado
abstract: This research investigates the application of machine learning-based regression models to estimate tissue perfusion parameters from multispectral imaging data. Our purpose is to establish a robust method for predicting the changes in deoxygenated and oxygenated hemoglobin at the fingertip using spectral unmixing algorithms. Multispectral images are processed to extract elementary spectral profiles or end-members and their fractional concentrations or abundances maps, providing a detailed analysis of hemoglobin distribution. The evaluation focuses on the relationship between the extracted abundances and changes in hemoglobin by using metrics, such as normalized reconstruction error and the adjusted coefficient of determination to quantify performance. As reference for validation, the estimated changes in deoxygenated and oxygenated hemoglobin, obtained by photoplethysmography, are compared with our proposal. In addition, perfusion maps representing the spatial distribution of changes in hemoglobin at the fingertip are generated from the proposed approach. The generalization capability of the regression models is further confirmed using crossvalidation, ensuring the robustness of the proposed approach across varying datasets. The most prominent results show a normalized reconstruction error of 0.009 ± 0.010, and adjusted coefficient of determination of 0.988 ± 0.011 using linear spectral unmixing and a random forest regressor. Tissue perfusion maps provide a detailed visual representation of the changes in deoxygenated and oxygenated hemoglobin on the fingertip, marking a significant advancement in perfusion monitoring.

title: Hybrid Domain-Decomposition/Physical-Optics Analysis of Finite Reconfigurable Reflectarrays Using Characteristic-Mode Macro Basis Functions
authors: Dijun Lin, Lars Manholm, Oskar Talcoth, Parisa Aghdam, Rob Maaskant
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter presents an efficient hybrid domain-decomposition/physical-optics framework for analyzing finite, large-scale reflectarrays and reconfigurable intelligent surfaces. The structure is decomposed into exterior and interior domains coupled through a generalized aperture-admittance formulation. The exterior response is approximated using physical optics, while element behavior is captured through admittance operators. Scalability to large arrays and many tuning states is achieved via a macro basis function expansion of the aperture current, which enables multi-state analysis by reusing pre-characterized element operators and updating only the elements that change state. Results for 8 x 8 and 20 x 20 arrays under different illuminations closely match full-wave references while substantially reducing memory usage and simulation time, demonstrating suitability for fast analysis and optimization of reconfigurable arrays.

title: Robust Real-Time Brake Gap Estimation for EMB Systems Via Legendre-Based Two-Stage MFFRLS With Adaptive M-Estimator
authors: Teng Zuo, Huaicheng Yao, Yang Zhang, Wenpeng Wei
abstract: Brake gap is important in achieving precise force control and ensuring reliability of electromechanical brake (EMB) systems in autonomous vehicles. However, it is typically unmeasurable and time-varying due to progressive brake pad wear. This article proposes a Legendre-based multiple forgetting factors recursive least-squares (MFFRLS) with adaptive M-estimator algorithm for robust real-time brake gap estimation. Specifically, the estimation problem is first formulated into a regression problem inspired by the EMB data relationship. Then, Legendre basis is adopted for regressor transformation to improve estimation stability. After that, the MFFRLS with adaptive M-estimator is proposed to enhance robustness of brake gap estimation across the full life cycle. A two-stage estimation strategy is further designed to reduce computational burden. Estimation convergence analyses are established using Hessian matrix and Lyapunov theory. Comparison studies using green brake pad against standard recursive least squares, extended Kalman filter, Legendre-based RLS, and MFFRLS approaches substantiate the high estimation precision of the proposed algorithm, with steady-state error to be 0.51, 1.42, 0.54, 0.43, and 0.03 rad, respectively. Further validations show the root-mean-square error and relative RMSE of estimation are less than 0.20 rad and 2.99<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$e^{-4}$</tex-math></inline-formula>% across green, moderate and severely worn brake pads, demonstrating the adaptiveness and robustness of the proposed algorithm.

title: A multi-task 3D deep learning framework for tree point cloud segmentation and parameter retrieval
authors: Yijun Zhong, Shuai Liu, Hua Sun
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: In applications such as automated forest inventory, large-scale forest scene analysis and reconstruction, efficiently and accurately resolving tree geometry and structural features from vast 3D point cloud data is a core requirement. This places urgent demands on multi-task capabilities, including individual tree extraction, wood-leaf component separation, and structural parameter retrieval. However, methods capable of effectively addressing these complex multi-task needs are scarce. Most existing point cloud segmentation techniques struggle to perform both instance and semantic segmentation simultaneously and are limited in handling large-scale forest point clouds. To address this gap, we propose a novel multi-task 3D deep learning framework that integrates individual-tree extraction, wood-leaf separation, and structural parameter retrieval into a unified end to-end pipeline. The key novelty lies in the design of a dual branch network, which incorporates a dynamic edge perception module to adaptively enhance boundary features and suppress noise, significantly improving segmentation precision at wood leaf interfaces and tree boundaries. The framework is evaluated on five large-scale public datasets and our self-built dataset, covering diverse forest types and LiDAR platforms. Results demonstrate that our method outperforms mainstream single task segmentation methods in both individual-tree segmentation and branch-leaf separation at both plot and tree levels, and enables accurate retrieval of critical tree parameters such as tree height, diameter at breast height, crown diameter, and volume. This work presents a novel and unified deep learning solution that advances the automation and intelligence of forest inventory and management.

title: Deep Learning-Based Estimation of Ground Reaction Forces in Parkinsonian Gait Using an Optimized Set of IMU Data
authors: Run Lin, Yingtian Tang, Jiawen Xu, Dongfei Huo, Lefan Wang, Helen Dawes, Dominic J. Farris, Dong Wang, Xijin Hua
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Accurate gait analysis in Parkinson’s disease (PD) typically relies on laboratory-based systems to capture biomechanical data, such as ground reaction forces (GRFs). Estimating GRFs using inertial measurement units (IMUs) provides a feasible alternative. However, this approach remains challenging in pathological gait like PD due to its high variability and complexity. Moreover, existing monitoring approaches often require multiple body-mounted sensors, which limit practicality and reduce patient compliance. To date, no study has investigated the application of deep learning approaches to address this challenge. This study proposes, for the first time, a deep learning framework to estimate bilateral vertical GRFs (vGRFs) in PD using an optimized set of wearable IMUs. A hybrid CNN-BiLSTM model was trained separately on data from 61 PD patients and 65 healthy controls (HC) using 13 IMUs. The model achieved high intra-subject accuracy (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.98) and strong inter-subject generalization (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.93 for HC, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.91 for PD). Sensor configuration was found to significantly influence estimation accuracy, with optimal sensor placement varying between PD patients and HC. For PD patients, estimation accuracy dropped markedly when reducing to a single IMU. The optimal configuration for PD used four IMUs. We identified a minimal setup with only two IMUs still enabled robust estimation. This compact setup offers a practical and scalable solution. Overall, the proposed approach supports the development of wearable vGRF-based gait analysis systems for Parkinsonian gait and potentially other pathological conditions, enabling accessible clinical assessments, remote monitoring, and personalized rehabilitation.

title: State-dependent neuromodulation reveals link between online and offline corticospinal excitability
authors: Fatemeh Khademi, Alireza Gharabaghi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: The post-intervention effects of non-invasive neuromodulation techniques are critical for their translational potential in neurorehabilitation and depend on the dynamic state of neural networks at the time of stimulation. In the motor system, state-dependent interventions can modulate corticospinal excitability (CSE), but the relationship between during-intervention (online) and post-intervention (offline) effects remains unclear. We tested whether online CSE changes during state-dependent neuromodulation are associated with offline plasticity of corticospinal pathways. Healthy participants underwent five combinations of single-pulse transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) and robotic peripheral stimulation (PS) controlled by event-related beta-band desynchronization (ERD) during motor imagery (MI). We quantified the run-wise evolution of motor-evoked potential (MEP) amplitudes during the intervention and related it to post-intervention changes in plateau corticospinal recruitment (MEPmax). MI-based interventions produced progressive online CSE increases, with the strongest facilitation when TMS and PS were delivered synchronously during ERD. Only this combined intervention showed a significant association between online MEP and offline MEPmax, whereas rest conditions showed no progressive online effects and reduced offline CSE. These results support online CSE as a biomarker for refining closed-loop neuromodulation and for developing individualized dosing strategies in translational neurorehabilitation.

title: $\mathcal {O}^{2}$-UCB: A Federated Multi-armed Bandit Mechanism in Zero Client-owned Data Network
authors: Hangfan Li, Yang Xu, Yibin Cai, Sheng Yue, Ziyu Peng, Yaoxue Zhang
abstract: The Federated Multi-Armed Bandit (FMAB) framework is proposed to facilitate collaborative model training in cloud-edge environments. Most existing FMAB-based systems assume that participants have personal datasets. This assumption becomes biased in scenarios with limited local data or when the discrepancy between historical data and online data cannot be quantified. Consequently, participants must passively collect data by offering services and gathering feedback from users within their charging areas. In addition, due to the mobility of the users, the number of requests is not stable. In such scenarios, effective model training requires addressing two key challenges. First, the allocation of resources for data collection at the edge is often mismatched with the actual number of service requests, resulting in limited training data and wasted resources. Second, in areas with sparse service requests, the lack of data further delays model adaptation. In this article, networks with these challenges are summarized as the training while collecting data federated bandit (TCF-bandit), and the over-area over-period upper confidence bound (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-UCB) algorithm is proposed to address two challenges. In the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-UCB algorithm, the cloud records request-generation patterns to mitigate resource waste caused by allocation mismatches resulting from unpredictable user mobility. Additionally, a weight-based global confidence radius is computed to assist areas with limited data in quickly identifying their optimal arms. Finally, we prove that the resource allocation waste and regret of the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-UCB algorithm exhibit sub-linear growth. We conduct experiments in different scenarios on the MovieLens, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 datasets to illustrate its superiority over SOTA methods by around 16.2%.

title: Guest Editorial: Multi-Satellite Collaborative Communications and Networking for Future Global Satellite Internet
authors: Shuai Wang, Mérouane Debbah, John S. Thompson, Lei Lei, Chunting Wang

title: SD-ReID: View-aware Stable Diffusion for Aerial-Ground Person Re-Identification
authors: Yuhao Wang, Xiang Hu, Lixin Wang, Pingping Zhang, Huchuan Lu
abstract: Aerial-Ground Person Re-IDentification (AG-ReID) aims to retrieve specific persons across cameras with different viewpoints. Previous works focus on designing discriminative models to maintain the identity consistency despite drastic changes in camera viewpoints. The core idea behind these methods is quite natural, but designing a view-robust model is a very challenging task. Moreover, they overlook the contribution of view-specific features in enhancing the model’s ability to represent persons. To address these issues, we propose a novel generative framework named SD-ReID for AG-ReID, which leverages generative models to mimic the feature distribution of different views while extracting robust identity representations. More specifically, we first train a ViT-based model to extract person representations along with controllable conditions, including identity and view conditions. We then fine-tune the Stable Diffusion (SD) model to enhance person representations guided by these controllable conditions. Furthermore, we introduce the View-Refined Decoder (VRD) to bridge the gap between instance-level and global-level features. Finally, both person representations and all-view features are employed to retrieve target persons. Extensive experiments on five AG-ReID benchmarks (i.e., CARGO, AG-ReIDv1, AG-ReIDv2, LAGPeR and G2APS-ReID) demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method. The source code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/924973292/SD-ReID.

title: Exploiting Cross-Task Synergy via Frequency-Driven Hierarchical Learning for Multi-Task Dense Prediction
authors: Yunzhi Zhuge, Xinzhuo Yu, Lu Zhang, Xu Jia, Jin Zhan, Huchuan Lu
abstract: Multi-task dense prediction improves pixel-level performance by leveraging shared representations and inter-task collaboration. However, existing approaches either rely on implicit task relationships or neglect frequency-domain cues that are essential for preserving fine-grained details and enhancing cross-task feature learning at multiple scales. As a result, they face persistent challenges in multi-scale feature fusion, effective task interaction, and accurate decoding. To address these issues, we propose a hierarchical frequency-driven framework, termed Hierarchical Frequency-Adaptive Network (HiFAN), that facilitates cross-task collaborative optimization via frequency-domain analysis. Specifically, we first design a task-adaptive fusion module that exploits multi-scale frequency-domain information to enhance spatial details. This module generates dynamic convolutional kernels with task-specific parameters and positional biases to adaptively accommodate diverse task requirements. Next, we introduce an efficient cross-task interaction module that leverages compact low-frequency representations to enable global context exchange across tasks. Finally, we present a high-frequency-aware decoder that mitigates feature smoothing and detail loss commonly introduced by Transformer-based decoders. We demonstrate the effectiveness of HiFAN on two standard multi-task learning benchmarks, PASCAL-Context and NYUD-v2, achieving strong and competitive performance across multiple tasks. The code and model weights are available in HiFAN.

title: Single Image Reflection Removal via Iterative Prompt Learning of Reflection Level
authors: Binbin Song, Jiantao Zhou, Shuning Xu, Xina Liu, Haiwei Wu, Xiaopeng Fan, Bihan Wen
abstract: Single-image reflection removal (SIRR) aims to restore the latent background layer from a reflection-contaminated image. Despite the promising progress achieved by deep learning-based methods, the roles of negative training samples and descriptive prompts for the reflection severity are underexplored in most existing deep SIRR approaches, limiting their reflection removal performance and generalization capability. In this work, we introduce a novel training framework that synergistically leverages learnable prompts and image data to optimize the restoration network. To this end, we define reflection levels corresponding to varying degrees of reflection interference on the background content and learn reflection-level prompts to supervise the SIRR process. We propose an Iterative Reflection Level Reduction (IRLR) framework composed of a Restoration Network Training Module (RNTM) and a Reflection Level Learning Module (RLLM). Specifically, RNTM predicts the background layer under the guidance of prompts learned by RLLM, while RLLM in turn refines these prompts using outputs from RNTM. The two modules are trained iteratively to progressively reduce the reflection levels of estimated background layers. To initialize the prompts, we construct a dedicated reflection-level dataset for pretraining. For adaptively supervising RNTM, we design a new reflection-level-aware strategy to address the challenge of directly aligning the output background with the minimal reflection level. Comprehensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on average performance across several released datasets, improving PSNR by 0.82 dB and SSIM by 0.0120, respectively. The source code and dataset are available at https://github.com/NamecantbeNULL/IRLR SIRR.

title: Enhancing Coverage and Sensing in Space–Air–Ground Networks via a UAV-Assisted ISAC Framework with Satellite Backhaul
authors: Zhaoxia Ding, Zhaogui Zeng, Shuaishuai Guo, Ruidong Li, Ji Zhong, Tong-Xing Zheng, Hong-Ning Dai, Dengwang Li, Xuran Li
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Space–air–ground integrated networks (SAGINs) are expected to support ubiquitous communication and sensing in next-generation wireless systems. In this paper, we develop a stochastic-geometry-based analytical framework for a three-tier integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) network composed of ground base stations (BSs), unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), and low-earth-orbit (LEO) satellites. In the proposed architecture, ground BSs are spatially distributed according to an independent homogeneous Poisson point process (HPPP), UAVs perform sensing and uplink transmission with horizontal locations modeled by an HPPP, and LEO satellites provide relay/backhaul support with ground projections following an independent HPPP. Analytical expressions for the hop-wise communication coverage probability and the single-UAV sensing detection probability are derived. The end-to-end coverage probability is rigorously formulated as a joint event, and a tractable product-form expression is obtained by exploiting the independence of the three-tier spatial processes. Monte Carlo simulations validate the analysis and illustrate how satellite relaying, UAV deployment parameters, and propagation conditions affect communication reliability and sensing detectability. The proposed framework provides useful benchmark-level insights for the design of SAGIN-enabled ISAC systems and establishes analytical building blocks for future studies on cooperative sensing fusion.

title: Interval State Estimation for Li-Ion Batteries Using Thermally Coupled Equivalent Hydraulic Model
authors: Youdao Ma, Zhenhua Wang, Alejandro Goldar, Michel Kinnaert, Yi Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: This article investigates an interval (i.e., set-valued) state estimation problem for lithium-ion (Li-ion) battery cells. A thermally coupled equivalent hydraulic model (TEHM) characterizes lithium exchange and thermal phenomena in the electrodes of a battery cell, which is modeled as a nonlinear system with state interval constraints. For this nonlinear system, we propose an iterative zonotope-based method to achieve accurate interval estimation and investigate its convergence. In the proposed method, we adopt a zonotopic DC (ZDC) programming (where DC stands for difference of convex) to handle the nonlinearties, and present a zonotope estimator to manage the interval constraints. We consider an interval hull-based optimization problem to facilitate the zonotope estimator design, which is physically interpretable and can be efficiently solved via linear programming. Finally, results on experimental battery cell data demonstrate that the proposed method can produce accurate interval estimation results.

title: LLM-Meta-SR: In-Context Learning for Evolving Selection Operators in Symbolic Regression_supp1-3696253.pdf
authors: Qi Chen
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized algorithm development, yet their application in symbolic regression, where algorithms automatically discover symbolic expressions from data, remains limited. In this paper, we propose a meta-learning framework that enables LLMs to automatically design selection operators for evolutionary symbolic regression algorithms. We first identify two key limitations in existing LLM-based algorithm evolution techniques: lack of semantic guidance and code bloat. The absence of semantic awareness can lead to ineffective exchanges of useful code components, while bloat results in unnecessarily complex components; both can hinder evolutionary learning progress or reduce the interpretability of the designed algorithm. To address these issues, we enhance the LLM-based evolution framework for meta-symbolic regression with two key innovations: a complementary, semantics-aware selection operator and bloat control. Additionally, we embed domain knowledge into the prompt, enabling the LLM to generate more effective and contextually relevant selection operators. Our experimental results on symbolic regression benchmarks show that LLMs can devise selection operators that outperform nine expert-designed baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance. Moreover, the evolved operator can further improve a state-of-the-art symbolic regression algorithm, achieving the best performance among 28 symbolic regression and other machine learning algorithms across 116 regression datasets. This demonstrates that LLMs can exceed expert-level algorithm design for symbolic regression.

title: An Optimized Deep Learning Model for Sleep Apnea Detection
authors: Malik Abualzait, Mahmoud H. Qutqut, Ali Ahmed, Fadi Almasalha
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Sleeping is a fundamental biological function necessary for physical and mental wellbeing and overall quality of life. Sleep disorders, such as sleep apnea (SA), cause signs like difficulty concentrating, memory lapses, fatigue, lethargy, and emotional instability. SA has attracted significant research interest, with many studies using Machine Learning (ML) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The effectiveness of such a domain can be enhanced via optimization techniques. This study uses a CNN with Gray Wolf Optimization (GWO) to optimize input features and enhance diagnostic performance. Our method is benchmarked against existing approaches. Results showed an accuracy of 98.68%, a specificity of 97.92%, and sensitivity of 98.24%, with improvements of 0.12%, 0.11%, and 0.10%, respectively. These gains were achieved at the cost of a 9% increase in processing time, indicating a tradeoff between accuracy and computational efficiency. The approach showed promise for integration into wearable ECG monitors for at-home SA screening.

title: Transparent OCR Pipeline for Single-Word Recognition with Hierarchical Segmentation, Deskewing, and OCR-Aware Spell Correction
authors: Md Moshahed Parvez, Nafis Ahmed, Nawshin Nawar Nijhum, Md. Rahinur Rahman, Sad Sami
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Optical Character Recognition (OCR) remains challenging on handwritten word images due to noise, skew, and visually confusable characters. This paper presents a modular and transparent framework for single-word recognition, emphasizing interpretability over heavy machine learning. The system integrates illumination correction, adaptive medianbased binarization, hierarchical row/column segmentation, and shear-based skew correction with safeguards against degenerate cases. Characters are extracted via connected-component analysis, size-filtered, normalized to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$128 \times 128$</tex> pixels, and recognized using template correlation against exemplars from the NIST Special Database 19 (SD19). Post-recognition, a dictionary-guided spell corrector tailored for OCR noise resolves frequent confusions (e.g., <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0 \leftrightarrow O, F \leftrightarrow E, B \leftrightarrow R$</tex>) through prioritized substitutions and weighted edit-distance search. Experiments on 240 self-written handwritten word images yielded a Word Recognition Rate (WRR) of 79.6 %. At the character level, accuracy improved from 81.4 % to 93.1 % after spell correction, while the Character Error Rate (CER) dropped from 18.6 % to 6.97 %. Comparative tests show that the proposed system performs at a similar level to baseline CNNs and modern OCR transformers trained on the same dataset, while maintaining transparency and lightweight implementation in MATLAB. The results confirm that rule-based OCR, when carefully engineered, remains a practical baseline for reliable single-word recognition.

title: Vehicle Theft Detection and Prevention System in Challenging Environment Using Deep Learning
authors: Razia Sultana, Puja Mazumdar, Safwat Mukarrama Choudhury
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Vehicle theft remains a persistent problem worldwide, with developing regions such as Bangladesh dealing with unique challenges due to limited surveillance infrastructure and slow emergency response. This paper proposes a real time proactive intelligent vehicle theft detection and prevention System that leverages advanced computer vision and edge computing technologies to detect suspicious activities and alert vehicle owners instantly to prevent such incidence. The system uses the YOLOv8 deep learning model for high accuracy object detection, including license plate recognition and human activity monitoring. Using a Raspberry Pi 4 as its processing unit, it performs local inference to ensure minimal latency, data privacy, and offline operability. License plates are extracted and analyzed using EasyOCR, and ultralytics while decision logic identifies theft scenarios by correlating human activity. Upon detecting unauthorized access or suspicious behavior, the system sets off a buzzer alarm along with sending an SMS alert via the SIM800L GSM module to the registered owner. Two different dataset featuring diverse illumination, angles, and real world scenarios was used to train and test the model, achieving over 89 % mAP in license plate detection and 96.5% accuracy in human activity recognition. The proposed system is flexible, reasonable, and suitable for deployment in urban, rural, and environments with limited resources. This work presents a scalable solution toward augmenting vehicular security utilizing AI driven edge intelligence.

title: Comparative Study of Parkinson's Disease Detection from Hand-Drawn Patterns Using Dual-Input Efficientnet and Custom CNN
authors: Meherin Tasfia, Abdullah Al Numan, Minhaj Ahmed Habil
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Parkinson's Disease (PD) is a common neurodegenerative disease affecting significantly motor functions of older people. It affects the nervous system which leads to impaired movement and thus hand writing. PD affected people tend to exhibit lower pressure and velocity while writing or sketching. This phenomenon can be observed by collecting spiral and wave drawings from both PD affected and healthy controls. This work highlights the potential of automated, non-invasive screening tools for early-stage Parkinson's diagnosis, offering promise for clinical decision support and improved patient outcomes. This paper presents a novel approach for early Parkinson's disease (PD) detection utilizing deep learning techniques on spiral and wave drawing data. Leveraging the EfficientNet convolutional neural network architecture, the proposed model effectively extracts key features from drawing tests, capturing subtle motor impairments characteristic of PD. Experimental evaluation on benchmark datasets demonstrates that the EfficientNet-based classifier achieves high accuracy, precision, and F1-score in distinguishing Parkinson's patients from healthy controls.

title: A Real-Time Sign Language Translator on Jetson Nano Using Mediapipe, MobileNet-V2 and LSTM
authors: Md. Yusuf, Takia Nawrin, Maftun Ahommed, Nafees Mansoor
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: The inability to communicate verbally isolates millions of deaf and hard-of-hearing people worldwide. The World Health Organization reports that more than 466 million people worldwide have disabling hearing loss. Sign languages are the primary means of communication for these people, but the lack of widespread sign-language literacy creates significant barriers in social, educational and professional settings. This article presents a complete design, implementation and evaluation of a real-time sign-language translation system running on an NVIDIA Jetson Nano. The system captures hand gestures with an on-board camera, extracts 3-D hand landmarks using Mediapipe Holistic, produces compact spatial features via a pre-trained MobileNet-V2, models temporal dynamics with a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network and converts recognized signs into text. Comprehensive evaluation demonstrates that the proposed system achieves 98% accuracy on American Sign Language (ASL) letters, runs at 25–30 frames per second on Jetson Nano and consumes under 10W of power. The combination of efficient hardware and lightweight deep learning makes the proposed system a practical bridge between sign-language users and the broader population.

title: Evaluating Temporal Fusion Transformers for Short-Horizon Urban Traffic Forecasting Under Real-World Deployment Constraints
authors: Khang Nguyen, Dennis O. Xu
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Reliable short-horizon traffic forecasting is critical for urban traffic management, freight logistics, and delivery reliability. While advanced temporal deep learning models have shown strong performance on benchmark time-series tasks, their operational benefit over established industry baselines remains unclear under real-world deployment constraints. This paper evaluates whether Temporal Fusion Transformers (TFTs) provide meaningful improvements over a widely deployed gradient-boosted decision tree (GBDT) baseline for short-term urban traffic congestion forecasting and route-level estimated time of arrival (ETA) prediction. Using probe-speed roadway data from the Georgia Department of Transportation, traffic state is modeled at the Traffic Message Channel (TMC) level via a normalized speed ratio. Both models predict identical multi-horizon targets at 5,15,30, and 60 minutes and are evaluated on the same chronologically held-out test set. Segment-level predictions are aggregated into route-level ETAs to assess comparative downstream utility under a static aggregation framework. Results show that the GBDT baseline outperforms the TFT at short horizons (5-15 minutes), while the TFT achieves lower error at longer horizons (30 - 60 minutes). These findings indicate that increased model complexity does not uniformly improve near-term forecasting and is primarily justified for longer-horizon planning scenarios.

title: Pattern Recognition of Cardiovascular Arrhythmia from ECG Signals using LabVIEW and Artificial Neural Network
authors: Sakhawat Hossen Rakib, A. S. M. Bakibillah, Muhammad Towfiqur Rahman, A. M. A. Zaidi, Md Shafiul Alam, Tasnim Tamanna Rafa
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This study proposes a unique method of pattern recognition and analysis of various types of Cardiovascular Arrhythmia from Electrocardiogram (ECG) signals. The analyzed features of the ECG signals are the PR-intervals, heart rates, QRS-width, QT-intervals, and the RR-intervals. Any eccentricity found in these classified features indicates the presence of a heart problem known as cardiovascular arrhythmia. The signal processing is accomplished using LabVIEW, and the extracted features were used to design a Multi-Layer Perceptron Artificial Neural Network (MLP-ANN) for the purpose of recognizing and distinguishing patterns between normal sinus rhythm and cardiovascular arrhythmia, which are Slow Term Atrial Fibrillation (STAF), Paroxysmal Atrial Fibrillation (PAF), and the Supraventricular Tachycardia (ST). The data required to train ANN was collected from the MIT-BIH database available on PhysioNet Database. This proposed method detects any early sign of classified cardiovascular arrhythmia from ECG signals by recognizing and classifying patterns of signals using state-of-the-art ANN algorithms, which can be life-saving in crucial conditions.

title: Classical Trajectory Planning for Dual-Camera Visual Servoing on Edge Systems
authors: Abilash Philip Madavath, Chandra Yuvesh Aubeeluck, Augustin Raju, Felix Hackelöer, Florian Zwanzig, Nicolas Pyschny
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Grasping moving objects in industrial environments requires precise synchronization between perception and robot actuation. While deep learning has advanced object detection, the latency introduced by Edge AI computation can degrade interception accuracy in high-speed conveyor applications. This paper presents a comparative analysis of trajectory prediction and interception algorithms implemented on an edge platform (NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano). The proposed system utilizes a dual-camera architecture, handing over control from a conveyor overhead predictor to a local wrist-mounted visual servo. We study two prediction algorithms (RANSAC-based linear extrapolation versus Kalman filtering) and five interception strategies (Analytical Linear, Fixed-Point Iteration, Newton-Raphson, Secant, and Bisection methods) against ground-truth data obtained via an OptiTrack motion capture system. Results indicate that while RANSAC offers superior fitting accuracy, the Kalman filter provides the low-latency performance required for real-time control. Among interception solvers, the Newton-Raphson method demonstrated the optimal balance between computational speed and stability. Furthermore, we analyze the impact of adding lead time on the interception success rate. The results quantify these trade-offs, establishing design guidelines for algorithm selection in real-time dynamic picking.

title: Performance Evaluation of a Hybrid Passive-Active Wristband for Efficient Parkinsonian Tremor Reduction
authors: Adam Koubbi, Badr Boughrara, Salma Sebbar, Yassine Salih-Alj
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Hybrid wrist-worn devices that combine passive damping with active feedback control offer a promising approach to suppressing Parkinsonian tremor while maintaining energy efficiency. Although several designs have been proposed, the relationship between system parameters and overall performance remains insufficiently characterized. This paper presents an evaluation of a hybrid wristband architecture in which a tuned-mass damper and a PID-controlled actuator operate in parallel. A two-degree-of-freedom forearm model is subjected to tremor excitation, and the system is assessed across varied damping and control configurations. Performance is quantified using three key metrics: root-mean-square (RMS) tremor attenuation, actuator control effort, and total energy consumption. The evaluation framework enables systematic analysis of design trade-offs and supports the development of wearable tremor-suppression systems that balance responsiveness with power efficiency.

title: Learning Compact 3D Gaussians via Feed-Forward Point Fusion
authors: Brandon Smart, Chuanxia Zheng, Iro Laina, Victor Adrian Prisacariu
abstract: We present Splatt3RFusion, a feed-forward neural network that, given a set of unposed and uncalibrated images, directly reconstructs a compact and high-quality 3D Gaussian Splat representation of a scene. Unlike prior pixel-aligned feed-forward methods that typically predict one 3D Gaussian primitive per pixel in each image - producing severe redundancy, duplication, and ghosting on one physical surface - our approach efficiently fuses points in 3D space through a multi-scale octree structure, yielding a compact and coherent representation. Built upon VGGT, a foundation model for pose-free 3D geometry prediction, Splatt3RFusion introduces a Gaussian prediction branch that infers primitive parameters using only photometric supervision. We also introduce the ability to control the number of 3D Gaussians generated at test-time, allowing for a controllable tradeoff between PSNR and the number of 3D Gaussian primitives used. The model is efficient, reducing both memory usage and rendering cost, while achieving state-of-the-art results on RealEstate10k and ScanNet++.

title: Parametric Gaussian Human Model: Generalizable Prior for Efficient and Realistic Human Avatar Modeling
authors: Cheng Peng, Jingxiang Sun, Yushuo Chen, Zhaoqi Su, Zhuo Su, Yebin Liu
abstract: Photorealistic and animatable human avatars are a key enabler for virtual/augmented reality, telepresence, and digital entertainment. While recent advances in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have greatly improved rendering quality and efficiency, existing methods still face fundamental challenges, including time-consuming per-subject optimization and poor generalization under sparse monocular inputs. In this work, we present the Parametric Gaussian Human Model (PGHM), a generalizable and efficient framework that integrates human priors into 3DGS for fast and high-fidelity avatar reconstruction from monocular videos. PGHM introduces two core components: (1) a UV-aligned latent identity map that compactly encodes subject-specific geometry and appearance into a learnable feature tensor; and (2) a disentangled Multi-Head U-Net that predicts Gaussian attributes by decomposing static, pose-dependent, and view-dependent components via conditioned decoders. This design enables robust rendering quality under challenging poses and viewpoints, while allowing efficient subject adaptation without requiring multiview capture or long optimization time. Experiments show that PGHM is significantly more efficient than optimization-from-scratch methods, requiring only approximately 20 minutes per subject to produce avatars with comparable visual quality, thereby demonstrating its practical applicability for real-world monocular avatar creation.

title: Unified Geometry and Color Compression Framework for Point Clouds via Generative Diffusion Priors
authors: Tianxin Huang, Gim Hee Lee
abstract: With the growth of 3D applications and the rapid increase in sensor-collected 3D point cloud data, there is a rising demand for efficient compression algorithms. Most existing learning-based compression methods handle geometry and color attributes separately, treating them as distinct tasks, making these methods challenging to apply directly to point clouds with colors. Besides, the limited capacities of training datasets also limit their generalizability across points with different distributions. In this work, we introduce a test-time unified geometry and color compression framework for 3D point clouds. Instead of training a compression model based on specific datasets, we adapt a pre-trained generative diffusion model to compress original colored point clouds into sparse sets, termed 'seeds', using prompt tuning. Decompression is then achieved through multiple denoising steps with separate sampling processes. Experiments on objects and indoor scenes demonstrate that our method has superior performances compared to existing baselines for the compression of geometry and color. Our codes would be released at https://github.com/Tianxinhuang/DiffCom.git.

title: DINO in the Room: Leveraging 2D Foundation Models for 3D Segmentation
authors: Karim Knaebel, Kadir Yilmaz, Daan De Geus, Alexander Hermans, David Adrian, Timm Linder, Bastian Leibe
abstract: Vision foundation models (VFMs) trained on large-scale image datasets provide high-quality features that have significantly advanced <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> visual recognition. However, their potential in 3D scene segmentation remains largely untapped, despite the common availability of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> images alongside 3D point cloud datasets. While significant research has been dedicated to 2D-3D fusion, recent state-of-the-art 3D methods predominantly focus on 3D data, leaving the integration of VFMs into 3D models underexplored. In this work, we challenge this trend by introducing DITR, a generally applicable approach that extracts <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> foundation model features, projects them to 3D, and finally injects them into a 3D point cloud segmentation model. DITR achieves state-of-the-art results on both indoor and outdoor 3D semantic segmentation benchmarks. To enable the use of VFMs even when images are unavailable during inference, we additionally propose to pretrain 3D models by distilling 2D foundation models. By initializing the 3D backbone with knowledge distilled from 2D VFMs, we create a strong basis for downstream 3D segmentation tasks, ultimately boosting performance across various datasets.

title: Enhancing 3D Object Tracking via Dual-Context Propagation and Temporal Context Fusion
authors: Peijing Jiang, Yuanping Zhang, Jinlong Pang, Zhongjun Lin
abstract: Point cloud-based 3D single object tracking (3D SOT) plays a pivotal role in applications such as autonomous driving and robotic vision. Despite recent progress, most existing approaches rely solely on current-frame features for target localization. This approach overlooks temporal information that is crucial for robust tracking under occlusion, appearance variations, and sparse point clouds. In addition, the effectiveness of 3D SOT largely depends on the quality of feature fusion between the target template and the search region. Traditional fusion strategies often suffer from limited interaction capacity and weak discriminative representation. To address these challenges, we propose DT-Tracker, which performs multi-layer bidirectional feature interaction and temporal cue propagation to improve tracking robustness and feature discrimination capability. Specifically, we introduce a Dual-Context Propagation Network that applies bidirectional cross-attention across multiple layers between the template and search region, enabling deep semantic alignment and progressive feature refinement. Furthermore, we design a Temporal Context Fusion module that adaptively incorporates temporal cues from historical fusion features into the current frame, effectively improving resilience to occlusion and appearance drift. Extensive experiments on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets demonstrate that DT-Tracker achieves competitive results compared to existing representative methods.

title: Robotic Welding with AI in the Loop
authors: Hamid Reza Saadati, Wojciech Kowalczyk, Sebastian Feldmann
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive overview of developing and implementing an AI-enabled intelligent robotic welding system. The primary objective of the project is to design and realize a fully automated welding solution that combines CAD-based path planning, real-time seam tracking, cloud-based data streaming, and AI-driven defect detection. The proposed system is capable of performing high-precision and consistent welding operations, thereby improving process efficiency, enhancing weld quality, and minimizing the need for manual intervention or conventional robot programming. Key components of the system include dynamic vision-based seam tracking integrated with arc sensing feedback, CAD file processing for optimized vision-based seam tracking, and cloud connectivity for remote monitoring and data analysis. Additionally, an AI model is deployed for real-time defect detection and classification, contributing to a closed-loop, intelligent welding process.
