title: Force Control of an Underactuated Tendon-Driven Manipulator_supp1-3688030.mp4
authors: Nicolas Jacques Serge Testard
abstract: This article investigates the application of force controls on a tendon-driven underactuated robotic arm. A direct use of classical hybrid force/motion control based on computed torque control can lead to instability for this type of system. Reformulating the hybrid force/motion control using the pseudo-computed torque control (PCTC) framework leads to a stable control strategy. Finally, it is shown that applying admittance control in combination with PCTC also results in stable behavior when exerting a force against a wall or in a cobotics context. These results are then validated through experimentation.

title: Noninvasive Voltage Measurement for Transmission Lines and Substations Using Lightweight Monitoring Devices Based on Torsion-Mirror Electric-Field Sensors
authors: Kun Ke, Liuting Yang, Ziheng Yao, Chengbo Hu, Zhen Wang, Yanjie Hu, Qing Yang
abstract: To address the demand for miniaturized and easily deployable nonintrusive voltage measurement in power systems, this article proposes a lightweight voltage measurement method and device based on a resonant torsional Micro-Electro-Mechanical System electric-field sensing structure. First, based on the spatial distribution characteristics of the electric field, a near-conductor electric-field measurement method is proposed, and a voltage reconstruction model using a multipoint differential electric-field array is established. Correction coefficients are further introduced to compensate for reconstruction errors under practical deployment conditions. A lightweight voltage monitoring device integrating a MEMS electric-field sensor, signal conditioning circuitry, electromagnetic shielding, and wireless communication is subsequently developed. The periodic modulation conversion mechanism of the torsional micromirror electric-field sensor is analyzed, and a detection system based on closed-loop gain control is proposed for real-time and accurate electric-field measurement. A nonintrusive voltage measurement experimental platform is also established. Experimental results show that the device exhibits good linear tracking characteristics over a voltage range of 5–100 kV, with maximum measurement errors for AC and DC voltages limited to 2.5% and 1.4%, respectively. Finally, practical applications on a 220 kV overhead transmission line and at a converter station are demonstrated, verifying the engineering feasibility and operational stability of the proposed nonintrusive voltage measurement scheme and supporting its application in power systems.

title: A Ka-Band Eight-Stream Phased-Array MIMO Receiver With Time-Hopping Blocker Rejection for 6G
authors: Minzhe Tang, Dongfan Xu, Yi Zhang, Yuxuan Liu, Zheng Li, Yuncheng Zhang, Yilun Chen, Minghao Fan, Ziyuan Ren, Jian Pang, Dingxin Xu, Chenxin Liu, Daxu Zhang, Duo Li, Hiroyuki Sakai, Kazuaki Kunihiro, Atsushi Shirane, Kenichi Okada
abstract: This article presents a Ka-band phased-array multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) receiver for sixth-generation (6G) wireless networks. A time-division (TD) MIMO architecture is combined with DP antenna array to support eight MIMO streams in the Ka band. The complex weights of each RF element in both polarization arrays are sequentially switched at the Nyquist rate to enable fast-stream switching for MIMO stream coexistence. The TD approach achieves full phased-array reuse and delivers high array gain and spatial selectivity for each stream at the expense of generating switching harmonics. To address this issue, a non-uniform time-hopping (TH) sequence is introduced in this article to spectrally spread the switching harmonics and mitigate the direct blocker folding. A proof-of-concept phased-array receiver is fabricated in 65-nm CMOS, integrating eight RF elements in a 7.2-mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> core area and consuming 335.2 mW. Combined with DP antennas, the phased-array receiver supports <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4 \times 4$</tex-math> </inline-formula> TD-MIMO across both polarizations and achieves a maximum data rate of 38.4Gb/s using eight 800-MHz 64-QAM signals in over-the-air (OTA) measurements. Benefiting from the TD-MIMO architecture, the phased-array receiver achieves 0.078 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and 5.23 mW per element per stream. With the proposed non-uniform TH (NUTH) dithering, deep notches are generated at switching harmonics and 42.9-dB alias suppression is achieved. The proposed phased-array receiver also demonstrates a 34-dB improvement in blocker tolerance, maintaining –30-dB EVM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rms</sub> under a 0-dBc in-band blocker sweeping across –90° to 90° field of view (FoV) in MIMO OTA measurements.

title: Carbon Emission-Based Sustainability Model For Photonic Neural Network Accelerators
authors: Siqin Liu, Avinash Karanth, Ahmed Louri
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: As the energy consumed in manufacturing and operating information and computing technologies (ICT) accounts for an increasing percentage of worldwide carbon emissions, there is a growing urgency to address the carbon footprint generated by the computing infrastructure. Although the world has witnessed a drastic increase in computing performance for large neural network models with the use of more transistors and miniaturized dimensions, the advanced fabrication of these highend integrated circuits incurs more energy and environmental costs than use-phase costs, making it a critical sustainability challenge. Emerging technology such as silicon photonics is being proposed for both communication and computation in neural network accelerators due to better performance-perwatt and higher bandwidth density compared to traditional electronics. In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive carbon emission analysis on hardware accelerators for neural network applications considering both traditional electrical and emerging silicon photonics technology. We propose a carbon-emissionbased sustainability model that considers photonic devices and interconnects. The model accounts for both the carbon emissions due to manufacturing as well as the lifetime operation of the devices. We compare three designs - electrical, photonics, and hybrid architectures - that explore the trade-offs between computational performance and environmental costs for different neural network models. With our proposed sustainability-aware design, we demonstrate that utilizing silicon photonics improves computational performance along with superior environmental sustainability.

title: Automatic Compact Model Parameter Extraction with an Enhanced Hybrid of Differential Evolution and Nelder-Mead Optimizers
authors: Fahad Ali Usmani, Zijian Song, Roberto Tinti
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: An industry-strength hybrid optimization approach, involving derivative-free global search followed by derivative-free local refinement, is proposed to solve the multi-dimensional problem of compact model parameter extraction and reduce its multi-objective nature while retaining the original device physical formulations. An intelligent transition method is employed to combine Differential Evolution and Nelder-Mead algorithms from a curated selection of machine learning-driven optimizer candidates, achieving better-fitting performance in various operating regions for the first time. Extraction results for compact models such as ASM-HEMT with 35 parameters and BSIM4 with 29 parameters in 3 steps show that a best-fit error of less than $0.2 \%$ is achieved with much less automatic extraction time of 1800 seconds compared to hours of global search alone and weeks of traditional manual parameter tuning. A new cost function is introduced to reduce sensitivity to outliers and introduce region-appropriate automatic weights while preserving device physical formulations. This approach helps reduce total optimization time by a factor of at least 2 X compared to global optimization alone and reduces dependency on modeling know-how.

title: The parameters of three classes of extended BCH codes
authors: Ketong Ren, Haode Yan, Zhengchun Zhou, Jun Zhang
abstract: The favorable algebraic properties and error-correcting performance of BCH codes have motivated extensive research and diverse applications, whereas the study of extended BCH codes remains relatively less explored. In this paper, we focus on the extended BCH code C(q,q+1,δ,h) over the finite field Fq. Specifically, we investigate the parameters of three families of extended BCH codes and the weight distributions of their duals by solving certain quadratic and quartic equations: C(q,q+1,3,1) is shown to be NMDS when gcd(q+1,3) = 1; C(q,q+1,δ,q) with 3 ≤ δ ≤ ⌈(q + 5)/2⌉ is proved to be AMDS; and C(q,q+1,3,q/2) is MDS for even q.

title: An Advanced Kronecker Product-Based Time-Delay Neural Network for Radio Frequency Power Amplifier Digital Predistortion
authors: Jianfeng Shao, Xi Hong, Wenjie Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: Neural networks (NNs) have emerged as the most prevalent and critical solution for digital predistortion (DPD) of radio frequency (RF) power amplifiers (PAs) in recent research. Existing schemes of DPD NNs can be categorized into two classes: those whose input datasets incorporate nonlinear characteristics derived from the memory polynomial (MP), and those that integrate a functional phase recovery block (PRB). These mainstream approaches of DPD NNs have been validated to be effective in both simulation studies and practical engineering implementations. However, existing schemes of DPD NNs lack a tailored fitting strategy for the inherent nonlinear characteristics of PAs, resulting in slow convergence and failure to achieve the desired performance metrics. To address this limitation, this article proposes an advanced Kronecker product-based time-delay NN (AKPTDNN). The proposed NN is built upon the block-oriented time-delay NN (BOTDNN) and the Kronecker product (KP)-based modules. It consists of multiple linear layers with nonlinear activation and KP operations, which are specifically designed to construct nonlinear characteristics in the form of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$x(n{\,}-{\,}m_{1})|x(n{\,}-{\,}m_{2})|^{p}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, which is based on the basis function of generalized MP (GMP) model. Furthermore, unlike KP-based modules, the proposed NN does not pass the terms, which contain the phase characteristics, through nonlinear activation functions, thus avoiding the corruption of phase characteristics. These design measures are intended to reduce the fitting burden on the NN, the effectiveness of which has been verified via NN training experiments. Performance validation using orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) signals with varying bandwidths demonstrates that AKPTDNN achieves faster convergence than the two reference schemes of DPD NNs, as quantified by the average normalized mean square error (NMSE) and adjacent channel power ratio (ACPR). Furthermore, comparative analysis of the performance distribution reveals that AKPTDNN exhibits a more stable performance range, indicating that it is less sensitive to NN initialization parameters.

title: Hierarchical Scene Fusion for Trajectory Prediction in Autonomous Driving
authors: Hui Li, Qinghua Lin, Ye Tao, Xiangzhen Kong, Xiaoxue Ai, Fahou Wang
abstract: In traffic scenarios, the trajectory of a target vehicle is influenced by the combined effects of road elements and surrounding vehicles within both its local and global driving field-of-view. The development of Vehicle-to-Everything communication allows autonomous vehicles to acquire broader dynamic scene information, but also imposes higher demands on interaction modeling. Therefore, accurately and efficiently modeling the interactions between vehicles and road information across different visual ranges is crucial for rational decision-making in autonomous driving. To provide a scalable solution compatible with such a dynamic scene, we propose Hierarchical Scene Fusion for Trajectory Prediction in Autonomous Driving. First, we propose a Local Indicator Neighborhood Attention module with relative position encoding. Based on a novel token-centric data representation, it efficiently captures social interactions between vehicles and roads within the local driving scene. Second, we propose a Global Indicator Tokens Attention module. This module utilizes Global Indicator Tokens to unify the mapping of the target vehicle's local driving field-of-view information onto the global driving scene, capturing long-range semantic interactions while overcoming information loss caused by excessive scene elements during global interaction. Finally, we propose an Agent-Specific Decoding Mechanism. This mechanism employs a dynamically weighted network to train an adaptive Bézier curve model, which regresses more accurate predicted trajectories based on the latent features of each target vehicle. Experimental results on the public Argoverse 1 and Argoverse 2 datasets demonstrate that our method achieves superior trajectory prediction performance with fewer parameters. The source code can be obtained at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/John122-art/MDC-HVA</uri>.

title: Turbo Equalization for High-Speed Passive Optical Networks
authors: Tianyuan Kong, Nebojša Stojanović, Tom Jonas Wettlin, Giuseppe Caruso, Balazs Matuz, Emna Ben Yacoub, Marvin Xhemrishi, Xianbo Dai, Stefano Calabrò, Maxim Kuschnerov, Stephan Pachnicke
abstract: High-speed passive optical networks (PONs) demand advanced digital signal processing (DSP) to achieve higher data rates under stringent bandwidth constraints. In this work, we present an analytical framework to estimate the theoretical turbo equalization (TE) gain for unit-memory channels of the form <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1+\alpha D$</tex-math></inline-formula>, applicable to arbitrary inter-symbol interference (ISI) levels <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula> and forward error correction (FEC) code rates. The proposed TE algorithm iteratively exchanges soft information between a log-domain MAP detector and an LDPC decoder based on the standardized (17280,14592) code. Simulation and experimental results for PAM2 and PAM4 signaling confirm significant performance improvements, closely approaching the predicted theoretical gains. We provide a combined theoretical and experimental assessment of TE gains in high-speed optical access networks, offering a practical solution to mitigate ISI and enhance throughput in bandwidth-limited PON receivers.

title: Universal Analytical Framework for the Secrecy Performance Evaluation of Multi-Carrier Systems Under Practical Rate-Adaptation Constraints
authors: Miguel López-Benítez
abstract: The secrecy performance analysis of multi-carrier systems often overlooks important practical limitations, such as constraints on the flexibility with which data rates can be individually adapted across sub-carriers in real-world systems. In this context, this letter presents novel analytical results based on the concept of effective SNR, which explicitly incorporates such constraints. The resulting analysis is universal in nature, as it remains valid and readily applicable to any fading scenario. The derived analytical results provide a powerful and tractable framework for evaluating the impact of both the number of subcarriers and the chosen data-rate configuration on the secrecy performance of multi-carrier systems under realistic operating conditions. A key outcome of this analysis is the demonstration that, contrary to common assumptions, the secrecy performance of multi-carrier systems does not necessarily improve with an increasing number of sub-carriers; instead, when practical rate-adaptation constraints are considered, it can in fact degrade.

title: Bridging Physics and Priors: A Unified Diffusion Framework for Underwater Image Restoration
authors: Chen Li, Zhe Sun, Xuelong Li
abstract: Underwater image restoration presents fundamental challenges in multi-modal learning and cross-domain synthesis due to complex optical degradation effects, such as wavelengthdependent light absorption and scattering. Prevailing learningbased methods face a fundamental “data-model dilemma” due to the scarcity of high-quality paired data, forcing them to rely on either physically unrealistic synthetic data, which creates a large domain gap, or biased pseudo-ground-truths, which limits their performance ceiling. To break this impasse, we propose a unified diffusion framework that bridges the gap between physical modeling and generative priors. Our framework first introduces OpenWater, a physics-informed parametric transfer framework that integrates multi-modal information from RGB imagery, depth maps, and physical degradation parameters to generate large-scale, physically-grounded paired datasets through cross-domain synthesis. Subsequently, we present Water2Air, a multi-prior fusion framework that leverages diffusion models to integrate physical priors, generative priors, and semantic guidance in a unified restoration process with comprehensive degradation-aware conditioning. A progressive, curriculum-based training strategy is employed to smoothly adapt the model from the synthetic to the real domain, enhancing its generalization capability. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that our method not only achieves state-of-theart performance quantitatively and qualitatively, but also often produces results perceptually superior to the provided reference images. Our work provides a new paradigm for underwater image restoration, and the code and dataset will be made publicly available.

title: MambaCell: A Self-Supervised Mamba Framework for Multi-Task Cell Representation Learning
authors: Lu Yu, Yutong Liu, Wensheng Xiang, Changjian Zhou
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) has become a groundbreaking tool in life science research. Moreover, advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have greatly catalyzed the development of cellular foundation models in transcriptomics. However, existing scRNA-seq models built on LLMs predominantly employ transformer architectures, which are constrained by quadratic computational complexity during inference, limiting their ability to process long sequences. Furthermore, current cellular foundation models often focus on a single objective like Masked Language Modeling (MLM), which limits their ability to capture semantically rich and biologically meaningful representations. Critically, the field lacks an efficient and general framework for scalable cell representation learning. To address these limitations, we introduce MambaCell, a multi-task self-supervised learning framework based on the bidirectional Mamba architecture. This novel framework integrates two complementary self-supervised tasks including Masked Gene Modeling (MGM) and Contrastive Learning (CL), enabling it to learn robust cell representations from large-scale, un-labeled datasets with reduced inference costs. Experimental results demonstrate that MambaCell achieves superior or comparable performance to state-of-the-art (SOTA) models on a series of downstream tasks, including cell type annotation, disease-related cell classification, single-cell batch integration, etc. Notably, MambaCell achieves faster inference speed and higher memory efficiency compared to transformer-based models with comparable parameters. These superior performance and efficiency gains underscore the effectiveness of MambaCell's architectural innovation, establishing it as a scalable solution for large-scale single-cell transcriptomic analysis.

title: HEART: Achieving Timely Multi-Model Training for Vehicle-Edge-Cloud-Integrated Hierarchical Federated Learning
authors: Xiaohong Yang, Minghui Liwang, Xianbin Wang, Zhipeng Cheng, Seyyedali Hosseinalipour, Huaiyu Dai, Zhenzhen Jiao
abstract: The rapid growth of AI-enabled Internet of Vehicles (IoV) calls for efficient Machine Learning (ML) solutions that can handle high vehicular mobility and decentralized data. This has motivated the emergence of Hierarchical Federated Learning over vehicle-edge-cloud architectures (VEC-HFL). Nevertheless, one aspect which is underexplored in the literature on VEC HFL is that vehicles often need to execute multiple ML tasks simultaneously, where this multi-model training environment in troduces crucial challenges. First, improper aggregation rules can lead to model obsolescence and prolonged training times. Second, vehicular mobility may result in inefficient data utilization by preventing the vehicles from returning their models to the network edge. Third, achieving a balanced resource allocation across diverse tasks becomes of paramount importance as it majorly affects the effectiveness of collaborative training. We take one of the first steps towards addressing these challenges via proposing a framework for multi-model training in dynamic VEC-HFL with the goal of minimizing global training latency while ensuring balanced training across various tasks, a problem that turns out to be NP-hard. To facilitate timely model training, we introduce a hybrid synchronous-asynchronous aggregation rule. Building on this, we present a novel method called Hybrid Evolutionary And gReedy allocaTion (HEART). The framework operates in two stages: first, it achieves balanced task scheduling through a hybrid heuristic approach that combines improved Particle SwarmOptimization (PSO) and Genetic Algorithms (GA); second, it employs a low-complexity greedy algorithm to determine the training priority of assigned tasks on vehicles. Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate the superiority of HEART over existing method.

title: Generating Textual Descriptions for Remote Sensing Images
authors: Maryam Mehmood, Ahsan Shahzad, Amin Ullah, Farhan Hussain, Samra Nawazish, Ruhul Amin Khalil
abstract: Generating descriptive and semantically meaningful captions for remote sensing images remains a challenging task due to the inherent complexity of aerial scenes, characterized by diverse spatial arrangements, scale variations, and ambiguous semantics. To address these challenges, this study introduces a hybrid deep learning framework that integrates a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), a Transformer encoder, and a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) decoder. In the proposed architecture, ResNet50 is employed to extract hierarchical visual features, while the Transformer encoder models long-range spatial dependencies and global contextual relationships. The resulting feature representations are refined through a fusion mechanism and subsequently utilized by an LSTM-based decoder to generate coherent and context-aware textual descriptions. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is evaluated on benchmark remote sensing datasets, namely UCM and RSICD. Experimental results indicate that the model achieves competitive performance, with BLEU-4 scores of 0.74 and 0.54, and CIDEr scores of 3.32 and 3.49, respectively. These findings demonstrate the capability of the proposed framework to generate accurate and contextually relevant captions for complex aerial imagery. The study highlights the potential of hybrid architectures in improving automated remote sensing image understanding and caption generation.

title: Improving Privacy in Iris Recognition Through Secure Matching of Protected Biometric Data
authors: Osama Ouda

title: Coordinated Voltage Support Control Scheme for Islanded Sending-End MMC-Wind Turbine System During Asymmetric Faults Considering Weak Grid Characteristic
authors: Jianan Yan, Xiaojie Shi, Tao Liu, Shi Liu, Zhiqiang Wang, Tianxiang Yin, Lei Lin
abstract: The weak grid characteristic (WGC) in the islanded sending-end modular multilevel converter (MMC)-wind turbine (WT) system narrows feasible regions and causes severe terminal voltage imbalance during asymmetric faults. Such characteristics invalidate conventional asymmetric voltage support control schemes for grid-connected inverters when applied to the islanded system. Moreover, existing asymmetric ride-through (ART) control schemes for the islanded scenario neither aim to enhance voltage support nor comprehensively consider the WGC. To address these issues, this paper reveals the voltage support principle from a circuit perspective and derives analytical expressions for boundaries of the feasible regions. On this basis, a stiff region is rationally configured, and a coordinated voltage support control scheme is proposed. The proposed control scheme not only mitigates the adverse effects of the WGC, but also offers three key advantages: 1) decentralized coordination relying solely on local signals, 2) maximum voltage support point tracking of the MMC and WTs, and 3) unaffected active current injection of WTs. The superior ART performance and adaptability of the proposed control scheme are validated through simulation and control-hardware-in-the-loop experimental results.

title: Electromagnetic Vibration Characteristics Analysis of Dual Three-phase Permanent Magnet Synchronous Linear Motor Considering High-Frequency Current Harmonics
authors: Yinhao Mao, Zhaolong Sun, Anmin Ding, Chuibing Huang
abstract: This paper investigates the electromagnetic vibration characteristics of a slotless dual three-phase permanent magnet linear synchronous motor (DTP-PMLSM), specifically addressing the vibration induced by high-frequency switching harmonics. Unlike conventional approaches, a refined coupled-field analytical model is established, explicitly accounting for the double-sided magnetic coupling effect and the substantial non-magnetic spacing. Based on this precise field calculation, analytical derivations reveal a compound high-frequency vibration mechanism. Crucially, a vectorial phase-cancellation effect is identified, proving that the harmonic self-excitation, rather than the permanent magnet (PM) interaction, acts as the absolute dominant excitation source in this symmetric topology. To characterize the structural dynamics, an Euler-Bernoulli beam model is proposed. Furthermore, a specialized high-acceleration dynamic trajectory strategy is implemented to overcome the inherent finite stroke constraint of linear motors for valid steady-state data acquisition. Experimental results show excellent agreement with the analytical predictions. Finally, to bridge theory and application, an active mitigation strategy via Carrier Phase-Shifted (CPS) PWM is experimentally implemented, successfully achieving a 10-12 dB reduction in high-frequency vibration. This study provides a rigorous theoretical basis and practical mitigation guidelines for high-precision linear propulsion systems.

title: Joint Deployment, User Association, and Power Allocation for Data Collection in UAV-Assisted Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: Yanping Liu, Kunkun Zhang, Xuming Fang, Ming Xiao, Fuhong Song, Qing Xue, Yaping Cui, Changfeng Ding
abstract: In recent years, uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) have become increasingly prevalent for collecting environmental data from various wireless sensors. However, existing research on employing UAVs to collect data from wireless sensors has often ignored the heterogeneous requirements of sensors. In this paper, we investigate joint deployment, user association, and power allocation for data collection in the UAV-assisted wireless sensor network to accommodate the heterogeneous requirements of sensors, where a novel satisfaction function is designed for three types of sensors, including sensors with delay requirements, sensors with energy consumption requirements, and sensors with both delay and energy consumption requirements. Leveraging the satisfaction function, we formulate the optimization problem aimed at jointly optimizing the positions of UAVs, the association between sensors and UAVs, and the power allocation of sensors to maximize overall satisfaction of sensors. In order to effectively address the considered problem, we decompose it into two subproblems, i.e., joint UAV deployment and user association subproblem, and transmission power allocation subproblem. An enhanced human evolutionary algorithm is developed to tackle the joint UAV deployment and user association subproblem, and the Lagrange dual method and gradient descent method are employed to solve the transmission power allocation subproblem. The suboptimal solution is achieved by iteratively addressing the two subproblems until convergence of the proposed enhanced Lagrange and gradient descent-based human evolutionary optimization algorithm is attained. Extensive simulations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm in enhancing overall satisfaction of sensors, underscoring its significant advantages in managing heterogeneous network environments.

title: CALSI: Context-Aware Layer Skipping Inference for On-Device LLM Serving
authors: Puhan Luo, Mu Yuan, Ningkang Zhang, Ruiguang Zhong, Junyang Zhang, Chengchen Hu, Xiang-Yang Li
abstract: The large language model (LLM) based on the Transformer architecture and its derived various applications have greatly changed people's lives. Considering some concerns such as privacy and network conditions, deploying LLM on smart devices has gradually become a research focus. In order to reduce the huge computation and storage overhead of LLMs, many works have studied model compression technology to reduce the model computation and parameter amount, thereby reducing the inference latency. This paper analyzes the characteristics of the on-device LLM service, including small batch size and latency focus, etc. We find the inefficiency of existing model compression technologies and new optimization opportunities, i.e., allocating different layers for different input tokens based on the task QoS requirements. Then we propose CALSI, a context-aware layer skipping LLM inference system for on-device serving. In the offline profiling phase, we analyze the importance of different layers of the model to different tokens and train a lightweight gated predictor. Then, we map the latency QoS requirements of different tasks with the predictor threshold. During the online inference phase, we adaptively allocate the layers that need to be computed to the specific token and design a KV cache delayed computation management mechanism to solve the KV cache missing problem caused by layer skipping. Experiments on real devices show that CALSI can achieve up to 24.1% latency reduction. CALSI has good generalization ability on various models and datasets and is compatible with other existing complementary optimization techniques.

title: Multi-AUV Underwater Cooperative Target Encirclement via Quantum-Assisted Software-Defined MARL
authors: Jialu Tian, Chuan Lin, Guangjie Han, Shengchao Zhu, Zhenyu Wang, Yunpeng Ma
abstract: In recent years, autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) swarms have demonstrated broad application prospects in marine exploration. This paper proposes a Quantum-Assisted Software-Defined Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning architecture (QA-SDMARL) to perform efficient decision for multi-AUV systems-driven cooperative underwater target encirclement. By integrating quantum computing and software-defined networking (SDN) with classical multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) techniques, the AUV swarm is divided into three functional layers: global training layer, local training layer, and application operation layer, to optimize task allocation and policy coordination. Specifically, the variational quantum circuits are employed to generate initial policy weights for QA-SDMARL, while the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) is adopted to enhance parameter diversity for efficient exploration. For optimizing the experience replay buffer in QA-SDMARL, this paper proposes a quantum-enhanced experience prioritization mechanism by combining quantum mutual information (QMI) and quantum entanglement strength (QES), along with an enhanced quantum annealing-based experience replay buffer update protocol. Further, this paper designs a multi-dimensional reward function for complex underwater encirclement tasks, to significantly improve adaptability and learning efficiency of QA-SDMARL in complex marine environments. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed QA-SDMARL achieves substantial breakthroughs in convergence speed, containment accuracy, and system stability, comparing to the mainstream research products.

title: IEEE Xplore Digital Library
authors: N/A

title: −188.4-dBc/Hz FoM Complementary VCO Employing Current Harmonic Cancellation Achieving 5-dB Phase Noise Reduction at 1/ f 3 Region
authors: Aulya Sholehah Wataawa Sau, Hapsah Aulia Azzahra, Muhammad Fakhri Mauludin, Youngwoo Ji, Xi Zhu, Jae-Won Nam, Jusung Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: A harmonic cancellation technique based on a phase manipulation technique is presented for Ku-band voltage-controlled oscillators (VCO), achieving significant phase noise reduction, especially at the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1/f^{3}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> region. The proposed VCO incorporates an auxiliary PMOS transistor with dynamically controlled biasing to generate additional high-order components to compensate for the intrinsic phase difference between the high-order harmonic components of the main PMOS and NMOS cross-coupled transistors, establishing the required <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$180^{o}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> phase difference (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta \theta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>) between them. The alignment, facilitated by the proposed technique, significantly suppresses the undesired high-order harmonic components entering the tank’s capacitive path and disturbing its natural frequency. The proposed VCO is fabricated in 65nm CMOS technology, achieving a phase noise of −33.78 dBc/Hz at 1 kHz and -112 dBc/Hz at 1 MHz. The phase noise improvement reaches 5.56 dB at the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1/f^{3}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> region compared to a conventional complementary VCO. With a low power consumption of 4.5 mW, the proposed VCO achieves an excellent figure-of-merit (FoM) of −188.4 dBc/Hz with a tuning range of 1.1 GHz (8.1%) operating from 13 GHz to 14.1 GHz.

title: A New SCAN Method for Unsupervised Learning: Developing an Auto-Labeling Framework in Semiconductor Manufacturing_supp1-3692570.pdf
authors: Shu-Kai S. Fan
abstract: This paper proposes a novel self-supervised clustering framework for automatic defect annotation in semiconductor manufacturing, aiming to reduce the heavy reliance on expert-labeled data. Building upon the Semantic Clustering by Adopting Nearest-neighbors (SCAN) model, we introduce a new SCAN methodology that integrates three key innovations: a redesigned pretext task, an enhanced SCAN model with neighbor loss, and a self-labeling mechanism. The new pretext task replaces the traditional ResNet18 backbone with MobileNetV2 and incorporates focal loss to address class imbalance, enabling more effective feature extraction from unlabeled wafer maps. The SCAN model is further refined through a neighbor loss function that leverages high-confidence samples to iteratively correct pseudo-labels, improving clustering accuracy. Additionally, we apply strong augmentation techniques and a confidence-based thresholding strategy to enhance model robustness. Experimental evaluations on the WM-811K and MixedWM38 datasets demonstrate that the proposed unsupervised clustering framework achieves a notable accuracy improvement (from 36.5% to 57.7% and 36.09% to 57.69%, respectively) compared to the original SCAN, with further gains up to 66.20% and 64.80% using high-confidence sample filtering. The results confirm that our approach significantly enhances classification performance in unsupervised settings and offers a practical solution for scalable, automated defect labeling in intelligent manufacturing environments. Note to Practitioners—This study introduces a method for automated data grouping, or pseudo-labeling, which reduces reliance on manual labeling in unsupervised learning. Using the new SCAN model, the model learns meaningful features, enabling more accurate clustering. High-confidence samples help correct pseudo-labels of nearby data points, enhancing clustering quality without expert labels. This approach shows promise in improving classification accuracy in self-supervised learning, particularly for semiconductor manufacturing, by minimizing manual annotation efforts and refining model performance in unsupervised scenarios.

title: MINE 2025–2026: Strong Global Engagement in Phase 1 and Exciting Opportunities Ahead [Education News]
authors: Nishad Mendis, David Celeita Rodriguez
abstract: Provides society information that may include news, reviews or technical notes that should be of interest to practitioners and researchers.

title: The First Desktop Computer: The History of the Olivetti Programma 101 [History]
authors: Aldo Boglietti
abstract: The Olivetti Programma 101 designed in the mid-60s and conceived by Pier Giorgio Perotto engineer of Olivetti Company in Ivrea Italy, was the first desktop personal computer in the world, ahead of its time compared to the computer revolution of the 70s. The Olivetti Programma 101 was a programmable, elegant and compact computer used even by NASA for the Apollo 11 mission. His history, summarized in this contribution, has represented a missed opportunity for Italian industry, which failed to fully exploit its technological primacy in the transition from office automation based on electromechanical systems to mass computing.

title: Direct Cardiac T1 Mapping with Subspace Modeling and Free-breathing Data Acquisition_supp1-3696845.pdf
authors: Chao Ma
abstract: Objective: Cardiac T1 mapping is an emerging magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) tool in the quantitative assessment of cardiomyopathies. Conventional techniques necessitate breath-holding during each scan, which can be challenging for certain patients. This work presents a novel T1 estimation framework based on subspace modeling and direct T1 estimation for cardiac T1 mapping with free-breathing data acquisition. Methods: Sparse (k,t)-space data is collected along a random radial trajectory using a free-breathing, ECG-gated, inversion-recovery fast low-angle shot (FLASH) sequence. The T1 relaxation model is incorporated into a direct reconstruction framework to perform end-to-end T1 mapping directly from the undersampled (k,t)-space data. The resultant image reconstruction problem is solved using the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) algorithm, which decomposes the optimization problem into subproblems, such as image reconstruction with a low-rank constraint, parametric fitting with a sparsity constraint, and total variation (TV) based image denoising problems. Results: The performance of the proposed method is evaluated by comparisons with the two-step, indirect approaches through numerical simulations and in-vivo experiments. Results demonstrate the benefits of the proposed direct approach in terms of T1 estimation bias and variance Conclusions: Direct cardiac T1 mapping with subspace modeling enables free-breathing cardiac T1 mapping with improved accuracy. Significance: This study demonstrates the potential utility of incorporating MR physics knowledge and subspace modeling into a direct estimation framework for improved cardiac T1 mapping with free-breathing acquisition.

title: Anchor-Driven Compact Gaussian Splatting for Dynamic Scene Reconstruction_supp2-3695021.mp4
authors: Yiling Xu
abstract: Existing 4D Gaussian Splatting methods typically rely on per-Gaussian deformation from a canonical space to target frames, which overlooks the strong redundancy among spatially and temporally adjacent Gaussian primitives and leads to suboptimal efficiency. To address this limitation, we propose ADC-GS++, an anchor-driven compact Gaussian splatting frame work for efficient and high-quality dynamic scene reconstruction. Specifically, ADC-GS++ organizes Gaussian primitives into an anchor-based canonical representation, enabling attribute sharing across local regions. To efficiently model dynamic scenes, we introduce a static–dynamic decomposition mechanism and further employ a coarse-to-fine deformation strategy driven by dynamic anchors at multiple granularities. In addition, a unified rate-distortion optimization is adopted to achieve a balanced trade-off between storage efficiency and reconstruction fidelity. Furthermore, a temporal significance-based anchor refinement strategy is employed to dynamically grow and prune anchors, allowing robust adaptation to complex and large-scale motions. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world dynamic scene datasets demonstrate that ADC-GS++ significantly improves rendering speed over deformation-based approaches by 300% 700%, while maintaining competitive rendering quality. More over, ADC-GS++ achieves a more favorable rate-distortion trade off, resulting in substantially reduced storage consumption across different bitrate settings.

title: NOVA: A Self-Supervised Graph Framework for Real-Time Anomaly Detection in Internet of Vehicles_supp1-3696324.docx
authors: Arash Heidari
abstract: The Internet of Vehicles (IoV) enables cooperative driving and real-time Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication but remains vulnerable to behavioral and structural anomalies due to its dynamic, decentralized nature. Existing deep learning methods either overlook topological inconsistencies or ignore communication feature fidelity, while random-walk sampling introduces contextual noise. In this paper, we propose Network Observation for Vehicular Anomalies (NOVA), a self-supervised graph-based framework that detects both behavioral and structural anomalies in IoV networks without labeled data. NOVA models vehicular communications as attributed graphs and employs intimacy-guided subgraph sampling to extract meaningful neighborhoods. A Graph Convolutional Network (GCN)–based generative module reconstructs node attributes to reveal behavioral deviations, while a contrastive module validates structural coherence through embedding comparisons of real and perturbed contexts. Their hybrid anomaly score enables accurate, scalable, and real-time detection of compromised nodes. Performance results show that NOVA achieves state-of-the-art performance (98.7% accuracy, 98.1% F1), real-time throughput (~4.7k events/s at 5k msg/s), and strong robustness (AUROC 0.99, AUPRC 0.98, FAR 0.05) with near-linear scalability (≤40 ms latency for 50k vehicles). By integrating generative and contrastive self-supervised learning with context-aware sampling, NOVA significantly enhances IoV security, reliability, and adaptability.

title: Integrative Analysis of Shared Molecular Signatures in Temporal Lobe Epilepsy and Alzheimer's Disease with Ligands Discovery Targeting SOD1
authors: Md. Mehedi Hasan Khan Siam, Ram Prosad Sarker, Md. Mehedi Hassan, Abanti Bhattacharjya, Bikash Kumar Paul, Touhid Bhuiyan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Temporal Lobe Epilepsy (TLE) and Alzheimer's Disease (AD) are prevalent neurological disorders with shared molecular mechanisms that remain poorly understood. This study integrates gene expression datasets from GEO (GSE256068, GSE48350) and NCBI gene information to identify candidate genes implicated in both TLE and AD. Sixteen key genes were identified and analyzed for differential expression, proteinprotein interactions, and pathway enrichment to uncover potential biomarkers and therapeutic targets. One of these was SOD1, which showed contrary direction of expression change in the two brain disorders (up-regulation in TLE and down regulation in AD) and is a crucial mediator of oxidative stress activity and thus for ligand-based analysis toward its disturbed expression pattern associated to TLE. Ligand mining and molecular docking were conducted using a Python-based Jupyter Notebook workflow, with data from RCSB Protein Data Bank. Candidate ligands were screened for drug-likeness and ADMET properties before being further ranked by their potential to affect the activity of SOD1. While the focus here has been on targeting SOD1 overexpression in TLE, the ligands may also have indirect benefits for AD by replenishing redox Bbalance and buffering oxidative stress, a shared pathological feature of these maladies. This integrative bioinformatics and cheminformatics approach provides insights into shared molecular mechanisms underlying TLE and AD and offers a framework for identifying candidate biomarkers and therapeutic strategies.

title: Tunable Optical and Electronic Properties of Ca-Doped $\varepsilon$ - $\text{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$: A First-Principles Study Toward Achieving $p$-Type Transparent Oxide Semiconductors
authors: Md. Billal Hossain, David Jevers Gourob Roy, Md. Nibir Islam, Syeda Rukaiya Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Epsilon gallium oxide (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon-\text{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex>) has drawn a lot of interest as a semiconductor material with a broad band-gap that may support future high-power electronic devices and ultraviolet photodetectors. The difficulty of achieving p-type conduction in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon-\text{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex>, however, continues to be a technological obstacle. Here, we present a thorough computational analysis of the structural, electronic, and optical characteristics of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon-\text{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex> and Ca-doped <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon-\text{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex> from first principles. Several studies using density functional theory demonstrates that Ca atoms can introduce acceptor states close to the valence band edge, raising the bandgap and indicating the possibility of p-type conduction. The hybridized <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Ca} 3 p$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{O} 2 p$</tex> orbitals make up the majority of the acceptor levels. The examination of the optical properties revealed that the system's optical activity changed as a result of doping, as evidenced by the increased dielectric function, absorption, reflectivity, and refractive index in the UV-visible region. The enhanced absorption and its adjustable band structure offer significant potential for the use of Ca-doped <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon$</tex> - <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex> as a candidate for transparent electronic devices and in optoelectronic applications that call for p-type oxide semiconductors, despite the decreased transparency.

title: A Low-Cost, Ultra Low-Power, and Scalable LoRa-Based Weather Balloon for Atmospheric Monitoring: A Bangladesh Perspective
authors: Md. Tanvir Hassan, Md. Samiul Islam Mobin, Sourav Karmokar, Tama Fouzder
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: To address the scarcity of accessible upper atmosphere data in regions such as Bangladesh, this research work presents the design and validation of a low-cost, easily scalable weather balloon. Our system uses a smart power-saving approach: an ESP32 microcontroller goes into deep sleep, waking up every ten minutes just long enough to grab data from its environmental and Global Positioning System (GPS) sensors and send it on its way. This approach allows a small 500 mAh battery to last an incredible 248 hours, which is much longer than any single flight. To make sure we do not lose any data, the readings are sent wirelessly via a LoRa module to a ground station and, at the same time, are saved to a microSD card right on the balloon. We tested the system in a 10-day ground test, and it performed perfectly, showing that our design is a reliable and affordable alternative to expensive commercial radiosondes. We believe that this new platform can really help expand opportunities for atmospheric research and education in places that have not had the opportunity before.

title: Machine Learning-Based Classification of Phishing Websites: Leveraging Website Behavior and Structure Features
authors: Ramij Wasith Rahat, Ashim Dey, Annesha Das
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Phishing is a prevalent cybercrime where attackers trick users into installing malicious programs or disclosing private information by using deceptive digital communication. Phishing attacks have become an alarming problem in recent days. An efficient and automated method of identifying phishing websites is necessary to mitigate the threat caused by scammers and other cyber fraudsters in the virtual world. Effective detection of phishing websites is still challenging with current technologies. To resolve the drawbacks of existing phishing attack detection techniques, we propose a machine learning-based method to identify websites as either phishing or legitimate. This method increases the accuracy of phishing website identification by using important features that are extracted from the dataset. For this research, a publicly accessible webpage phishing detection dataset is used that contains multiple features characterizing website behavior and structure. The dataset includes both phishing and legitimate website samples, providing sufficient diversity for comprehensive model training as well as validation. Different classification models, including XGBoost, Gradient Boosting, Random Forest, Decision Tree, etc., are assessed using 5-Fold Cross Validation, and achieved the highest accuracy score of 96.91% with the XGBoost Classifier model. Ensemble-based models regularly outperformed Non-Ensemble models. This approach improves user protection against cyber threats by minimizing false negatives, maximizing phishing detection accuracy and precision, and offering scalability for real-time implementation in websites or security systems. We anticipate that this work will aid robust phishing-website detection and enhance user security.

title: Self-Reconfiguration of Modular Robotic Systems and Programmable Matter Using Reinforcement Learning
authors: Khodor Ghayad, Jad Bassil, Abdallah Makhoul, Roger Achkar, Jad Nassar
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Modular self-reconfigurable robots adapt their morphology to diverse tasks. Finding the sequences of movements for each module to reach a target configuration is computationally difficult due to exponential state-action spaces, especially in large configurations. While existing algorithmic methods are scalable, they often require manual, task-specific design; meanwhile, learning-based approaches have struggled to scale. This paper applies Q-learning to a system of 2D hexagonal lattice-based modular robots. By utilizing a position-independent state representation and connectivity-aware learning, we demonstrate that the system can successfully self-reconfigure and generalize to untrained initial configurations. Evaluation results from simulation show that with 100 training episodes, the learned policy achieves 100% success on the trained configuration and successfully generalizes to three distinct untrained initial configurations, including a spatially inverted scenario.

title: Learning-Assisted Proactive Reattachment in Tree-Structured VLC-VANETs
authors: Rim Touhami, Maha Sliti
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Vehicular visible light communication (VLC) can complement V2X, but links are sensitive to LoS/FoV alignment, mobility, and ambient-light noise. In treestructured VLC-VANETs, these effects lead to parentlink degradation and reactive reattachments that increase tail delay and packet loss. We propose a learning-assisted proactive reattachment trigger in which each vehicle runs a lightweight local predictor to anticipate imminent QoS violations from observable features (parent-link SNR, short-term SNR trend, relative motion, and basic topology/QoS indicators). When the predicted risk exceeds a threshold, the vehicle proactively switches to a feasible neighbor using the same QoS/SNR parent-selection score as the baseline, while dwell-time, hysteresis, and cooldown safeguards bound ping-pong and signaling overhead. Simulations across vehicle densities and illumination levels show higher packet delivery and lower high-percentile delay than reactive baselines, with reattachment churn kept bounded.

title: Would Learning Help? Adaptive CRC-QC-LDPC Selection for Integrity in 5G-NR V2X
authors: Sarah Al-Shareeda, Gulcihan Ozdemir, Arouj Fatima, Mădălin-Dorin Pop, Bander A. Jabr, Yasser Bin Salamah, Jacques Demerjian
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communications impose stringent physical-layer integrity requirements, particularly under short-packet transmission and mobility-induced channel variation. This paper studies whether standard-compliant online selection of Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC) polynomials and Quasi-Cyclic Low-Density Parity-Check (QC-LDPC) coding rates can reduce silent (undetected) errors in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$mathbf{5 G}$</tex> New Radio (5GNR) V2X links. The joint configuration problem is formulated as a lightweight Contextual Bandit (CB) with a small, discrete action space, and a discounted LinUCB policy is evaluated against greedy online adaptation and a conservative fixed baseline. A 5G-NR-compliant physical-layer simulation is developed using Sionna, modeling mobility through time-correlated Rayleigh fading, where vehicle speed governs channel correlation, and non-stationary interference via a two-state Markov process. The learning agent operates on coarse receiver feedback, including a noisy Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) estimate and indicators of burst interference and deep fades, and targets minimization of the Undetected Error Probability (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$P_{U E}$</tex>) while accounting for the Detected Error Probability (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$P_{D E}$</tex>). Overall, our objective is to delineate the mobility regimes in which learning-assisted CRC-QC-LDPC configuration improves physical-layer integrity in 5G-NR V2X systems. Our results indicate that learningassisted adaptation is most effective at low to moderate mobility, reducing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$P_{U E}$</tex> by up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0 - 7 0 \%}$</tex> relative to greedy selection in the low-SNR regime (-5 to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5 \mathbf{~ d B}$</tex>) and approaching the best fixed configuration at higher <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$E_{b} / N_{0}$</tex>. At high mobility (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\geq 180 \mathbf{~ k m} / \mathbf{h}$</tex>), fast channel decorrelation weakens temporal predictability, limiting the effectiveness of online learning and reducing performance differences across policies.

title: Frequency Diversity 3D Printed DRA for Wireless Applications
authors: Jana M. AlNatour, Layan T. AbuRayyan, Raneem F. Abdelrahim, Yanal S. Faouri
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: This paper presents and investigates a 3D-printed multiband frequency-reconfigurable dielectric resonator antenna (DRA) for wireless applications. The proposed antenna design comprises four non-identical boxes separated by a plusshaped patch. The main reason for the variation between the boxes is the different PLA infill percentages of each box (20%, 60%, 70%, and 100%,respectively); hence, distinct electric permittivity and loss tangent values were achieved for each box. The capability of frequency tuning is obtained by changing the state of the four switches implemented using PIN diodes installed between the center of each box and the plus patch, resulting in different resonant frequencies in each state.

title: Proposal and Experimental Evaluation of a Wire-Driven Flap-Type Non-Return Valve for Sampling in Small Lunar and Planetary Rovers
authors: Ryo Oyori, Shotaro Date, Kojiro Iizuka
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: In subsurface sampling using coring during lunar and planetary exploration, a non-return valve is essential for reliably retaining collected samples. Conventional mechanisms such as core catchers and flexible tubes have encountered issues including sand leakage and structural complexity. To minimize penetration resistance and achieve sample collection with a simple mechanism, this study proposes a wire-driven flap-type nonreturn valve that operates using only vertical motion. Because the actuation wire has a very small cross-sectional area, its influence on penetration resistance is expected to be minimal. The sampling performance of the proposed mechanism was experimentally evaluated in terms of sampling success rate, collected mass, and sampling efficiency. The sampler successfully retained regolith in all 25 trials, demonstrating stable and reliable operation in a granular environment. These results confirm the feasibility of using a wire-driven flap mechanism as a simple and lightweight non-return valve for coring systems. Future work will focus on integrating the mechanism with a vibratory penetration system to realize a lightweight, high-retention subsurface sampling device.

title: Modeling and Verification of Reconfigurable Distributed Control Systems
authors: Friederike Bruns, Bianca Wiesmayr, Felix Schmid, Lisa Wünderlich, Andreas Rauh
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Distributed control systems increasingly rely on heterogeneous, mixed-criticality communication that must meet diverse real-time requirements, adapt to dynamic modes, and ensure safety and correctness. Traditional design workflows often treat communication and control logic separately, limiting early verification and complicating system integration. The proposed design flow supports offline verification of reconfigurable communication architectures while ensuring predictable execution under strict timing constraints. Communication is modeled explicitly through logical messages and physical channels in a contract-based design flow, allowing integration of real-time or quality-of-service properties into the application model. This enables the specification of assumption-guarantee contracts, supports early syntactic and semantic checks, and incorporates dynamic validation of time traces. A battery production case study demonstrates communication heterogeneity and dynamic operating modes in a tool-supported modeling environment.

title: RGS-DR: Deferred Reflections and Residual Shading in 2D Gaussian Splatting
authors: Georgios Kouros, Minye Wu, Tinne Tuytelaars
abstract: In this work, we address specular appearance in inverse rendering using 2D Gaussian splatting with deferred shading and argue for a refinement stage to improve specular detail, thereby bridging the gap with reconstruction-only methods. Our pipeline estimates editable material properties and environment illumination while employing a directional residual pass that captures leftover view-dependent effects for further refining novel view synthesis. In contrast to per-Gaussian shading with shortest-axis normals and normal residuals, which tends to result in more noisy geometry and specular appearance, a pixel-deferred surfel formulation with specular residuals yields sharper highlights, cleaner materials, and improved editability. We evaluate our approach on rendering and reconstruction quality on three popular datasets featuring glossy objects, and also demonstrate high-quality relighting and material editing. The source code is available at https://github.com/gkouros/RGS-DR.

title: Shape Classification of Consecutive Obstacles for Water-Driven Soft Robot Using Reservoir Computing
authors: Junya Mieno, Hidenobu Sumioka, Takashi Takuma
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Soft robots are highly adaptable but difficult to sense because of their deformable structures. This study addresses shape recognition of a consecutively shape-changed pathway using water pressure of a front chamber from a developed waterdriven soft robot. To estimate the path shape through which the robot moved, we used reservoir computing (RC). We then propose two processes for accurately estimating path shapes. The first step is to reset RC to its internal state. Cycle-wise classification with internal state reset, “batch classification” assumes that the path shape does not suddenly change abruptly for one cycle. The second step involves dividing each cycle into smaller segments, performing sequential classification, and determining the final estimate through majority voting in each cycle. Experiments showed that the robot moved through a path of consecutively changing between two shapes, and the proposed system achieved 93% accuracy in the first half, 90% in the second half, and 91% overall with 200 reservoir units, demonstrating the effectiveness of RC for shape estimation for a consecutively shape-changed pathway.

title: LegacyAvatars: Volumetric Face Avatars for Traditional Graphics Pipelines
authors: Safa C. Medin, Gengyan Li, Ziqian Bai, Ruofei Du, Leonhard Helminger, Yinda Zhang, Stephan J. Garbin, Philip L. Davidson, Gregory W. Wornell, Thabo Beeler, Abhimitra Meka
abstract: We introduce a novel representation for efficient classical rendering of photorealistic 3D face avatars. Leveraging recent advances in radiance fields anchored to parametric face models, our approach achieves controllable volumetric rendering of complex facial features, including hair, skin, and eyes. At enrollment time, we learn a set of radiance manifolds in 3D space to extract an explicit layered mesh, along with appearance and warp textures. During deployment, this allows us to control and animate the face through simple linear blending and alpha compositing of textures over a static mesh. This explicit representation also enables the generated avatar to be efficiently streamed online and then rendered using classical mesh and shader-based rendering on legacy graphics platforms, eliminating the need for any custom engineering or integration. https://syntec-research.github.io/LegacyAvatars/

title: EgoMDM: Diffusion-Based Human Motion Synthesis from Sparse Egocentric Sensors
authors: Soyong Shin, Anuj Pahuja, Alexander Richard, Kris Kitani, Jason Saragih, Yuhua Chen, Weipeng Xu, Eni Halilaj, Timur Bagautdinov
abstract: Accurate three-dimensional (3D) human motion tracking is essential for immersive augmented reality <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(A R)$</tex> and virtual reality (VR) applications, allowing users to engage with virtual environments through realistic full-body avatars. Achieving this level of detail, however, is challenging when the driving signals are sparse, typically coming only from upper-body sensors, such as head-mounted devices and hand controllers. To address this challenge, we propose EgoMDM (Egocentric Motion Diffusion Model), an end-to-end diffusion-based framework designed to reconstruct full-body motion from sparse tracking signals. EgoMDM models human motion in a conditional autoregressive manner using a unidirectional recurrent neural network, making it well-suited for real-time applications. By embedding local-to-global translation, forward and inverse kinematics, and foot-contact detection within the diffusion framework, EgoMDM achieves seamless, end-to-end motion synthesis, effectively reducing artifacts like foot sliding and ground penetration. Additionally, EgoMDM is conditioned on the user's body scale, allowing it to generalize across a diverse population and produce consistent avatar shapes over time. In our extensive experiments on the AMASS motion capture dataset, EgoMDM achieves state-of-the-art performance in both motion tracking accuracy and synthesis quality, demonstrating its robustness and adaptability across various human motion scenarios. Furthermore, EgoMDM significantly outperforms the existing models when tested on real signal inputs, highlighting its robustness and applicability to the real-world data. See the project page at: https://yohanshin.github.io/egomdm.github.io/

title: Reflect3r: Single-View 3D Stereo Reconstruction Aided by Mirror Reflections
authors: Jing Wu, Zirui Wang, Iro Laina, Victor Adrian Prisacariu
abstract: Mirror reflections are common in everyday environments and can provide stereo information within a single capture, as the real and reflected virtual views are visible simultaneously. We exploit this property by treating the reflection as an auxiliary view and designing a transformation that constructs a physically valid virtual camera, allowing direct pixel-domain generation of the virtual view while adhering to the real-world imaging process. This enables a multi-view stereo setup from a single image, simplifying the imaging process, making it compatible with powerful feedforward reconstruction models for generalizable and robust 3D reconstruction. To further exploit the geometric symmetry introduced by mirrors, we propose a symmetric-aware loss to refine pose estimation. Our framework also naturally extends to dynamic scenes, where each frame contains a mirror reflection, enabling efficient per-frame geometry recovery. For quantitative evaluation, we provide a fully customizable synthetic dataset of 16 Blender scenes, each with ground-truth point clouds and camera poses. Extensive experiments on real-world data and synthetic data are conducted to illustrate the effectiveness of our method. Code and Blender scenes are available in: https://github.com/jingwu2121/reflect3r.git.
