title: ST-SNN-DSFE: A Spatio-Temporal Hybrid Network for EEG-based Emotion Recognition
authors: Shuwei Zhang, Jiqing Zhang, Xinning Wang, Peigen Yang, Bo Dong, Xiaopeng Wei, Xin Yang
abstract: Emotion recognition based on electroencephalogram (EEG) signals has significant potential for applications in mental health and human-computer interaction. However, existing methods often fail to fully exploit the intricate temporal dynamics and spatial dependencies inherent in EEG data. To address these challenges, we propose ST-SNN-DSFE, a novel framework that integrates temporal and spatial feature extraction to improve recognition performance. From the temporal perspective, Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) are employed to capture fine-grained temporal dynamics, leveraging their biological plausibility and efficiency in processing temporal spikes. Additionally, the inherent characteristics of SNNs provide a slight denoising effect, which is advantageous for handling the noisy nature of EEG signals and enhancing the robustness of temporal feature extraction. From the spatial perspective, multi-scale convolutions are utilized to extract global physical information and positional encodings from EEG signals comprehensively, while prior knowledge is incorporated to define two distinct subgraph partitioning strategies based on brain regions and hemispherical divisions to extract local functional information and functional virtual positional encodings, enabling robust modeling of functional connectivity. Finally, Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) are employed to fuse the temporal and spatial features by integrating feature embeddings and positional relationships, improving emotion recognition performance. Extensive experiments on two benchmark datasets (SEED and SEED-IV) demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, achieving state-of-the-art performance in both subject-dependent and subject-independent scenarios.

title: Quantum Depth-First Search-Based Reliability Assessment of Composite Generation and Transmission Systems
authors: Hejun Yang, Yue Liu, Yiwen Jiang, Dabo Zhang
abstract: The acceleration of classical composite generation and transmission system reliability assessment is mainly achieved through algorithmic innovations at the state sampling stage. Typical approaches include combining machine learning techniques or scenario reduction methods with Monte Carlo simulation. The former improves computational efficiency at the expense of partial accuracy, while the latter relies on a large number of samples and thus reduces the effective number of reliability samples. In contrast, quantum computing possesses a theoretical speed advantage over classical computing owing to its unique superposition and entanglement properties, and it has been demonstrated to achieve quantum supremacy. Motivated by this potential, this paper proposes a composite generation and transmission system reliability assessment method based on quantum-inspired algorithms. First, a quantum depth-first search (DFS) algorithm considering path connectivity is developed to construct a quantum model of the initial node and transmission paths. This model remains invariant with respect to changes in system fault states and enables the direct identification of subsystems connected to the initial node. Second, a quantum-inspired reliability assessment framework for composite generation and transmission systems is proposed, in which a quantum depth-first search model and a quantum power flow calculation model are established. By alternately integrating quantum-inspired computation with classical computation, the reliability indices of the composite generation and transmission system can be efficiently evaluated. Finally, two designed test systems and the RBTS system are analyzed. The results demonstrate the effectiveness and correctness of the proposed quantum depth-first search algorithm and the quantum-inspired composite generation and transmission system reliability assessment model.

title: Early Class Exclusion in Hyperdimensional Computing
authors: Rémy Duboucheix, Mohsen Asghari, Sébastien Le Beux, Otmane Ait Mohamed, Ron Mankarious
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Hyperdimensional computing (HDC) is a lightweight machine-learning paradigm in which data are encoded as high-dimensional hypervectors, enabling error-resilient and hardware-friendly computation. This makes HDC well suited for low-resource embedded systems that must balance performance and energy. However, existing HDC accelerators require full similarity checks between query and class hypervectors, incurring significant computational and energy cost. We propose an HDC algorithm that progressively excludes low-similarity classes by iterating over segmented hypervectors, reducing the number of required comparisons without storing full class representations. Our RTL accelerator reduces execution time by $3.7 \times$ and energy consumption by $28.7 \%$, with only a $3.2 \%$ accuracy loss.

title: Detecting Hardware Trojans in Quantum Circuits Based on CodeBERT model
authors: Min-Chao Huang, Chin-Wei Tien, Sy-Yen Kuo
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: With the rapid advancement of quantum computing, the security of quantum circuits has become a critical concern, particularly against hardware Trojans that can be stealthily inserted during design or compilation. Even the insertion of a single quantum gate, such as Pauli-X, Hadamard, CNOT, or SWAP, may significantly alter circuit behavior. In this work, we propose an automated detection framework based on the pretrained Code-BERT model, originally developed for programming languages, to identify Trojan-induced anomalies in circuits represented in Quantum Assembly Language (QASM). By treating gate sequences as structured code, our approach leverages Transformer-based contextual learning without requiring manual feature engineering. Experiments on over 2,500 samples constructed from benchmark circuits demonstrate that our model achieves $96.1 \%$ accuracy and $97.0 \%$ F1 score in binary classification, with recall exceeding $97.5 \%$ for all Trojan types. In multiclass classification, the model reaches $91.1 \%$ accuracy, successfully distinguishing different Trojan gate insertions. Compared with a convolutional neural network (CNN) baseline, CodeBERT consistently achieves superior recall and robustness. These results highlight the effectiveness and scalability of CodeBERT for securing quantum circuits against hardware Trojan threats.

title: Security-Aware Printed-Circuit-Board Routing Based on Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Katherine Shu -Min Li, Ching-Han Lai, Fang-Chi Wu, Sying-Jyan Wang
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Modern electronic devices demand higher performance and more secure printed circuit boards (PCBs). In particular, requirements such as high-frequency operation, highspeed signal integrity, and enhanced security have become increasingly critical in modern PCB design. In this paper, we propose an integrated framework that incorporates test point insertion directly into the PCB routing process, addressing both testability and security concerns during the design stage. We prioritize nets by assigning routing sequences before trace generation. The $\mathbf{A}^{*}$ search algorithm is then employed for multilayer routing, utilizing a customized heuristic function to minimize total trace length based on the number of board layers. To determine optimal locations, we adopt a reinforcement learning (RL) approach, where an agent learns to select appropriate insertion actions guided by a carefully designed reward function. Experimental results demonstrate that this approach achieves 100% routing success and full test point coverage across all evaluated designs. The resulting design improves accessibility for electrical testing and establishes a foundation for subsequent security assessments.

title: Unsupervised Learning Based Hardware Trojan Detection Method for RTL Designs
authors: Sying-Jyan Wang, Hou-Cheng Chen, Katherine Shu-Min Li
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Extensive research has been carried on Hardware Trojan (HT) detection. Most detection methods at the register transfer level (RTL) are based on supervised learning models. However, supervised methods require the training data to contain Trojan circuits and often involve additional efforts to address class imbalance, which limits their applicability in real-world scenarios. In this paper, we propose a HT detection method based on an unsupervised learning model that is trained using only normal signals. Experimental results show that the proposed model achieves an average accuracy of 97.9, demonstrating the feasibility of applying unsupervised learning to RTL hardware Trojan detection and offering a new perspective for future research in this field.

title: Bit-Flexible Systolic Architecture: Optimizing Processing Elements with Application-Specific Floating-Point Truncation
authors: Dantu Nandini Devi, Madhav Rao
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Leveraging hardware-level approximate computing in Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) enables faster computation, improved power efficiency, and a reduced design footprint. This paper proposes a unified framework for designing approximate floating-point multipliers optimized for error-resilient applications. By adopting a right-shift-based multiplication algorithm for combinational logic and integrating multiple compressor configurations with unique error profiles, the design space is significantly expanded across FP32, TF32, and BF16 formats. A multi-objective optimization using NSGA-II is employed to explore this space efficiently, targeting truncation depth, approximation range, and compressor arrangement. Evaluations across image processing, CNNs, and JPEG compression demonstrate around $15 \%$ improvement in the critical path delay and over $50 \%$ gains in power consumption and silicon footprint with minimal loss in output quality. In some CNN scenarios, approximate multipliers even improve inference accuracy, revealing their potential not only for efficiency but also for enhanced generalisation. Furthermore, the framework and hardware design files are made available for further usage to the designers’ and researchers community.

title: Finite Element Framework for Electrostatics and Optical Simulations in IGZO TFTs—Part 2: Application
authors: Pietro Rinaudo, Adrian Chasin, Ben Kaczer, Ying Zhao, Harold Dekkers, Subhali Subhechha, Attilio Belmonte, Valeri Afanas'ev, Ingrid De Wolf, Jacopo Franco
abstract: We apply our finite element-based framework, introduced in Part 1, to understand the effects of channel thickness and doping on the degradation of indium–gallium–zinc oxide (IGZO)-based thin-film transistors (TFTs) with different architectures. By taking into account only experimentally extracted band alignments and amorphous semiconductor-specific physics, it is possible to explain apparent discrepancies in previously reported positive bias temperature instabilities (PBTI) trends. Our analysis shows that common PBTI benchmarks used in standard inversion-mode bulk Si devices cannot be employed in thin-film accumulation-mode IGZO devices. Finally, based on these insights, we propose device optimization strategies to reduce the electron trapping component of PBTI in amorphous IGZO TFTs.

title: AdaptCloud: Information-driven adaptive multimodal fusion for cloud-robust land cover classification
authors: Huiting Li, Jingru Zhu, Tianyi Xu, Hao Wu, Jun Zhang, Jie Chen, Guoqing Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Land cover classification (LCC) is essential for resource management and socioeconomic analysis but is often hindered by clouds that obscure spectra and disrupt spatial–temporal consistency. Current optical and SAR fusion methods aimed at mitigating these impacts lack cloud conditioned reliability modeling, which restricts their generalization ability. To address this issue, we propose AdaptCloud, an information-driven adaptive multimodal fusion framework designed for cloud robust LCC. AdaptCloud mitigates cloud induced information degradation by constructing an information exploitation chain that treats cloud cover as a limited information condition, progressively enhancing class discriminability, observational completeness, and overall reliability. Specifically, for enhanced category discrimination, AdaptCloud leverages prior information from foundational model to strengthen feature extraction capabilities under cloud occlusion, while category adaptive fine-tuning optimizes feature extraction for specific category. By leveraging occlusion stable low frequency optical structures and high frequency SAR texture priors, AdaptCloud performs bidirectional injection and pixel level multiscale fusion to improve information integrity. Furthermore, to enhance key information reliability, AdaptCloud aligns modalities via cross modality attention and integrates sparse filtering to preserve stable features and suppress degradation, enabling robust multimodal fusion under diverse cloud conditions. Systematic evaluations on four cloud occluded datasets, DDHRNet Korea, DDHRNet Xi’an, PIE-RGB-SAR, and CSU-OPT-SAR, show that AdaptCloud outperforms state-of-the-art baselines for land-cover mapping under cloud cover, achieving mIoU gains of 0.71% to 19.64% and demonstrating robust performance in cloud prone regions. Additionally, the CSU-OPT-SAR dataset is established to overcome the lack of multi-seasonal data, demonstrating AdaptCloud’s strong generalization for cross-regional and cross-temporal LCC.

title: Diffusion Feature Completion–Driven Textual Dual Distillation for Multi-modal Remote Sensing Classification with Missing Modality
authors: Dan Xu, Wenqian Dong, Song Xiao, Jiahui Qu, Yunsong Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: In recent years, research on joint classification of multi-modal remote sensing data has achieved remarkable progress. However, constrained by imaging conditions or sensor issues, missing modality frequently occurs in practice. Knowledge distillation, as one of the mainstream strategies to deal with missing modality, is widely used due to its excellent transfer ability. However, most existing knowledge distillation methods focus on directly transferring available modal information through feature alignment or predictive constraints, without explicitly modeling or refining the latent semantic structure of missing modality. Furthermore, the heterogeneity between different modalities makes effective alignment difficult through direct transfer, thus limiting the utilization of cross-modal complementary information and consequently hindering model performance improvement in remote sensing scenarios with missing modality. Therefore, in this paper, we propose a diffusion feature completion-driven textual dual distillation model (DFC-TD<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) for multi-modal remote sensing image classification with missing modality. This model explicitly models the missing modality, introduces textual semantic information to guide cross-modal feature transfer, and achieves multi-constraint collaborative optimization under the supervision of classification labels, thereby effectively improving the overall performance of multi-modal classification tasks with missing modality. Specifically, a shared-constrained diffusion feature completion network (SCFC) is designed, which uses shared features extracted and frozen from multi-modal data as constraints to guide the diffusion model to effectively model and complete missing modal features, thereby generating semantically consistent and more discriminative modal representations. Furthermore, a text-guided dual distillation network (TGD<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) is designed to guide cross-modal feature alignment using text semantics and combine it with classification label supervision to construct a feature-label dual constraint, which effectively improves feature discriminativeness and supervision efficiency, thereby mitigating cross-modal representation heterogeneity and improving the stability and effectiveness of knowledge transfer. Experimental results on three public datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of this method in dealing with missing modality.

title: Edge-based Approximate Caching for Fast and Scalable Text-to-Image Diffusion Models
authors: Shuyun Luo, Dongmiao Ying, Zhiyi Luo, Weiqiang Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Text-to-image generation applications based on diffusion models face substantial challenges in computational efficiency and latency, particularly in time-sensitive scenarios, due to the inherently iterative denoising process. Although approximate caching techniques can reduce denoising iterations by reusing intermediate states of diffusion models, existing approaches fail to adequately capture user request behaviors. It is observed that users tend to issue a large number of prompt requests within short time intervals (bursty patterns), and that prompts from the same user often exhibit high similarity over short time periods (temporal locality). In this work, we define and formalize the Intermediate State Selection (ISS) problem to minimize denoising iterations. We further prove the NP-hardness of the ISS problem via a polynomial-time reduction from the Dominating Set problem. We then exploit both characteristics of prompt requests and present EdgeDiffusion, a novel edge-cloud cooperative framework in which the cloud retains image generation, while prompts caching and intermediate state selection are offloaded to edge servers. Specifically, we design an ISS algorithm that optimizes state reuse by leveraging temporal locality and an adaptive caching strategy tailored to bursty patterns. Experimental results on real-world datasets demonstrate that EdgeDiffusion achieves 18.3%-77.3% computational savings over baseline strategies (NIRVANA, qLRU-AC, LRU and LFU), while maintaining 98% quality of images.

title:
A 0.0035-mm
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    86-dB-SNR 1.25-MHz-BW Noise-Shaping SAR ADC Enabling kT/C Noise Shaping
authors: Xing Wang, Zhishuai Zhang, Yi Zhong, Nan Sun, Lu Jie
abstract: This work presents a compact second-order noise shaping successive approximation register (NS-SAR) analog-to-digital converter (ADC) featuring a novel kT/C noise shaping technique. The proposed kT/C noise shaping technique selectively suppresses kT/C noise within the signal bandwidth of an oversampled SAR ADC. It avoids using a wideband noise extraction amplifier as in the conventional kT/C noise-canceling method. Thus, it achieves a greatly reduced input sampling capacitance, high power efficiency, and low input noise penalty. A prototype ADC implemented in a 28-nm process achieves 85.8 dB signal-to-noise-and-distortion ratio (SNDR) across a 1.25 MHz bandwidth with sampling capacitors of only 150 fF and off-chip mismatch calibration. The power consumption at 40 MS/s is 0.9 mW, resulting in a Schreier figure of merit (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text {FoM}_{S}$ </tex-math></inline-formula>) of 177.2 dB. The ADC occupies an area of 0.0035-mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, which is the smallest among all published ADCs with over 70 dB SNDR with calibration.

title:
A Highly Integrated Primary-Side Zero-Voltage Switching Active Clamp Flyback Control IC for a 5-cm
                    <sup>3</sup>
                    5-V/1-A AC–DC Converter With Adaptive Frequency and Deadtime Control
authors: Akiyoshi Tanaka, Shan He, Reza Mounesi, Xinjian Liu, Omar Faruqe, Nugaira Gahan Mim, Daniel S. Truesdell, Adel Nasiri, Benton H. Calhoun
abstract: This article presents a miniaturized 5-W output AC–DC converter for Internet of Things (IoT) and smart building powered by wall power. The implemented control IC for an active clamp flyback (ACF) AC–DC converter achieves up to 500-kHz switching operation to reduce passive elements compared to conventional passive clamp flyback and secondary-side regulation flyback converters. Toward further miniaturization of the system, the implemented IC can support adaptive frequency control and zero-voltage switching (ZVS) without high-voltage (HV) components. The chip can adaptively operate from 300- to over 500-kHz switching frequency with dynamic deadtime control techniques. This work reduces the magnetizing inductance of the transformer by 60% compared to previous works, down <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$85~{\mathrm {\mu \text {H} }}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. The full AC–DC system volume occupies 4.99 cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>, which is 50% smaller than prior works, and achieves a power density of 1.00 W/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> at 5 V/1 A from 85- to 265-Vac input with a peak efficiency of 81.0%.

title: Frequency Domain Mixing and Boundary Modulation Network for SAR Small Target Detection
authors: Wenbin Gao, Zhi Yuan, Donglin Jing
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: As SAR imaging resolution gradually improves to the sub-meter level, the fine scattering structures of both targets and background clutter in the scene are simultaneously restored with high fidelity. The degree of feature aliasing between small targets (such as small ships) and speckle noise or nearshore clutter is significantly intensified, and the difficulty of stable feature extraction is greatly increased. On the one hand, traditional multi-scale methods (feature pyramids, fixed-scale convolutions) use discrete hierarchical designs, which fail to adapt to the continuous scale variations of SAR small objects and lead to inefficient feature extraction. On the other hand, SAR small objects have significant local radar scattering differences; even different regions of the same ship show drastic feature changes, which severely reduces intra-class feature similarity and consistency. To address these issues, this paper proposes a SAR ship detection method based on frequency-domain mixing and part-aware representation. First, a frequency-domain differential decoupling module is designed, which realizes adaptive frequency band division through multi-scale Gaussian filtering and filters key ship frequency features combined with a dynamic masking mechanism. This module can not only suppress speckle noise but also construct a continuous-scale feature mapping space. Second, a part-aware dynamic convolution module is developed to fuse local morphological features and global contextual information for generating dynamic dilation rate convolution kernels, so as to accurately depict target boundary contours in dense scenes and suppress feature aliasing between targets. Comparative experiments on two mainstream datasets, HRSID and MSAR-1.0, demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in small target detection, dense scene adaptability, and robustness to strong noise.

title: Neural Wave Propagation for Surgical Video Action Recognition: A New Dataset and Baseline
authors: Tianxiang Chen, Weibin Wang, Zhentao Tan, Ru Zhou, Yue Wu, Ziyang Wang, Le Lu, Mingliang Wang, Zi Ye
abstract: Accurate and efficient recognition of surgical actions in videos is critical for advancing AI-driven surgical robotics. However, current surgical video action recognition (SVAR) datasets suffer from limitations such as small scale, low resolution, inconsistent annotations, and insufficient action coverage. Most latest video recognition models are trained on large-scale common datasets and underperform in SVAR due to architectures that suppress high-frequency visual details (crucial for recognizing surgical tools and motions) and lack a strong spatial inductive bias, requiring extensive training data for good convergence. This is particularly challenging in the surgical domain, where data access is limited. Therefore, a new baseline is required. To address these issues, we introduce LapSurg-230K, an SVAR dataset of 7,569 high-resolution laparoscopic surgical video clips with 230,246 frames, well-annotated for 11 key actions across 9 surgery types. It supports both full and progressive data volume evaluation settings. We further propose WaveR, an attention-free baseline based on physical wave propagation. WaveR embeds an innate physical inductive bias: each video patch acts as a wave source that propagates waves toward action-critical regions (e.g., instrument tips), adaptively aggregating spatial-temporal context while preserving high-frequency surgical cues. This mechanism eliminates dependency on massive training data. Experiments demonstrate WaveR’s robustness under extreme data scarcity (≤ 30% training samples), achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on both surgical video action recognition and phase recognition tasks. The complete dataset, licensed under CC-BY 4.0, is available at [data link]. Our code is available at [code link].

title: Two-Tier PEFT Adapter Switching for Environment- and SNR-Adaptive Channel Estimation
authors: Joo Won Lee, Kae Won Choi
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Deep learning-based channel estimation can outperform classical methods, but adapting pre-trained estimators to new propagation environments and operating conditions remains challenging. We propose a two-tier parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) adapter switching framework that uses a shared base model together with lightweight adapters. Tier 1 adapts the estimator to a new propagation environment, while Tier 2 switches signal-to-noise-ratio (SNR)-specific adapters to better match the current operating regime. We evaluate LoRA, DoRA, and IA3 on a purpose-built Transformer channel estimator with self-attention and cross-attention components, transferred from Rural Macro to Indoor Factory environments with only 500 target-domain training samples. Low-rank PEFT methods outperform the mixed-SNR full fine-tuning baseline across all evaluated SNR levels, indicating that low-rank constraints provide beneficial regularization in the limited-data regime. Additional evaluations at 5 dB and 15 dB further show that specialization gains are not limited to the originally evaluated extreme operating points. A token-size ablation over s = 6, 12, 24 confirms that the original choice s = 12 yields the best average normalized mean squared error (NMSE) in the evaluated setting. Compared with classical estimators, the neural estimator is strongest at low SNR, where a learned channel prior supports nonlinear denoising, while classical methods remain superior at high SNR in the well-conditioned interpolation regime. For four deployment variants, LoRA/DoRA switching reduces storage from 28.7 MB to 8.0 MB with only modest one-time switching overhead and deployed latency close to the full-model baseline, whereas IA3 attains the smallest payload with higher runtime cost in our implementation.

title: Applications of Solid-state Transformers for Electric Power Grid HEMP/GMD Resilience
authors: Timothy Donnelly, Connor Lehman, Lee Rashkin
publish: IEEE Open Access Journal of Power and Energy
abstract: A high-altitude electromagnetic pulse (HEMP) or geomagnetic disturbance (GMD) can disrupt power grids by inducing low-frequency common-mode (CM) currents. When these currents flow through grounded transformers, they can bias the magnetic core, driving it into saturation and reducing performance or damaging equipment. This work presents a solid-state transformer (SST) to replace vulnerable assets and improve grid resilience. The paper reviews half-cycle saturation in conventional transformers, then describes an SST architecture that neutralizes and redirects CM currents during HEMP/GMD events. A prototype SST is built and validated, demonstrating stable CM-disturbance operation and protection of nearby transformers. Finally, large-scale simulations show how coordinated SST deployment mitigates CM disturbances and strengthens overall grid resilience.

title: Resilient Secure Control of Networked Industrial Control Systems under AI-Driven Feedback Poisoning via ACK Bundling
authors: Xiao Cai, Yanbin Sun, Yanli Chen, Jinglei Tan, Kaibo Shi, Jun Cheng, Shiping Wen, Zhihong Tian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: This paper investigates resilient secure control for networked industrial systems under AI-driven feedback poisoning attacks. Such attacks exploit feedback channels and timing regularities to degrade closed-loop performance while remaining difficult to detect. A data-driven poisoning model is constructed based on a real-world SWaT dataset, enabling the emulation of adversarial feedback manipulation beyond idealized assumptions. An acknowledgment (ACK) bundling mechanism is introduced to regulate feedback transmission by aggregating ACKs within a tunable time window. The bundling window Tb is adaptively adjusted via gradient descent (GD), facilitating a flexible trade off between control performance and communication efficiency in adversarial environments. In addition, a lightweight Lyapunov Krasovskii functional (LKF) is developed to establish closed loop stability in the presence of communication delays and poisoned feedback. Simulation results on a networked unmanned surface vehicle (USV) platform demonstrate improved tracking performance and robustness against feedback poisoning under constrained communication conditions. These results indicate that the proposed framework provides a practical and effective solution for secure networked industrial control.

title: Current-Sensor Fault-Tolerant Control for PMSM Drives via Offset-Free Phase Extended Generalized Integrator
authors: Zifeng Chen, Ruiqi Xu, Xijian Lin, Shun Cai, Dianxun Xiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: Conventional current sensor fault-tolerant control methods in Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) drive systems face dual challenges: measurement drift due to current sampling errors and complex nonlinear observer structures. These issues result in inaccurate phase current reconstruction and ambiguous stability characteristics under faulty conditions. This letter proposes an offset-free fault-tolerant control strategy using a single-phase current sensor based on the Offset-free Phase Extended Generalized Integrator (OPEGI) to address these issues. By employing the OPEGI algorithm to reconstruct three-phase currents, the core innovations of this letter include: 1) achieving current reconstruction in the abc axis during current sensor signal loss faults and remedying the stability defects of conventional schemes; 2) real-time suppression of offset errors in current measurement, enhancing the accuracy of current signal measurement. The effectiveness of this method has been validated through experiments conducted on a three-phase 4 kW PMSM setup.

title: Enhanced Circle Method for Discriminating Incipient Faults in Power Transformers Using Dissolved Gas Analysis
authors: Abdelmoumene Hechifa, Chouaib Labiod, Abdelaziz Lakehal, Redha Meneceur, Ali Bebboukha, Said Djaballah
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Power transformers are essential components in electrical power systems, ensuring the continuous transmission and distribution of electricity. Dissolved Gas Analysis (DGA) has become a widely accepted method for monitoring the condition of transformers and detecting incipient faults. Among the various DGA methods, graphical techniques have gained significant popularity in recent years. In this study, an Improved Circle Method is proposed as an enhanced extension of the Circle 1 and Circle 2 methods to address the limitations of traditional DGA methods in distinguishing overlapping fault types. The method employs a sigmoid activation function to control the convergence and divergence distances between fault samples, significantly improving the separation of electrical and thermal faults. By utilizing Gray Wolf Optimization (GWO), optimal angles are determined for the best fault separation. Experimental validation using the IEC TC10 database demonstrated that the Improved Circle Method achieves an overall accuracy of 79.49%, which can be considered an improvement over both Circle 1 and Circle 2 methods. The results confirm that the proposed method serves as a reliable alternative to other conventional graphical techniques.

title: Reconstruction of curing temperature field evolution in epoxy composite insulators based on sparse monitoring data and reduced order modeling_supp1-3696940.docx
authors: Ying Lin
abstract: Non-uniform temperature distribution during epoxy resin curing is a primary source of internal stress development. However, existing experimental techniques cannot capture the global evolution of internal temperature fields, while conventional numerical simulations are challenged by strong coupling among material properties, temperature, and degree of cure. In this work, a global reconstruction model for curing temperature fields is developed for cycloaliphatic epoxy insulators based on sparse monitoring data and reduced-order modeling. The reconstruction accuracy and generalization capability of the proposed model are systematically evaluated. The results confirm that the proposed model enables accurate reconstruction of the curing temperature field evolution, with a reconstruction time of less than 0.05 ms. Reconstruction accuracy improves with increasing numbers of monitoring points and snapshots, although the improvement gradually saturates. Further improvements are achieved through optimized snapshot selection and reduced monitoring errors. The model developed at 130 °C successfully predicts temperature fields at lower curing temperatures with a maximum error below 1.6 °C, and maintains errors within 2.6 °C for epoxy systems exhibiting different curing kinetics. Higher curing temperatures yield more diverse thermal field characteristics, resulting in improved generalization performance. This work establishes a global curing temperature field reconstruction strategy, providing a quantitative foundation for enhancing the reliability of epoxy composite manufacturing.

title: Knee Point-Guided Online Cooperative Path Planning for UAVs in Dynamic Multi-Objective Environments_supp1-3694877.pdf
authors: YUE XU
abstract: Online path planning for unmanned aerial vehicles requires dynamic trade-offs between path length and safety risks in complex and dynamically changing threat environments. However, most of recent researches either do not consider multi-UAV cooperative operations or do not incorporate online responses to dynamic threats. To address these limitations, a knee point-guided dynamic multi-objective path planning method is proposed in this paper. The main contributions are as follows: 1). A dual-UAV cooperative jamming mechanism is proposed to actively respond to threat risks and improve mission success rates; 2). A dynamic role-switching mechanism is developed under a leader-follower framework to enhance the system’s flexibility in responding to threats; 3). A knee point-guided change response mechanism is constructed, which integrates neighborhood sampling and imbalanced transfer learning to adaptively adjust the response strategy based on the degree of environmental change. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed mothed can effectively and rapidly respond to environmental changes, enabling UAVs to generate feasible and efficient online flight paths under complex dynamic threat conditions.

title: Design and Characterization of a Tendon-Driven Soft Robotic Gripper for Lightweight Manipulation
authors: Matthew Lee, Indie Heinemann-Rueda, Yen-Lin Han
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: This paper presents the design, fabrication, computational, and experimental validation of a tendon-driven soft robotic gripper for lightweight manipulation tasks with relevance to assistive hand function. The gripper features a bio-inspired three-joint finger design fabricated as a single monolithic piece from thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) via fused deposition modeling (FDM) 3D printing, eliminating assembly requirements and potential failure points. Comprehensive finite element analysis using hyperelastic Mooney-Rivlin material models validates the mechanical design through full-finger and individual joint simulations. Full-finger analysis under 1 N tensile load characterizes overall bending behavior, while individual joint simulations under a 0.1 N load reveal detailed stress distributions. The gripper integrates with a HiWonder xArm robotic arm platform in a three-finger radial configuration, actuated by a single servo motor via fishing line tendons. Preprogrammed motion sequences enable execution of manipulation tasks without requiring low-level servo control. Experimental pick-and-place testing across objects of varying weights and geometries demonstrates the gripper's functional manipulation capabilities, with qualitative observations confirming passive conformability to object contours and stable grasping for cylindrical, spherical, cubic, and irregular objects. The computational and experimental findings validate the design's fundamental grasping mechanics and establish the viability of single-piece 3D-printed tendon-driven grippers as an accessible platform for future soft robotic assistive technology research.

title: A Vision-Based Four-Cable-Driven Positioning System with Cascade Control: Experimental Evaluation
authors: Gianmarco Humpiri, Piero Reyes, Medard Quispe, Elvis J. Alegria
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Effective water quality monitoring in large-scale aquaculture remains a challenge due to the limited spatial coverage of fixed sensors and the operational constraints of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). To address this, this paper presents the design and validation of a scalable four-cable-driven parallel robot (CDPR) with a four-corner architecture tailored for autonomous sensor deployment. The system implements a cascade control strategy, integrating a low-level motor current loop with a high-level vision-based kinematic loop to ensure precise end-effector positioning under dynamic uncertainties. Although the mechanical design supports five degrees of freedom (5-DoF), this study focuses on validating the translational performance required for environmental sensing. Experimental validation was conducted using square and circular trajectories under varying payloads. Results demonstrate that the system maintains a consistently low normalized tracking error even under maximum load conditions, confirming the robustness and feasibility of the proposed architecture as a cost-effective solution for smart aquaculture monitoring.

title: GALA: Guided Attention with Language Alignment for Open Vocabulary Gaussian Splatting
authors: Elena Alegret, Kunyi Li, Sen Wang, Siyun Liang, Michael Niemeyer, Stefano Gasperini, Nassir Navab, Federico Tombari
abstract: 3D scene reconstruction and understanding have gained increasing popularity, yet existing methods struggle to capture fine-grained, language-aware 3D representations from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> images. In this paper, we present GALA, a novel framework for open-vocabulary 3D scene understanding with 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). GALA distills a scenespecific 3D instance feature field via self-supervised contrastive learning. To further extend this to generalized language feature fields, we introduce a core contribution of GALA, a cross-attention module with two learnable codebooks that encode view-independent semantic embeddings. This design not only ensures intra-instance feature similarity but also supports seamless 2D and 3D open-vocabulary queries. It reduces memory consumption by avoiding perGaussian high-dimensional feature learning. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate GALA's remarkable open-vocabulary performance on both <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> and 3D.

title: SCIGaussian-D: Dynamic Scene Reconstruction from a Single Snapshot Compressive Image
authors: Yunhao Li, Yuze Yang, Yanan Hu, Xiaoyue Li, Yong Tang, Xin Yuan, Peidong Liu
abstract: In this paper, we explore the potential of snapshot compressive imaging (SCI) for dynamic 3D scene reconstruction from a single temporal compressed image. SCI is a low-cost imaging technique that captures high-dimensional information-such as temporal data-using <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> sensors and coded masks, significantly reducing data bandwidth while offering inherent privacy advantages. While recent advances in Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have enabled 3D reconstruction from SCI measurements, these methods are fundamentally limited to static scenes and fail to generalize to dynamic content. To address this, we propose SCIGaussian-D, a novel framework that enables dynamic 3D reconstruction from a single SCI image. Our method represents the scene with 3D Gaussians defined in a canonical space and models motion using learnable deformation fields. By incorporating the SCI imaging model into the training loop, SCIGaussian-D directly reconstructs the dynamic 3D scene and recovers the corresponding camera motion from a single SCI. We evaluate our method on both synthetic and real SCI datasets, demonstrating significant improvements in reconstruction quality over existing baselines. Our results establish a new state of the art for dynamic scene reconstruction within the SCI framework, paving the way for practical applications in high-speed imaging and real-time scene rendering.

title: Pixels to Pursuit: Reinforcement Learning-Based Control Strategies for UAV-AGV Collaborative Systems
authors: Ankit Mehra, Darshankumar Prajapati, Pushkar Kumar, Ashish Rana, Urvashi Goswami, Amit Shukla
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: This paper presents reinforcement learning-based control strategies for vision-based UAV tracking by AGVs. We developed custom YOLOv8 object detection models achieving 98 % accuracy and implemented DQN and DDPG controllers trained in simulation environments. Experimental validation demonstrates DDPG outperforms PID, SMC, and DQN controllers with superior tracking accuracy and reduced settling time. The approach enables robust UAV-AGV collaboration in GPS-denied environments with potential applications in agriculture and search-and-rescue operations.

title: Towards a Data-Driven Approach for Early Identification of at-Risk Students in Higher Education
authors: Ahmed Abrar Zayad, Ashim Dey, Annesha Das
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Predicting students attrition is key for higher education institutions, as it allows for timely and proper intervention for those identified as dropout students. This study proposes a machine learning procedure to assess the likelihood of student attrition using academic records, demographic features, and macroeconomic indicators (i.e., GDP and inflation) at the time of enrollment. In contrast to prior work that predicts Graduate, Enrolled, or Dropout outcomes, this study focuses on distinguishing the pupils who actually discontinued their studies from the students who graduated instead. Predictions were made after the first two semesters to facilitate early intervention. Since the dataset is highly imbalanced, SMOTE was applied for class balancing. Hyperparameter tuning using RandomSearchCV was performed, which helped improve the model's performance. Several classification algorithms, including XGBoost, Logistic Regression, Random Forest, and a Voting Classifier, were evaluated using multiple performance metrics. The tuned Voting Classifier attained the maximum level of accuracy of 92.15 %, demonstrating the effectiveness of machine learning algorithms with optimized parameters. The feature importance chart generated according to the voting classifier also shows that the average approval rate and whether a student is aware of their tuition payments are some of the most important factors for a student's outcome. These results demonstrate the possibilities of data-driven approaches in effectively helping students at risk and using resources.

title: Genetic Algorithm with Optimised Extra Tree Ensemble for Enhanced Intrusion Detection in IoMT Networks
authors: Rubiat Jahan Momtahina, Abdullah Rajib, Ashraful Goni, Fuad Hasan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Clinical efficiency has been remarkably enhanced by the integration of Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) into the healthcare system. But at the same time, due to the integration of IoMT, the attack surface for cyber threats is also increased. In the case of domain-specific environments, current machine learning and deep learning models have shown a deficiency in computational scalability and adaptability. To reinforce intrusion detection in IoMT networks, a Genetic Algorithm with Optimised Extra Tree Ensemble (GA-ExtraTree) is developed in this work. For selecting discriminative features from the MedSec-2025 dataset, the Genetic Algorithm is used, and this also reduces dimensionality and redundancy. On the other hand, highly accurate classification with decreased computational load has been guaranteed by the optimised Extra Tree ensemble. Compared to the current baseline approaches, the proposed model performs better by achieving 99.35% detection accuracy. According to experimental outcomes, GAExtraTree is identified as a high-performing, low-overhead, and domain-transferable architecture for intrusion detection in realtime IoMT, and it will strengthen the robustness of the security infrastructure of modern medical network systems.

title: A Hyper-Heuristic Framework for Human-In-The-Loop Combinatorial Optimization
authors: Kassem Danach, Jomana Al Haj Hassan, Samir Haddad, Jinane Sayah, Merhej Joseph, Kallab Chadi
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Human-in-the-loop combinatorial optimization problems are characterized by dynamic objectives, evolving constraints, and frequent human intervention, posing significant challenges to classical automated optimization methods. This paper proposes a categorized selection hyper-heuristic framework designed to explicitly incorporate human feedback into the optimization process. Low-level heuristics are organized into constructive, improvement, perturbation, and reconstructive categories, enabling adaptive control of search behavior in response to both algorithmic performance and human acceptance signals. The framework integrates a two-stage heuristic selection mechanism, interaction-aware acceptance criteria, and termination conditions tailored to non-stationary optimization environments. The proposed approach is evaluated on a nurse rostering problem, a representative NP-hard combinatorial optimization task. Experimental results indicate that the proposed framework reduces the average number of human interventions by approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 5 \%}$</tex> compared to a noncategorized hyper-heuristic, while improving solution stability by more than 50 % and maintaining competitive objective values. These results demonstrate that explicit heuristic categorization and acceptance-aware control provide effective mechanisms for addressing the challenges of human-in-the-loop combinatorial optimization.

title: Stomata-Inspired Reconfigurable Square Ring Slot Antennas for 5G Applications
authors: Buthaina Al Siyabi, Hassan Al-Lawati, Mohammed Bait-Suwailam, Joseph A. Jervase
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: This paper presents two antennas of a bioinspired reconfigurable square-ring slot for sub- 6 GHz 5 G applications, functionally motivated by the dynamic opening and closing behavior of plant stomata. By employing a single PIN diode, the proposed antennas simultaneously enable frequency and polarization reconfiguration, thereby reducing circuit complexity, biasing overhead, and power consumption. The antennas are fabricated on a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 6 - m m}$</tex> thick FR4 substrate with a relative permittivity of 4.4 and a loss tangent of 0.02. The first antenna is for frequency-reconfigurable mode, the antenna operates in a triple-band state at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.6 \text{GHz}, 4.95 \text{GHz}$</tex>, and 6.85 GHz when the diode is ON, and in a dual-band state at 3.55 GHz and 6.8 GHz when the diode is OFF. Furthermore, the second antenna is for polarization reconfiguration, which is achieved by switching between linear (ON state) and circular polarization (OFF state), with a minimum axial ratio of 0.62 dB at 3.5 GHz. Owing to their compact geometry, low implementation complexity, and multifunctional reconfigurability, the proposed biomimetic antennas represent an efficient and cost-effective solution for adaptive sub-6 GHz 5G and Internet-of-Things (IoT) wireless systems.

title: DDoS Attack Detection in Cloud Systems Using a Hybrid Stacked Ensemble
authors: Nematullah Khan, Raad Alharthi, Fahad Alzahrani, Mourad Benmalek, Abdelouahid Derhab
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks remain a major threat to cloud infrastructure, while many existing defenses provide only coarse protocol-level detection and struggle to adapt to evolving attack patterns. This paper proposes an AI-driven stacked ensemble for cloud DDoS detection and finegrained TCP-based attack typing. The framework uses two stages: (i) an SVM for binary flow classification (benign vs. non-benign) and (ii) three deep meta-learners (LSTM, CNN, and Autoencoder) to classify 17 TCP-based DDoS variants and suspicious traffic, with majority voting for the final decision. Experiments on the AWS-collected BCCC-cPacket-Cloud-DDoS2024 dataset show 94.28% accuracy for the SVM, 94.20% for the best meta-learner, and 92.72% accuracy for the ensemble, achieving a weighted F1-score of 0.92. Results indicate strong performance on volumetric attacks and stable behavior across diverse categories, supporting the practicality of heterogeneous ensembles for cloud DDoS defense.

title: Adaptive First-Person Shooter: Real-Time Game Difficulty Adjustment Based on Player Stress Levels
authors: Georges Chaiban, Nabil Moussa, Bilal Zahraman, Joseph Azar
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Video games, especially first-person shooter (FPS) games, often involve intense gameplay that can elicit aggressive responses. This paper proposes a stress-adaptive FPS game that dynamically adjusts gameplay based on the player's real-time emotional state. The system combines speech analysis via a large language model (LLM), heart rate monitoring, and in-game performance metrics to compute an aggression score using a weighted algorithm. Based on this score, the game applies inverse difficulty scaling and, when extreme aggression is detected, transitions the player to a calming environment with relaxation tasks. Grounded in established psychological frameworks including the General Aggression Model and Flow Theory, this work demonstrates how real-time multi-modal emotion monitoring can be integrated into the gaming domain to create an adaptive, personalized experience focused on player well-being.

title: Shadow Distance Analysis of Agrivoltaic System in Bangladesh: From Geometry to Simulation
authors: Zihan Rejwana, Mohammad Tasnimul Hasan, Md. Mosaddequr Rahman
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This study compares two methods for calculating shadow distance in agrivoltaic systems: geometrical analysis and 3D modeling. Simulations were conducted for an agricultural site during the extended shading period of winter to assess model performance. A MATLAB program was developed using modified equations for geometrical calculations, while SketchUp was employed for 3D simulation and validation. The results indicate that the geometrical model maintains an average error of less than 6 % compared to the 3D approach. These findings suggest that geometrical modeling can serve as a reliable tool for shadow assessment in agrivoltaic system design.

title: MawaNet: A Custom Deep Classifier for Credit Card Fraud Detection
authors: Md Nuruzzaman, Afsana Nadia, Md Masum Billal, Khan Md Shibli Nomani, Md Tarikul Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Credit card fraud detection is still challenging in the financial industry. It indicates the necessity of modern machine learning techniques to detect abnormal transactions without erroneously classifying normal account activities. The existing studies heavily rely on traditional machine learning and deep learning approaches, with a lack of optimization and regularization for tabular data. We propose a deep learning architecture called MawaNet for the detection of credit card fraudulent activity with proper regularization to work effectively on tabular data. The extensive experiments have been applied to ten different machine learning algorithms. MawaNet (our proposed model) has been compared with the most popular machine learning models, such as AdaBoost Classifier, Decision Tree Classifier, Gaussian Naïve Bayes, Gradient Boosting Classifier, Logistic Regression, Passive Aggressive Classifier, Quadratic Discriminant Analysis, Ridge Classifier, Self-Training Classifier, and SGD Classifier. The performance has been evaluated through precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy. MawaNet achieves the highest score <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(99.99 \%)$</tex> in all performance evaluation metrics. The proposed architecture outperforms other traditional methods. The Decision Tree Classifier performs well with the score of 99.78 % across all metrics. These results demonstrate that the deep learning architecture is able to capture the non-linear and obscured patterns in transactional data, offering financial institutions a strong candidate for use in real-time systems to detect fraud.

title: Machine Learning Approaches for RADAR-Based Warhead Target Classification Under Clutter Conditions
authors: Md. Abdur Rouf, Md. Selim Hossain, Mst. Azmira Khanam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This study presents a machine learning method that effectively classifies warhead-type targets from non-warhead-type targets using a simulated radar dataset. Key radar characteristics, including Radar Cross Section (RCS), Doppler shift, speed, angle, frequency, micro-Doppler signatures, and range profiles, were incorporated into a synthetic dataset. The dataset is generated in two different scenarios in realistic conditions with additional environmental clutter, such as rain and urban reflections, and another one in ideal conditions without clutter. A Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier showed notable performance degradation 53.75 % when trained on cluttered data, but it achieved nearperfect accuracy of 99.5% on the clean dataset. The Principal Component Analysis was used as a feature optimization method to suppress the clutter, including variance, and reduce feature redundancy. The classification accuracy for the clutter condition increased to 85.7 % with the incorporation of PCA. These findings underscore the importance of feature optimization in enhancing radar-based classification performance in challenging operating environments.

title: Integrating Pathway Enrichment with Graph Attention Networks for Pan-Cancer Classification
authors: Anika Tasnim, Propa Punam, Md. Salahuddin Fahad, Tasnim Ahmed
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Large-scale pan-cancer transcriptomic resources such as TCGA provide unprecedented opportunities to identify features that are both predictive and biologically meaningful. However, the high dimensionality and heterogeneity of RNA-seq data pose challenges for robust feature selection and generalizable classification. In this study, we present a hybrid computational framework that integrates mutual information and LASSO filtering with pathway-guided Graph Attention Networks (GATs) to extract a compact set of significant features from TCGA PanCancer RNA-seq data spanning 33 tumor types (n = 10,826 samples). Our pipeline reduced more than 60,000 genes to 32 key features while preserving predictive power. Using SMOTEbalanced training and multiple classifiers (Random Forest, Light-GBM, XGBoost, DNN, and an ensemble model), we achieved 96.21% overall classification accuracy and consistently high precision, recall, and F1-scores across cancer types. Functional enrichment analysis of the selected features revealed biologically coherent pathways associated with tumorigenesis, highlighting the potential of our approach to identify functionally relevant and highly discriminative transcriptomic signatures in pan-cancer studies.

title: Secure Storage and Privacy-Preserving Scanpath Comparison via Garbled Circuits in Eye Tracking ETRA008
authors: Suleyman Ozdel, Amr Nader, Yasmeen Abdrabou, Enkelejda Kasneci
publish: Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction
abstract: None

title: PromptX: A Cognitive Agent Platform with Long-term Memory
authors: Binhao Wang, Jianglin Huang, Xiao Hu, Shan Jiang, Maolin Wang, Ching-Ho Yang, Jian Jiang, Junhao Ye, Yaozu Cen, Rui Zeng, Yingtong Zhou, Yingjie Luo, Guanjie Wu, Wangzhong Xu, Feiyu Zhou, Xiangyu Zhao
publish: Companion Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2026
abstract: None

title: No Evidence, No Problem: When Less is More for Out of Context Multimodal Misinformation Detection
authors: Siyuan Chen, Usman Naseem, Mehwish Nasim
publish: Companion Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2026
abstract: None

title: Temperature-Dependent Current–Voltage Model for Emerging GAA NS FETs Using a Physics-Inspired Neural Network
authors: Yiming Li-, Yun Tai, Min-Hui Chuang
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Temperature effects are incorporated into a compact modeling framework for gate-all-around (GAA) nanosheet (NS) MOSFETs based on a fundamental device current equation and two different artificial neural networks (ANNs) from $-75^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $125^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The first ANN is responsible for predicting key temperature-dependent physical coefficients embedded in the Grove-Frohman formulation, enabling the model to capture the dominant thermal trends of drain current and major parameter variations. The second ANN is constructed as a residual correction network that generates bias-dependent correction factors to compensate nonlinearities not fully captured by the core physics equation. Applying this deep-learning model to various circuit simulations and comparing it with raw data across different temperatures consistently yields excellent results.

title: Evolving Landscape of Attacks on AI Hardware and Robust Defenses
authors: Habibur Rahaman, Sudipta Paria, Atri Chatterjee, Swarup Bhunia
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: The rapid deployment of artificial intelligence (AI) systems has introduced a new class of security vulnerabilities rooted in hardware behavior. Unlike software-only threats, hardware-oriented attacks exploit physical effects and micro-architectural characteristics of the computing substrate, allowing adversaries to induce faults, extract sensitive information, or stealthily manipulate inference outcomes. Recent studies show that deep neural network (DNN) accelerators are particularly susceptible to such attacks, including selective bit-flip fault injection via Rowhammer or voltage glitching, side-channel leakage through power and electromagnetic emanations, and hardware Trojan insertion during fabrication or third-party intellectual property (IP) integration. Even a small number of hardware modifications can result in severe accuracy degradation, targeted misclassification, or denial-of-service. In this paper, we cover the evolving landscape of hardware-based attack vectors targeting AI accelerators (e.g., DNNs) and the corresponding defense methods. We systematically categorize fault-injection, side-channel, and hardware Trojan attacks; examine their threat models and practical impact on DNN inference; and review existing protection techniques. We emphasize lightweight defenses for resource-constrained edge platforms, highlighting AI Performance Counters as passive, low-overhead observability primitives that capture execution-level signatures of neural workloads for runtime anomaly detection. We outline open challenges and future research directions toward resilient and trustworthy AI hardware systems.

title: Descrambling the Scrambler: Experimental Extraction of Data Scrambling Keys in COTS NAND Flash
authors: Matchima Buddhanoy, Habib Ur Rahman, Aleksandar Milenkovic, Sudeep Pasricha, Biswajit Ray
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Data scramblers in today’s NAND flash-based solidstate drives (SSDs) randomize stored bit patterns to mitigate pattern-dependent errors, improve data retention, and increase error-correcting code (ECC) effectiveness. Despite their importance and widespread use, the on-die scrambling mechanisms and keys remain proprietary and opaque to system designers, tool builders, and forensic analysts. This paper presents the first experimental demonstration of scrambling key extraction from unmodified commercial-off-the-shelf (COTS) NAND flash chips. By manipulating page write operations to force all cells into a known physical state, we successfully recover the underlying scrambling keys without any vendor support or controller modification. The extracted keys exhibit consistent structural patterns across devices, typically 16–64 bytes depending on the NAND technology, and repeat periodically before transitioning to new sequences. The keys are page-dependent, block-independent, and consistent across chips of the same part number, enabling full reconstruction from a single block or device. Our results demonstrate the generality of the method across both single-level cell (SLC) and multi-level cell (MLC) NAND flash architectures. The ability to reconstruct scrambling keys from raw NAND enables controller-independent reliability analysis, validation of instant data-sanitization schemes, and higher-fidelity forensic reconstruction from raw memory dumps.

title: Enhanced Hybrid Temporal Computing Using Deterministic Summations for Ultra-Low-Power Accelerators
authors: Sachin Sachdeva, Jincong Lu, Wantong Li, X.-D. Tan Sheldon
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: This paper presents an accuracy-enhanced Hybrid Temporal Computing (E-HTC) framework for ultra-low-power hardware accelerators using deterministic additions. The framework is inspired by the recently proposed HTC architecture, which leverages pulse-rate and temporal data encoding to reduce switching activity and energy consumption but suffers accuracy loss due to its use of a multiplexer (MUX) for scaled addition. To address this limitation, we propose two bitstream addition schemes: (1) an Exact Multiple-input Binary Accumulator (EMBA), which performs precise binary accumulation over multiple-input bitstreams, and (2) a Deterministic Threshold-based Scaled Adder (DTSA), which employs threshold-based logic for scaled addition. These adders are integrated into a multiplier–accumulator (MAC) unit supporting both unipolar and bipolar encodings. To validate the framework, we implement two hardware accelerators: a Finite Impulse Response (FIR) filter and an 8-point Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)/iDCT engine. Experimental results on a $4 \times 4$ MAC show that, in unipolar mode, E-HTC matches the RMSE of state-of-the-art Counter-Based Stochastic Computing (CBSC) MAC, improves accuracy by $94\%$ over MUX-based HTC, and reduces power and area by up to $23 \%$ and $7 \%$ compared to MUX-based HTC and by over $64 \%$ and $74 \%$ compared to CBSC. In bipolar mode, the EHTC MAC achieves an RMSE of ${2. 0 9 \%}$—an ${8 3 \%}$ improvement over bipolar MUX-based HTC and approaches bipolar CBSC’s RMSE of $1.40 \%$ with area and power savings of up to $28 \%$ and $43\%$ relative to MUX-based HTC and around $76\%$ each relative to CBSC. In FIR filter experiments, both E-HTC variants achieve PSNR gains of ${3}-{5} {~ d B}~({3 0}-{4 5} {\%}$ RMSE reduction) over MUX-based HTC, while delivering up to $13 \%$ power and $3\%$ area savings. Compared to CBSC, E-HTC reduces area by nearly $64 \%$ and power by $62 \%$. For DCT/iDCT, E-HTC boosts PSNR by $10-13 \mathrm{~dB}$ ($70-75 \%$ RMSE reduction) while providing substantial area and power savings over both MUX- and CBSC-based designs.

title: Mixed-Precision Booth Factored Systolic Array Design for Accelerating Neural Networks
authors: Sneha Dandekar, S Bhavana, Madhav Rao
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Hardware Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) accelerators are expected to be power-efficient and occupy minimal die footprint. Systolic Array (SA) Architecture is a group of processing elements that are reused in the datapath flow to satisfy the above demands of hardware accelerator design. In this work, we present SA architecture which augments mixed precision Radix-4 based Booth encoded partial products for each of the PEs in the SA. The proposed architecture supports arithmetic approximations across the SA, allowing designers to explore trade-offs between hardware characteristics, and computational accuracy. A meta-heuristic approach, based on NSGA-II algorithm allows for exploring the large design space of mixed-precision booth encoding per PE to evolve Pareto-optimal SA configurations that balance hardware efficiency with neural network model accuracy. The SA designs were evaluated and individually evolved from different CNNs, such as ResNet-18, VGG11, and LeNet-5, which were trained on various datasets. The post-synthesis results using Genus (Cadence) tool with 45 nm gpdk technology libraries for the proposed approximate booth encoding factored SA designs demonstrated maximum of $12 \%$ improvement in the product of area-power-delay (PAPD) over the state-of-the-art (SOTA) designs, while preserving model accuracy. These results highlight the effectiveness of fine-grained architectural-level approximation in achieving efficient CNN acceleration without compromising on the output quality. All the design files are made freely available for easy adoption and further usage by the researchers and designers community.

title: Edge AI-based Anomaly Behavior Analysis for Industrial Control Systems
authors: Zhanglong Yang, Qinxuan Shi, Yu-Zheng Lin, Soheil Salehi, Pratik Satam, Sicong Shao
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Modern Industrial Control Systems (ICS) increasingly operate across heterogeneous compute tiers, from cloud servers to resource-constrained edge nodes, making it difficult to deploy a single anomaly detection solution that is both accurate and lightweight. The rapid evolution of the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) has exacerbated this divide, creating a fragmented security landscape in which critical remote edge devices often lack the sophisticated monitoring capabilities found in central command centers, thus creating blind spots that sophisticated adversaries can exploit, particularly in geographically distributed edge devices. To address this gap, we present EAI-ABA (Edge AI-based Anomaly Behavior Analysis) architecture, an edge-first architecture for anomaly behavior analysis that scales from lightweight, on-device monitoring to stronger back-end analysis while preserving detection quality. At its core is AIC-AT, which extends the Anomaly Transformer by fitting an EM-optimized Gaussian Mixture Model to anomaly scores and using AIC to select mixture complexity, enabling adaptive, data-driven thresholding without manual tuning. EAI-ABA provides two coordinated modes: Edge mode delivers low-latency ondevice anomaly detection and response, leveraging TorchAO quantization and the ExecuTorch runtime to reduce memory and compute cost, and pairing detection with a compact, modematched RAG-based assistant that summarizes evidence and recommends mitigation actions locally. When additional resources are available, Performance mode runs the full pipeline on a server for deeper analysis and maximum stability, with LLM configurations scaled accordingly for richer investigation support. Experiments on SWaT ICS testbed show that Performance mode achieves an $F_{1}$ score of $96.33 \%$, outperforming strong baselines, while Edge mode significantly reduces computational cost with only a small $F_{1}$ drop, enabling fast on-site detection and reliable long-term operation.

title: A 100-GHz High-Gain Airborne Antenna System With Ground-to-Air Flight Demonstration Toward HAPS Feeder Links
authors: Toshiyuki Nishibori, Nozomi Okada, Kimihiro Kimura, Toshio Sato, Kazuhiko Tamesue, Kunihisa Jitsuno, Takuro Sato, Tetsuya Kawanishi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents a high-gain airborne antenna system operating in the 100-GHz band and its ground-to-air flight demonstration toward feeder links for high-altitude platform stations (HAPSs). Although wireless communication at frequencies around 100 GHz suffers from increased free-space path loss, this limitation can be effectively mitigated by employing high-gain reflector antennas. We developed a compact airborne antenna system based on a 0.3-m-diameter Cassegrain–Nasmyth configuration fabricated using high-precision composite materials, achieving a high measured gain of 48.3 dBi. The antenna is equipped with a combined coarse and fine beam-adjustment mechanism, enabling precise beam pointing and tracking suitable for installation on aircraft. In addition, a prototype communication system covering the 92–105-GHz band, including ground-station antenna systems, transmitters (TXs), and receivers (RXs), was constructed. Experimental demonstrations were conducted for both ground-to-ground and ground-to-air links. The ground-to-ground experiment achieved a transmission distance of 4.4 km, while the ground-to-air flight experiment, performed using an aircraft flying at an altitude of 9500 ft, achieved a link distance of 3.5 km. In both cases, data rates of up to 4 Gb/s were successfully achieved using a bandwidth of 1.25 GHz. These results demonstrate the feasibility of high-speed ground-to-air wireless communication in the 100-GHz band and confirm the potential of high-gain airborne antenna systems as a key enabling technology for future HAPS feeder links.

title: Accelerating Time-Evolving Multiphysics Uncertainty Quantification of Millimeter Wave Components Using Reusable Preconditioning
authors: Hao-Xuan Zhang, Shi-Wei Guo, Lin-Peng Yu, Yi-Yao Wang, Hao Xie, Liang Zhou, Wen-Yan Yin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: Transient electromagnetic (EM)–thermal– mechanical uncertainty quantification (UQ) is computationally demanding because it requires repeated solutions of many closely related EM systems across time steps, coupling iterations, and random samples. This article presents an efficient finite-element multiphysics framework that couples the frequency-domain Helmholtz equationwith time-domain heat conduction and evaluates thermomechanical responses through small-deformation thermoelasticity. To avoid repeated remeshing in large-sample studies, thin metallization layers are modeled by a reduced-dimensional thermal formulation on a fixed mesh. To accelerate the repeated EM solving process, a reusable LU-based preconditioning strategy is incorporated into the coupling loop. UQ is then performed by nonintrusive polynomial chaos expansion (PCE), from which confidence bands and Sobol sensitivity indices are extracted for both time- and frequency-domain quantities of interest (QoI). Numerical results on a substrate-integrated waveguide (SIW) filter and a silicon-based D-band diplexer confirm that reusable preconditioning and fixed-mesh thin-layer modeling can substantially reduce the computational cost of large-sample transient UQ. This makes the proposed framework suitable for reliability-oriented analysis and design of millimeter-wave components operating under high-power and high-temperature conditions.

title: Modular Synthesis of Coupled-Resonator Filters Using Divide-and-Conquer Paradigms
authors: Qiuyi Wu, Feng Tang, Xiaoqi Yang, Zihan Wang, Yimin Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents a one-step method for decomposing a network into cascaded independent subnetworks, contrasting with the conventional “gather-in” approach and following the divide-and-conquer (D&C) paradigm. By modeling the network with ABCD matrices and leveraging the invariance of external Y-parameters, the decomposition reduces to a simple system of linear equations, requiring minimal numerical effort. The method applies to all typical configurations—including fully canonical or nonfully canonical networks, with or without frequency-dependent couplings (FDCs)—and can be extended recursively to handle complex high-order networks with arbitrary orders and transmission-zero allocations. Its effectiveness is demonstrated through various examples, including a dual-band filter with a hybrid cascaded–parallel topology, a structure that challenges traditional analytical techniques. Simulation and measurement results confirm the robustness of the algorithm and its potential for practical engineering applications.

title: Compensation of Frequency Shift Induced by the Electron Layer in Relativistic Magnetron
authors: Dong-Hyun Oh, Woo-Sang Lee, Jung-Hoon Han
abstract: The relativistic magnetron (RM) has attracted significant attention as a high-power microwave (HPM) source. Due to its narrowband characteristics, achieving a precise target frequency is a critical requirement in the initial design process. However, a frequency shift phenomenon observed in eigenmode and particle-in-cell (PIC) simulations complicates accurate frequency tuning. This shift is primarily attributed to the formation of a dense electron layer around the cathode during operation, which acts as a virtual conductor and effectively expands the cathode radius. To address this discrepancy, we propose two geometric compensation techniques: a posteriori and a priori methods. The a posteriori approach determines the effective extended boundary by explicitly analyzing the simulated space-charge distribution. In contrast, the priori method analytically predicts this effective expansion by utilizing the double-stream characteristics of the space-charge density, thereby eliminating the need for preliminary PIC simulations. The proposed compensation methods were verified using four magnetron models with distinct structural parameters. While the uncompensated resonant frequencies exhibited significant discrepancies ranging from 28 to 85MHz compared to the actual oscillation frequencies, the application of the proposed methods successfully reduced this frequency difference to within 25MHz across all models.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Circuits and Systems

title: Dynamic Escaping Population Driven Evolutionary Algorithm for Multi-objective Optimization Problems with Deceptive Constraints
authors: Xinyu Zhou, Weifeng Lin, KunJie Yu, Kangjia Qiao
abstract: Deceptive constrained multi-objective optimization problems (DCMOPs) pose severe challenges to constrained multi-objective evolutionary algorithms (CMOEAs), as constraint violation (CV) values become unreliable. In this paper, a dynamic escaping population-driven CMOEA, named DEPEA, is proposed by maintaining three cooperative populations: an escaping population (EP), an auxiliary population (AP), and a main population (MP). The EP employs slow and rapid escape modes to regulate its escaping behavior at different evolutionary stages, the AP aims to converge toward the unconstrained Pareto front (UPF), and the MP focuses on approximating the constrained Pareto front (CPF). To avoid mutual interference between the AP and EP while controlling the escaping behavior of the EP, a one-way fine-grained cooperation mechanism is designed. In addition, a boundary diversity enhancement strategy is proposed to strengthen exploration along boundary regions. Experimental studies conducted on four benchmark suites and six real-world constrained optimization problems demonstrate that DEPEA achieves superior performance compared with thirteen state-of-the-art CMOEAs.

title: Communication-Constrained Remote Power Grid Resilience Enhancement: Coordinated Planning of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles and Mobile Energy Storage Vehicles
authors: Hongzhen Wang, Boyu Qin, Shidong Hong, Fan Li, Tao Ding
abstract: The underdeveloped power and communication infrastructures in remote areas are prone to simultaneous failure during extreme disasters, exacerbating the difficulty of power system recovery. Mobile energy storage vehicles (MESVs) and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) can achieve the coordination of power and communication, providing precise and effective power support for remote power grid.
