title: Introducing the New IAS Membership Department [Membership News]
authors: Payman Dehghanian
abstract: Provides society information that may include news, reviews or technical notes that should be of interest to practitioners and researchers.

title: Thrill of the Build
authors: Gary Singh

title: Systematic Use Case Modeling and Specification Analysis of Transportation Systems in the Era of Software-Defined Vehicles
authors: Sebastian Siegl
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Transportation systems are built up with the integration of numerous mechatronic systems that collectively enable the desired functionalities. The term software-defined vehicle (SDV) represents a paradigm shift in engineering. While the physical hardware components-such as sensors, actuators, and electronic control units-are modified in relatively slower development cycles due to manufacturing constraints and validation requirements, the software changes evolve at a higher iteration frequency. This accelerated software development enables faster deployment of new features to continuously optimize the performance and usability, or to remediate security vulnerabilities and perform maintenance updates. Various approaches have been published, adressing the rising system complexity with hierarchical decomposition, to gain insight, analyse, and maintain the system's performance characteristics from a system's approach to modelling. Various load situations, hereinafter referred to as use cases, can be analysed in detail. An open question is often how structured all use cases have been covered. For this purpose, expert knowledge or Monte Carlo simulation is often used. However, both approaches have the disadvantage that they have not built and structured the use cases through a clear methodology. In this paper, we present a modeling method to systematically describe the use cases for a transportation system using a structured modeling process. In this context, modeling already focuses on the fact that all relevant and possible use cases are considered during modeling, so that statements can also be made about completeness and significance in the analysis of the use cases of the system. We present the application and results of the method using an example of a use case model of an exemplary vehicle's themal energy system and a safety feature, as it could be part of autonomous mobility.

title: InterPose: Learning to Generate Human-Object Interactions from Large-Scale Web Videos
authors: Yangsong Zhang, Abdul Ahad Butt, Gül Varol, Ivan Laptev
abstract: Human motion generation has shown great advances powered by recent diffusion models and large-scale motion capture data. Most of existing works, however, currently target animation of isolated people in empty scenes. Meanwhile, synthesizing realistic human-object interactions in complex 3D scenes remains a critical challenge in computer graphics and robotics. One obstacle towards generating versatile high-fidelity humanobject interactions is the lack of large-scale datasets with diverse object manipulations. Indeed, existing motion capture data is typically restricted to single people and manipulations of limited sets of objects. To address this issue, we propose an automatic motion extraction pipeline and use it to collect interaction-rich human motions. Our new dataset InterPose contains 73.8 K sequences of 3D human motions and corresponding text captions automatically obtained from 45.8 K videos with human-object interactions. We perform extensive experiments and demonstrate InterPose to bring significant improvements to state-of-the-art methods for human motion generation. Moreover, using InterPose we develop an LLM-based agent enabling zero-shot animation of people interacting with diverse objects and scenes.

title: C3PO: Canonicalization of 3D Pose from Partial Views With Generalizable Correspondence Features
authors: Yu Chi, Leonhard Sommer, Olaf Dünkel, Dominik Muhle, Daniel Cremers, Christian Theobalt, Adam Kortylewski
abstract: Progress in 3D object understanding relies on the category-level canonicalization of 3D objects, i.e., bringing 3D instances into a consistent position and orientation. Most related works assume complete 3D representations, while real-world applications often require solving the more challenging task of canonicalizing from partial views, i.e., short videos that cover only a part of the object. We introduce C3PO, a method capable of canonicalizing partial views from arbitrary object categories by enforcing geometric and feature-level appearance consistency of overlapping views. We represent partial views as 3D point clouds obtained via structure-from-motion, where each point carries a feature vector that is extracted from 2 D images using a novel feature extractor capable of estimating generalizable correspondence features. Notably, our correspondence features are learned on a large dataset and generalize to object categories not seen during training. On top of this, we introduce an efficient pairwise-registration framework that aligns partial object representations into a globally consistent canonical frame. Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that C3PO significantly outperforms existing methods.

title: Dynamic Modeling and Transmission Characteristic Analysis of Integrated Linear Joints
authors: Shengbei Wang, Yan Huo, Huijun Wang, Ning Dong, Shangjun Ma
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: To analyze the coupled behavior of integrated linear joints in precision robotics, this study develops a multibody dynamics framework to perform dynamic modeling and transmission characteristic analysis. The proposed model integrates the inverted planetary roller screw (IPRS) with a frameless motor, taking into account the centripetal excitations caused by rotor eccentricity and utilizing a continuous friction model. A set of global dynamic equations is established to comprehensively describe the radial, axial, and torsional interactions within the system. The results characterize the non-uniformity in internal load distribution and the coupling effects between radial and axial motions. Furthermore, a comprehensive analysis across time-domain, frequency-domain, and phase-space indicates that the joint maintains stability with quasi-periodic motion under complex excitation. This work establishes the fundamental connections between internal mechanical excitations and motion accuracy, providing a theoretical basis for refining the structure and precision control of integrated robotic actuators.

title: Vibrations of a Delta Robot in the Perspective of Energy Harvesting
authors: Michele Tonan, Matteo Bottin, Alberto Doria, Giulio Rosati
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Delta robots are characterized by large accelerations and vibrations, hence they are a benchmark case for the application of Vibration Energy Harvesting (VEH) technologies in the field of robotics. Experimental modal analysis is used to find the natural frequencies of the first modes of vibration of a Delta robot. The comparison with the frequency spectrum of the acceleration due to the planned motion shows that resonance phenomena are not present, and the robot experiences low frequency forced vibrations <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(f<20 ~\text{Hz}$</tex>) with harmonics that depend on the shape of the planned trajectory. A piezoelectric vibration harvester is designed to scavenge energy from such low-frequency forced vibrations. Experimental tests showed that the harvester is able to generate large voltages <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\approx 20 \mathbf{V}$</tex>) that by means of a proper converter will make it possible to feed sensor nodes.

title: Time-Optimal L1 Guidance for Straight-Line Path Following in 3D
authors: Pradyumn Mahajan, Sayantan Pal, Sikha Hota
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: This work presents a modified L1 guidance law that generates smooth and time-efficient trajectories for Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) to converge to straight-line paths in three-dimensional (3D) space. This approach employs the L1 guidance law to converge to the straight-line segment of the time-optimal but curvature-discontinuous 3D Dubins path before transitioning to the desired straight line path. In this way, this law significantly reduces the convergence time of L1 guidance while maintaining its curvature continuity (C2-continuous path) and respecting the curvature bounds defined by the minimum turn radius of the UAV. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed guidance law through comparisons with classical L1 guidance and Dubins paths.

title: FreeFix: Boosting 3D Gaussian Splatting via Fine-Tuning-Free Diffusion Models
authors: Hongyu Zhou, Zisen Shao, Sheng Miao, Pan Wang, Dongfeng Bai, Bingbing Liu, Yiyi Liao
abstract: Neural Radiance Fields and 3D Gaussian Splatting have advanced novel view synthesis, yet still rely on dense inputs and often degrade at extrapolated views. Recent approaches leverage generative models, such as diffusion models, to provide additional supervision, but face a tradeoff between generalization and fidelity: fine-tuning diffusion models for artifact removal improves fidelity but risks overfitting, while fine-tuning-free methods preserve generalization but often yield lower fidelity. We introduce FreeFix, a fine-tuning-free approach that pushes the boundary of this trade-off by enhancing extrapolated rendering with pretrained image diffusion models. We present an interleaved 2D-3D refinement strategy, showing that image diffusion models can be leveraged for consistent refinement without relying on costly video diffusion models. Furthermore, we take a closer look at the guidance signal for 2D refinement and propose a per-pixel confidence mask to identify uncertain regions for targeted improvement. Experiments across multiple datasets show that FreeFix improves multiframe consistency and achieves performance comparable to or surpassing fine-tuning-based methods, while retaining strong generalization ability. Our project page is at https://xdimlab.github.io/freefix.

title: Frequency-Aware Gaussian Splatting Decomposition
authors: Yishai Lavi, Leo Segre, Shai Avidan
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3D-GS) enables efficient novel view synthesis, but treats all frequencies uniformly, making it difficult to separate coarse structure from fine detail. Recent works have started to exploit frequency signals, but lack explicit frequency decomposition of the 3D representation itself. We propose a frequency-aware decomposition that organizes 3D Gaussians into groups corresponding to Laplacian-pyramid subbands of the input images. Each group is trained with spatial frequency regularization to confine it to its target frequency, while higher-frequency bands use signed residual colors to capture fine details that may be missed by lower-frequency reconstructions. A progressive coarse-to-fine training schedule stabilizes the decomposition. Our method achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality and rendering speed among all LOD-capable methods. In addition to improved interpretability, our method enables dynamic level-of-detail rendering, progressive streaming, foveated rendering, promptable 3D focus, and artistic filtering. Our code will be made publicly available.

title: Making Minimal Solvers Inverse-Free Using Null Space Computation
authors: Hassan Bozorgmanesh, Janne Heikkilä
abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel resultant-based method for solving polynomial systems of equations that are commonly encountered in computer vision, particularly as minimal problems. Unlike traditional algorithms that rely on matrix inversion, the primary merits of the proposed method are the numerical stability of its formulation and the lack of need to compute the inverse of a matrix by leveraging null space computations. Additionally, its formulation paves the way for more computations to be performed in the offline stage by using the sparsity of coefficient matrices, thereby reducing the computational load in the online stage. This inverse-free formulation is especially suited for sparse systems and offers improved robustness in scenarios where matrix inversion is unstable or infeasible. Experimental results demonstrate better accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods such as SRBM and GAPS across a variety of camera geometry problems.

title: Fillerbuster: Unified Generative Scene Completion Model for Casual Captures
authors: Ethan Weber, Norman Müller, Yash Kant, Vasu Agrawal, Michael Zollhöfer, Angjoo Kanazawa, Christian Richardt
abstract: We present Fillerbuster, a unified model that completes unknown regions of a 3D scene with a multi-view latent diffusion transformer. Casual captures are often sparse and miss surrounding content behind objects or above the scene. Existing methods are not suitable for this challenge as they focus on making known pixels look good with sparse-view priors, or on creating missing sides of objects from just one or two photos. In reality, we often have hundreds of input frames and want to complete areas that are missing and unobserved from the input frames. Our solution is to train a generative model that can consume a large context of input frames while generating unknown target views and recovering image poses when camera parameters are unknown. We show results where we complete partial captures on two existing datasets. We also present an uncalibrated scene completion task where our unified model predicts both poses and creates new content. We open-source our framework for integration into popular reconstruction platforms like Nerfstudio or Gsplat. We present a flexible, unified inpainting framework to predict many images and poses together, where all inputs are jointly inpainted, and it could be extended to predict more modalities such as depth.

title: Analysis on the Potential Key Feature of FSO in 5G
authors: Nayeema Rouf Nobonita, Faria Tarannum Rutba, Sabbir Ahmed Nahian, Ehtesham Al Shahriar Adnan, Bobby Barua
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Free-Space Optical (FSO) communication appears as a promising solution to fulfill high data rate, high bandwidth and low cost 5 G network secure transmission as it's free from Electromagnetic Interference (EI). This study examines the performance of different modulation schemes OOK, BPSK, QPSK, 8-PSK and M-ary PPM <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(M=2,4,8,16)$</tex> for link distance of 3000 m under moderate atmospheric turbulence condition of Bangladesh. A system model for each modulation form has been developed, accounting for fluctuations of intensity due to turbulence and additive white Gaussian noise. The atmospheric channel is designed using Gamma-Gamma distribution, both capturing small scales and large scale disturbance effects, while Rytov variance is used to determine the strength of disruption. Simulation results show that better efficiency and low bit failure speed of 16-PPM than other modulation schemes. These findings provide insight into the choice of 16-PPM for optimal modulation plans of reliable FSO connections at high speed 5G networks under moderate weather conditions.

title: Freshness Assessment of Fruits and Vegetables Using Dual Attention Vision Transformer
authors: Rupa Rani Paul, Rajkumar Biswas, Chinmoy Modak Turjo, Tonmoy Ghosh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Accurate assessment of produce freshness plays a critical role in maintaining food safety standards, ensuring quality assurance, and minimizing post-harvest economic losses. This research presents an automated classification framework utilizing a Dual Attention Vision Transformer (DaViT) architecture to tell the difference for identifying whether fruits and vegetables are fresh or decayed. The methodology leverages a comprehensive dataset comprising 28,189 images across 14 distinct fruit and vegetable categories, with each category containing of fresh and rotten classes, making it a total of 28 classes. The dataset distribution follows a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7 1 \% - 9 \% - 2 0 \%$</tex> divided for training, testing, and validation phases, respectively. The DaViT model undergoes fine-tuning procedures using pre-trained ImageNet weights as initialization parameters. Performance evaluation demonstrates exceptional classification capabilities with 99.04% accuracy, alongside 99% recall and 99% precision metrics. These results surpass the present advanced techniques methodologies in the domain. The proposed automated system reduces reliance on manual inspection processes while minimizing human-induced errors, offering a robust and scalable solution for integration into modern agricultural monitoring systems and intelligent food supply chain management frameworks.

title: Syntheto-PCOS: A Fairness-Aware Generative Learning Framework for Robust Assessment of Metabolic Dysfunction in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
authors: Syeda Zaina Rohana Sneha, Mumtahina Mozumder, Farida Akter Banu, Rafshia Mahbuba Ayshe
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) diagnosis using machine learning is often hindered by limited and imbalanced clinical data, leading to biased and unreliable models. This paper introduces Syntheto-PCOS, a fairness-aware framework that addresses this challenge through generative data augmentation. We employ a Conditional Tabular Generative Adversarial Network (CTGAN) to create high-fidelity synthetic data, effectively doubling our dataset and balancing the class distribution. A suite of five machine learning models was trained on this augmented data, with RandomForest and LightGBM optimized via Bayesian techniques. Our results show that the ensemble models achieved superior performance, with the optimized LightGBM model attaining an AUC of 0.893 and a clinically significant diagnostic recall of 68.8%. Feature importance and SHAP analysis confirmed that the model's predictions are driven by established clinical markers (e.g., follicle counts, AMH), ensuring both interpretability and clinical relevance. The Syntheto-PCOS framework demonstrates a robust methodology for developing accurate and trustworthy diagnostic tools in the face of common medical data limitations.

title: Identifying Patterns in Bangla Fake News Using Word2Vec and Attention Based CNN
authors: Rakibul Hasan Nirob, Jeba Wasima, Francis Rudra D Cruze, Fizar Ahmed, Md. Nasimul Kader
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: The rapid dissemination of misinformation poses a significant challenge to the integrity of online information, especially for low resource languages such as Bangla. Despite being spoken by millions, Bangla still lacks reliable systems for automated fake news detection. This work presents a novel framework that combines multiple feature extraction methods with deep learning (Dl) architectures to address this gap. We employ Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TFIDF), CountVectorizer(CV), Global Vectors for Word Representation(GloVe) and Word2Vec embeddings for text encoding and utilize an attention based Convolutional Neural Network (AttCNN) to enhance contextual understanding and capture subtle linguistic patterns. The proposed method demonstrates scalability for large scale datasets while maintaining strong classification performance. Through extensive experimentation, our model has been tested and proven robust with the Att-CNN using Word2Vec embeddings achieving state of the art accuracy of 98.42 % in Bangla fake news detection.

title: A Reputation Based Consensus Mechanism Inspired by Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)
authors: Takia Nawrin, Md. Yusuf, Maftun Ahommed, Fahmeda Hossain Orna, Iffat Jahan Kona, Nafees Mansoor
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Enhanced and transparent data exchange is crucial for enterprises when it comes to averting fraud and ensuring operational dependability. Efficient collaboration is usually compromised with centralized systems that are likely to be manipulated, susceptible to unauthorized access, and lacking in trust. Controlled, auditable transactions have been shown to offer a powerful solution to permissioned blockchains, with frameworks such as Hyperledger Fabric adding support to modular consensus and privacy preserving designs. Nevertheless, conventional consensus protocols, like Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) and Proof of Authority (PoA) are restricted in terms of scalability, centralization threats, and fixed selection of the leaders. The article presents a Reputation-Based Consensus Mechanism (RBCM), which is based on the ant colony optimization inspired clustering and dynamically changing reputation scoring. Blended with Hyperledger Fabric, RBCM evaluates node trustworthiness, dynamically chooses trustworthy leaders and alleviates malicious behavior. According to theoretical analysis, RBCM offers a higher level of security, scalability and fairness, which make it very suitable in enterprise applications.

title: Hybrid CNN Framework Leveraging VGG16 for Multi-Class Brain Tumor Classification
authors: MD. Emtiaz Ahmed, Md Sifat Ullah Sayem, Amjad Hossain Piash, Afroza Akter
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Brain tumors are a critical health issue with a significant impact worldwide. Their detection and classification are crucial for effective treatment. Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) serves as a reliable diagnostic tool for visualizing brain anomalies. To enhance diagnostic efficiency and reduce human error, automated computer-assisted approaches are essential. In this research, we introduce a hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model that combines features from a pre-trained VGG16 model and a custom CNN architecture to classify brain tumors using MRI images. The custom CNN is composed of several convolutional layers with increasing filter sizes. Two publicly available datasets were utilized: one from Kaggle, comprising 6,217 images across four classes, and another from Figshare, containing 3,064 images across three classes. Once the data was preprocessed and divided into training and testing sets, our proposed model was trained using a multi-input fusion approach. We obtained an accuracy, precision, recall, and f1-score of 95.12%, 93%, 94%, and 93%, respectively, from our proposed model using the Kaggle dataset and 95.31%, 95%, 97%, and 96% by using Figshare dataset. These results demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed model for reliable brain tumor diagnosis using MRI images.

title: Tariff-Sensitive and Seasonally Adaptive Sizing of an On-Grid Solar-Wind EV Charging Station in Coastal Bangladesh
authors: Taseen Rahman, Nadman Ahmed Chowdhury, Zannatul Musta, Murshida Rahman, Hamza Mubarak, A.S. Nazmul Huda, Shameem Ahmad
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This paper presents a tariff-sensitive and seasonally adaptive techno-economic analysis of an on-grid solar-wind electric vehicle (EV) charging station for the coastal region of Cox's Bazar, Bangladesh, using the HOMER Pro simulation tool. The study integrates Bangladesh's net-metering framework with two distinct EV-load profiles, representing the high tourist season and the low off-season, to assess how tariff variations and seasonal demand influence system design and cost. Multiple grid sellback rates ranging from 0.034 to 0.067 $ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$/ \text{kWh}$</tex>, were analysed under a fixed grid import rate of 0.087 $ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$/ \text{kWh}$</tex> to determine the least-cost configurations. Results indicate that both tariff and seasonal dynamics significantly impact system performance and the renewable fraction. During the optimal setup of 458 kW photovoltaic (PV) capacity with EO20 wind turbines achieved a levelized cost of energy (LCOE) of around $ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.041 / \text{kWh}$</tex> and a renewable fraction of 69 %, whereas the off-season condition favored a 344 kW PV system only with an LCOE of $ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.066 / \text{kWh}$</tex>. The findings emphasize the need for tariff-aware and seasonally responsive renewable energy sizing strategies to support cost-effective EV-charging infrastructure expansion in Bangladesh's coastal zones.

title: The Role of Model Architecture in Breast Cancer Classification: A Comparison of CNN and Transformer Paradigms on Ultrasound Data
authors: Siam Tahsin Bhuiyan, Halima Khatun, Samiul Karim Mazumder, Fatin Israq, Riyadul Islam, Rashedur Rahman, Ashraful Islam, Saadia Binte Alam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Breast cancer is the leading malignancy in women globally, and early detection is critical for improving survival. Deep learning has shown promise in medical image analysis, yet the effects of model architecture remain underexplored. This study conducts a comparative evaluation of four models: DenseNet121 (pure CNN), ConvNeXt (modernized CNN with transformer-inspired design), ViT-B/16 (pure Vision Transformer), and Swin-B (hierarchical transformer with CNN-like inductive biases) for breast ultrasound classification using the public BUSI dataset of 780 images. Results demonstrate that DenseNet121 exhibits limitations due to its reliance on local receptive fields, which restricts its ability to capture distributed pathological features. ConvNeXt outperforms DenseNet121 across all metrics, with 83% accuracy and 91% AUROC, highlighting the benefits of transformer-inspired refinements within CNNs. ViT-B/16 achieves the highest AUROC of 94%, underscoring the strength of global self-attention for capturing distributed pathological cues. Swin-B attains the most balanced performance, with 86% accuracy, 85% recall, and 0.92 AUROC, reflecting the effectiveness of combining global attention with hierarchical locality. These findings suggest a clear performance hierarchy, where hybrid transformer architectures such as Swin-B offer the most clinically reliable balance between sensitivity and specificity. Future research should explore hybrid models that integrate CNN inductive priors with transformer-based global reasoning to further enhance robustness in real-world diagnostic applications.

title: Vision Meets Convolution: A Hybrid Approach for Brain Tumor Classification from MRI Scans
authors: Abdur Rab Dhruba, Tasnim Jannat Yeamoni
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Due to the high diagnosis mortality, brain tumors represent a significant challenge in medical imaging. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) has become a widespread method for recognizing and monitoring brain tumors, but interpreting these scans remains a task that relies heavily on the expertise of a radiologist. This article presents a hybrid model that integrates Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with Vision Transformers (ViT) to improve the accuracy of classification of brain tumors from MRI scans. Unlike conventional hybrids that simply concatenate CNN and transformer features, the proposed hybrid model shows that high-level feature embeddings restructured by EfficientNetV2B0, enabling a lightweight global attention mechanism without the patch extraction step used in standard ViT Models. Train and evaluate the model using data records of 2622 MRI images, including glioma, meningioma, pituitary tumors, and no tumor categories. The hybrid approach achieved an impressive accuracy of 97.71 %, surpassing the traditional CNN model (96.80 %) and ViT model (85.58 %). Furthermore, the hybrid model showed a reduction in loss (0.15) compared to the other methods. This study highlights the effectiveness of a combination of local characterization extraction and global attentional mechanisms to improve the accuracy of tumor detection and provides a reliable tool for automated classification of brain tumors.

title: Agentic AI in Distributed SDN: Synchronization and Design Framework
authors: Rawan Alsheikh, Etimad Fadel, Nadine Akkari
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Large-scale Software-Defined Networking (SDN) deployments are increasingly adopting Physically Distributed but Logically Centralized (PDLC) control planes, in which multiple controllers must coordinate to maintain a consistent global view of the network. In practice, this creates a persistent trade-off between state consistency and synchronization overhead under limited control-plane budgets. Existing AI-based synchronization solutions often rely on centralized learning and decision components, which can reduce scalability and introduce single-point-of-failure-like dependence. This paper presents a design framework for adaptive synchronization in PDLC SDN from an agentic AI perspective. The objective is to express synchronization as a budget-aware decision process that can be learned by controller-resident agents while enabling cooperative learning without centralized run-time action selection. We model synchronization as a budget-aware Markov Decision Process (MDP) with explicit state, action, and reward definitions that capture staleness, constrained neighbor selection, and benefit-cost tradeoffs. Based on this formulation, we adopt Centralized Training and Decentralized Execution (CTDE): decentralized Actors make local synchronization decisions at run time, while a trainingtime Critic provides coordination signals to stabilize multi-agent learning. The framework is instantiated with Communication Multi-Agent Actor-Critic (C-MAAC) and extended with an Attention-Based Critic (ABC-MAAC), where attention suppresses low-impact inter-controller relations and emphasizes influential ones before value estimation. Complexity verification shows linear per-update scaling for C-MAAC and an additional quadratic interaction term for ABC-MAAC due to pairwise attention, while preserving decentralized, budget-aware run-time execution.

title: Evaluating Deep Learning and Ensemble Techniques for Cyberbullying Detection in Bengali Social Media with Feature Fusion
authors: Tasnim Begum Srity, Humaira Saida Tasnima, Ummul Humaira, Sayma Alam Suha
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Cyberbullying, broadly defined as harassment facilitated by digital and information communication technologies, has been identified as a prevalent and significant societal problem in the digital age. The spread of social media sites has eased online communication, but little has been done to detect cyberbullying in non English speaking languages especially Bengali language compared to English. Thus, this study investigates the detection of offensive Bengali text through a comparative analysis of machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), and ensemble models, including logistic regression, random forest, long shortterm memory (LSTM), and bidirectional LSTM (Bi-LSTM). An ensemble voting system is proposed here to label content as offensive or non-offensive in Bengali language to increase the general performance. The dataset comprises 43,998 comments from Bengali social media, sourced from the Cyberbullying Bangla dataset, which were subsequently preprocessed through cleaning, tokenization, stemming, and noise removal. Additionally, the data are transformed using ensemble feature extraction techniques, including TF-IDF, one-hot encoding, and Word2Vec. The models are then trained and tested on binary classification scenarios and LSTM and Bi-LSTM performed with the highest accuracy of 87.6 % with the ensemble model performing at 79 %. Comparisons were made of the measures of precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC area, which showed greater efficacy of deep learning strategies on large datasets. The contribution of this work is automated detection of cyberbullying in Bengali, which can be used to safeguard vulnerable users in local online communities.

title: Satellite-IoT Access Technologies and Simulation Frameworks in 6G Non-Terrestrial Networks: Limitations and Modeling Challenges
authors: Khaoula Kacem, Nawel Zangar, Soumaya Hamouda
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Non-Terrestrial Networks (NTNs) are expected to play a key role in sixth-generation (6G) systems by extending Internet of Things (IoT) connectivity beyond terrestrial coverage. Satellite-based IoT enables large-scale deployments in remote and underserved regions; however, it introduces structural challenges related to large coverage footprints, time-varying propagation conditions, and predominantly ALOHA-based uncoordinated random access under massive device density. This paper presents a structured survey of NTN-IoT access technologies and their associated simulation frameworks, focusing on scalability limitations and modeling constraints, particularly those induced by large-scale random access contention in satellite beams. Cellular and non-cellular solutions, including LoRaWAN Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) and Long Range Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (LR-FHSS), are comparatively analyzed in terms of access robustness and density-dependent performance trade-offs. Existing simulation tools are further examined with respect to their ability to represent NTN geometry, dynamic propagation behavior, large-scale random access contention, and waveform-specific mechanisms. The study highlights persistent fragmentation in current evaluation approaches and emphasizes the need for unified and reproducible modeling methodologies to enable fair performance comparison of future NTN-IoT systems.

title: ULTRARAM: An Emerging Memory Technology for NVM and Low-Power Neuromorphic Systems
authors: Abhishek Kumar, Peter D. Hodgson, James Ashforth-Pook, Manus Hayne, Avirup Dasgupta
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: ULTRARAM is an emerging memory technology exhibiting high endurance ($\gt10^{7} \mathbf{P} / \mathbf{E}$ cycles), ultra-high retention ($\gt1000$ years), and ultra-low switching energy per unit area. This compound-semiconductor-based non-volatile memory (NVM) works on the principle of triple-barrier resonant tunneling (TBRT) using InAs/AlSb heterostructures. Single memory cells have been fabricated on GaAs and Si substrates. A physicsbased compact model is proposed to accurately capture the realtime trapping/de-trapping of charges in the floating gate (FG) and utilized for synapse simulations. Benchmarking with other emerging memories highlight the promise of this technology as a next-generation non-volatile memory. Specifically, a circuit-level macro-model is employed to evaluate and benchmark the onchip learning performance in terms of area, latency, energy, and accuracy of an ULTRARAM synaptic core.

title: Accelerating Post-Quantum Cryptography via LLM-Driven Hardware-Software Co-Design
authors: Yuchao Liao, Tosiron Adegbija, Roman Lysecky
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Post-quantum cryptography (PQC) is crucial for securing data against emerging quantum threats. However, its algorithms are computationally complex and difficult to implement efficiently on hardware. In this paper, we explore the potential of Large Language Models (LLMs) to accelerate the hardware-software co-design process for PQC, with a focus on the FALCON digital signature scheme. We present a novel framework that leverages LLMs to analyze PQC algorithms, identify performance-critical components, and generate candidate hardware descriptions for FPGA implementation. We present the first quantitative comparison between LLM-driven synthesis and conventional HLS-based approaches for low-level compute-intensive kernels in FALCON, showing that human-in-the-loop LLM-generated accelerators can achieve up to $2.6 \times$ speedup in kernel execution time with shorter critical paths, while highlighting trade-offs in resource utilization and power consumption. Our results suggest that LLMs can minimize design effort and development time by automating FPGA accelerator design iterations for PQC algorithms, offering a promising new direction for rapid and adaptive PQC accelerator design on FPGAs.

title: Closing the Loop: Overcoming the Tool-Feedback Gap in Agentic Hardware Design
authors: Mohammadnavid Tarighat, Kevin Immanuel Gubbi, Neusha Javidnia, Noah Marosok, Soheil Zibakhsh-Shabgahi, Marcus Halm, Mahdi Pirayesh Shirazi Nejad, Setareh Rafatirad, Farinaz Koushanfar, Houman Homayoun
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: LLM-driven hardware design remains unreliable because it is largely open-loop, producing syntactically correct RTL without the iterative grounding provided by EDA tools. We refer to this disconnect as the Tool-Feedback Gap, which is further compounded by PPA blindness, where post-synthesis power, performance, and area constraints are ignored. This paper surveys emerging closed-loop, agentic EDA systems that integrate simulation and synthesis, and highlights a growing shift toward multi-agent orchestration with specialized planner, executor, judge, and debug roles to filter noisy tool outputs and mitigate context-switching failures. It further calls for benchmarks that measure convergence efficiency and PPA-aware optimization, rather than simple pass/fail simulation, and warns that autonomous feedback loops can amplify security risks such as vulnerability injection, hardware Trojans, and vacuous verification. These risks motivate the need for standardized tool interfaces, dynamic routing to specialized smaller models, and reasoning-based repair to ensure safe and reliable agentic hardware design.

title: Verifying Hardware Resource Isolation Using Mandatory Access Control
authors: Christopher Nokes, Kostas Amberiadis, D. Richard Kuhn, Edwards E. Reed, Michael Zuzak
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Resource isolation plays a crucial role in ensuring the security of hardware systems. Existing hardware security verification measures can identify improper isolation of hardware resources, but coverage is incomplete. As the search space increases with hardware size, the chances of an integrity flaw slipping through conventional verification methodologies also increase. In this work, we propose the application of Mandatory Access Control (MAC) models to the hardware resource isolation problem as an improvement to existing System-on-Chip (SoC) verification environments. To do so, we extend the definitions of subject and object, conventionally defined for the software domain, to a hardware context, allowing existing time-tested MAC policy models and verification methodologies to be directly applied to test the isolation of shared hardware resources. Using these definitions, we frame hardware isolation as a MAC problem and adopt the Biba interpretation of the Bell-LaPadula (BLP) model for hardware resource isolation. We use this model to propose a security verification methodology, in which subject-to-object interactions are defined as transactions, and are verified with respect to a security policy. Transactions that violate this security policy constitute a hardware isolation violation. We then use this transaction-based methodology to implement SystemVerilog-based verification environments for three open-source RISC-V cores. These environments are used to discover six instances of improper resource isolation (one novel, two previously documented, and three artificial test cases) with a simulation runtime overhead of less than $10 \%$. These violations constitute security vulnerabilities in hardware, which a malicious user could exploit to access or modify memory regions or hardware peripherals that should be isolated, thereby violating trust.

title: AdPrST:An Adversarial Graph Deep Learning Pre-clustering Framework for Deciphering Spatiotemporal Structures in Spatially Resolved Transcriptomics
authors: Haoyuan Ma, Shensi Huang, Haiyun Wang, Jianping Zhao, Junfeng Xia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: Spatially Resolved Transcriptomics (SRT) has revolutionized our understanding of gene expression within tissue microenvironments, yet accurately deciphering spatiotemporal structures—encompassing spatial domain identification, trajectory inference, and pseudo-spatiotemporal map construction—in complex tissues remains a formidable challenge. AdPrST begins with a pre-clustering process on gene expression data to establish initial domain groupings. It then constructs dual-view graph structures using K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) for local similarities and r-radius for broader spatial contexts. Through adversarial self-supervised contrast, leveraging Wasserstein distance-based GANs and contrastive learning, AdPrST generates robust low dimensional embeddings for each view. These embeddings are fused via a dot-product attention mechanism, guided by pre clustering labels, to achieve accurate spatial domain identification. Benchmarking across multiple datasets demonstrated AdPrST's superior performance over state-of-the-art methods, highlighting its potential to advance spatial transcriptomics research by elucidating spatial functional patterns and developmental trajectories. In particular, AdPrST excels in infer encing spatiotemporal structures, reconstructing developmental sequences and temporal features in complex tissues.

title: Direction of Arrival Estimation for Linear Arrays via Taylor-Expanded Polynomial Root Finding
authors: Lu Li, Maoshen Jia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: We propose a direction of arrival (DOA) estimation method for linear arrays via Taylor-expanded polynomial root finding. Based on Taylor expansion, we represent the array signal as an infinite Taylor series across microphones. Inspired by truncation analysis from spherical harmonic expansion, we derive a closed-form upper bound on the truncation order, enabling a finite-term approximation with controllable relative error. Within this framework, we extend some conventional narrowband DOA estimation methods—including steered response power with phase transform, minimum variance distortionless response (MVDR), multiple signal classification (MUSIC), and the propagator method—by developing polynomial root-finding versions. These extensions allow direct calculation of source directions for narrowband (single frequency) case without constructing spatial spectra and per forming peak searches. Furthermore, for broadband signals, we propose a kernel density estimation–based broadband spatial spectrum construction method, which can integrate source directions calculated at different frequencies. Broadband DOA estimation is then achieved by searching for the peaks of the spatial spectrum. Finally, simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms some existing methods in DOA estimation performance for compact linear arrays under low-to-moderate reverberation conditions.

title: Challenges of the DTT ECH Launchers and Options Under Study
authors: Alessandro Bruschi, Massimiliano Bonesso, Daniele Busi, Carlo Cesari, Roman Chomicz, Razvan Dima, Francesco Fanale, Pierluigi Fanelli, Giacomo Favero, Eleonora Gajetti, Saul Garavaglia, Gustavo Granucci, Alfredo Pagliaro, Adriano Pepato, Paola Platania, Natale Rispoli, Afra Romano, Alessandra Salvitti, Laura Savoldi, Alessandro Simonetto, Matteo Turcato, Fabio Zanon
abstract: The electron cyclotron heating (ECH) launchers for the Divertor Tokamak Test (DTT) facility are currently being designed. The aim is to inject power in different plasma locations with high flexibility to allow plasma current ramp-up and ramp-down, central heating with tailored deposition profiles, and neoclassical tearing modes (NTMs) stabilization. Two antennas are developed, one for the equatorial port with six beam lines and one for the upper port with two beam lines, with similar front-steerable mirror modules. Each beam-line module is composed of a cooled corrugated waveguide launching a beam toward a fixed shaping mirror (M1) and then to the plasma-facing plane mirror (M2), movable around two axes, both cooled to sustain the high heat load due to the ohmic losses of the incident 1 MW beam and the plasma radiation. Thermal loads also include plasma and microwave stray radiation. Main structural loads on the mirrors and their supports, including the launcher supporting baseplate, are forces and torques due to the induced electro-magnetic (EM) currents, during normal operation and disruptions, interacting with the high magnetic field in which the mirrors and supports are immersed. Thermal and structural challenges, in particular on the M1 and M2 mirrors, have opposite solutions that require a delicate trade-off: optimal cooling requires high-thermal-conductivity materials such as copper alloys, while low-electrically-conductive materials are needed to have lower induced currents during disruptions. Solutions under study for the mirror cooling for minimal deformations and stresses, and for drive mechanisms, are presented. The possible reduction of induced currents during disruptions using different materials, supporting structures, and layouts is discussed.

title: Multichannel Reflectometer for Core-Localized Electron Density Fluctuation Measurement on the HL-3 Tokamak
authors: P. W. Shi, Z. C. Yang, W. Chen, L. W. Hu, A. S. Liang, M. Jiang, Z. B. Shi
abstract: A novel multichannel microwave reflectometer has been developed for core-localized density fluctuation measurement on the HL-3 tokamak. The microwave diagnostic is characterized by having 16 independent microwave sources, achieving beam separation or combination through a multiplexer technique, and optimizing signal quality via remote control of amplifiers. Sixteen working points are arranged in a wide frequency region of 50–75 GHz, so that the microwave diagnostic has a large measurement range. Depending on detail requirement of tokamak operation, the reflectometer can work with ordinary or extraordinary waves. It is sensitive to density fluctuation and the corresponding maximum measurable wavenumber reaches <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2.16~\rm cm^{-1}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Recently, the microwave reflectometer has been installed on HL-3 tokamak and utilized to measure the spatial location of pressure gradient-driven kinetic ballooning modes.

title: Fusing Electromagnetic Physics and Deep Learning for Intelligent Prediction of Vacuum Degree in Circuit Breakers
authors: Xian Cheng, Yadong Yu, Guowei Ge, Xiangyang Wang
abstract: Vacuum degree is a key parameter determining the breaking and insulation performance of vacuum circuit breakers (VCBs). Its deterioration over time can lead to performance failure. Therefore, achieving high-precision online monitoring of the vacuum degree is essential for ensuring the operational reliability of VCBs. This study employs electromagnetic radiation waves generated by vacuum discharge as indirect indicators of vacuum degree and investigates their physical mechanism and detectable lower limits under different conditions. On this basis, a classification model integrating temporal convolutional network (TCN), bidirectional gated recurrent unit (BiGRU), and an attention mechanism is developed to learn discharge event features from the waveforms and classify internal pressure states. Experimental results show that the method supports vacuum degree monitoring from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.0\times 10^{-3}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> to 10 Pa, achieves 92% precision, and outperforms support vector machine (SVM), kernel extreme learning machine (KELM), and back propagation neural network (BP-NN). This approach provides a practical and efficient solution for real-time condition monitoring of VCBs.

title: Insight Into the Insight Toolkit
authors: Terry S. Yoo
abstract: As a federally funded initiative, the Insight Toolkit (ITK) is intended to make a lasting software investment for biomedical image computing, enabling our communities with open-source algorithms for image segmentation and registration. Its 25-year success has been the result of a combination of carefully integrated factors: executive support, project management expertise, user involvement and skilled developers, the adoption of agile processes and tools, and maintaining clear business objectives in our execution. This history of ITK covers these factors, from the inception of the toolkit through its agile implementation and continuous testing and integration. The story includes motivations and fortunate enabling opportunities and ends with summary advice on how to succeed in creating a successful project of open-source software.

title: Robust Real-Time Brake Gap Estimation for EMB Systems Via Legendre-Based Two-Stage MFFRLS With Adaptive M-Estimator_supp1-3692736.pdf
authors: Wenpeng Wei
abstract: Brake gap is important in achieving precise force control and ensuring reliability of electromechanical brake (EMB) systems in autonomous vehicles. However, it is typically unmeasurable and time-varying due to progressive brake pad wear. This article proposes a Legendre-based multiple forgetting factors recursive least-squares (MFFRLS) with adaptive M-estimator algorithm for robust real-time brake gap estimation. Specifically, the estimation problem is first formulated into a regression problem inspired by the EMB data relationship. Then, Legendre basis is adopted for regressor transformation to improve estimation stability. After that, the MFFRLS with adaptive M-estimator is proposed to enhance robustness of brake gap estimation across the full life cycle. A two-stage estimation strategy is further designed to reduce computational burden. Estimation convergence analyses are established using Hessian matrix and Lyapunov theory. Comparison studies using green brake pad against standard recursive least squares, extended Kalman filter, Legendre-based RLS, and MFFRLS approaches substantiate the high estimation precision of the proposed algorithm, with steady-state error to be 0.51, 1.42, 0.54, 0.43, and 0.03 rad, respectively. Further validations show the root-mean-square error and relative RMSE of estimation are less than 0.20 rad and 2.99<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$e^{-4}$</tex-math></inline-formula>% across green, moderate and severely worn brake pads, demonstrating the adaptiveness and robustness of the proposed algorithm.

title: Programmable In-Network Aggregation for Communication-Aware Federated Learning in 5G RANs
authors: Emilio Paolini, Andrea Pinto, Luca Valcarenghi, Flavio Esposito
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data, making it attractive for privacy-preserving applications at the wireless edge. However, when executed over real 5G networks, FL performance degrades due to uplink congestion, heterogeneous client capabilities, and intermittent connectivity. Most existing approaches attempt to mitigate these issues indirectly by optimizing clients (through adaptive participation, local training, or selection strategies) or by optimizing models (via pruning, quantization, or compression), but they ignore potential network bottlenecks. This paper introduces FLAG, an FL architecture that embeds innetwork aggregation directly into 5G gNodeBs, transforming the network into an active participant in the learning process. In particular, FLAG performs parameter aggregation at line rate within the 5G Service Data Adaptation Protocol layer and incorporates three mechanisms: Partial-Contribution Correction for loss-tolerant averaging, a timer-driven pipeline for real-time scheduling, and a deadline-based grouping strategy to mitigate stragglers. Experiments with realistic wireless emulation show that FLAG achieves up to 5.1× faster time-to-accuracy and maintains accuracy within 0.8% of a loss-free baseline, while reducing gNB-to-server bandwidth by aggregating pergNB rather than per-client. FLAG requires no modifications to clients or the parameter server, demonstrating how 5G-aware system design can make federated learning scalable, efficient, and resilient under real-world wireless conditions.

title: NL-DPE: An Analog In-memory Non-Linear Dot Product Engine for Efficient CNN and LLM Inference
authors: Lei Zhao, Luca Buonanno, Archit Gajjar, John Moon, Aishwarya Natarajan, Sergey Serebryakov, Ron M. Roth, Xia Sheng, Youtao Zhang, Paolo Faraboschi, Jim Ignowski, Giacomo Pedretti
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Resistive Random Access Memory (RRAM) based in-memory computing (IMC) accelerators offer significant performance and energy advantages for deep neural networks (DNNs), but face three major limitations: (1) they support only static dot-product operations and cannot accelerate arbitrary non-linear functions or data-dependent multiplications essential to modern LLMs; (2) they demand large, power-hungry analog-to-digital converter (ADC) circuits; and (3) mapping model weights to device conductance introduces errors from cell nonidealities. These challenges hinder scalable and accurate IMC acceleration as models grow. We propose NL-DPE, a Non-Linear Dot Product Engine that overcomes these barriers. NL-DPE augments crosspoint arrays with RRAM-based Analog Content Addressable Memory (ACAM) to execute arbitrary non-linear functions and data-dependent matrix multiplications in the analog domain by transforming them into decision trees, fully eliminating ADCs. To address device noise, NL-DPE uses software-based Noise Aware Fine-tuning (NAF), requiring no in-device calibration. Experiments show that NL-DPE delivers 28× energy efficiency and 249× speedup over a GPU baseline, and 22× energy efficiency and 245× speedup over existing IMC accelerators, while maintaining high accuracy.

title: DSF-DETR: End-to-End SAR Ship Detection via Spatial-Frequency State Space Modeling and Dynamic Attention
authors: Yunqi Zhang, Lin Bai, Wenqing Zhou, Danni Xue, Wentao Liu, Meng Hui
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) ship detection sup ports maritime surveillance, but remains difficult due to multiplicative speckle, complex coastal clutter, and large-scale variation in ship size. CNN-based detectors often trade noise suppression for detail and typically rely on post-processing such as non-maximum suppression (NMS). To overcome these limitations, we propose DSF-DETR, an end-to-end Transformer detector that decouples spatial and frequency processing to explicitly disentangle noise-sensitive fluctuations from target structures. Its Decoupled Spatial-Frequency Backbone (DSF-Net) extracts local geometry in a spatial branch, while a frequency branch combines a Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) with State Space Models (SSMs) to aggregate global context and suppress noise sensitive components. To avoid redundant computation over homogeneous background, Content-Aware Dynamic Attention (CADA) sparsifies the encoder, and an Efficient Re-parameterized Feature Pyramid (ERFP) strengthens multi-scale fusion and is re-parameterized into a single-branch structure for inference. Experiments on SSDD, HRSID and the SAR-Ship-Dataset show that DSF-DETR improves AP50 over RT-DETR by 2.0, 2.3, and 1.4 points, respectively, while reducing the parameters by 44.5% in SSDD. On the NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX, the re parameterized model delivers a 53.3% FPS gain. Our code will be available at https://github.com/CHD-IPAC/DSF-DETR.

title: Generative AI for Electromagnetic Metamaterials and Metasurfaces: A Comprehensive Review of Deep Learning, Evolutionary, and Physics-Informed Approaches
authors: Sumaia Jahan Mishu, Yaser Mike Banad, Sarah Sharif
abstract: The rapid advancement of generative learning has transformed the inverse design of electromagnetic (EM) metamaterials and metasurfaces by enabling more efficient, adaptive, and high-performance design workflows. However, existing surveys often discuss data-driven EM design in broad terms, while a unified review focused specifically on generative design pipelines across deep generative models, search-based and physics-driven optimization methods, and hybrid solver-in-the-loop frameworks remains limited. This review provides a comprehensive analysis of generative-learning-based methodologies for EM metamaterials and metasurfaces.We organize the field into three major groups: deep generative models, including generative adversarial networks, variational autoencoders, autoregressive models, diffusion models, and normalizing-flow-based approaches; search-based and physics-driven optimization methods, including genetic algorithms, reinforcement learning, and topology optimization; and hybrid pipelines that combine these paradigms to improve physical fidelity, constraint handling, and deployability. Beyond surveying these method families, we examine how practical considerations such as dataset requirements, computational cost, validation strategy, and fabrication feasibility shape their real-world applicability. Finally, we discuss emerging trends, open challenges, and future directions for generative inverse design in next-generation EM metamaterials and metasurfaces.

title: Chaotic Dynamics Forced to Evolve Outside Sphere with Positive Divergence
authors: Jiri Petrzela
abstract: This paper contributes to discovering a new autonomous third-order deterministic dynamical system that is chaotic and exhibits some predefined unique properties. Mentioned uniqueness belongs to feature that stands hidden from the viewpoint of external observations: specific shaping of vector field divergence. Positive divergence inside a sphere with predefined radius forces strange attractor to evolve mostly outside these places. For novel mathematical model derived, it will be demonstrated that chaos is neither numerical artifact nor long transient motion. To prove robustness and correctness of numerical analysis, the dynamical system will be designed as a lumped electronic circuit and practically constructed. Finally, experimental measurement concludes a good correspondence between theory and practice.

title: Additively Manufactured X-Band Diplexer using Laser Beam Powder Bed Fusion Technique
authors: Aleksandr Voronov, Vicente Boria, Lluc Sempere, Mirko Kunowsky, Carmen Bachiller
abstract: This work presents the additive manufacturing of an X-Band diplexer composed of two filters: one centered at 9.5 GHz and another at 10.5 GHz, both with a bandwidth of 250 MHz, plus a power divider. The whole set was implemented in rectangular waveguide technology, manufactured in a single block. Good agreement between simulated and measured results is achieved, with return losses (RL) lower than 20 dB, insertion losses (IL) around 0.3 dB in both filters, and isolation of 30 dB between them. For demonstration purposes, the set was used for the characterization in anechoic chamber of the directivity, gain and radiation efficiency of an additive manufactured horn antenna. The diplexer was implemented using Powder Bed Fusion Laser Beam, (PBF-L/M) technology on a metal alloy (AlSi10Mg). The horn antenna was manufactured via Vat Photopolymerization by exposure to Masked UV light (VPP-UVM) technology, using a polymeric resin (Gray from Applylabwork), subsequently metallized. Mechanical optimization for the additive manufacturing of the design allows the development of an ergonomic single-part device, enabling perfectly squared inner cavities. This reduces weight, volume, and manufacturing waste, while maintaining the electromagnetic response of the diplexer.

title: Complementary Standard and Inverted Power Normalized Gammachirp Cepstral Approach for Noise-Robust Speaker Identification
authors: Youssef Zouhir, Mohamed Zarka, Lilia El Amraoui, Kais Ouni
abstract: Despite the natural ability of humans to recognize speakers in noisy environments, automatic speaker recognition systems still face significant performance decrease under such conditions. This paper introduces a complementary feature extraction approach that concatenates the “inverted Power Normalized Gammachirp Cepstral” (invPNGC) coefficients with the original PNGC coefficients in order to enhance speaker recognition robustness in challenging acoustic environments. The invPNGC approach integrates two key innovations: (1) an “Inverted Gammachirp FilterBank” (invGcFB) using an “inverted Equivalent Rectangular Bandwidth” (invERB) rate scale to provide high resolution at high frequencies, effectively capturing speaker-specific consonantal information often de-emphasized by traditional methods, and (2) a “Noise Suppression Module” (NSM) inspired by human auditory masking. Furthermore, the concatenation of the two coefficients yields the cmpPNGC to capture a comprehensive spectral representation. Comprehensive experiments on the TIMIT database mixed with AURORA noise types demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms baseline techniques (PNGC, PNCC, MFCC, FDLP, and FDLP-Mel) using “Gaussian Mixture Model-Universal Background Model” (GMM-UBM) for speaker modeling. For instance, in Exhibition noise, cmpPNGC, invPNGC and PNGC achieved an average recognition accuracy of 77.38%, 73.87% and 66.75% (0-30dB SNR) respectively, compared to 50.35% for PNCC, 48.43% for MFCC, 49.96% for FDLP, and 36.45% for FDLP-Mel. These results confirm the efficacy of prioritizing high-frequency spectral cues for robust speaker identification. These findings suggest that the cmpPNGC feature offers a reliable solution for biometric authentication and forensic voice analysis systems deployed in challenging noise conditions.

title: ISAC Channel Measurements and Closed-Loop Modeling with Bidirectional Parameter Coupling in Industrial IoT Scenarios
authors: Zengyao Bi, Xi Liao, Li Wang, Xiangquan Zheng, Yang Wang, Jie Zhang
abstract: With the advent of 6G networks, integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) is a key enabling technology for native perception in complex industrial Internet of Things (IoT) environments, which demands accurate ISAC channel models. However, most existing models mainly capture correlation at the cluster level, making it difficult to characterize finer-grained parameter relationships and intra-cluster variations, and lack an effective mechanism for bidirectional parameter coupling between communication and sensing channels in industrial IoT scenarios. This paper proposes a shared-cluster geometry-based stochastic ISAC (SC-GBSM-ISAC) channel model for industrial IoT. The model is parameterized using channel measurements at 38 GHz and 132 GHz in a high-clutter indoor factory (InF (A)) scenario. Compared with existing ISAC channel models, the proposed model has the following features. First, a closed-loop “sensing–communication–sensing” channel modeling framework is constructed based on 3GPP TR 38.901, which integrates bidirectional parameter interaction between communication and sensing channels. Second, by selectively reusing sensing-target clusters to construct shared clusters for communication-channel modeling, the model introduces a physically consistent shared-scattering structure while preserving the statistical characteristics specified in 3GPP TR 38.901. Third, by combining scatterer information inferred from the communication channel with a scenario-dependent sensing probability model, the sensing background component is reconstructed. Finally, measurement-simulation comparisons in an independent low-clutter InF (B) scenario demonstrate that the proposed model effectively reproduces bidirectional parameter coupling and primary spatio-temporal statistical metrics at 38/132 GHz, validating its accuracy and cross-scenario consistency in the measured scenarios.

title: EvTurb: Event Camera Guided Turbulence Removal
authors: Yixing Liu, Minggui Teng, Yifei Xia, Peiqi Duan, Boxin Shi
abstract: Atmospheric turbulence degrades image quality by introducing spatially varying blur and geometric tilt distortions, posing significant challenges to downstream computer vision tasks including segmentation and recognition. Existing single-image and multi-frame methods struggle with the highly ill-posed nature of this problem due to the compositional complexity of turbulence-induced distortions. To address this, we propose EvTurb, an event guided turbulence removal framework that leverages high-speed event streams to decouple blur and tilt effects. EvTurb uses event-derived cues in a novel two-step event-guided network: event integrals are first employed as empirical guidance to reduce blur in the coarse outputs. This is followed by employing a variance map, derived from raw event streams, to eliminate the tilt distortion for the refined outputs. Additionally, we present TurbEvent, the first real-captured dataset featuring diverse turbulence scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that EvTurb surpasses state-of-the-art methods while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Image Segmentation With Large Language Models: A Survey With Perspectives for Intelligent Transportation Systems
authors: Sanjeda Akter, Ibne Farabi Shihab, Anuj Sharma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) with computer vision is profoundly transforming perception tasks like image segmentation. For intelligent transportation systems (ITS), where accurate scene understanding is critical for safety and efficiency, this new paradigm offers unprecedented capabilities. This survey systematically reviews the emerging field of LLM-augmented image segmentation, focusing on its applications, challenges, and future directions within ITS. We provide a taxonomy of current approaches based on their prompting mechanisms and core architectures, and we highlight how these innovations can enhance road scene understanding for autonomous driving, traffic monitoring, and infrastructure maintenance. Finally, we identify key challenges, including real-time performance and safety-critical reliability, and outline a perspective centered on explainable, human-centric AI as a prerequisite for the successful deployment of this technology in next-generation transportation systems.

title: IEEE Systems, Man, and Cybernetics Society Information
authors: N/A

title: Longitudinal Feature Disentanglement With Cross-Time Contrastive Learning for Alzheimer's Disease Diagnosis
authors: Dong Dai, Na Zhang, Hao Zhang, Yan Tang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Longitudinal structural Magnetic Resonance Imaging (sMRI) is pivotal for capturing dynamic brain atrophy patterns associated with the progression of Alzheimer's Disease (AD). However, effectively leveraging longitudinal data remains challenging due to the difficulty in modeling complex inter-temporal correlations and the presence of inherent noise and redundancy. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel Longitudinal Feature Disentanglement Network for AD diagnosis and progression prediction. Specifically, we design a lightweight 3D convolutional encoder with a weight-sharing strategy to extract spatial representations from dual-time-point images. A Variational Autoencoder (VAE)-based mechanism is then introduced to disentangle these representations into stable features that encapsulate disease-specific identity and marginal features that account for noise and irrelevant variations. To further enhance the disentanglement capability, we incorporate a cross-time-point contrastive learning mechanism to enforce the temporal consistency of stable features, alongside a longitudinal feature reconstruction module that ensures the preservation of comprehensive information by recombining decoupled features. Extensive experiments conducted on the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) dataset demonstrate that our proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both AD diagnosis and MCI-to-AD conversion prediction tasks. Furthermore, visualization analysis validates the model's effectiveness in isolating discriminative brain regions and interpreting the decision-making process.

title: Real-Time Seat-Based Sitting Action Recognition Using Soft Pressure Sensors and Embedded Machine Learning_supp1-3693752.mp4
authors: Chi Cuong Vu
abstract: Seat-based sitting behavior monitoring is an important research area due to its impact on spinal health, work performance, and long-term comfort. However, challenges remain in accuracy, stability, cost, deployment flexibility, and data privacy. This paper proposes an integrated system combining soft pressure sensors, embedded machine learning, and a flexible communication architecture. The pressure sensor, fabricated from conductive textile and Au film, achieves a sensitivity of up to 0.46 kPa⁻¹ with response/recovery times of approximately 60/50 ms. The compact machine learning model (4.34 KB) achieves 99.7% accuracy in recognizing six sitting actions, including trunk tilting and sitting transitions. All processing and inference are performed locally on the embedded board, with only classification results transmitted to the cloud to enhance data security. The system focuses on trunk tilt and sitting transition recognition rather than full-body ergonomic posture assessment. These results demonstrate the feasibility of real-time, resource-efficient monitoring of sitting actions on edge IoT devices. This pilot study used data from one participant, with a fixed chair and sensor setup in a single recording session. Therefore, the results show feasibility under controlled conditions, not generalizability across subjects.

title:
Vision-Enhanced Vertical PM
                    <sub>2.5</sub>
                    Monitoring Using Mobile Network-Based UAV Platforms and Generative AI Inference Interpretation
authors: Ang-Hsun Tsai, Chao-Yang Lee, Feng-Cheng Tsai, Chen-Hao Li
abstract: Traditional air quality monitoring is often constrained by fixed spatial deployments and the logistical costs of specialized sensors. This paper presents an intelligent mobile-network-enabled UAV platform for vertical air quality sensing, utilizing a dual-stage analytical framework: quantitative vision-based inference and generative AI-driven interpretation. The platform extracts atmospheric transmittance via the Dark Channel Prior (DCP) algorithm, which is then fused with meteorological parameters. To address the complex non-linear coupling of atmospheric variables, a Random Forest Regression (RFR) model is optimized through 7-dimensional polynomial feature engineering and deployed on a 5G Core Network virtual host for real-time estimation. To enhance data interpretability, the platform integrates Google’s Gemini AI for semantic inference interpretation, contextualizing vertical pollution profiles within public health frameworks and synthesizing localized causal hypotheses. Experimental results validate the model’s efficacy, achieving a Coefficient of Determination (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) of 0.954 and a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of only 1.27 μg/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>. This research establishes a scalable, low-latency ecosystem for 3D spatial air quality characterization, providing a robust framework for smart city environmental governance.

authors: N/A
