title: Subspace Knowledge for Spectral Compressed Sensing_supp1-3697412.pdf
authors: Hongbin Li
abstract: We consider the problem of recovering a spectrally sparse signal from its partially observed entries. Traditional approaches leverage the low-rank property of such signals in the Hankel lifting domain. In this domain, we further incorporate subspace knowledge, initially estimated from test data and updated during signal recovery, to enhance performance. To quantify the impact of subspace knowledge on this enhancement, we propose a metric that integrates QR decompositions with the subspace direction number to assess its accuracy, as reflected in the recovery guarantee. The established guarantee demonstrates the local optimality of the proposed nonconvex method for signal recovery. Based on the local optimality, the proposed method achieves exact recovery with high probability, provided that the initial subspace estimate is sufficiently accurate to meet certain conditions. Compared to prior methods without subspace knowledge and those that exploit such knowledge, our method reduces the sample complexity by three and two logarithmic factors, respectively. Furthermore, we employ lowrank factorization techniques to incorporate prior information of the Hankel matrix rank, thereby enhancing the recovery precision and computational efficiency of the proposed method. The proposed method is efficiently optimized in an alternating minimization framework. Experimental results demonstrate the superior recovery precision and reduced sample complexity of the proposed method compared to existing methods, as well as its enhanced performance through low-rank factorization.

title: An IEEE802.15.4a/z/ab compatible IR-UWB 2TRX with full-duplex radar sensing and aliasing suppressing semi-synchronous TX
authors: Anoop Narayan Bhat, Erwin Allebes, Elbert Bechthum, Zule Xu, Stan Van Der Ven, Mohieddine El Soussi, Johan Van Den Heuvel, Paul Van Zeijl, Paul Mateman, Murat Eskiyerli, Stefano Traferro, Amirashkan Farsaei, Evgenii Tiurin, Sahan Gamage, Pepijn Boer, Igor Petrov, Jochem Govers, Huib Visser, Martijn Hijdra, Sharada Prasad Shantharam, Ivo Marques, Gururaja Kasanadi Ramachandra, Kaijie Ding, Ran Li, Aasish Boora, Peter Vis, Mario Konijnenburg, Arjan Breeschoten, Peng Zhang, Yao-Hong Liu, Christian Bachmann
abstract: This letter presents an 802.15.4ab/a/z compatible IR-UWB 2TRX highlighting a full-duplex-based radar, a semi-synchronous TX and TRX’s digital baseband. A capacitive tuning technique proposed in the electrical balance duplexer (EBD)-based duplex RF front-end (RF-FE) improves TX-antenna insertion loss by 1.4dB and the sensitivity of TX-RX isolation by 3.4dB. The TRX achieves >30dB isolation while transmitting 500MHz pulses centered at 8GHz over 2VSWR antenna variation. The proposed asynchronous first-order-hold (FoH) switched-capacitor power amplifier (SCPA) with a delay-locked-loop (DLL)-based calibration in TX achieves <−50dBr alias sidelobes, 5dB lower compared to the state of the art (SoTA). The digital baseband supports next generation 802.15.4ab’s sensing packets and high data rate (HDR) mode. The 2TRX is implemented on a 22nm CMOS process.

title: A 65-nm Fully Integrated Reconfigurable Charge Pump With Joint Optimization of Conduction and Switching Losses for Micro-Energy Harvesting Applications
authors: Shi Kai Yau, Harikrishnan Ramiah, Kishore Kumar Pakkirisami Churchill, Andrea Ballo, Tian Siang Ho, Yong Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This paper presents a fully integrated dual-loss-optimized reconfigurable charge pump for ultra-low-power energy harvesting applications. Conventional charge pumps typically optimize either the conduction loss or switching loss, leading to degradation of efficiency outside the narrow operating region. To overcome this limitation, the proposed architecture jointly optimizes both loss mechanisms through three synergistic circuit techniques. Firstly, a capacitor-free dynamic PMOS array ring oscillator adaptively scales the switching frequency to suppress unnecessary switching loss. Secondly, an adaptive pathway selection charge pump dynamically modulates the effective transistor widths to maintain efficient charge transfer from subthreshold to above-threshold operation. Thirdly, a compact successive-stage NMOS gate booster micro-cell achieves local enhancement of the gate-to-source drive of the NMOS charge transfer switches to reduce the conduction loss at low input voltages. Collectively, these techniques mitigate the dominant loss mechanism across different operating regimes, thereby enabling efficient operation over an extended power dynamic range. Fabricated in a 65-nm CMOS process, the prototype occupies an area of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$359\times 809~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m with a total on-chip capacitance of 313 pF, including the pumping, boosting, and load capacitors. Measurement results demonstrate a peak power conversion efficiency of 67.59% at a minimum input voltage of 180 mV under a load of 800 k<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Omega $</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Finally, the proposed charge pump provides a highly efficient and compact power management solution, well-suited for autonomous and self-sustained operation in micro-energy harvesting applications.

title: A Full-Digital Heterogeneous Computing-in-Memory With Dual-Bit Serialized Weight Stream and Ping-Pong Computing for Efficient Transformer Acceleration
authors: Dengfeng Wang, Liukai Xu, Shuai Yuan, Weifeng He, Guanghui He, Fengbin Tu, Xueqing Li, Yanan Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have demonstrated remarkable performance across various computer vision tasks. However, the self-attention mechanism in transformers incurs high processing loads and costly off-chip data movement in conventional von Neumann architectures. This paper proposes a full-digital heterogeneous Computing-in-Memory (CIM) in-situ hybridizing differential RRAM and SRAM to enable efficient on-chip weight storage and flexible row-column dual-directional access for efficient transformer acceleration. A Ping-Pong computing dataflow is introduced to maximize the throughput density by allocating static and dynamic computations across CIM macros. A low-toggle weight tuning algorithm is proposed to minimize the dynamic power under the dual-bit-serial CIM scheme by introducing only small weight rounding distance. Besides, a patch-channel skip mechanism is used to reduce the redundant computing for dynamic matrix with high sparsity. Experimental results on different transformer models show that the proposed DBS-CIM architecture reduces layer-wise computing energy by up to 59.9% compared to conventional digital CIM. Compared to prior homogeneous CIM designs, it achieves $2.0\times $ to $3.3\times $ higher end-to-end energy efficiency, and outperforms existing heterogeneous CIM designs by $2.13\times $ in computational density.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: Accurate Lowpass-to-Bandpass Frequency Transformation for Distributed-Element Resonator Filter Design
authors: Juseop Lee, Keebaek Lee
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: In this letter, we introduce an accurate lowpass-to-bandpass (LP–BP) frequency transformation tailored for transmission-line wideband bandpass filter design. The conventional LP–BP transformation is entirely accurate for shunt-series lumped-element resonator filters. When applied to wideband transmission-line filters, it introduces discrepancies between the specified target bandwidth and the bandwidth of the filter topologies, primarily due to approximations related to the transformation. On the contrary, the proposed frequency transformation achieves the desired target bandwidth directly, without requiring any preadjustment or iterative correction.

title: A 26–42-GHz Broadband 2.8-dB NF LNA With Multidiode Thermo-Adaptive Biasing for SATCOM Applications
authors: Zhongliang Li, Qiangji Wang, Yan Wang, Guixiang Jin, Wenbin Wang, Jiangyuan Liang, Wensong Wang, Jiawei Xu, Jiawen Wang, Na Yan, Hao Huang, Weifeng Sun, Zhongyuan Fang
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter presents a broadband low-noise amplifier (LNA) with a wide operating temperature range, suitable for satellite communications (SATCOMs). An on-chip multidiode thermo-adaptive biasing circuit has been presented. The biasing circuit is composed of a series diode and a resistor. The variable gain is realized through a variable-transconductance topology. The proposed LNA employs pole-tuning technology to extend the bandwidth. Besides, a stability network and an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$RC$</tex-math> </inline-formula> feedback network are designed to increase gain flatness and enhance stability. The circuit is implemented using a 0.15-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m GaAs pHEMT process with a chip area of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2.50\times 1.00$</tex-math> </inline-formula> mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Measurement results demonstrate that the proposed LNA achieves an average gain of 22 dB and a noise figure (NF) of 2.8 dB across the operating frequency range from 26 to 42 GHz. It maintains stable performance over a temperature range from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$- 55~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$85~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C, with a gain variation of less than 0.0186 dB/°C and an NF variation of less than 0.002 dB/°C. In addition, the measured output 1-dB compression points (OP1dB) are 12 dBm.

title: Deep Learning-Based Leaf Diseases Detection in Indoor Ornamental Plants: A Comparative Study of YOLOv8, YOLOv9, and YOLOv11
authors: Nishad Mahmud Opu, Md Saiful Islam, Ruma Akter, Kazi Maisha Jannath, Shahriar Ahmed, Rakibul Islam Rafi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Accurate leaf disease detection in indoor ornamental plants remains a critical challenge due to reliance on subjective visual inspection and the high cost of molecular diagnostics. This work addresses the need for an efficient and lightweight system to monitor plant health and prevent disease spread in controlled environments. The main objective is to develop and evaluate a comprehensive end-to-end detection model, based on modern YOLO architectures, to identify and localize nine disease and healthy classes across three species: Money Plant, Snake Plant, and Spider Plant. A custom dataset of 787 highresolution images was collected under variable lighting and backgrounds, annotated and expanded to 3,935 images via systematic augmentation. Three variants-YOLOv8s, YOLOv9s, and the YOLOv11s were trained on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$640 \times 640$</tex> inputs with AdamW optimization and evaluated using Precision, Recall, mAP@50, and mAP@50-95. YOLOv11s achieved superior performance, recording 97.4% mAP @ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$50,89.3 \% \text{mAP}$</tex> @ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$50-95,93.7 \%$</tex> Precision, and 96.7% Recall on the held-out test set. Class-wise analysis revealed consistently high recall, with minor confusions among visually similar disease symptoms. To enhance interpretability, occlusion sensitivity and RISE (Randomized Input Sampling for Explanation) analyses were employed, confirming that the model focuses on disease-relevant leaf regions while suppressing background features. The YOLOv11s framework delivers an accurate and interpretable solution for indoor plant disease monitoring, enabling early diagnosis and timely intervention in ornamental plant care.

title: Effect of Augmentations on CNN Based Models for Congenital Heart Disease Detection from Chest X-Rays
authors: Sauharda Sarkar, Farha Yesmin, Md. Abrar Sayed Tasin, Fatin Israq, Siam Tahsin Bhuiyan, Rashedur Rahman, Saadia Binte Alam, Ashraful Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Congenital heart disease is one of the most common cardiovascular diseases, and its early detection is critical for effective treatment. Although chest X-ray imaging is affordable and accessible in both rural and urban health centers, manual interpretation for the diagnosis of CHD is challenging and prone to incorrect diagnoses. In this study, we classify chest X-ray images into four classes: normal, atrial septal defect (ASD), ventricular septal defect (VSD), and patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) based on deep learning models. A publicly available Kaggle competition dataset was used in our study, which consists of 828 X-ray images. Four Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models are trained and evaluated. Experimental results show that EfficientNetB1 with moderate augmentation achieved the highest test accuracy of 69.51 %, followed by DenseNet121 with 66.46 %, while MobileNetV2 yielded the worst performance at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6 0. 9 8 \%}$</tex>. These findings show the augmentation effect on deep learning models for automated CHD detection from chest X-ray images.

title: OvaSignal AI: An Explainable and Real-Time Machine Learning Framework for Early Prediction of Ovarian Cancer
authors: Esrath Kanon, Md Sadi Al Huda, Md Shahidul Khan Pappo, Md Shamsul Alam, Kazi Tanvir, Md Asraf Ali, Syamimi Mardiah Shaharum
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)

title: Quantum Multiple Rotation Averaging
authors: Shuteng Wang, Natacha Kuete Meli, Michael Moller, Vladislav Golyanik
abstract: Multiple rotation averaging (MRA) is a fundamental optimization problem in 3D vision and robotics that aims to recover globally consistent absolute rotations from noisy relative measurements Established classical methods, such as L1-IRLS and Shonan, face limitations including local minima susceptibility and reliance on convex relaxations that fail to preserve the exact manifold geometry, leading to reduced accuracy in high-noise scenarios. We introduce IQARS (Iterative Quantum Annealing for Rotation Synchronization), the first algorithm that reformulates MRA as a sequence of local quadratic non-convex sub-problems executable on quantum annealers after binarization, to leverage inherent hardware advantages. IQARS removes convex relaxation dependence and better preserves non-Euclidean rotation manifold geometry while leveraging quantum tunneling and parallelism for efficient solution space exploration. We evaluate IQARS's performance on synthetic and real-world datasets. While current annealers remain in their nascent phase and only support solving problems of limited scale with constrained performance, we observed that IQARS on D-Wave annealers can already achieve <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 12 {\%}$</tex> higher accuracy than Shonan-the bestperforming classical method evaluated empirically. Project page: https://4dqv.mpi-inf.mpg.de/QMRA/.

title: SocialGen: Modeling Multi-Human Social Interaction with Language Models
authors: Heng Yu, Juze Zhang, Changan Chen, Tiange Xiang, Yusu Fang, Juan Carlos Niebles, Ehsan Adeli
abstract: Human interactions in everyday life are inherently social, involving engagements with diverse individuals across various contexts. Modeling these social interactions is fundamental to a wide range of real-world applications. In this paper, we introduce SocialGen, the first unified motionlanguage model capable of modeling interaction behaviors among varying numbers of individuals, to address this crucial yet challenging problem. Unlike prior methods that are limited to two-person interactions, we propose a novel social motion representation that supports tokenizing the motions of an arbitrary number of individuals and aligning them with the language space. This alignment enables the model to leverage rich, pretrained linguistic knowledge to better understand and reason about human social behaviors. To tackle the challenges of data scarcity, we curate a comprehensive multi-human interaction dataset, SocialX, enriched with textual annotations. Leveraging this dataset, we establish the first comprehensive benchmark for multihuman interaction tasks. Our method achieves state-of-theart performance across motion-language tasks, setting a new standard for multi-human interaction modeling. Our dataset and source code will be made publicly available.

title: TexAvatars: Hybrid Texel-3D Representations for Stable Rigging of Photorealistic Gaussian Head Avatars
authors: Jaeseong Lee, Junyeong Ahn, Taewoong Kang, Jaegul Choo
abstract: Constructing drivable and photorealistic 3D head avatars has become a central task in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$A R / X R$</tex>, enabling immersive and expressive user experiences. With the emergence of high-fidelity, efficient representations such as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 D$</tex> Gaussians, recent works have pushed toward ultra-detailed head avatars. Existing approaches typically fall into two categories: rule-based analytic rigging or neural networkbased deformation fields. While effective in constrained settings, both approaches often fail to generalize to unseen expressions and poses-particularly in extreme reenactment scenarios. Other methods constrain Gaussians to the global texel space of 3DMMs to reduce rendering complexity. However, these texel-based avatars tend to underutilize the underlying mesh structure. They apply minimal analytic deformation and rely heavily on neural regressors and heuristic regularization in UV space, which weakens geometric consistency and limits extrapolation to complex, out-of-distribution deformations. To address these limitations, we introduce TexAvatars, a hybrid avatar representation that combines the explicit geometric grounding of analytic rigging with the spatial continuity of texel space. Our approach predicts local geometric attributes in UV space via CNNs, but drives 3D deformation through mesh-aware Jacobians, enabling smooth and semantically meaningful transitions across triangle boundaries. This hybrid design separates semantic modeling from geometric control, resulting in improved generalization, interpretability, and stability. Furthermore, TexAvatars captures fine-grained expression effects-including muscle-induced wrinkles, glabellar lines, and realistic mouth cavity geom-etry-with high fidelity. Our method achieves state-of-theart performance under extreme pose and expression vari-ations, demonstrating strong generalization in challenging head reenactment settings.

title: Prescribed-Time ESO-Based Robust Repetitive Learning Control for Cable-Driven Rehabilitation Exoskeleton Robots
authors: Chunjie Xiao, Qiang Chen, Yun Cheng, Guanbin Gao, Xiongxiong He
abstract: This article proposes a prescribed-time extended state observer (PTESO) based robust repetitive learning control (RLC) scheme for cable-driven rehabilitation exoskeleton robots (CDRER). Based on the repetitive characteristics of rehabilitation training, the uncertain dynamics in CDRER are divided into two separated parts, i.e., periodic part and nonperiodic part. The periodic part is involved in an unknown bounded desired input, and a fully saturated repetitive learning law with a continuous function is proposed, such that the estimation of the desired input is strictly confined within a predefined region and its continuity is guaranteed. Then, by constructing a bounded and continuous gain function in the ESO design, the observation error of the nonperiodic part converges into a small adjustable domain at a preset time and maintains it in the later time without switching, such that the demand of rapid disturbance rejection in the exoskeleton is satisfied. The proposed control scheme enables the CDRER to provide satisfactory steady-state tracking performance as specified by the therapist during repetitive rehabilitation tasks, and comparative experiments on a cable-driven knee exoskeleton validate its effectiveness.

title: Power Converters for Green Hydrogen Production: An Application-Oriented Review
authors: Tayebeh Faghihi, Shadi Khodakaramzadeh, Pavol Bauer, Hani Vahedi
abstract: The decarbonization of energy systems has positioned green hydrogen as a pivotal energy carrier, with water electrolysis emerging as the most mature and scalable technology for sustainable hydrogen production. When powered by renewable sources such as solar photovoltaics (PVs) and wind, electrolyzers enable a carbon-neutral pathway while requiring advanced power conversion systems. This article presents an application-oriented review of power electronic converter technologies for renewable-powered water electrolysis, based on reported studies and commercially implemented systems. The discussion begins with an overview of the main types of electrolyzers and their distinct electrical and dynamic characteristics. Renewable energy supply characteristics are then examined, highlighting intermittency, variability, and integration challenges associated with solar, wind, and hybrid sources. Technical requirements for power converters, such as efficiency and cost, reliability, voltage and current regulation, power quality, scalability, system complexity, and isolation, are reviewed based on literature and commercial systems. The review further surveys AC–DC rectifier topologies, from diode-based designs to advanced controlled and multilevel configurations, as well as nonisolated and isolated DC–DC converters. Practical design considerations and topology selection strategies for large-scale electrolyzer plants are discussed, with reference to existing hydrogen production facilities and commercial power converter solutions. This review provides a consolidated reference for researchers and engineers, bridging electrolyzer operational requirements with state-of-the-art power electronics solutions for renewable hydrogen generation.

title: APTQ+: Attention-FFN-aware Post-Training Quantization for a Layer-wise LLM Accelerator on FPGA
authors: Ziyi Guan, Zhengfei Chen, Dongkuan Wu, Ao Shen, Yifan Guo, Yupeng Su, Graziano Chesi, Mingqiang Huang, Ngai Wong, Hao Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) deliver impressive performance in NLP, but their enormous parameter counts and computational costs pose severe challenges for deployment on resource-limited or specialized hardware. In this work, we propose APTQ+, an Attention-FFN-aware Post-Training Quantization framework that leverages second-order Hessian information to optimize both the self-attention and feed-forward network (FFN) sub-layers of transformers, including those with Softmax and SwiGLU activation. APTQ+ enables accurate, hardware-friendly 4-bit quantization via a symmetric INT4 scheme (with per-tensor scaling), and is co-designed with an end-to-end compiler for seamless FPGA deployment. Additionally, we introduce an integrated toolchain that combines algorithms, evaluation, compilation, and deployment. We demonstrate 4-bit inference on real LLMs (LLaMA-2 7B, LLaMA-3 8B, Qwen2, etc.) with negligible accuracy loss: for example, APTQ+ yields a WikiText-2 perplexity of 5.66 on LLaMA-2 7B (vs. 5.53 in FP16) and a mean zero-shot accuracy of 72.27% on a LLaMA-3 8B model (vs. 72.83% FP16). Moreover, APTQ+ outperforms prior 4-bit methods (e.g., GPTQ and AWQ) in both perplexity and zero-shot evaluations. Our FPGA-based accelerator delivers up to ×3.29 higher energy efficiency than an NVIDIA A100 GPU while achieving comparable throughput and latency. To the best of our knowledge, this represents the first-of-its-kind end-to-end INT4 FPGA realization of an LLM, establishing a new state-of-the-art for low-bit deployment. A reference implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/EasonGZY/AutoAPTQ.

title: Strategic CSR Communication: A Comparative Study of Legitimation in Chinese and American Energy Companies
authors: Changhong Li, Yingqui Wang
abstract: Background: While corporate social responsibility (CSR) reporting has been widely studied, fewer works have systematically examined how legitimation strategies are discursively constructed across generic structure in energy-sector disclosures. Literature review: Prior research has explored microlinguistic features and move structures, but little attention has been paid to how rhetorical strategies of legitimation are embedded across generic stages. Research questions: 1. How are the five major legitimation strategies linguistically realized across the move-step structures of Chinese and American energy CSR reports? 2. How do legitimation strategies co-occur with different discourse types of Chinese and American energy CSR reports? 3. How do the external factors influence the selection of legitimation strategies in CSR reporting practices? Methodology: This study analyzes 40 CSR reports (2020-2023) from Chinese and American energy companies using a corpus-assisted and move-step-based approach that integrates critical genre analysis and an integrated analytical legitimation framework. Lexical bundles and grammatical features are annotated across generic stages and discourse types to identify legitimation strategies and trace how they vary by national context and external pressures. Results and conclusions: This study identifies eight moves and 13 steps—including a newly observed move, M8: Elaboration of performance in the public health crisis—as well as four discourse types. Chinese reports emphasize authorization and moral narrative, while American reports favor rationalization, analogies of moralization and cosmos narrative. These strategies are embedded across moves and discourse types, adapting to institutional logics and external pressures.

title: Characterization of the Stability of DRAM Read Disturbances
authors: Roberto Capoferri, Alessandro Barenghi, Luca Breveglieri, Khalil Gammoh, Niccolò Izzo, Gerardo Pelosi
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Read disturbance phenomena, such as RowHammer and RowPress, are well-known causes for data corruption in DRAM memories, which have been shown to constitute exploitable security vulnerabilities. Indeed, both phenomena alter the memory locations nearby a row that is targeted by an attacker, potentially leading to writing data in memory locations that should not be accessible by her. In this work, we take a constructive point of view on the read disturbance phenomena. Observing that the data alteration patterns depend on the single device instance, we characterize them with the intent of building a Physically Unclonable Function (PUF). To this end, we specifically analyze the repeatability of data alteration and the information content of the bitflip patterns in modern DDR5 DRAM memories. This analysis would also enable a PUF implementation fully contained within the DRAM module. Our experimental results on multiple DDR5 DRAM chips show that the bitflip patterns caused by read disturbances are remarkably stable when considering a single DRAM row, and are essentially independent across rows in the same bank, or across different devices. These results, combined with a significant information content coming from the unique bitflip pattern produced by a read disturbance, suggest that read-disturbance-based PUFs are promising candidates for future implementations.

title: EEG Ensemble Learning Framework for Prognostic Prediction of Post-rTMS Motor Recovery in Stroke
authors: Tianyu Jia, Haiyang Long, Diantong Xie, Yixu Zhao, Yifan Wu, Yifeng Li, Linhong Mo, Xinyu Guan, Dario Farina
abstract: Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) is a promising neuromodulatory therapy for post-stroke rehabilitation, offering the potential to modulate brain network dynamics. However, its clinical effectiveness remains debated due to the high inter-individual variability in patient responses, largely attributed to heterogeneous patterns of neural reorganization after stroke. In this study, we proposed an EEG ensemble learning framework (EEG-ELF) to predict motor recovery outcomes following rTMS therapy in stroke patients. The framework was evaluated on EEG recordings acquired during a motor imagery task from 20 individuals with subcortical stroke, collected both before and after a four-week rTMS intervention, along with the upper-limb Fugl-Meyer Assessment (FMA) scores. EEG-ELF's prediction performance was assessed through both regression and classification tasks using leave-one-subject-out cross-validation. The results demonstrated a strong correlation between predicted and actual recovery rates (Pearson's R = 0.92, p <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$< $</tex-math></inline-formula> 0.001), and the framework achieved 95% accuracy in classifying patients into good and poor prognosis groups. These findings suggest that EEG-ELF can accurately predict individual motor outcomes from pre-treatment EEG features, highlighting its potential to guide personalized rTMS interventions through data-driven stratification of stroke patients.

title: A Hierarchical Deep Reinforcement Learning Model for Joint Optimization of Urban Traffic Congestion and Cost in Intelligent Transportation Systems
authors: Zixin Zhu
abstract: Urban traffic congestion poses a persistent challenge to modern cities, resulting in increased travel delay, energy consumption, and economic loss. As a critical problem in intelligent transportation systems, urban congestion management requires coordinated and adaptive decision-making across both supply-side and demand-side control mechanisms. Existing traffic management approaches usually address congestion from either the supply side through traffic signal control or the demand side via congestion pricing, while their intrinsic interactions and multi-scale coordination remain insufficiently explored. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes a hierarchical deep reinforcement learning framework, termed JCPO-DRL, for joint congestion and pricing optimization in urban traffic networks. The framework decomposes the joint control task into a two-layer decision process, where an upper-layer policy regulates network-level congestion pricing at a coarse temporal scale and a lower-layer policy performs adaptive traffic signal control conditioned on pricing decisions, enabling coordinated supply–demand regulation while avoiding the curse of dimensionality. Extensive experiments under diverse traffic scenarios show that JCPO-DRL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art signal-only and pricing-only baselines, reducing average travel delay by up to 19% and improving traffic throughput by up to 4% compared with representative baselines, and maintaining moderate congestion pricing cost. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of hierarchical joint optimization and highlight the potential of the proposed framework for intelligent urban traffic management in intelligent transportation systems.

title: Learning Policy-Dependent Traversability for Terrain-Aware Quadruped Navigation_supp1-3696520.mp4
authors: Jiahu Qin
abstract: Quadruped robots have exhibited highly adaptive locomotion capabilities, yet reliable navigation on complex mixed unstructured terrain remains a major challenge. A key difficulty is that existing navigation frameworks typically rely on geometric costmaps, which fail to capture the fact that terrain traversability is inherently policy-dependent, as different locomotion controllers exhibit distinct contact strategies, dynamic limits, and behavioral capabilities. To address this issue, we present a unified framework that learns and exploits policy-dependent traversability for large-scale autonomous navigation with quadrupedal robots. We first develop a data-driven traversability estimator that predicts the traversability of each terrain region under a given locomotion policy, trained from a kernel-based traversability sample map aggregated from simulated policy rollouts. This estimator is used to construct a terrain-aware costmap with policy-dependent feasibility constraints. Then, a two-stage planning pipeline with grid-based search and trajectory optimization is proposed to generate smooth, policy-feasible global trajectories for execution by the corresponding locomotion policy. Finally, we extend the framework to multiple locomotion policies, enabling automatic policy switching based on predicted traversability and enhancing adaptability to diverse terrain conditions. Experiments across large and challenging environments demonstrate that our approach significantly improves navigation reliability, terrain adaptability, and long-horizon autonomy for quadrupedal robots.

title: Secure Connected $k$-Coverage in Spatial Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: Dakshanya Maddala, Habib M. Ammari
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Coverage in spatial wireless sensor networks (WSNs) is an open problem. In particular, the problem of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{k}$</tex>-coverage in spatial (or three-dimensional, 3D for short) WSNs, where every point in a spatial field of interest (FoI) is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex>-covered (i.e., covered by at least <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex> sensors simultaneously), is more challenging. In this paper, we investigate the problem of connected <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex> coverage in spatial WSNs, where a FoI is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex>-covered while all participating sensors are connected to each other. This study leverages convex polyhedral space-fillers, which helps produce an irregular hexagonal prism-based tessellation, named Irregular Honeycomb Network. The latter serves as a medium to achieve <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{k}$</tex>-coverage in spatial WSNs. First, we propose a sensor placement strategy to ensure full <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{k}$</tex>-coverage of a spatial FoI. Second, we suggest a secure communication protocol using RSA public key cryptography to achieve secure spatial connected <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex> coverage. We corroborate our analysis with various simulation results.

title: Towards Label-Efficient Breast Cancer Detection in Histology with Self and Semi-Supervised Learning
authors: Ishra Naznin, Tanjim Mahmud, Abubokor Hanip, Mohammad Shahadat Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Accurate detection of breast cancer using histopathological images is critical for early diagnosis and therapy. However, the scarcity of labeled medical data is a substantial hindrance to supervised deep learning systems. In this paper, we offer a label-efficient approach that uses both self-supervised and semi-supervised learning to enhance breast cancer classification using the BreakHis 400X histology dataset. We improve the SimCLR contrastive learning system by incorporating a multi-pair contrastive loss that uses multiple augmented views per image, resulting in richer and more robust feature representations from unlabeled data. The pretrained encoder is then fine-tuned with FixMatch, a semisupervised learning technique that combines pseudo-labeling with consistency regularization to make the most of limited labeled data and copious unlabeled samples. To highlight the benefits of our combined method, we experiment with linear evaluations on the pretrained representations and compare performance to FixMatch-only baselines. Experimental results show that our multi-pair SimCLR pretraining followed by FixMatch fine-tuning increases classification accuracy while greatly lowering the requirement for extensive annotations. This study demonstrates the feasibility of merging advanced self- and semi-supervised learning approaches for scalable and reliable breast cancer diagnosis in histopathology, paving the way for practical clinical implementation with limited expert labeling.

title: KaoLRM: Repurposing Pre-Trained Large Reconstruction Models for Parametric 3D Face Reconstruction
authors: Qingtian Zhu, Xu Cao, Zhixiang Wang, Yinqiang Zheng, Takafumi Taketomi
abstract: We propose KaoLRM to re-target the learned prior of the Large Reconstruction Model (LRM) for parametric 3D face reconstruction from single-view images. Parametric 3D Morphable Models (3DMMs) have been widely used for facial reconstruction due to their compact and interpretable parameterization, yet existing 3DMM regressors often exhibit poor consistency across varying viewpoints. To address this, we harness the pre-trained 3D prior of LRM and incorporate FLAME-based 2D Gaussian Splatting into LRM's rendering pipeline. Specifically, KaoLRM projects LRM's pre-trained triplane features into the FLAME parameter space to recover geometry, and models appearance via 2D Gaussian primitives that are tightly coupled to the FLAME mesh. The rich prior enables the FLAME regressor to be aware of the 3D structure, leading to accu-rate and robust reconstructions under self-occlusions and diverse viewpoints. Experiments on both controlled and in-the-wild benchmarks demonstrate that KaoLRM achieves superior reconstruction accuracy and cross-view consistency, while existing methods remain sensitive to viewpoint variations. The code is released at https://github.com/CyberAgentAILab/KaoLRM.

title: Control-Oriented Modeling of Electrode Material for Mechanical Notching and Stacking Processes of Battery Manufacturing
authors: Andreas Rauh, Lisa Wünderlich
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: The processes of notching and stacking represent two crucial stages of battery cell manufacturing that have a significant influence on the quality of the produced cells from the perspective of lifetime. In addition, these stages are also crucial concerning safety, especially, when the production of cells with high energy density is concerned. This is due to the fact that imperfect cutting edge quality in notching (due to burr) may lead to sharp edges that cut through separator foils when stacking is executed. This may produce internal short circuits in produced cells that are possibly not detected during the formation stage but only show up during operation when internal pressure and temperature variations in battery cells lead to a gradual extension of this defect. The same is also true for mechanical deformations of electrodes during their transport within the stacking process which may lead to wrinkles as well as cracks. Therefore, this paper presents a control-oriented modeling procedure for flexible electrodes that can be used for forecasting deflections in elastic and plastic regimes during notching and stacking and therefore is inevitable for a model-based design of control strategies for both handling stages. The core of this modeling approach is the replacement of the governing partial differential equation for the material deformation by a model that is discretized in space and time. For that purpose, the Crank-Nicolson approach is employed due to its favorable property of preserving the stability properties when discretizing the underlying infinite-dimensional model.

title: GMT: Goal-Conditioned Multimodal Transformer for 6-DOF Object Trajectory Synthesis in 3D Scenes
authors: Huajian Zeng, Abhishek Saroha, Daniel Cremers, Xi Wang
abstract: Synthesizing controllable 6-DOF object manipulation trajectories in 3D environments is essential for enabling robots to interact with complex scenes, yet remains challenging due to the need for accurate spatial reasoning, physical feasibility, and multimodal scene understanding. Existing approaches often rely on 2D or partial 3D representations, limiting their ability to capture full scene geometry and constraining trajectory precision. We present GMT, a multimodal transformer framework that generates realistic and goal-directed object trajectories by jointly leveraging 3D bounding box geometry, point cloud context, semantic object categories, and target end poses. The model represents trajectories as continuous 6-DOF pose sequences and employs a tailored conditioning strategy that fuses geometric, semantic, contextual, and goal-oriented information. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that GMT outperforms state-of-the-art human motion and human-object interaction baselines, such as CHOIS and GIMO, achieving substantial gains in spatial accuracy and orientation control. Our method establishes a new benchmark for learning-based manipulation planning and shows strong generalization to diverse objects and cluttered 3D environments.

title: A Discrete Space Vector Modulation-based Model-Free Predictive Control with Improved Super-Twisting Sliding Mode Observer for Permanent Magnet Linear Synchronous Machine
authors: Chengwei Luo, Ying Zou, Zhimeng Rao, Ming Li
abstract: Due to its simple implementation and excellent performance, model predictive control (MPC) is applied to permanent magnet linear synchronous machine (PMLSM) drive systems. However, the selected voltage vector (VV) in conventional MPC has large error and thrust fluctuation. In addition, it also exhibits strong parameter dependency, and mismatched parameters can deteriorate control performance. Thereof, to further improve control performance, a discrete space vector modulation based MPC (DSVM-MPC) strategy is proposed, which redefined the action region of the VV and redesigned the optimal VV search algorithm based on the action region. Compared with conventional methods, the proposed method can obtain more accurate VV under similar computational burden. Moreover, a model free predictive control (MFPC) strategy based on an improved super-twist sliding mode observer (ST-SMO) is designed, which introduces an exponential term in the convergence rate function, not only has higher control accuracy, but also improves convergence speed. To filter out the dc-offset signals in the sampling currents, a two-degree of-freedom (TDF) filter is also designed for DSVM-MFPC. Finally, the superiority of the proposed method is verified through simulations and experiments on the prototype.

title: Time-Frequency and Multi-Scale Learning Network for Long-Term Cloud Workload Prediction
authors: Weijian Zheng, Weiping Zheng, Yiqi Chen, Zichen Wang, Yimin Mao
abstract: Accurate long-term workload prediction (LTWP) is crucial for effective resource management in cloud environments, where workload dynamics are characterized by high variability, noise, and long-range temporal dependencies. Existing methods, such as linear models and PatchTST-based models, ofen struggle with capturing long-range dependencies, handling noise, and adapting to diverse workload patterns. To address these issues, this paper proposes TFMSNet, a novel time-frequency and multi-scale learning network for LTWP. TFMSNet integrates low-frequency wavelet features to capture global workload trends and high-frequency components to model local variations and change points, enabling more comprehensive temporal representation. Its multi-branch architecture allows for efficient capture of multi-scale dependencies, enhancing adaptability to diverse and dynamic workloads. Extensive experiments on five real-world datasets—Azure-2017, Alibaba-2018, Google-2019, Alibaba-2020 and Alibaba-2021—demonstrate that TFMSNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models in performance metrics of MAE, MSE, RMSE and MAPE across multiple prediction horizons. Ablation studies confirm the critical roles of frequency-enhanced attention and multi-branch architecture in improving accuracy. Moreover, the model's robustness under noisy conditions and its efficiency with shorter input sequences further highlight its practical applicability for real time, resource-constrained cloud environments. Code is available at: https://github.com/jiange6daobao/TFMSNet-Workload Forecasting

title: TRIM: Tactile Road Interactive Mapping for Real-Time Haptic Feedback
authors: Huimin Jiao, Yaoyu Duan, Haodong Zhang, Lizhi Song, Mingjin Xu, Xingwei Guo
abstract: Accurate tactile perception of ground surfaces is crucial for non-visual navigation and terrain awareness in various assistive applications. However, existing tactile interfaces often fail to provide high-resolution, low-latency, and cost-effective feedback across diverse terrains. This study presents an interactive tactile map that integrates Digital Elevation Model (DEM) processing, reconstructed tactile input via a piezoresistive sensor array, and vibration feedback based on the 2D funneling illusion. Through an algorithmic rendering pipeline, the system transforms multidimensional DEM attributes—elevation, slope, and roughness—into dynamic tactile signals with fine spatial detail. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed rendering algorithm achieves sub-millisecond latency and significantly enhances environmental perception, confirming its effectiveness for real-time tactile terrain representation.

title: Vibration Reduction of Nonswitching High Frequency Band in FSCW IPMSMs With Harmonic-Oriented Slotted Rotor
authors: Zhuting Zhao, Renxin Xiao, Wenxiang Zhao, Yuhua Sun, Yunhan Zhou
abstract: In this article, a harmonic-oriented slotted rotor is proposed to significantly reduce vibration without sacrificing the torque performance in the fractional-slot concentrated-winding (FSCW) interior permanent magnet synchronous machines (IPMSMs). First, the generation mechanism of nonswitching high frequency band vibration is revealed. Unlike conventional theory, nonswitching high frequency band vibration dominates instead of the low frequency band vibration in the 10-slot/8-pole FSCW IPMSMs. Second, a harmonic-oriented slotted rotor is designed by constructing the compensation function. The required air-gap permeance phase angle is determined based on the permanent magnet flux density distribution. In this way, the specific permanent magnet flux density harmonics can be directly reduced. Finally, the FSCW IPMSM with harmonic-oriented slotted rotor is manufactured and tested. The experimental results verify the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: A 28-nm Fully-Integrated Reconfigurable CMOS Simulated Coherent Ising Machine With Dynamic Pump-Rate Control and Spin Freezing
authors: Qiaoyi Fu, Yifeng Zhou, Qinchao Cai, Wenzhen An, Renlong Li, Lei Liao, Zhuojun Chen
abstract: Combinatorial optimization problems (COPs) play a critical role in various domains but are computationally challenging. Although coherent Ising machines (CIMs) exploit quantum-inspired dynamics to solve COPs, their optical implementations suffer from bulky components and high system cost. In this work, we present the first fully-integrated CMOS-based simulated CIM (SCIM). The proposed design integrates a Booth-optimized multiplier–accumulator and piecewise linear activation circuit, thereby reducing power consumption and area. A reconfigurable fully-connected topology enables direct mapping of a wide range of COPs, supporting variable spin counts and coupling bit-widths. We further introduce a dynamic pump-rate control that accelerates escape from high-energy states and enables fine-grained search near the bifurcation point, along with a spin freezing (SF) technique that stabilizes converged spins and eliminates redundant updates. Fabricated in 28-nm CMOS technology, SCIM occupies 0.354 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and consumes only 2.952 mW. For MAX-CUT benchmarks, it achieves time-to-solution (TTS) ranging from 0.15 to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$30~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>s, corresponding to a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula>–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1653\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> speedup over state-of-the-art hardware implementations in terms of normalized TTS. Furthermore, the number partitioning problem (NPP) and multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) detection are performed using the proposed SCIM, which achieves high-performance solving, thereby demonstrating a reconfigurable, power-efficient, and high-speed approach for physics-inspired combinatorial optimization.

title: Ultra-Flat Gain Profile With Raman Amplification Employing Combined Coherent and Incoherent Pumps_supp1-3696771.pdf
authors: Zoltan Papp
abstract: Distributed Raman amplification is essential for enhancing capacity in wideband optical networks; yet achieving an ultra-flat gain profile remains a significant design challenge. This work presents a validated numerical model incorporating both coherent pump and spectrally broad incoherent pump sources. Model accuracy was rigorously confirmed by demonstrating excellent agreement (average deviation <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$< 0.1\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula>) between simulation and experiment across various forward, backward, and multi-directional pumping schemes. Leveraging this validated model, we employed an improved simplex optimization algorithm to target ultra-flat amplification across <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$95 \rm { nm}$</tex-math></inline-formula> C+L bands in a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$40 \rm { km}$</tex-math></inline-formula> fiber. By implementing a hybrid nine-pump scheme (four first-order incoherent and five second-order coherent pumps) to meet practical power limits, we achieved an unprecedented peak-to-peak gain flatness of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\le 0.04$</tex-math></inline-formula> dB over the 95nm bandwidth, with a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\leq 0.01\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula> standard deviation at different target gain values of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$9\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$8\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Furthermore, we demonstrate the versatility of the system in generating arbitrary gain profiles with high precision. The best performing scheme was thoroughly tested by a Monte Carlo-based stability investigation, resulting in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\leq 0.04\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula> standard deviation of gain for practical power and wavelength variations. This result confirms the superior capability of optimally configured hybrid incoherent/coherent pumping for minimizing gain ripple and enabling reconfigurable gain shaping in future high-capacity WDM systems.

title: 3D-Printed Terahertz Porous Fiber for Polarization Control
authors: Liying Lang, Mengqi Gao, Jing Zhang, Yiyang Chen, Mingshuo Fang, Shuai Li, Haisu Li, Yang Cao, Jianqiang Gu
abstract: Polarization-maintaining terahertz fibers are crucial for terahertz wired links and enabling advanced polarization-division multiplexing. While terahertz porous fibers offer great potential for low-loss broadband wave transmission in protected environments, achieving stable polarization control with a reproducible, robust design remains challenging. In this work, we propose a terahertz birefringent porous fiber in a simplified concentric elliptical ring geometry. Through systematic investigation into fiber guiding properties, a porous fiber optimized for operation at 140 GHz is demonstrated. Owing to the enhanced field confinement within its inside air regions, this porous fiber achieves a birefringence on the order of 10⁻² and lower transmission loss compared to its hollow-core counterpart. Such a design is subsequently fabricated using stereolithography 3D printing. We then experimentally characterize the effective refractive index, transmission loss, and near-field imaging of the 3D-printed prototypes, which show good agreement with theoretical predictions. Furthermore, a polarization rotator is implemented by introducing a controlled 90° helical twist along the fiber, achieving a polarization extinction ratio of ∼20 dB across the D-band. We believe that this work provides a practical platform for polarization control in terahertz guided wave, opening new pathways toward integrated terahertz systems for communication and sensing.

title: Expressing Emotion Through Heartbeat: A Soft Robotic Heart Integrated With a Soft Sensor for Arts in Robotics_supp2-3687384.mp4
authors: Jian ZHU
abstract: The heartbeat is both a physiological signal and a universal expression of emotion. This work presents a pneumatically actuated soft robotic heart for interactive art. A fully 3D-printed, shielded soft capacitive bending sensor is integrated onto the heart’s surface, providing stable, real-time displacement feedback without affecting its motion. A lightweight multilayer perceptron (MLP) model estimates deformation from the sensor signal and enables closed loop control. Emotional states are selected via a graphical interface or inferred from ambient light to modulate target heart rates (0.5–3 Hz) for displacement control. Physiologically inspired trajectories derived from left ventricular volume data (Wiggers diagram) are reproduced at 0.5 Hz and 1 Hz. A state-switched proportional–derivative (PD) controller drives the heart to track these targets with good accuracy and robustness. In public exhibitions, the heart was paired with the poetic narrative “To My Love. ” The work shows how soft actuation, soft sensing, machine learning, and feedback control can be combined with physiological modeling and artistic expression in a single soft robotic platform.

title: Is Mamba Reliable for Medical Imaging?
authors: Banafsheh Saber Latibari, Najmeh Nazari, Daniel Brignac, Hossein Sayadi, Houman Homayoun, Abhijit Mahalanobis
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: State-space models like Mamba offer linear-time sequence processing and low memory, making them attractive for medical imaging. However, their robustness under realistic software and hardware threat models remains underexplored. This paper evaluates Mamba on multiple MedMNIST classification benchmarks under input-level attacks, including white-box adversarial perturbations (FGSM/PGD), occlusion-based PatchDrop, and common acquisition corruptions (Gaussian noise and defocus blur) as well as hardware-inspired fault attacks emulated in software via targeted and random bit-flip injections into weights and activations. We profile vulnerabilities and quantify impacts on accuracy indicating that defenses are needed for deployment.

title: A Method for Determining the DC Capacitance of Solid Dielectric Capacitors: A Case Study
authors: A Hemavathi, Thomas John, P Anagha, Priyanka Jain, R.P. Aloysius
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper presents a method for estimating the DC capacitance of solid dielectric capacitors. Using a high-precision capacitance bridge, we measured the capacitance-frequency profiles of polypropylene capacitors (ranging from 100 pF to 100 nF) from 10 kHz down to 50 Hz. The measurements demonstrate a clear frequency dependence, where capacitance decreases as frequency increases. We observed that the capacitance-frequency profile can be approximated by a sum of decaying exponentials. Consequently, we derived an empirical formula to extrapolate the measured data down to millihertz frequencies. The values calculated using this formula align closely with measured data in the overlapping frequency range, providing a smooth extrapolation from 10 kHz down to DC. Notably, the DC capacitance of the tested polypropylene capacitors was found to be approximately 0.1% higher than their calibrated values at 1 kHz. Establishing accurate DC values is critical for the application of these capacitors in precision DC metrology.

title: Physical Origins of the Effect of p-Type Work Function Metal Removal on the Electrical Characteristics in Advanced Gate-All-Around CMOS FET
authors: Kenzo Manabe, Misako Morota, Hiroyuki Ota, Yukinori Morita, Toshifumi Irisawa, Yoshihiro Hayashi
abstract: We investigated the effect of the removal process (wet etching) of p-type work function metal (WFM) (TiN) on electrical characteristics such as effective work function (EWF) and leakage characteristics of high-k (HK) gate dielectric in the nMOS region for CMOS application. We found that the EWF decreased with increasing wet etching amount for TiN removal. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis revealed that the EWF dependence on wet etching amount comes from the Ti residual on the HK, implying that the Ti residual increased the EWF. We proposed that the above EWF increase is attributed to the Ti-residual-induced increase in oxygen content in n-type WFM. We also found that the leakage current increased with increasing wet etching amount for TiN removal. The theoretical calculation of leakage current revealed that the main reason for the increase in leakage current is not the wet damage of the gate dielectric but the EWF decrease induced by the decrease in Ti residual by wet treatment.

title: A Thermally Controlled Multifunctional Polarization-Independent Metasurface for 6G Applications
authors: Ajeet Singh Verma, Somak Bhattacharyya
abstract: This article presents a multifunctional metasurface (MS) structure maintaining subwavelength thickness and periodicity with compact geometry. The proposed structure can simultaneously function as a transmittive, reflective, and absorptive type MS in distinct frequency bands in the terahertz (THz) region, utilizing a single metalayer design. The metalayer consists of three concentric rings, where one of them is made of vanadium dioxide (VO<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>), while the other two are made up of gold. The metalayer is sandwiched between two distinct silicon dioxide (SiO<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) layers, where the bottom SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> layer is VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> backed. The structure offers functional switchability to act as a dual stopband (0.74 THz each), separated by a narrow transmission band (0.2 THz) to dual absorption bands (0.11 and 1.11 THz) separated by a reflection band (1.25 THz), attributed to the thermal properties of VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>. The proposed MS offers wide angular stability up to 60° incident angles for both TE and TM polarizations, while the fourfold symmetry of the meta-atom makes the structure polarization-insensitive in nature. Furthermore, the performance of the proposed structure is validated by incorporating an in-house developed equivalent circuit model (ECM). Exhibiting significantly superior features in comparison to the state-of-the-art literature, the proposed structure can be utilized for sensing and communication for selective signal modulation in the next generation 6G communication.

title: Emo-gestor: 3D Co-speech Body Generation Based on Multimodal Emotion-Driven
authors: Qiyun Yang, Daowu Yang, Ruihui Li, Yunchuan Qin, Xu Zhou, Zhuo Tang
abstract: The task of generating vivid and diverse 3D co-speech body movements relies on the coordination of linguistic and non-linguistic cues. A key challenge lies in maintaining rhythmic consistency while effectively integrating semantic and emotional information. In this paper, we propose Emo-gestor, a multimodal emotion-driven framework for 3D co-speech body generation, which can synthesize personalized and emotionally consistent full-body gestures in a unified multimodal setting. Considering that emotional signals are often entangled within audio, text, and facial modalities, we separately extract and encode emotional features from each modality, and construct a unified multimodal emotional representation through an emotion embedding mechanism to guide the generation of rhythmically synchronized and emotionally expressive gestures. Then we introduce a conditional diffusion model with a Transformer to ensure the alignment of gesture content with emotional cues. To enhance the model’s capability of capturing long-term dependencies and mitigate emotional non-smoothness caused by selfattention mechanisms, we propose a Multimodal Emotion Body Reconstruction Module, which integrates Emotion linear convolution into the self-attention mechanism to linearly capture longrange dependencies while preserving short-term detail modeling. Experimental results demonstrate that Emo-gestor significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of emotional consistency, naturalness, and personalization.

title:
R
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    TT: High-Accuracy 3D Localization Using Ray-Tracing-Based Wi-Fi RTT Virtual Fingerprints for Challenging Scenarios
authors: Xin Wang, Xikun Liu, Zhen Lyu, Ruijie Xu, Weisong Wen
abstract: Wi-Fi fingerprinting based on signal strength adapts well to complex indoor signal distributions but is limited by low positioning accuracy and the high cost of fingerprint database construction. Wi-Fi round-trip time (RTT) positioning can achieve meter-level accuracy in open environments but suffers from significant signal reflection effects in complex indoor spaces. This paper proposes R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>TT (Ray-Tracing Round-Trip Time), a high-precision 3D indoor positioning system that combines the advantages of fingerprint-based and ranging-based methods by leveraging virtual RTT ranging fingerprints. More importantly, virtual fingerprint generation eliminates the need for labor-intensive data collection, enabling efficient 3D fingerprint construction. The proposed method consists of two stages: (1) constructing a virtual RTT fingerprint database using low-cost light detection and ranging (LiDAR) and ray tracing techniques and (2) employing deep learning to capture signal reflection characteristics for robust and high-accuracy localization. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that in complex, reflective environments, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>TT achieves a 3D positioning error of approximately 1.3 meters, improving 3D accuracy by over 65% compared to traditional least squares.

title: Active Security Resilient Optimal Strategy for Safe and Stable Autonomous Driving under CAN Bus Attacks
authors: Guoqiang Li, Zhenyang Li, Yu Lu, Hongru Zhang
abstract: The Controller Area Network (CAN) is the core component of in-vehicle network communications, which is highly vulnerable to malicious cyberattacks due to the lack of authentication and encryption, leading to serious threat to the safety of autonomous driving. To solve this issue, this paper proposes an innovative active security resilient optimal strategy for distributed drive electric vehicles (DDEVs) to defend against various CAN bus attacks for safe and stable autonomous driving. First, a data-driven system modeling approach for DDEVs is designed to describe the nonlinear dynamics accurately under three typical CAN attacks on vehicle position and heading states. Then, a novel multi-sensor-information-based detection method with sliding window cumulative residual is developed to detect the attack timely. When the CAN attack is detected, the abnormal signals are removed and replaced with image-based state estimates before being fed into the controller. Once the attack defense is activated, a novel integrated optimal control method is proposed, jointly optimizing the steering and four-wheel torques to achieve precise path tracking and stable vehicle motion control. Extensive simulations on a MATLAB/Simulink and CarSim co-simulation platform validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach on the attack detection, the secure state estimation, and the optimal control, and hardware-in-the-loop experiments further confirm the real-time feasibility of the proposed control algorithm.

title: RIDE: Boosting 3-D Object Detection for LiDAR Point Clouds via Rotation-Invariant Analysis_supp1-3693889.pdf
authors: Xianzhi Li
abstract: The rotation robustness property has drawn much attention to point cloud analysis, whereas it still poses a critical challenge in 3D object detection. When subjected to arbitrary rotation, most existing detectors fail to produce expected outputs due to the poor rotation robustness. In this paper, we present RIDE, a pioneering exploration of Rotation-Invariance for the 3D LiDAR-point-based object DEtector, with the key idea of designing rotation-invariant features from LiDAR scenes and then effectively incorporating them into existing 3D detectors. Specifically, we design a bi-feature extractor that extracts 1) object-aware features that are sensitive to rotation but preserve geometry well, and 2) rotation-invariant features, which lose geometric information to a certain extent but are robust to rotation. These two kinds of features complement each other to decode 3D proposals that are robust to arbitrary rotations. Particularly, our RIDE is compatible and easy to plug into the existing one-stage and two-stage 3D detectors, and boosts both detection performance and rotation robustness. Extensive experiments on the standard benchmarks showcase that the mean average precision (mAP) and rotation robustness can be significantly boosted by integrating with our RIDE, with +5.6% mAP and 111% rotation robustness improvement on KITTI, +5.1% and 34% improvement correspondingly on nuScenes. The code is available at https://github.com/CentauriStar/RIDE

title: Less Signals, More Understanding: Channel-Capacity Codebook Design for Digital Task-Oriented Semantic Communication
authors: Anbang Zhang, Shuaishuai Guo, Chenyuan Feng, Hongyang Du, Haojin Li, Chen Sun, Haijun Zhang
abstract: Discrete representation has emerged as a powerful tool in task-oriented semantic communication (ToSC), offering compact, interpretable, and efficient representations well-suited for low-power edge intelligence scenarios. Its inherent digital nature aligns seamlessly with hardware-friendly deployment and robust storage/transmission protocols. However, current ToSC frameworks often decouple semantic-aware discrete mapping from the underlying channel characteristics and task demands, which leads to suboptimal communication performance and degraded task utility under variable wireless conditions. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a channel-aware discrete semantic coding framework tailored for low-power edge networks. Leveraging a Wasserstein-regularized objective, our approach (WS-VQ) aligns discrete code activations with optimal input distributions, thereby improving semantic fidelity, robustness, and task accuracy. Extensive experiments on the inference tasks across diverse signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) regimes show that our method achieves notable gains in accuracy and communication efficiency. This work provides new insights into integrating discrete semantics and channel optimization, paving the way for the widespread adoption of semantic communication in future digital infrastructures.

title: The adjustment of the turn-to-turn contact resistance of REBCO tape by stripe shape stainless-steel coating
authors: Fulong Sun, Hongli Suo, Zili Zhang, Chengyang Li, Benzhe Zhou, Hongbo Sun, Lei Wang, Jianhua Liu, Qiuliang Wang
abstract: In this paper, the stripe shape stainless-steel coating on the commercial REBCO tape was attempted to adjust the turn-to-turn contact resistance. The original plan is to use a stripe SS coating to create the space between the two REBCO tapes and maintain the path on the SS coating part, which can significantly increase the contact resistance by decreasing the contact area. In real conditions, the SS coating did not reach its goal but created alternative pathways of SS-SS contact and Cu-Cu contact, which can also partially adjust the contact resistance. It is believed that the contact resistance can be controlled in a larger range by changing the coating materials.

title: Learning Invariant Representation for Out-of-distribution Generalization in Photoplethysmography Biometrics
authors: Ran Yi, Yuwen Huang, Gongping Yang, Yilong Yin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science

title: SecureDFL: A Secure Distributed Federated Learning Framework Against Poisoning Attacks
authors: Amir Javadpour, Forough Ja'fari, Tarik Taleb, Fatih Turkmen, Chafika Benzaïd, Mohammad Shojafar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Distributed Federated Learning (DFL) extends federated learning from a single-server topology to broader graph-based coordination settings that may include multiple interacting aggregation nodes and, in some deployments, externally distributed training resources. While this broader topology improves scalability and flexibility, it also enlarges the poisoning attack surface across participants, aggregators, communication channels, and execution resources. This paper presents SecureDFL, a defense-in-depth framework for poisoning-resilient DFL. SecureDFL integrates authenticated communication, protected aggregation based on additive homomorphic encryption, trust-aware orchestration, multi-point validation, and reinforcement-learning-based mutation of resource mappings under a unified system design. The contribution of this work therefore lies in the integrated security architecture and its end-to-end implementation for graph-based DFL, rather than in introducing a standalone cryptographic primitive. Experiments on benchmark datasets, seven threat-model-aligned poisoning scenarios, ablation studies, and a prototype-scale Kubernetes testbed show that SecureDFL preserves strong model performance under attack and substantially improves attack resilience relative to the evaluated baselines, achieving up to a 98.5% reduction in attack success rate in the reported settings with only modest additional overhead. These results support the practical feasibility of the proposed architecture, while the manuscript explicitly delineates its current limits regarding comprehensive collusion analysis, colluding trust manipulation, extremely heterogeneous non-IID conditions, and fully adaptive multi-stage attacks.

title: LightSim: A Flexible, Fast Modeling Framework for Holistic Photonic Computing Systems
authors: Yuxuan Lin, Guohao Dai, Shanshi Huang, Yuzhe Ma
