title: Cognition-Driven Sentiment Analysis: Leveraging Affective Knowledge in Graph Convolutional Networks for Teaching Evaluations_supp1-3690536.pdf
authors: Shoufei Han
abstract: With the proliferation of massive open online courses, teaching evaluation texts have become an important source of feedback, providing valuable affective and cognitive insights into students’ learning experiences. Although automated sentiment analysis techniques have been developed, existing methods still struggle to capture domain-specific affective knowledge and to systematically model the affective and cognitive processes inherent in educational contexts. To address these challenges, we propose an affective cognition-enhanced graph convolutional network (ACEGCN), which simulates a human-like sentiment reasoning process by integrating domain-specific affective cognition knowledge with syntactic information. Specifically, we construct a domain-specific affective cognition knowledge graph by aligning sentiment entities with a general knowledge base. The resulting knowledge is then integrated into the input sentence to form a knowledge-enriched sentence tree, from which semantically enhanced dependency graphs are constructed using a multigranularity syntactic masking mechanism. Finally, we employ a graph convolutional network to capture key sentiment paths and model complex semantic dependencies. Experimental results across three real-world teaching evaluation datasets demonstrate that ACEGCN significantly outperforms baseline models, suggesting its potential to enhance teaching feedback analysis and help improve teaching quality.

title: Modelling of FEOL and BEOL Amorphous Gallium Oxide-based Ferroelectric FETs with a Metal Interlayer for Multilevel Operation in Short Channel Devices
authors: Chiara Rossi, Daniel Lizzit, Charlotte Van Dijck, Thanh Luan Phan, Catherine Dubourdieu, David Esseni
publish: IEEE Journal of the Electron Devices Society
abstract: Ferroelectric field effect transistors (FeFETs) are among the most promising candidates for the implementation of artificial synapses for neuromorphic computing. However, their scaling poses big challenges, as multilevel behavior is hindered by the two-level or very-few-level polarization behavior reported for devices featuring an aggressive lateral scaling. Moreover, the switching and stabilization of ferroelectric polarization, and consequently the device operation, is inherently linked to high levels of charge trapping in the gate stack, limiting the reliability and increasing the variability of the FeFETs. In this work we investigate, by means of calibrated TCAD simulations, FeFETs integrating a metal interlayer inside the gate stack. FeFETs are designed so that, by properly biasing the device, the metal interlayer can get charged thanks to charge tunneling through the dielectric layer. We first investigate a conventional front-end-of-line implementation, consisting of an aggressively scaled FeFET featuring a ferroelectric hafnium zirconium oxide and a dielectric hafnium oxide in the gate stack, a single-crystal silicon channel, and highly-doped source and drain pockets. Then we consider a back-end-of-line compatible device structure featuring lightly– doped amorphous gallium-oxide channel material, with Schottky-type tungsten contacts. We show that, for both architectures, the charge in the metal interlayer can effectively stabilize the ferroelectric polarization, even for a negligible charge trapping in the dielectric stack. Moreover, the interplay between ferroelectric polarization and charge in the metal interlayer enables a multilevel operation even for a uniform ferroelectric polarization.

title: Platform Architecture Approach for Managing Traceability, Visibility, and Risk Management in Freight Transportation
authors: Jonathan Quiroga-Amaya, Erwin J. Sacoto-Cabera, Katiana Tafur-Landazabal, Andrés Castellanos-Ramírez, Jesús Estrada-Gallardo, César Viloria-Núñez

title: Open-Source IEC-Compliant Reconstruction of Turbulent Offshore Wind from Low-Resolution Measurements for Aeroelastic-Relevant Energy Assessment
authors: Luciane A. S. Pedotti, Héctor A. Duarte, Samir C. Gómez, Pedro C. Mello, Renato M. Monaro, Maurício B. C. Salles

title: BSFL: Secure and Efficient Blockchain-Based Split Federated Learning for Internet of Vehicles
authors: Zixu Jiang, Cheng Chi, Yizhong Liu, Haohua Du, Zixiao Jia, Tairan Ding, Qianhong Wu, Zhenyu Guan, Dawei Li, Willy Susilo
abstract: The rapid development of the automotive industry and the Internet of Vehicles (IoV) has led to an exponential growth of distributed vehicular data, driving the need for secure and efficient collaborative machine learning solutions. However, existing distributed collaborative machine learning (DCML) approaches, such as federated learning and split learning, face significant challenges in IoV scenarios, including limited training efficiency, centralized aggregation vulnerabilities, and constrained privacy and model protection. To address these issues, we propose a blockchain-based split federated learning (BSFL) scheme for IoV applications. BSFL non-trivially combines federated learning and split learning to enable vehicles with low computational power to participate in parallel training, improving both model accuracy and training efficiency. By utilizing blockchain as a decentralized infrastructure, BSFL eliminates the risks of single points of failure and ensures model consistency through Byzantine fault-tolerant consensus. Furthermore, we design a noise addition mechanism based on differential privacy to safeguard client data privacy and model security. Formal security analysis and extensive experiments demonstrate that BSFL achieves enhanced privacy, security, and training performance. Comparing to related DCML schemes, BSFL reduces computational overhead by up to 88.84% and client training time by up to 29.49% while maintaining comparable accuracy. When training on ResNet-50 based on CIFAR10, BSFL achieved an accuracy of 93.15%. And the verification process for each model’s training results on the blockchain requires 1.49 ms.

title: Evaluating and Mitigating LLM-as-a-Judge Bias in Communication Systems
authors: Jiaxin Gao, Chen Chen, Yanwen Jia, Xueluan Gong, Kwok-Yan Lam, Qian Wang
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly being used to autonomously evaluate the quality of content in communication systems, e.g., to assess responses in telecom customer support chatbots. However, the impartiality of these AI “judges” is not guaranteed, and any biases in their evaluation criteria could skew outcomes and undermine user trust. In this paper, we systematically investigate judgment biases across 6 LLM-as-a-judge models spanning both prompt-based and fine-tuned judges under the pointwise scoring setting, encompassing 11 types of biases that cover both implicit and explicit forms. We observed that state-of-the-art LLM judges demonstrate robustness to biased inputs, generally assigning them lower scores than the corresponding clean samples. We further found that fine-tuning an LLM on high-scoring yet biased responses can significantly degrade its performance, highlighting the risk of training on biased data. We also discovered that the judged scores correlate with task difficulty: a challenging dataset like GPQA yields lower average scores, whereas an open-ended reasoning dataset (e.g., JudgeLM-val) sees higher average scores. Finally, we proposed four potential mitigation strategies to ensure fair and reliable AI judging in practical communication scenarios.

title: Efficiently Combining Iterated Local Search and Decomposition: A Comprehensive Study on Multi-Objective Binary Landscapes
authors: Bilel Derbel
abstract: We propose a hybrid framework for binary multi-objective combinatorial optimization that integrates iterated local search with decomposition. The key idea is to perform local search in a cooperative manner when solving the aggregated subproblems induced by decomposition, while using perturbation to escape local optima.We systematically investigate different design options in which local search is employed either as a standalone component or as a tightly integrated cooperative search engine. In particular, we introduce a method that embeds local search within decomposition in a fine-grained manner, enabling cooperative neighborhood exploration to accelerate convergence toward high-quality solutions. In this framework, perturbation acts as a soft-restart mechanism when locally optimal solutions are reached for individual sub-problems. We conduct an extensive experimental study on a broad range of binary landscapes to assess the effectiveness of the proposed hybridization in comparison with recently proposed techniques from the literature. Our empirical results demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently achieves superior results and exhibits a strong anytime efficiency. In addition, we analyze the impact of problem structure and key algorithmic components, such as perturbation strength and the choice of scalarization function, providing insights into the behavior and dynamics of the proposed hybrid approach.

title: Enhanced High-Temperature Reliability of Wire-Bonded 3D SiC Chip Packaging Using Patterned Ti/TaSi₂/Pt Metallization and SiO₂ Insulation
authors: Jiaqi Shi, Buddhi S. Lamsal, Feng Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: This paper presents an improved wire-bonding-based three-dimensional (3D) silicon carbide (SiC) integrated circuit (IC) packaging structure designed for high-temperature operation up to 600 °C. The work targets packaging of SiC logic and memory chips, addressing the need for reliable system-level integration in extreme environments such as aerospace propulsion control and planetary surface exploration. A tantalum silicide (TaSi₂) layer was introduced between titanium (Ti) and platinum (Pt) layers to act as a diffusion barrier against oxygen ingress and gold interdiffusion. In addition, a silicon dioxide (SiO₂) insulation layer was incorporated to suppress leakage currents. Furthermore, patterned metallization was implemented to replicate the pad geometry of practical SiC IC devices. During the 600 °C thermal aging test, daisy chain resistance decreased from approximately 4.7 Ω to 3.2 Ω and stabilized. The wire pull strength stabilized at about 3.7 gram-force (gf) after 2 days of thermal exposure. The die shear strength for chip-to-substrate attachment increased from 11.5 kilogram-force (kgf) to approximately 14 kgf. The die shear strength for chip-to-chip attachment remained stable at around 10.5 kgf. All measured pull and shear strengths satisfied MIL-STD-883 Methods 2011.9 and 2019.9, confirming high interconnect integrity. These results demonstrate the feasibility of a 3D wire-bonded SiC IC packaging platform with Ti/TaSi₂/Pt metallization and SiO₂ insulation, enabling long-duration, high-temperature operation of SiC logic and memory systems in harsh environments.

title: Ultra-thin broadband optical skin based on graphene-stack electrochemical structure via ion intercalation tuning
authors: Xing Cao, Kaixi Bi, Longhao Liu, Guangchen Yin, Jinxian Dong, Lei Hu, Yichi Zhang, Linyu Mei, Xiujian Chou
abstract: Smart optical skins play a critical role in camouflage and intelligent thermal management. Nevertheless, realizing broadband optical modulation within ultra-thin display architectures continue to pose a substantial technical challenge. Graphene’s zero-bandgap structure facilitates broadband optical absorption modulation through the continuous modulation of its Fermi level. Among various methods, electrochemical intercalation stands out for its low power consumption and robust cycling stability based on an ultra-thin film architecture. This study introduces an innovative optical display panel based on the lithium-ion electrochemical intercalation method. Experimental results demonstrate that dynamic modulation of the Fermi level achieves a reflectance modulation of 43.07% across the visible spectrum and a transmittance modulation of 35.75% within the long-wave infrared region. A 64-pixel array device—fabricated using a fully flexible material system—achieves a total integration thickness of only 225 μm. The device exhibits a rapid spectral switching time of 491 ms, while maintaining pixel dimensions below 3 mm × 3 mm. Moreover, the modulated graphene sustains a temperature differential of 17.2 °C with respect to the background temperature of the heat source. This study establishes both a robust physical foundation and a scalable technical framework to advance the development of multispectral surfaces and next-generation wearable electronics.

title: Agentic Continual Adaptation: Enabling Lifelong Learning in Wireless Channel Estimation
authors: Ahsan Bilal, Muhammad Ahmed Mohsin, Muhammad Umer, Muhammad Ali Jamshed, Ayesha Mohsin, John M. Cioffi, Dean F. Hougen
abstract: As wireless networks evolve toward 6G and beyond, deep learning-based channel estimators face a fundamental challenge: their performance degrades when operating conditions deviate from training distributions. This phenomenon, known as distribution shift, manifests through temporal non-stationarity due to user mobility and operational regime changes across varying signal-to-noise ratios (SNR). Traditional sequential training approaches suffer from catastrophic forgetting, where adaptation to new conditions erases previously learned knowledge. This article introduces Agentic Continual Adaptation (ACA), a framework that formulates continual learning as reinforcement-based sequential decision-making to enable continuous adaptation without forgetting. ACA integrates three synergistic mechanisms: an anchor memory that detects forgetting in real-time through continuous performance monitoring on representative samples, a Reinforcement Learning (RL) agent that dynamically selects from seven adaptation strategies balancing plasticity and stability, and a forgetting-aware rollback mechanism that rejects parameter updates exceeding adaptive degradation thresholds. Experimental evaluation across ten SNR regimes demonstrates 4.3% average improvement in channel estimation accuracy with peak gains of 13.6% at challenging low-SNR conditions, while triggering rollback on only 7.5% of parameter updates. This work provides a practical framework for continual adaptation in next-generation wireless networks, addressing an important part of the gap between laboratory performance and real-world deployment.

title: An Efficient Approach with Theoretical Guarantees to Simultaneously Reconstruct Activity and Attenuation Sinogram for TOF-PET_supp1-3697651.pdf
authors: Chong Chen
abstract: In positron emission tomography (PET), it is indispensable to perform attenuation correction in order to obtain quantitatively accurate activity (tracer distribution) in the body. Generally, this is carried out based on the regulated or synthetic attenuation map obtained by computed tomography or magnetic resonance imaging respectively. However, except for errors in the attenuation correction factors obtained, the additional scan not only brings in more radiation dose and/or increases the scanning time but also leads to severe misalignments induced by the body motion during and between the two sequential scans. To address these issues, based on Maximum Likelihood estimation, we propose a new mathematical model for simultaneously reconstructing the Activity and Attenuation Sinogram, referred to as MLAAS, from time-of-flight (TOF)-PET emission data only. Particularly, in the proposed model, we make full use of the exclusively exponential form of the attenuation correction factors, and consider the constraint on a total amount of the activity in some mask region. Furthermore, we analyze the well-posedness of the model, including the existence, uniqueness and stability of its solution. We propose an alternating update algorithm to solve the model, and also analyze its convergence. Finally, numerical experiments with various emission data demonstrate that the proposed method MLAAS is of numerical convergence and robust to noise, and outperforms some state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency, and enables TOF-PET to have the capability of autonomous attenuation correction.

title: Deconvolution method using B-splines
authors: Zdeněk Kopecký, Věra Mazánková, David Trunec
abstract: In this paper, we present a new algorithm to solve a deconvolution problem, which is mathematically described by the Fredholm integral equation of the first kind. This problem has to be solved in many measurements. The solution of this problem with experimental data is ill-conditioned and therefore difficult to solve numerically. It was assumed that the measured data have a Poisson distribution. The maximum likelihood method was used for the solution and the function sought was assumed to be a spline represented in a B-spline basis. The algorithm was tested using known data and it was found that the errors are smaller than those of the usually used methods. The results of the developed algorithm for the deconvolution of the neutron spectroscopy measurement with organic scintillators are presented.

title: A*VL: Incorporating A* and Visual Perception for High-Precision Vehicle Localization
authors: Huei-Yung Lin, Yu-Xuan Chen, Hao-Che Hsu
abstract: With continuous advances of autonomous driving technologies, the demand for precise vehicle positioning has grown significantly. Standard civilian GPS still suffers from positioning errors of approximately 3 to 5 meters, which is insufficient for rapidly evolving advanced driver assistance systems requiring the lane-level accuracy. In this paper, we present A*VL, a high-precision vehicle localization framework based on driving images and satellite maps. Instead of relying on the expensive LiDAR systems or high-definition maps, the proposed method extracts environmental features from driving scenes through lane detection, semantic segmentation, and geolocation, while satellite image retrieve from coarse GPS coordinates. More importantly, we incorporate the A*-based path-aware loss into training process, which transforms road boundary constraints into learnable penalty signals and also encourages the predicted localization results to remain consistent with road topology rather than merely fitting coordinate values. In addition, several datasets containing satellite maps and latitude/longitude annotations of diverse road scenes are established for evaluation and benchmarking. Experimental results and ablation analyses demonstrate that the proposed A* constraint effectively improves vehicle positioning performance. Several datasets containing satellite maps and latitude/longitude coordinates of various road scenes are established for evaluation and benchmarking. Code and datasets are available publicly at https://github.com/JimHsu0007/vehicle-positioning.

title: HeXinTimer: An AI-Enhanced STA Framework Supporting CCS and SI with Signoff Accuracy
authors: Chao Yan, Zishu Wu, Jingjia Xu, Ziwei Liu, Boyuan Liu, Xi Chen, Zefeng Wang
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: STA tools play a critical role in signoff, requiring exceptional accuracy to ensure circuit reliability. HeXinTimer is an industry-grade STA solution designed to support advanced modeling with high precision. This work introduces the following key contributions: (1) a sophisticated stage delay calculation methodology that incorporates Composite Current Source (CCS) driver and receiver models with distributed RC networks, achieving superior timing accuracy; (2) a hybrid framework that combines traditional SI flow with AI predictors, utilizing internal tool data for both training and inference, which significantly enhances accuracy and performance in delta delay estimation; (3) a robust and efficient root-finding algorithm that integrates the Traub-Ostrowski iteration and bisection method to enhance computational performance; and (4) a stage-based testing and comparison framework for comprehensive validation. HeXinTimer achieves accuracy within $3\%$ of SPICE results, while maintaining performance comparable to industry-leading tools.

title: Secure RIS-Aided Multicasting: Globally Optimal Beam Management and Discrete-Phase RIS Configuration
authors: Luis F. Abanto-Leon, Setareh Maghsudi
abstract: Reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) are poised to revolutionize wireless multicasting by enabling extended coverage and reliable operation in obstructed environments. These benefits, however, can be undermined by security vulnerabilities arising from practical deployment factors. This work addresses three such critical factors, (i) the discrete nature of RIS phase shifts, (ii) the presence of colluding eavesdroppers, and (iii) the inefficiency of static illumination beams, each threatening security if not properly accounted for in system design. To mitigate these issues, we formulate a joint resource allocation problem that minimizes the wiretap signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) across all eavesdroppers by co-optimizing the RIS configuration and the base station (BS) beam management. This yields a complex, nonconvex mixed-integer nonlinear program (MINLP), which we equivalently reformulate into a tractable mixed-integer quadratically constrained program (MIQCP) solvable to global optimality. Numerical results confirm that the proposed scheme significantly bolsters security, suppressing the wiretap SNR by up to 58% compared to existing baselines.

title: Inhomogeneous Plasma Parameters Inversion Based on Convolutional Neural Network
authors: Liping Gan, Lixin Guo, Haichao Zhan, Hao Du
abstract: Electron density, collision frequency, and plasma thickness are important parameters for characterizing plasma properties. Thus, obtaining these parameters accurately is a prerequisite for studying the interaction between plasma and electromagnetic (EM) waves. In this study, the electron density, collision frequency, and thickness of inhomogeneous plasma were estimated through a convolutional neural network (CNN). Compared with traditional plasma diagnostic methods, the CNN avoids complicated calculations and experimental construction. The performance of the proposed approach was evaluated and analyzed by comparison with the backpropagation (BP) neural network. Results indicate that the CNN can be used to estimate plasma parameters based on the reflection and transmission waves. Furthermore, the CNN provides better performance than the BP neural network to retrieve plasma electron density distribution, and the electron density distribution mean absolute percentage errors (MAPEs) are less than 2%. The greater the range variation of electron density, the greater the relative errors will be. Importantly, through the CNN, multiple plasma parameters can be inverted simultaneously. For collision frequency and plasma thickness, the outputs of the CNN are also consistent with the test data. This study provides an important theoretical reference for diagnosing inhomogeneous plasma parameters.

title: Force Control of an Underactuated Tendon-Driven Manipulator_supp2-3688030.mp4
authors: Nicolas Jacques Serge Testard
abstract: This article investigates the application of force controls on a tendon-driven underactuated robotic arm. A direct use of classical hybrid force/motion control based on computed torque control can lead to instability for this type of system. Reformulating the hybrid force/motion control using the pseudo-computed torque control (PCTC) framework leads to a stable control strategy. Finally, it is shown that applying admittance control in combination with PCTC also results in stable behavior when exerting a force against a wall or in a cobotics context. These results are then validated through experimentation.

title: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Circuits and Systems Publication Information
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Circuits and Systems

title: Two-Stage Nested Magnetic Field Control System Based on Time-Frequency Domain Collaboration for Magnetically Shielded Room
authors: Yimin Chen, Suhang Li, Bangcheng Han, Xiaoting Hong, Haifeng Zhang, Fengwen Zhao, Rui Feng
abstract: The complex electromagnetic disturbances often result in excessively high magnetic field noise. This article proposes a two-stage nested magnetic field control system based on time-frequency domain collaboration for a magnetically shielded room (MSR) to suppress the disturbance magnetic field and reduce magnetic field noise. The system creates a high-bandwidth external control system by placing external auxiliary coils and a fluxgate sensor outside the MSR, avoiding mutual interference with the internal control system. Subsequently, the system uses a self-tuning proportional-integral-derivative (PID) based on pole placement control and the gradient algorithm based on discrete Fourier transform to obtain the optimal internal noise level. This proposed method has effectively increased the control bandwidth while avoiding the design of complex two-stage decoupling strategies. Compared with the traditional only internal control system, the average noise suppression ability within the target frequency band has been improved by more than 20<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf{\%}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>.

title: Breaking the Communication-Accuracy Trade-off: A Sparsified Information Diffusion Framework for Multi-Agent Collaborative Perception_supp1-3689715.pdf
authors: Xinlei Chen
abstract: The growing relevance of multi-agent systems has drawn increasing focus on communication-efficient filters for collaborative perception to alleviate the system's communication burden. While the event-triggered (ET) mechanism can improve communication efficiency in collaborative state estimation, an inevitable trade-off exists between estimation accuracy and communication cost in ET filters. This paper proposes a fast and accurate ET diffusion-based filter for real-time multi-agent collaborative target tracking, aiming to reduce the system's data transmission without compromise in tracking performance. The proposed filter achieves improved tracking accuracy, reduced data transmission, and accelerated convergence using an error-minimized ET cubature information filter (CIF) for local estimation, and a correlation-aware diffusion strategy for global fusion. The experimental results confirm the scalability of the proposed EDC-CIF algorithm and demonstrate its efficacy in simultaneously reducing estimation error and computation time while significantly enhancing communication efficiency.

title: Future-Trend-Aware Filter-Based PD-MRAC Method for Quadrotors With Unknown Strong Disturbances_supp1-3696040.mp4
authors: Yanhua Yang
abstract: Robust flight in complex and windy environments is critical for both single and multiple quadrotors. Existing methods either learn disturbance model at high computational cost or use error-based adaptive control with a speed-stability trade-off that makes tuning difficult. To address these issues, this paper proposes a future-trend-aware filter-based PD-MRAC (Proportional-Derivative Model Reference Adaptive Control) for single quadrotor and a distributed PD-MRAC for multiple quadrotor formation. By embedding a trend-aware derivative term in the adaptive update laws, the controller obtains anticipatory information about the error evolution, enabling rapid adaptation while mitigating oscillations. For more disturbance-sensitive multi-quadrotors, we design a robust distributed protocol under a directed graph, improving resilience to disturbances. The approach maintains low computational cost and supports fast adaptive updates. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments validate improvement. For single quadrotor, RMSE reduced by around 57% versus the baselines and by around 12% versus the DJI Mavic 2. For multi-quadrotors, formation results show enhanced robustness in simulation and effective real-world indoor/outdoor experiments under strong winds. Our project page is at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://xiongtao-shi.github.io/PD-MRAC/</uri>.

title: A 40k fps 99.5 dB Wide Dynamic Range PWM Image Sensor Featuring a Low-Power Pixel Array
authors: Xiaopei Shi, Ruoyu Ji, Kaiming Nie, Zhiyuan Gao, Jing Gao, Yu Xin, Jiangtao Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This paper presents a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$500\times 1000$</tex-math> </inline-formula> array pulse-width modulation (PWM) sensor with wide dynamic range, low power consumption fabricated in 110-nm CIS FSI process. The prototype demonstrates key performance metrics with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$17\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m pixel pitch, achieving 40k fps full-resolution imaging while maintaining 21.5% fill factor and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$20.40\times 11.08$</tex-math> </inline-formula> mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> die size. The chip achieves 99.5 dB dynamic range while maintaining 37.5 pJ/pixel<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\cdot $</tex-math> </inline-formula>frame energy efficiency under dark conditions. Dynamic power management scheme implements adaptive row activation achieving 0.75W dark-current power consumption. By encoding scene information in compressed bit-stream format, the sensor provides high spatiotemporal resolution while eliminating redundancy in raw data transmission. Quantizing light intensity directly in the temporal domain, instead of the voltage domain, enables a wide dynamic range to be achieved at a substantially lower power cost. This parallelized architecture shows strong potential for intelligent vision systems requiring real-time high-speed processing capabilities.

title: System-Level Fault-Tolerant Reconfiguration of 3-D VLSI Processor Arrays Under Multicomponent Failures
authors: Hao Ding, Xiangyong Wang, Yang Xu, Junyan Qian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Three-dimensional (3-D) VLSI processor arrays offer high integration density and scalability, but their increasing system and interconnect complexity pose significant challenges to reliable reconfiguration under permanent hardware failures. Most existing reconfiguration approaches primarily target processing element (PE) faults and provide limited support for interconnect-related failures, such as switch and link faults, which can severely constrain feasible reconfiguration solutions and the achievable size of fault-free subarrays at the system level. This article investigates system-level fault-tolerant reconfiguration of reconfigurable 3-D VLSI processor arrays under multicomponent failures, including PE, switch, and link faults. We first analyze the system-level impact of different fault types and show that interconnect-level faults, especially switch failures, impose more pronounced constraints on reconfiguration effectiveness than PE faults. Based on this observation, a general plane-exclusion mechanism is introduced that can be integrated with representative PE-only reconfiguration schemes to enhance tolerance to switch and link failures. Furthermore, a fault-transformation preprocessing mechanism is developed to model switch failures as equivalent link disconnections, enabling unified system-level handling of heterogeneous faults and improving structural robustness. To mitigate excessive interconnect overhead introduced during reconfiguration, a long-interconnect optimization strategy is incorporated. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed techniques significantly improve reconfiguration capability and scalability. In particular, the fault-transformation mechanism achieves over 90% of the theoretical upper bound in PE utilization under high switch fault densities, nearly doubling PE utilization compared with baseline methods, while the interconnect optimization reduces the number of long interconnects by more than 10% on average across all evaluated cases.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: Subtype-Specific Biomarker Discovery in Leukemia: Bridging Expression Profiling and Machine Learning
authors: Anik Dev Nath, Md Al Amin, Sajib Acharjee Dip, Mahjebin Jerin, Bikash Kumar Paul, Touhid Bhuiyan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Leukemia is a common type of blood cancer that can occur in people of any age. Detecting it early and diagnosing it correctly are very important for successful treatment and better recovery. This study presents a simplified machine learning pipeline for analyzing leukemia cancer subtypes using high-dimensional gene expression data. Initially, variance-based filtering was applied to remove low-information genes, preserving discriminative features for better model performance. Univariate feature selection using ANOVA F-test identified the top 30 genes, followed by t-tests to detect differentially expressed genes (DEGs) for biomarker discovery. For leveraging balanced class weights to address subtype imbalance, machine learning models ware utilized to capture gene expression patterns. Experimental results showed strong performance, achieving 98% accuracy and 99% weighted F1-score for Support Vector Machine(SVM). This research project supports reliable leukemia subtype classification and biomarker discovery, advancing precision oncology.

title: Integrating Post-Quantum Cryptography and AI-Driven Security in Multi-Tier Network Architectures
authors: Mustafa El Bizri, Ahmad M. El-Hajj, Layth Sliman, Ali M. Haidar
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Recent studies quantify post-quantum cryptography (PQC) overhead in isolated TLS handshakes, while intrusion detection research focuses on legacy traffic patterns, leaving the intersection of PQC deployment and security monitoring unexplored. This work bridges this gap with the first systematic assessment of multi-hop PQC networks integrated with AI-based intrusion detection. Through systematic evaluation of four deployment configurations, we quantify that multi-hop PQC incurs <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$17-18 ~\text{ms}$</tex> latency overhead, exceeding single-hop predictions yet operationally feasible. Critically, Isolation Forest maintains 98.11 % detection accuracy despite PQC-modified traffic, significantly outperforming Random Forest <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(94.75 \%, p<0.001)$</tex>. These findings establish that networks can adopt quantum-resistant cryptography without compromising threat detection, providing foundations for integrated security architectures. While results are simulation-based, they offer critical insights for guiding realworld PQC-IDS deployments.

title: Towards Real-Time Service Classification in Tor: A Supervised Machine Learning Approach
authors: Reem Khalife, Bassem Haidar, Carole Bassil
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: The Tor network uses multiple layers of encryption to preserve users anonymity. However, this protection often attracts malicious actors as it reduces the effectiveness of traffic monitoring mechanisms. In this work, we assess the feasibility of using machine learning to perform service level classification of Tor traffic in real-time. We evaluate five supervised learning algorithms: LightGBM, CatBoost, Random Forest, C4.5 and C5.0 trained on a combined dataset consisting of the ISCXTor2016 dataset and a self-collected Tor traffic dataset. A systematic pipeline is developed to analyze model performance under varying preprocessing strategies and feature representation. Among the evaluated models, LightGBM achieved the highest predictive performance, reaching an F1-score of 0.994 when using identifierinclusive features and 0.885 under an identifier-exclusive setting. The top performing models were then deployed in a real-time classification framework. To measure how the system behaves in practice, we introduce two operational metrics, responsiveness and packet efficiency, to quantify real-time performance. Experimental results demonstrate that LightGBM and Random Forest maintain robust performance in live settings, with browsing, audio, and file-transfer services consistently classified with high accuracy.

title: Enhanced Linux Malware Detection Using Control Flow Graphs with Graph2Vec Embedding and Graph Attention Networks
authors: Sorifa Alam, Francis Rudra D Cruze, Rakibul Hasan Nirob, Md. Ruhul Kuddus
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Malware detection in Linux remains a challenging task to modern computing systems as existing techniques struggle to address the constant evolution of sophisticated threats and the advanced obfuscation techniques. Our research investigates graph-based techniques, efficiently examining the limitations in control flow feature extraction from Executable and Linkable Format (ELF) files by capturing their execution pattern and basic blocks information to improve malware detection accuracy, scalability and robustness against new obfuscation techniques. In this work, we propose a Linux malware detection framework that generates Control Flow Graphs (CFGs) from ELF files, embeds them using Graph2Vec and classifies them using Graph Attention Networks (GATs). We have conducted the experiment on recently released 1,385 Linux malware samples from VirusShare and 1,500 benign ELF samples from the Labeled-Elfs repository. Our experimental results illustrate that Graph2Vec with GAT achieves 97.05 % accuracy, 99.46 % area under the curve (AUC) with balanced Precision, Recall and F1-score of 97.05 %. These results demonstrate the framework's success to identify both known and unknown malware samples in ELF files by showing the importance of selecting appropriate feature extraction techniques and machine learning models, as well as the efficacy of graph-based techniques to identify newly sophisticated malware behaviors more efficiently.

title: Ensemble Deep Learning for Automated Multiclass Classification of Gastrointestinal Disorders in Endoscopic Images
authors: Shovan Samanta Turzo, Shafin Ibnul Mohasin, K.M. Arafat Islam, Towsifur Rahman, Md Jahidul Islam Moon, Hossain Mohammad Shuvo, Md Jahidul Islam Mozumdar, Maimun Nahar Rahi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: The approach described in our work is novel in terms of identifying and categorizing aboard medical images of GI related conditions, which include Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD), Esophagitis, Polyps, and Ulcerative Colitis. Chronic digestive disorders and diseases related to GERD include: Esophagitis - swelling of the esophagus, also due to persistent GERD. Four cutting-edge deep learning architectures namely: DeepLabV3+, U-Net, YOLOv4, and combination of all three modality were trained and assessed to address the constraints on their diagnosis of the diseases. This barrier was addressed by a set of 8000 augmented images. DeepLab V3+ was staked with the precision of classification of 94% and U-Net with 82% while the average precision of the YOLOv4 system is 95%. The ensemble model that incorporated the optimal components of all the models combined attained the very high level of 98% accuracy since it succeeded in enhancing the overall performance in diagnosing factors. In this research, ensemble learning has shown promise as a medical image classifier, especially of hard-to-detect gastrointestinal conditions like GERD and Esophagitis. We believe that our approach will open up the way towards more efficient systematized and automated early detection and classification processes which, on a long-run, will help in making clinical decision support systems in gastroenterological care.

title: CST-Logo Shaped Super Wideband Microstrip Antenna with ML-Driven Gain Prediction for Next-Generation Wireless Systems
authors: Anik Paul, Farhin Sultana, Md. Farhad Hossain, Md. Azad Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This paper introduces the design and performance evaluation of a CST-logo shaped super wideband (SWB) microstrip patch antenna optimized for next-generation wireless systems. The antenna is implemented on a Rogers RO4003C substrate (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\epsilon_{R}=3.55$</tex>, thickness 1.6 mm) with annealed copper metallization, achieving a broad impedance bandwidth of 38.6 GHz, spanning from 23.6 GHz to 62.2 GHz. Simulation results confirm excellent impedance matching, with over 85% of the operating frequencies exhibiting return loss below 15 dB, and a VSWR consistently maintained between 1 and 1.7. The proposed antenna delivers stable gain characteristics across the band, ranging from 6 dBi to a peak of 9.45 dBi, with a maximum radiation efficiency of 83%. To further improve performance evaluation, machine learning (ML) is employed to predict gain variation based on slot geometry parameters. Seven regression-based ML models were investigated, with Support Vector Machine (SVM) providing the most accurate prediction of gain trends. This work covers multiple mmWave communication windows, including the 5G FR2 band, Ka-band satellite links, and the 60 GHz ISM band, the proposed antenna demonstrates strong potential for integration into next-generation wireless and high-speed communication systems.

title: A Comprehensive Survey of Driver Behavior Classification in Smart Transportation Systems: From Rule-Based Models to AI Agents
authors: Chaima Tayachi, Olfa Dridi, Mohamed Barkaoui, Habib Fathallah
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Driver behavior classification has emerged as a critical component in intelligent transportation systems, advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS) and semi-autonomous vehicles. The accurate detection of abnormal driving behaviors such as distraction, drowsiness, aggression and impaired driving is essential for improving road safety and traffic efficiency. This paper presents a comprehensive and structured review of recent advancements in driver behavior analysis. It cover traditional computational algorithm, Machine Learning (ML), Deep Learning (DL), hybrid ML/DL frameworks and emerging AI agent-based systems. We systematically analyze methodological trends, input modalities (visual, physiological, vehicle-based, environmental and smartphone sensors) and evaluation strategies. Particular emphasis is placed on multimodal fusion techniques and contextaware AI agents that extend conventional classifiers toward adaptive and decision-capable monitoring systems. This review further identifies critical research challenges, including privacy preservation, cross-driver generalization, real-time deployment constraints and optimal multimodal fusion strategies. Finally, future research directions are outlined, highlighting the role of explainable AI, lightweight embedded architectures and intelligent agent frameworks for next-generation driver monitoring systems.

title: Smokeseer: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Smoke Removal and Scene Reconstruction
authors: Neham Jain, Andrew Jong, Sebastian Scherer, Ioannis Gkioulekas
abstract: Smoke in real-world scenes can severely degrade image quality and hamper visibility. Recent image restoration methods either rely on data-driven priors that are susceptible to hallucinations, or are limited to static low-density smoke. We introduce SmokeSeer, a method for simultaneous 3D scene reconstruction and smoke removal from multi-view video sequences. Our method uses thermal and RGB images, leveraging the reduced scattering in thermal images to see through smoke. We build upon 3D Gaussian splatting to fuse information from the two image modalities, and decompose the scene into smoke and non-smoke components. Unlike prior work, SmokeSeer handles a broad range of smoke densities and adapts to temporally varying smoke. We validate our method on synthetic data and a new real-world smoke dataset with RGB and thermal images. We provide an opensource implementation and data on the project website.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>https://imaging.cs.cmu.edu/smokeseer

title: SNAP: Towards Segmenting Anything in Any Point Cloud
authors: Aniket Gupta, Hanhui Wang, Charles Saunders, Aruni RoyChowdhury, Hanumant Singh, Huaizu Jiang
abstract: Interactive 3D point cloud segmentation enables efficient annotation of complex 3D scenes through user-guided prompts. However, current approaches are typically restricted in scope to a single domain (indoor or outdoor), and to a single form of user interaction (either spatial clicks or textual prompts). Moreover, training on multiple datasets often leads to negative transfer, resulting in domain-specific tools that lack generalizability. To address these limitations, we present SNAP (Segment aNything in Any Point cloud), a unified model for interactive 3D segmentation that supports both point-based and text-based prompts across diverse domains. Our approach achieves cross-domain generalizability by training on 7 datasets spanning indoor, outdoor, and aerial environments, while employing domain-adaptive normalization to prevent negative transfer. For text-prompted segmentation, we automatically generate mask proposals without human intervention and match them against CLIP embeddings of textual queries, enabling both panoptic and open-vocabulary segmentation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SNAP consistently delivers high-quality segmentation results. We achieve state-of-the-art performance on 8 out of 9 zero-shot benchmarks for spatial-prompted segmentation and demonstrate competitive results on all 5 text-prompted benchmarks. These results show that a unified model can match or exceed specialized domain-specific approaches, providing a practical tool for scalable 3D annotation. Project page is at https://neu-vi.github.io/SNAP/

title: Event-Based Multi-Range Radiance Separation and 3D Reconstruction via Line-Scan Pseudo-Square Illumination
authors: Ryuji Hashimoto, Yuta Asano, Shin Ishihara, Bohan Yu, Chu Zhou, Boxin Shi, Imari Sato
abstract: Decomposing scene radiance into physically meaningful components, including direct reflection, interreflection, and scattering, enables a deeper understanding of scene appearance. In this paper, we propose the first method to perform multi-range radiance component separation using only events captured by an event camera, without requiring any additional frame-based measurements. Our approach scans the scene by swiping line-shaped illumination across it, while exploiting the event camera's high temporal resolution and wide dynamic range to recover both direct and multiple global components corresponding to different light propagation distances. To address the noise inherent in event-integration-based radiance recovery, we present a pixel-wise calibration strategy that leverages the reproducibility of per-pixel noise patterns. We demonstrate that this calibration is highly effective in suppressing noise, enabling stable recovery from subtle signals. Moreover, we show that by detecting the timing at which the scanning line passes each pixel, the same line-scan event data can be exploited for coarse 3D reconstruction. Experimental results on real scenes show that our event-based approach achieves faster and finer component separation, while also enabling coarse depth estimation without the exposure control required by frame-based cameras.

title: Data-Efficient Inference of Neural Fluid Fields via SciML Foundation Model
authors: Yuqiu Liu, Jingxuan Xu, Mauricio Soroco, Yunchao Wei, Wuyang Chen
abstract: Recent developments in 3D vision have enabled significant progress in inferring neural fluid fields and realistic rendering of fluid dynamics. However, these methods require dense captures of real-world flow, which demand specialized lab setups, making the process costly and challenging. Scientific machine learning (SciML) foundation models, pretrained on extensive simulations of partial differential equations (PDEs), encode rich multiphysics knowledge and thus provide promising sources of domain priors for inferring fluid fields. Nevertheless, the transferability of these foundation models to real-world vision problems remains largely underexplored. In this work, we demonstrate that SciML foundation models can significantly reduce the data costs of inferring real-world 3D fluid dynamics with improved generalization. Our method leverages strong forecasting capabilities and meaningful representations of SciML foundation models. We equip neural fluid fields with a novel collaborative training that utilizes augmented frames, and fluid features extracted by our foundation model. We demonstrate significant advancements in both quantitative metrics and visual quality over previous methods, improving 9∼36% peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) in future prediction with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$25 \sim 50 \%$</tex> reduction in the number of training frames, thereby showcasing the practical applicability of SciML foundation models in real-world fluid dynamics. We release our code at: https://github.com/delta-lab-ai/SciML-HY.

title: Structure-Grounded Training Strategies Aid Generalization in Stereo Matching
authors: Liangxun Ou, Yuhui Liu, Zhenyang Li, Xiaoyang Bai, Yifan Peng
abstract: Stereo matching networks can suffer from generalization challenges when trained on synthetic data and deployed in real-world settings. While existing methods rely on fine-tuning or pre-trained vision foundation models for cross-domain robustness, we revisit this gap from a training perspective and explore a structure-grounded training design that directly improves generalization of RNN-based stereo matching models using only a limited amount of synthetic stereo data, without changing the network architecture or adding any inference overhead. Specifically, we target all three main modules of a typical stereo matching pipeline: in cost volume construction, we enhance geometric cues through data augmentation; in context encoding, we strengthen semantic guidance via auxiliary multitask context supervision; in recurrent disparity refinement, we regulate update dynamics with depth-update regularization. Experiments on multiple mainstream architectures and diverse real-world datasets suggest consistent gains in robustness, improving RAFT-Stereo by 6.6% on KITTI 2015, IGEV-Stereo by 13.7 % on Middlebury, and DLNR by 55.4 % on ETH3D. These insights reveal the previously overlooked importance of structure-grounded training design for achieving reliable stereo depth estimation under data-scarce, domain-shifted conditions.

title: Automated Detection and CNC Trajectory Generation for Pitting Repair in Aluminum Cylinder Heads Using YOLOv5
authors: Jose Flores, José Magallanes, Joel Figeroa
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: The maintenance of aluminum cylinder heads in internal combustion engines still relies on manual visual inspection and artisanal repair planning, leading to inconsistent results, long intervention times, and a high rate of rework. Although industry has made progress in inspection and machining systems, there are currently no integrated solutions capable of automatically detecting defects and generating CNC toolpaths for their repair, representing a significant technological gap. To address this limitation, this study proposes an automated methodology that combines image processing, pit detection using YOLOv5, and a geometric conversion module that transforms each detected defect into a CNC trajectory adapted to its morphology. This workflow enables, for the first time, an automated closing of the inspection-repair loop. Experimental results show 94.7% accuracy in defect detection, processing times below 10 s per image, and a mean error of 0.28 mm in trajectory generation, validated on 300 real images. The proposed solution demonstrates the feasibility of integrating automatic detection with assisted repair, reducing operator dependence and process variability, and providing a significant advancement toward automation in automotive remanufacturing. This integrated approach demonstrates a viable solution for closing the inspection-repair loop in automotive remanufacturing, reducing human dependency and process variability.

title: Learning Cooperative Task Allocation for Heterogeneous Multi-AUV Teams with QMIX, CommNet, and Task-Conditioned Cross-Agent Attention
authors: Hailin Wang, Shuo Li, Yiqun Wang, Tianyou Qiu, Yiping Li
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Heterogeneous multi-autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) missions require robust cooperative task allocation under partial observability and unreliable acoustic communication. This paper presents a value-based multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework that integrates centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) using QMIX, explicit interagent message passing via CommNet, and task-conditioned crossagent attention for selective information fusion. A 3D underwater task-allocation simulator is developed to model heterogeneous AUV capabilities, discrete motion primitives, and a configurable underwater communication interface, including range-limited adjacency, message corruption, and optional physical-layer constraints. To address training instability in such environments, a stabilization-oriented training strategy is employed, incorporating long-horizon exploration, conservative learning rates, soft target network updates, gradient clipping, and reward shaping. In an underwater search scenario with five heterogeneous AUVs and ten tasks, the learned policy consistently achieves full task completion, completing all tasks with a mean episodic return of 199.2 over 50 evaluation episodes.

title: A Lightweight Dual-Modality Biometric Classifier for Hand and Iris Images
authors: Refat-E-Jannat, Jeba Tasnia, Amimoy Kumar Biswas, Umme Rumman, Jakirul Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Strengthening digital system security, especially by shielding consumers from online fraud and illegal account breaches, is the main goal of this thesis. Unauthorized access to private data has grown to be a major worry in today's connected society, and strong and dependable authentication systems are desperately needed to protect users.A complicated convolutional neural network (CNN) based multimodal biometric identification approach has been presented to overcome this difficulty. Convolutional, pooling, dense layers, and concatenation are used to conduct feature fusion. This method uses restricted and strategic data augmentation and minimum preprocessing to optimize accuracy and resilience by combining several biometric features. With a Test Accuracy of 98.94 %, Genuine Acceptance Rate (GAR) of 97.87 %, Weighted F1 Score(test set) of 98.87 %, and most importantly an Equal Error Rate (EER) of 0.0000, the assessment demonstrates that the model produced extremely encouraging results. The False Acceptance Rate (FAR) and False Rejection Rate (FRR) of the system are identical, therefore EER is a crucial parameter in biometric security. The system's ability to nearly flawlessly differentiate between authentic users and imposters is demonstrated by its zero EER, which ensures far greater security than many current methods.The methodology is also appropriate for real-time biometric authentication because it takes an average of about <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 2 1}$</tex> milliseconds each step. These findings demonstrate that the suggested approach is a good option for high security applications as it not only improves accuracy but also offers more defense against unauthorized access and unethical intrusions.

title: Advanced Seed Classification and Defect Detection Using Convolutional Neural Networks and Unsupervised Learning Techniques
authors: Sirazzum Munira, Atiya Ibnat Lamia, MD. Sakhawat Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This paper introduces a hybrid deep learning framework for automated seed classification and defect detection in agricultural quality control. The system integrates supervised learning through Custom Convolutional Neural Networks (CustomCNN) and Attention-based CNNs (AttentionCNN) with unsupervised techniques including clustering algorithms and autoencoders. Working with high-resolution microscopic soybean seed images, the supervised models achieve test accuracies of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{9 7. 6 5 \%}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{9 7. 5 5 \%}$</tex> respectively for multi-class classification. The unsupervised autoencoder successfully identifies 312 anomalies through reconstruction error analysis. Clustering techniques partition RGB pixel data into three quality classes with silhouette scores up to 0.6810. This hybrid approach addresses key challenges in agricultural image analysis by enabling both accurate classification of labeled data and anomaly detection in unlabeled datasets, with processing times of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{8 0}-\text{1 2 0 m s}$</tex> per seed suitable for real-time deployment in seed sorting facilities.

title: Coherent 1.2–25 μm Supercontinuum in a 1 mm CdTe-on-CdS Rib Waveguide
authors: Jannatul Nayem Priya, Rakayet Rafi, Sagar Mutsuddi, M R Karim, Mohammad Istiaque Reja
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: In this work, a CdTe-on-CdS rib waveguide is designed to generate a broadband mid-infrared supercontinuum (SC) ranging from about 1.2 to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$25 \mu ~\mathrm{m}$</tex>. The waveguide is pumped with a 25-femtosecond pulse at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.90 \mu ~\mathrm{m}$</tex>, with around 2.5 kW of peak power. Mode simulations were done in COMSOL, and pulse propagation was modeled using the generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation (GNLSE). A 1 -mm-long waveguide, when pumped, generates a spectrum stretching from 1.2 to almost <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{2 5 ~} \mu \mathrm{m}$</tex>. The broadening is driven first by self-phase modulation and then, after compression, by soliton fission with dispersive waves taking energy outward. Numerical modeling with the generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation—accounting for loss, Raman response, and self-steepening—has shown that even a 1 mm device can produce a wide, flat spectrum. The coherence stays above 0.9 for wavelengths over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.4 \mu ~\mathrm{m}$</tex>. Such a device is considered a plausible fit for spectroscopy and mid-IR imaging/sensing, provided that long-wavelength losses are managed during fabrication.

title: Robustness Analysis of Neuromorphic Embodied AI Robot for Associative Learning against Adversarial Stickers
authors: Tianze Liu, Kang Jun Bai, Hongyu An
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Neuromorphic embodied Artificial Intelligence (AI) robots have shown the capability to perform associative learning, emulating how animals learn through interactions with their environments. Through such process, the neuromorphic embodied AI robot can memorize concurrent stimuli, such as vibrations and visual cues. However, these sensory inputs often contain perturbations, such as visual adversarial stickers, that can distort perception and interfere with learned associations. This study presents a robust vision processing framework inspired by the animal visual cortex and system, e.g., V1, for a neuromorphic robot capable of detecting and suppressing the interference from adversarial stickers. The proposed system integrates this visual processing module with a neuromorphic embodied AI robot, along with an associative learning mechanism. The robot is evaluated in an open-field maze, where it learns to associate neutral visual landmarks with vibration as an aversive stimulus. Both simulation and experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach maintains high navigation accuracy and stable spatial memory even in the presence of adversarial stickers. Analyzes of trajectories and neural firing patterns further demonstrate that the neuromorphic embodied AI robot with enhanced visual system effectively resists deceptive input and preserves accurate associative learning.

title: BM-HPR Mul: Error Tolerant Hardware Efficient Booth Modified High Precision Redundant Multiplier
authors: Chandan Kumar N S, Yerasi Manoj Reddy, Madhav Rao
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: High-precision and fault-tolerant multipliers are indispensable in energy-constrained computing platforms that operate under transient and process-induced errors. Conventional Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) ensures strong reliability but suffers from high area and power overheads. The High-Precision Redundancy Multiplier (HPR-Mul) mitigates these costs through selective redundancy however, its tunable parameter introduces synthesis irregularities and inconsistent fault resilience. This work presents the Booth-Modified High-Precision Redundancy Multiplier (BM-HPR), which integrates Radix-4 Booth encoding into both full-precision (FP) and reduced-precision (RP) paths while eliminating the tunable parameter to achieve a fixed, synthesis-friendly datapath. The proposed design preserves the compact area of HPR while achieving up to 27.7% area reduction, 65.1% lower power consumption, and 31.8% faster delay compared to TMR. Additionally, it improves delay and power by 19.1% and 53.7%, respectively, over HPR. Fault-injection using random bit-flip models confirms bounded error rates suitable for error-resilient applications. When deployed in a $\mu$-law companding audio system, BM-HPR maintains a stable signal-tonoise ratio (SNR) and low error variance under injected faults, demonstrating its practical applicability. Implemented using the OpenROAD 45 nm flow, BM-HPR demonstrates a reproducible, energy-efficient, and fault-tolerant multiplier architecture suitable for embedded and real-time signal-processing workloads. All design files are made publicly available for reproducibility.

title: Large Scale Tunnel Air-Ground Collaboration With FLISP: Fast LiDAR-IMU Synchronized Path Planner
authors: Fenghe Guo, Runjie Shen, Chenyang Sun, Junrui Zhang, Quanxi Zhan, Yongchun Wang, Junjie Zhang
abstract: Hydropower tunnel inspection is critical for infrastructure integrity yet remains inefficient and hazardous using manual methods. We propose FLISP (Fast LiDAR-IMU Synchronized Path Planner), a mapless planning framework for cooperative UGV-UAV inspection. Unlike traditional map-based paradigms, FLISP features three core contributions: (1) a unified architecture where a single UGV-mounted LiDAR-IMU suite drives synchronized path generation for both platforms; (2) platform-specific solvers utilizing an enhanced Firefly Algorithm for UGV obstacle avoidance and a dynamic iterative optimizer for UAV flight; and (3) a hierarchical refinement strategy ensuring kinematic feasibility without state estimation drift. Benchmarks in a 1.2 km operational tunnel demonstrate that FLISP circumvents structural bottlenecks of map-based methods, eliminating map rasterization overhead (Fast-LIO2 + A*) and sampling instability (LIO-SAM + RRT*). FLISP achieves a 100% success rate with ∼7 ms latency, representing a 7-fold speedup over grid-based and three-order-of-magnitude improvement over sampling-based baselines. Validated in operational hydropower tunnels, this approach offers a scalable solution for robotic inspection in feature-degraded linear infrastructure. A demonstration video is available at https://youtu.be/Y ezs1PfLJ4, and the code at https://github.com/ArchibaldGuo/FLISP.git.
