title: A Lightweight Deep Learning Framework for Channel Estimation in IRS-Assisted OFDM Systems with Element Grouping
authors: Ying Wang, Bowen Jiang
abstract: Deep learning-based channel estimation in IRS-assisted OFDM systems faces high computational complexity. To address this, we propose LightCENet, a lightweight network that integrates residual learning, dilated convolution, and channel attention for efficient channel state information (CSI) acquisition with IRS element grouping. It introduces lightweight denoising and super-resolution modules to refine initial low-dimensional Least square (LS) estimates and recover full CSI through frequency-spatial interpolation. Simulations demonstrate that LightCENet achieves superior estimation accuracy over benchmarks while maintaining low complexity.

title: Enhanced Multiagent Reinforcement-Learning-Aided Adaptive Fractional-Order EADRC for Load Frequency Control of Multiarea Power Systems
authors: Xianyu Wang, Congzhi Huang, Shuzhi Sam Ge
abstract: High penetration of inverter-based renewables introduces pronounced volatility and heterogeneity to power systems. Coordinating heterogeneous frequency regulation units (FRUs) for robust load frequency control (LFC) remains challenging due to their disparate dynamics and capacities. To improve the frequency regulation capability of FRUs, an adaptive fractional-order error-based active disturbance rejection control (FO-EADRC) approach is proposed in this article. First, a fractional-order extended state observer (FO-ESO) is designed to reconstruct and compensate for the total disturbance of each FRU. Each FRU is equipped with an independent FO-EADRC controller, enabling the modular and independent regulation. Next, the closed-loop system stability based on FO-EADRC is analyzed via fractional-order (FO) theory. To further maximize the frequency regulation performance, a multiagent gated recurrent unit (GRU) soft actor–critic (SAC) algorithm is proposed to tune FO-EADRC gains online. Case studies are performed on a two-area power system. Quantitative results indicate that the proposed method yields maximum reductions of 64.5% in frequency deviation and 81.9% in the integral of absolute error compared with conventional methods, reducing the frequency deviation variance by 82.9% under ±50% parameter perturbations. These results demonstrate the effectiveness, significant superiority, and robustness of the adaptive FO-EADRC.

title: Simulated Target Discrimination via Multi-Channel Time-Frequency Correlation Statistical Features
authors: Zhikang Lin, Jialei Liu, Jiazhi Ma, Longfei Shi
publish: IEEE Aerospace and Electronic Systems Magazine
abstract: With dynamically simulated false targets generated by airborne miniature simulation platforms becoming increasingly similar to genuine targets, radar discrimination faces mounting challenges. Due to the difficulty of accessing publicly available echo data from such platforms, this paper constructs a simulated dataset. Unlike conventional methods that directly leverage intrinsic features of genuine targets for discrimination, the proposed approach exploits multi-channel statistical discrepancies for identification, without focusing solely on the inherent characteristics of true or false targets, thereby avoiding reliance on real echo data. The method first constructs echo models for both genuine and simulated false targets by leveraging differences in radar cross-section (RCS) under various transmitter–receiver geometries and employing fundamental radar waveforms to build the simulated echo dataset. The resulting simulated false target echoes exhibit high similarity to genuine target echoes. Subsequently, a multi-channel time-frequency correlation statistical feature is proposed. This is achieved by applying Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) to echoes from each transmitter–receiver channel to extract time-frequency complex matrices, and then computing the mean of the correlation coefficients among these matrices across all channels. Under multiple observations, genuine and simulated false targets show distinct differences in this feature. Finally, a classifier is designed for training and discrimination. Simulation results demonstrate that, under signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions ranging from –18 dB to 0 dB, the proposed method achieves a discrimination accuracy of at least 83.9% and a Kappa coefficient of at least 0.678 for various types of genuine and simulated targets.

title: Optimized HARQ Schemes Employing Multi-Rate Coupled LDPC Codes
authors: Yiyun Jian, Lukasz Lopacinski, Eckhard Grass
abstract: This paper presents a hybrid automatic repeat request (HARQ) scheme based on multi-rate coupled low-density parity-check (LDPC) codes, which can be efficiently implemented by existing 5G New Radio (NR) LDPC matrices. The HARQ scheme enables coupling among parity-check matrix blocks with different code rates, making it well-suited for adaptive modulation and coding (AMC) and ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) applications. To enhance reliability and goodput, the HARQ mechanism can be integrated with incremental redundancy (IR). Simulation results demonstrate that HARQ-IR with multi-rate coupled LDPC codes achieves superior performance, providing up to 10% gains in goodput over conventional HARQ-IR. Additionally, the proposed scheme outperforms single-rate coupled HARQ-IR by an approximately 4% goodput gain, and the optimized multi-rate coupling results in a 0.2 dB reduction in the required signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) at a bit error rate (BER) of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−7</sup>. This approach can be readily extended to support the coupling of different LDPC codes with varying information and codeword lengths. Moreover, the latency, memory usage, and computational complexity are analyzed, showing that multi-rate coupling improves successful decoding and reduces the average age of information (AoI) at high SNR with only a limited increase in complexity.

title: On Interpretation and Feature Extraction of Micro-Doppler Response in Reflected Global Navigation Satellite System Signals to Dynamic Sea Surface
authors: Feng Wang, Chuanrui Tan, Weichen Sun, Xiangchao Ma, Faping Lu, ZhiChao Xu, Linfeng Wang, Jie Li, Lei Yang, Bo Zhang, Dongkai Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Conventional coastal Global Navigation Satellite System-Reflectometry (GNSS-R) for sea state retrieval relies on the decorrelation of GNSS signals caused by rough sea surfaces, lacking the capacity to capture wave motion. This paper demonstrates the detectability of wave motion. Although specular reflection is insensitive to the horizontal component of wave motion, it remains modulated by the vertical wave motion, resulting in a weak time-varying oscillation in the Doppler frequency, referred to as micro-Doppler. A Doppler spectrum, which is related to wave height spectrum, can be derived from the micro-Doppler time series. Besides wave modulation, reflected GNSS signals contain certain low-frequency interferences. To mitigate such interference and reliably extract the micro-Doppler component, a novel method combining dynamic programming and empirical mode decomposition is proposed. Some characteristics of the micro-Doppler are defined to quantize its dependencies on wind speed, wind direction, wave direction, swell, and mean wave period. A significant finding lies in the existence of off-specular scattering in GNSS-R forward geometry, thereby establishing a physical basis for wind direction retrieval. The experimentally obtained relationships between these characteristics and ocean parameters agree well with theoretical predictions, confirming the modulation of wave motion on reflected GNSS signals. The wave height spectrum is derived from the Doppler spectrum. Wind speed, wind direction, significant wave height and mean wave period are retrieved, with root mean square errors (RMSEs) of 2.00 m/s, 32.0°, 0.50 m and 1.5 s, respectively.

title: Current Adaptive Terminal Sliding Mode Control and Neutral Point Clamping Control Strategy for Two-Three-Level DAB Converters
authors: Sen Yang, Xiangyang Xing, Yang Liu, Rui Zhang, Wenlong Ding, Yan Li, Xi Wen
abstract: Two-three-level (2/3L) dual active bridge (DAB) converters with neutral point clamping (NPC) structures are applicable to low- and medium-voltage DC conversion systems. Maintaining stable output voltage and current of the 2/3L DAB converter is a primary challenge. Under uncertain conditions and disturbances, the response speed and robustness of the DAB converter’s output are significantly reduced. Traditional sliding mode control suffers from issues such as chattering and parameter design challenges. Additionally, capacitor-voltage imbalance on the output side increases voltage stress, thereby compromising converter stability. While the fixed-switching-state (FSS) strategy effectively balances output voltages, it offers only a single balancing mode and exhibits limitations under varying voltage error conditions. This article proposes a novel high-performance control strategy for a 2/3L NPC DAB converter under transient conditions. The strategy incorporates two control loops, i.e., a current tracking loop and a voltage balancing loop. The current tracking loop employs a frequency-domain-based adaptive terminal sliding mode controller for output-side current, effectively addressing output uncertainties and disturbances while ensuring dynamic response speed and steady-state performance. For the voltage-balancing loop, this article proposes an extended-fixed-switching-state (EFSS) method. By selecting different modes under varying voltage error conditions, it optimizes voltage balancing, effectively reducing voltage error ripple and enhancing balancing performance. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed scheme is verified experimentally.

title: Prescribed-Time Control of Disturbed Unicycle Mobile Robots Under Output Constraints
authors: Jinyu Ni, Chengyi He, Xiucai Huang, Kai Zhao, Yongduan Song
abstract: In this article, we study the prescribed-time regulation problem for unicycle mobile robots subject to unknown external disturbances, with bounded one- and two-sided time-varying output constraints being considered. We initially transform the under-actuated robot model into a square system relative to the hand position, and again convert the latter into an integrator with unknown disturbances via control gain matrix feedback. For such an integrator, we design a nominal controller in the absence of constraints to regulate the output to its corresponding constraint boundary at an arbitrarily prescribed time, which is able to guarantee the boundedness of all closed-loop signals. Then, by resorting to a family of time-explicit barrier functions (BFs) and some sufficient barrier conditions, we redesign a continuous constrained controller to override the nominal one whenever the constraint violation is imminent, which solves the prescribed-time regulation problem under constraints and maintains the uniform boundedness of all closed-loop signals. Finally, numerical simulations and practical experiments are conducted to validate the efficacy of our methodology.

title: Practical Prescribed-Time Tracking Control for Nonlinear Systems With Prescribed Transient Performance
authors: Xiujun Chai, Yuanlong Li, Lei Qiao
abstract: In this article, we consider the practical prescribed-time (PPT) tracking control problem for nonlinear systems under prescribed transient performance. For this problem, time-varying piecewise functions play an important role. However, most existing results on the PPT control are dependent on a specially defined piecewise function. To reduce the conservativeness resulting from the special piecewise function, we introduce a class of time-varying piecewise functions that contain this widely used piecewise function, and establish a set of sufficient conditions under which the PPT stability of the nonlinear systems can be determined. Moreover, our proposed method can be utilized in controller design such that the high-order strict-feedback systems can achieve the PPT stability with prescribed transient performance. It is noteworthy that our method doesn’t use barrier Lyapunov functions and transfer functions. Simulation and experiment results demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method.

title: An Improved Ultra-Local Model-Based for Model-Free Speed and Current Control of Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors
authors: Yifei Yang, Xiaodong Sun, Zhihui Huang, Teng Li
abstract: To enhance the performance of the speed loop and current loop of permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM), this article proposes a model-free sliding mode speed controller (MFSMSC) and a model-free predictive current controller (MFPCC) based on an improved ultra-local model. First, the conventional MFPCC with an extended state observer (ESO) based on the ultra-local model significantly improves the robustness against motor parameter mismatches. However, in the conventional MFPCC, the estimated disturbance term includes both linear terms with system states and unknown nonlinear terms, which leads to inaccuracies in the observation results. Therefore, a new ultra-local model is proposed to improve the estimation accuracy of disturbances. In addition, an improved sliding mode observer (SMO) is used instead of the ESO to enhance the high-frequency noise suppression performance. On the other hand, to improve the dynamic performance and robustness of the speed loop, a MFSMSC is proposed to replace the conventional proportional-integral (PI) control. Comparative experiments are conducted on a surface-mounted PMSM (SPMSM) test platform to verify the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed control method.

title: Accelerating Machine Learning Applications through Optimized Tensor Decompositions
authors: Emma Andrews, Prabhat Mishra
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Tensors are fundamental in representing highdimensional data across various application domains, including machine learning and quantum many-body simulation. Calculations on high-dimensional data represented as tensors are costly in terms of memory, power, and computation time. Tensor decomposition can reduce the dimensionality of the highdimensional tensors by representing them as a sum or product of several low-dimensional tensors. In this paper, we present an automated framework for tensor decomposition for a series of operations in an application. Specifically, this framework automatically determines the beneficial tensor decomposition based on resource constraints. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework can drastically reduce memory requirements while providing accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art methods.

title: Multi-Node Timing and Power Estimation with Adapter-Based Transfer Learning
authors: Luis Peña Treviñoa, Eric Guerra Ribeirof, Lirida Navinera, Fady Abouzeidf, Philippe Rochef
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: In recent years, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have shown promise in predicting post-route timing and power metrics early in the physical design process. However, a major challenge lies in their limited adaptability to different technology nodes. The training datasets for these models are highly dependent on specific technology nodes, corner parameters, and process variations, limiting their generalizability across various use cases. This constraint reduces their effectiveness in real-world applications, where performance analysis is required across different technology nodes, as in heterogeneous 3D integrated circuits. To address these challenges, we propose a framework based on Graph Neural Networks enhanced with ParameterEfficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) techniques. This approach enables rapid and accurate estimation of power and timing metrics across different technology nodes or corners without requiring full retraining of a large pre-trained model. Experimental results on open-source benchmark designs spanning three technology nodes demonstrate that our framework achieves up to two orders of magnitude runtime improvement, reduces error compared to commercial tools, and streamlines model training adaptability without compromising prediction accuracy.

title: The Influence of Series Chip Number on the Performance of High-Voltage Deep-Ultraviolet LED With a Long Stripe Array
authors: Yuling Wu, Linhao Wang, Chong Wang, Linbo Hao, Kai Guo, Xiaona Zhang, Chunshuang Chu, Kangkai Tian, Haoyan Liu, Naixin Liu, Yonghui Zhang, Zi-Hui Zhang, Jianchang Yan
abstract: In this work, high-voltage (HV) deep-ultraviolet (DUV) light-emitting diodes (LEDs) with a long-stripe array are designed and fabricated with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10\times 18$</tex-math> </inline-formula>mil<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> commercial-grade chips as basic units. Unlike conventional designs that fix the total active area in a square arrangement, this work adopts a fixed single-chip-area strategy and a long-stripe array to enhance heat dissipation. As the number of series-connected chips increases, the light output power (LOP) improves while showing a clear saturation trend beyond three chips. At an input power of 0.5 W, the four-chip device achieves an external quantum efficiency (EQE) that is 19.6% higher than that of the single-chip device. However, increasing the chip number from three to four yields a marginal gain of only 1.8%, indicating rapid saturation. This saturation originates from two competing mechanisms. The light extraction efficiency (LEE) continuously decreases due to the elongated light propagation path. Meanwhile, the self-heating effect intensifies nonlinearly with the chip number, as evidenced by the increasing peak wavelength redshift from 0 to 1.3 nm from LED I to LED IV at 80 mA. Beyond four chips, the negative impact of self-heating outweighs the benefit of adding more chips. Furthermore, it has been found that the ideality factor decreases more rapidly for HV DUV LEDs with more series chips due to the more rapid reduction in series resistance caused by the increased chip temperature. This study provides a quantitative design basis for optimizing the number of series chips in high-power HV DUV LEDs and reveals the competing roles of optical loss and thermal effects in determining the performance saturation limit.

title: Local Test for Unitarily Invariant Properties of Bipartite Quantum States
authors: Kean Chen, Qisheng Wang, Zhicheng Zhang
abstract: We study the power of local test for bipartite quantum states. Our central result is that, for properties of bipartite pure states, unitary invariance on one part implies an optimal (over all global testers) local tester acting only on the other part. As an application, we demonstrate • Purified samples offer no advantage in property testing of mixed states. • A matching lower bound Ω(r2/ε2) for testing the Schmidt rank of bipartite states with perfect completeness, settling an open question raised in the survey of Montanaro and de Wolf (ToC 2016). • A lower bound Ω(( √ n+ √ r)· √ r/ε2) for testing whether an n-partite state is a matrix product state of bond dimension r or ε-far, improving the prior lower bounds Ω( √ n/ε2) by Soleimanifar and Wright (SODA 2022) and Ω( √ r) by Aaronson et al. (ITCS 2024). • A matching lower bound Ω(d/ε2) for testing whether a d-dimensional bipartite state is maximally entangled or ε-far, showing that the algorithm of O’Donnell and Wright (STOC 2015) is optimal for this task. • A query lower bound Ω(d/ √ Δ) for the d-dimensional entanglement entropy problem with gap Δ, improving the prior lower bounds Ω( 4 √ d) by She and Yuen (ITCS 2023) and Ω(1/ √ Δ) by Wang and Zhang (SICOMP 2025) and Weggemans (Quantum 2025). Moreover, we extend our central result to a robust version where the tested states are subject to noise and are not guaranteed to be pure: in this case, one-way LOCC is sufficient to realize the optimal tester.

title: PFDetector: An Accurate and Low-Overhead Method for Detecting Periodic Flows in Network Traffic
authors: Wenkai Mo, Bing Xiong, Qida Xiao, Wen Li, Jinyuan Zhao
abstract: Periodic flows are characterized by repeated appearances at regular time intervals in network traffic. These flows may conceal low-rate DoS attacks that gradually exhaust server resources while evading network traffic detection. Therefore, it is crucial to accurately detect periodic flows for network security. However, existing work on periodic-flow detection often struggles to achieve high detection accuracy, due to the resource constraints of its typically deployed devices such as switches. To resolve this issue, this paper proposes an accurate low-overhead detection method of periodic flows called PFDetector, which consists of two components: new-flow detector and periodic-flow identifier. The first component adopts a Bloom filter to rapidly detect first-appearing flows and skip repeated flows within each time window, significantly reducing computational and memory overhead of subsequent periodic-flow detection. The second component records potential periodic flows and tracks their periodicity for reporting real periodic flows. For the second component, we design a stable flow replacement strategy, which prioritizes flows with higher arrival frequencies and smaller inter-arrival time standard deviations under memory constraints. We further derive the false positive rate of the new-flow detector and the error bound of the periodic-flow identifier by theoretical analysis. Finally, we verify the performance of our proposed method PFDetector by experiments on real network traffic traces. Experimental results indicate that the PFDetector improves detection precision by approximately 4.5%, increases recall by approximately 8%, and reduces the average relative error by more than two times, compared to the state-of-the-art methods.

title: A Low-Cost Dual-Antenna Approach for GNSS Signal Authentication Against Constrained Distributed Spoofing
authors: Yanfeng Hu, Cheng Chen, Shaofeng Bian
abstract: Owing to its cost-effectiveness and dependable accuracy, the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) receiver has become an essential positioning utility across diverse navigation applications. However, its reliance on inherently insecure wireless signals renders it vulnerable to spoofing attacks, posing a significant threat to safety-critical operations. Most existing countermeasures predicate on the common hypothesis that all spoofing signals originate from a single direction, exhibiting performance degradation against distributed spoofing or mixed signal scenarios. Conventional array antennas offer excellent spatial resolution but suffer from high costs and calibration requirements. To address these limitations, this paper presents a novel low-cost dual-antenna approach that innovatively integrates dynamic baseline rotation with Pearson correlation analysis of C/N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">o</sub> sequences. This method extracts spatial signatures by exploiting motion-induced phase differences, enabling reliable detection of spoofing clusters from common directions without requiring specialized hardware or receiver modifications. Experimental results validate the core mechanism: when two satellite signals originate from the same direction, detection probability exceeds 99.5% with 0.01% false probability, providing principled verification for constrained distributed spoofing (where multiple spoofed signals emanate from a single source). The system's hardware independence (based on C/N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">o</sub> data output) and compact design (baseline <1m) enable compatibility with vehicular and UAV platforms, where natural platform motion enhances baseline variability and spatial feature diversity. Simulations and real-world tests demonstrate efficacy against distributed-like spoofing scenarios, offering a practical, multipath-resistant solution for GNSS security enhancement.

title: A Farewell Message From the Editor-in-Chief
authors: Nirwan Ansari, Octavia A. Dobre

title: A Threat Assessment Method for Air Targets Considering Spatiotemporal Interactions Among Multiple Targets
authors: Zhouwei Lou, Weiyi Ge, Ke Xie, Fei Gong, Kanjian Zhang, Shuo Shan, Haikun Wei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Timely and accurate threat assessment of enemy targets is crucial for enhancing command and decision-making capabilities. In modern combat systems, multi-target cooperation and confrontation are widespread, leading to significant spatio-temporal interactions among targets. However, existing methods still focus primarily on assessing single enemy targets and fail to adequately capture the interdependencies among multiple targets. To address this issue, this paper proposes a multi-target-to- multi-target threat assessment framework. Within this framework, a graph neural network model considering spatiotemporal interactions among multiple targets (STIGNN) is proposed, which incorporates an innovatively designed multi-dimensional spatial feature fusion module and an improved temporal convolutional network, used to model complex spatial interaction relationships and temporal dynamics, respectively. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed STIGNN model outperforms existing methods across multiple evaluation metrics. Furthermore, it enables an in-depth analysis of individual target contributions to the overall threat situation, providing interpretable and targeted decision support.

title: Flow-Opt: Scalable Centralized Multi-Robot Trajectory Optimization with Flow Matching and Differentiable Optimization_supp1-3696443.mp4
authors: Arun Kumar Singh
abstract: Centralized trajectory optimization in the joint space of multiple robots allows access to a larger feasible space that can result in smoother trajectories, especially while planning in tight spaces. Unfortunately, it is often computationally intractable beyond a very small swarm size. In this paper, we propose Flow-Opt, a fast learning-based approach for providing high-quality approximations of centralized multi-robot trajectory optimization. Specifically, we reduce the problem to first learning a generative model to sample different candidate trajectories and then using a learned Safety-Filter(SF) to ensure fast inference-time constraint satisfaction. We propose a flow-matching model based on a diffusion transformer (DiT) augmented with state and map encoders, as the generative model. We develop a custom solver for our SF and equip it with a neural network that predicts context-specific initialization. The initialization network is trained in a self-supervised manner, taking advantage of the differentiability of the SF solver. We advance the state-of-the-art in the following respects. First, we show that we can generate trajectories for tens of robots in cluttered environments in a few tens of milliseconds. This is several times faster than existing centralized optimization approaches. Moreover, our approach generates smoother trajectories orders of magnitude faster than competing baselines based on diffusion models. Second, each component of our approach can be batched, allowing us to solve a few tens of problem instances in a fraction of a second. We believe this is the first such result; no existing approach provides such capabilities. Finally, our approach can generate a diverse set of trajectories between a given set of start and goal locations, which can capture different collision-avoidance behaviors.

title: Flow-Opt: Scalable Centralized Multi-Robot Trajectory Optimization with Flow Matching and Differentiable Optimization
authors: Simon Idoko, Prajyot Jadhav, Arun Kumar Singh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Centralized trajectory optimization in the joint space of multiple robots allows access to a larger feasible space that can result in smoother trajectories, especially while planning in tight spaces. Unfortunately, it is often computationally intractable beyond a very small swarm size. In this paper, we propose Flow-Opt, a fast learning-based approach for providing high-quality approximations of centralized multi-robot trajectory optimization. Specifically, we reduce the problem to first learning a generative model to sample different candidate trajectories and then using a learned Safety-Filter(SF) to ensure fast inference-time constraint satisfaction. We propose a flow-matching model based on a diffusion transformer (DiT) augmented with state and map encoders, as the generative model. We develop a custom solver for our SF and equip it with a neural network that predicts context-specific initialization. The initialization network is trained in a self-supervised manner, taking advantage of the differentiability of the SF solver. We advance the state-of-the-art in the following respects. First, we show that we can generate trajectories for tens of robots in cluttered environments in a few tens of milliseconds. This is several times faster than existing centralized optimization approaches. Moreover, our approach generates smoother trajectories orders of magnitude faster than competing baselines based on diffusion models. Second, each component of our approach can be batched, allowing us to solve a few tens of problem instances in a fraction of a second. We believe this is the first such result; no existing approach provides such capabilities. Finally, our approach can generate a diverse set of trajectories between a given set of start and goal locations, which can capture different collision-avoidance behaviors.

title: Learning-Based Statistical Refinement for Denoising_supp1-3691286.pdf
authors: Rihuan Ke
abstract: This work proposes a learning-based statistical refinement method for improving the denoising results of a given denoiser without knowing the precise noise distribution or accessing clean images or calibration data. While there are many existing successful denoising approaches for handling different kinds of noise, they typically require accurate modeling of the images and the noise (implicitly or explicitly), and hence the denoising results can be suboptimal due to different practical factors such as imperfect models, unreliable noise assumptions, or low-quality data. In particular, when clean image samples are not available and there is a lack of knowledge of the underlying noise distribution, which is the case in various practical situations, the results may not align well with the noise statistics. The unawareness of useful statistical information leads to suboptimal results. This work aims to make the best use of the statistical information to improve the consistency between the given denoising results and the noise statistics, under the assumption that the noise is conditionally pixel-wise independent given the clean signal. A method, based on a Bayesian formulation of an auxiliary signal in the noisy data, is proposed for evaluating the consistency of the denoising results, without precise information on noise distribution. By leveraging the statistical information from noisy data, the method enhances the statistical noise consistency and improves denoising quality.

title: Online Outcome-Driven Configuration Selection With Adaptive Grouping for Live Video Streams_supp1-3690872.pdf
authors: Xiangxiang Dai
abstract: Optimizing live video stream configurations, such as resolution, is essential for balancing inference accuracy and bitrate, thereby reducing data volume and conserving network resources in intelligent video analytics. Existing systems predominantly rely on offline or periodic strategies using historical data to determine optimal configurations. However, these methods may fall short in managing bitrate, which is highly sensitive to dynamic variations in video content and can benefit from real-time adjustments. To address these limitations, we propose the <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">O <inline-formula><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$_{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> MEGA</monospace> (Online Outcome-driven Grouping Configuration Adaptation) framework. Motivated by unique experimental observations, <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">O <inline-formula><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$_{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> MEGA</monospace> adapts configurations in an “outcome-driven” manner by directly utilizing real-time online bitrate outcomes from encoded video streams. This approach allows efficient and lightweight integration of outcome feedback into configuration decisions without incurring significant analysis overhead that could negate the benefits of adaptation. Moreover, <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">O <inline-formula><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$_{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> MEGA</monospace> introduces a dual grouping mechanism that enables cross-camera collaboration on configuration sharing, expediting configuration selection in densely deployed camera networks. Unlike existing methods that rely solely on spatio-temporal correlations, our framework groups cameras based on real-time outcome feedback, thus adaptively optimizing group-level performance online. We provide theoretical guarantees to ensure the interpretability and effectiveness of the framework. Experimental results on a testbed with four NVIDIA development kits and an edge server demonstrate up to 6.8% accuracy improvement or 13.2% data volume reduction compared to the state-of-the-art under four typical vision queries.

title: RD-PCN: A Secure Role-Differentiated Payment Channel Network for Heterogeneous Nodes_supp1-3692826.pdf
authors: Ning Lu
abstract: Scalability is a critical challenge for blockchain-based cryptocurrencies and has become a significant bottleneck in large-scale applications such as retail. Payment channel networks (PCNs) are among the most promising solutions to this challenge. However, nodes in real-world PCNs exhibit heterogeneous behaviors and capabilities, causing existing schemes to perform poorly when deployed in practical environments. In this paper, we first demonstrate, through a measurement study, the existence of node heterogeneity in real-world PCNs; and then propose RD-PCN, a role-differentiated PCN that accommodates such heterogeneity by explicitly separating node roles and responsibilities. We address two key challenges in realizing RD-PCN. To improve channel fund utilization, RD-PCN introduces multi-party payment channels (MPCs) to selectively connect nodes at the network edge. We accordingly design a PCN-compatible MPC, termed PC-MPC, representing the first practical solution for deploying MPCs in PCNs. To securely outsource routing tasks to resource-rich nodes, we design a privacy-preserving and fair routing scheme based on trusted execution environments and the proposed topology synchronization mechanism. We prove the security of PC-MPC and the routing scheme under the universally composable framework. Experiments show that RD-PCN improves the payment success ratio by at least 18.9% compared to representative schemes, while increasing fund utilization by 60%.

title: Robustness of Prompting: Enhancing Robustness of Large Language Models Against Prompt Attacks
authors: Lin Mu, Guowei Chu, Li Ni, Lei Sang, Yiwen Zhang
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance across various tasks by effectively utilizing a prompting strategy. However, they are highly sensitive to input perturbations, such as typographical errors or slight character order errors, which can significantly impair their performance. Despite advances in prompting techniques such as Chain-of-Thought and automatic prompt generation, developing a prompting strategy that explicitly mitigates the negative impact of such perturbations remains an open challenge. To bridge this gap, we propose Robustness of Prompting (RoP), a novel prompting strategy aimed at enhancing the robustness of LLMs. RoP consists of two stages: Error Correction and Guidance. In the Error Correction stage, RoP applies diverse perturbation methods to generate adversarial examples, which are used to generate prompts that correct input errors automatically. In the Guidance stage, RoP generates an optimal guidance prompt based on the corrected input, guiding the model to generate more robust and accurate inferences. Through comprehensive experiments spanning arithmetic, commonsense, and logical reasoning tasks, we demonstrate that RoP significantly improves LLMs’ robustness against adversarial perturbations. Crucially, it preserves model accuracy with only minimal degradation compared to clean input scenarios, thereby establishing RoP as a practical and effective approach for enhancing LLM robustness in real-world applications.1

title: FlexGO: A Unified Overlay for General Graph Neural Network Acceleration
authors: Pramath Balisavira Guruprasanna, Rishov Sarkar, Cong Hao
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) provide abundant parallel computing resources suitable for energyefficient and low-latency inference of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Specialized FPGA-based GNN accelerators are inflexible. To address this limitation, prior works individually accelerate multiple GNN classes to cope with the increasing demand for multi-family GNN inference. However, the reconfiguration overhead incurred from model and parameter variations across such accelerators can be fatal for latency-critical applications. Overlay processors significantly reduce FPGA reconfiguration time with compiler frameworks that map software-compiled GNN executables to a fixed GNN-agnostic FPGA microarchitecture. Limitations of state-of-the-art (SOTA) overlay processors include performance degradation and involvement of complex compiler frameworks. To retain generality and simultaneously inhibit performance loss, in this paper, we propose FlexGO, a compiler-free workload-agnostic unified overlay with both GNNspecific and GNN-agnostic hardware units for real-time inference. First, we propose a simple hardware-decoded instruction set for the run-time selection of GNN inference. Second, we implement a graph-preprocessing-free and scalable dataflow architecture that can effectively parallelize and optimize the node embedding, edge embedding, and message-passing stages of inference. Third, we achieve $33.74 {\%}, 58.51 {\%}, 61.55 {\%}$, and 63.58% reduction in DSPs, LUTs, FFs, and BRAMs, respectively, against the cumulative resource utilization for all the supported GNNs’ specialized implementations. Fourth, we implement our architecture on the Xilinx Alveo U50 FPGA board to evaluate the on-board end-toend performance. FlexGO achieves a speedup of up to 16.23$\mathbf{2 7 2. 3 5} \times$ against CPU (Intel 6226R) and 1.31-514.80 $\times$ against GPU (Nvidia A6000) (with batch sizes 1 through 1024). We also outperform a SOTA software-programmable NPU accelerator by an average speedup of $\mathbf{1. 5 5} \times$ for two models against their latency and throughput-optimized configurations across two datasets. We open-source our implementation code and bitstreams for all our design configurations on GitHub.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>https://github.com/sharc-lab/FlexGO.git

title: Inter-Pixel Binary Edge-Detection (IPBED) Array
authors: Md Rahatul Islam Udoy, Md Mazharul Islam, Garrett Rose, Ahmedullah Aziz
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Edge detection is a core operation in computer vision, providing compact boundary cues for segmentation, recognition, tracking, and navigation. This paper presents an inter-pixel binary edge-detection (IPBED) pixel array design that decides edge or no edge directly on pixel boundaries and evaluates the design in circuit and array-level simulation. Only rail-to-rail binary (edge or no edge) signals traverse the array and periphery, which reduces bandwidth, improves noise tolerance, and simplifies readout. Threshold placement is tunable at design time (through device sizing and material choice) and in real time (by timing control). A layout-level prototype in $\mathbf{6 5 - n m}$ CMOS achieves a $\mathbf{6. 8 5 -} \mu \mathrm{m}$ pixel pitch, which explicitly includes the pixel and processing circuitry. The power-delay-product is about 4.6 fJ per pixel (including processing circuitry). Monte Carlo analysis over $\mathbf{1 0, 0 0 0}$ instances indicates robustness to device variation. To keep the design lightweight, we retain only vertical and horizontal neighbor comparisons. Even so, on-scene data show that the emitted edge maps agree with standard software detectors, with $\mathrm{F} 1 \approx 0.75-0.85$ versus a morphology baseline, Sobel, Prewitt, and Canny. IPBED is intended as an application-specific sensor for edge-centric tasks.

title: VAE-Enabled Design Space Exploration for Heterogeneous Approximate Matrix Multiplication Accelerators
authors: Niranjan Gopal, A Nishith, Madhav Rao
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Matrix multiplication underpins a wide range of compute-intensive workloads, from image processing to deep learning inference. This work presents a novel hardware accelerator architecture that combines Strassen’s algorithm, systolic array optimizations, and approximate computing to enhance efficiency in area and power. At the leaf recursion level of Strassen’s algorithm, conventional exact multipliers (EMs) are selectively replaced with approximate multipliers (AMs). To preserve accuracy in critical paths, time-shared EMs are retained, balancing efficiency with correctness. The accelerator is built around multiple $64 \times 64$ systolic arrays comprising 4096 processing elements (PEs). Each PE dynamically selects from ten available AMs, producing a vast discrete design space. To navigate this combinatorial complexity, we propose a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) that compresses PE-level approximation configurations into a compact latent space. A multi-objective evolutionary algorithm (MOEA) then searches this space to optimize hardware-level metrics (area, power, delay) alongside application-level performance indicators such as SSIM, PSNR, and classification accuracy. Our design maintains numerical stability across recursive layers, ensuring the practical realization of Strassen’s asymptotic advantage over conventional multiplication. Through MOEA-driven exploration, we identified accelerator designs that reduce hardware footprint by up to 33% and improve power-delay product by 21%, with only a 0.9% drop in CNN classification accuracy. By decoding latent codes back to full PE configurations, the VAE enables fast and high-quality exploration, generating a composite Pareto frontier of optimized designs. Overall, this framework demonstrates effective co-optimization of hardware efficiency and application accuracy, providing low-cost, high-performance matrix multiplication tailored for approximate computing workloads.

title: STREAMLOCK: Stream Cipher-Enabled Cryptographic Logic Locking
authors: Nahush Tambe, Dhruva Aklekar, Naseeruddin Lodge, Vineet Chadalavada, Fareena Saqib
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: As semiconductor supply chains become increasingly complex and globally distributed, the risks of intellectual property (IP) piracy, reverse engineering, and hardware tampering continue to intensify. Logic locking is a widely adopted countermeasure, but traditional schemes such as XOR/XNOR-based locking and SARLock provide limited and often brittle resilience against modern attack vectors, and their effectiveness deteriorates as circuit complexity grows. We propose STREAMLOCK, a lightweight stream-cipher-driven logic locking mechanism that integrates a 128-bit Nonlinear Feedback Shift Register (NLFSR)-based keystream generator into classical circuits. By leveraging nonlinear diffusion and dynamic keystream behavior, stream ciphers enhance resistance against unauthorized key recovery. Experimental evaluation on ISCAS’85 benchmarks shows that the proposed scheme maintains 100% functional correctness with the correct key, while a single-bit key error causes approximately 50% output corruption, demonstrating strong diffusion. Despite this increased security, STREAMLOCK incurs moderate area and power overhead, while maintaining interface resource usage comparable to or lower than conventional techniques. These results highlight that lightweight stream ciphers offer a practical and scalable approach for hardware IP protection, achieving strong security with efficient resource utilization.

title: BERT-HIT: A Transformer-Based Approach for Hardware Trojan Detection in Gate-Level Netlists
authors: Lizi Zhang, Navid Nader Tehrani, Azadeh Davoodi, Rasit Onur Topaloglu

title: A Compact Gray-Code Quantum Read-only Memory for the NISQ Era
authors: Hao-Yu Lu, Yu-Ting Kao, Yeong-Jar Chang, Chao-Hung Wang, Darsen Lu

title: IEEE Guide for the Selection, Testing, Application, and Installation of 300 V to 500 kV Cables Having Radial-Moisture Barriers and/or Longitudinal Water Blocking
authors: N/A
abstract: Detailed information relating to the design, testing, application, and installation of various types of electrical cables to reduce the deleterious effect of moisture and chemical ingress and resultant failures in service is provided in this guide. This includes single and multiconductor cables over a complete range of voltage ratings. Testing criteria and installation methods are also covered along with many technical references.

title: Simple and Highly Sensitive Detection of GDF15 in Human Urine Using Magnetic Nanoparticle Aggregation
authors: Shin Yabukami, Toru Murayama, Loi Tonthat, Mitsuki Kitakaze, Takeya Sato, Takaaki Abe

title: Bit Efficient Toeplitz Covariance Estimation
authors: Hongwei Xu, Zai Yang
abstract: This paper addresses the problem of estimating Toeplitz covariance matrices from partial entries of randomly quantized samples. To balance the trade-offs among the number of samples, the number of observed entries per sample, and the data resolution, we propose a ruler-based quantized Toeplitz covariance estimator. We derive non-asymptotic upper and lower bounds for the proposed estimator, and analyze the corresponding convergence rates. Our results characterize how sparse observation and coarse quantization affect the performance of the proposed estimator and suggest that reducing data resolution within a certain range has limited impact on estimation accuracy. Numerical experiments are provided to validate the theoretical findings.

title: TTM-KCOSBLS: Open-Set Fault Diagnosis and Few-Shot Class Incremental Learning Framework of Wind Turbine Transmission System with Thermal Twin Model
authors: Shiyu Lin, Hongshan Zhao

title: SAIL-Recon: Large SfM by Augmenting Scene Regression with Localization
authors: Junyuan Deng, Heng Li, Tao Xie, Weiqiang Ren, Qian Zhang, Ping Tan, Xiaoyang Guo
abstract: Scene regression methods, such as VGGT [86], solve the Structure-from-Motion (SfM) problem by directly regressing camera poses and 3D scene structures from input images. They demonstrate impressive performance in handling images under extreme viewpoint changes. However, these methods struggle to handle a large number of input images. To address this problem, we introduce SAIL-Recon, a feed-forward Transformer for large scale SfM, by augmenting the scene regression network with visual localization capabilities. Specifically, our method first computes a neural scene representation tokens from a subset of anchor images. The regression network is then fine-tuned to reconstruct all input images conditioned on this neural scene representation. Comprehensive experiments show that our method not only scales efficiently to large-scale scenes, but also achieves state-of-the-art results on both camera pose estimation and novel view synthesis benchmarks, including TUM-RGBD, CO3Dv2, and Tanks & Temples. Code and models are publicly available here.

title: FastMap: Revisiting Structure from Motion Through First-Order Optimization
authors: Jiahao Li, Haochen Wang, Muhammad Zubair Irshad, Igor Vasiljevic, Matthew R. Walter, Vitor Campagnolo Guizilini, Greg Shakhnarovich

title: SpotLight: Shadow-Guided Object Relighting via Diffusion
authors: Frédéric Fortier-Chouinard, Zitian Zhang, Louis-Etienne Messier, Mathieu Garon, Anand Bhattad, Jean-François Lalonde
abstract: Recent work has shown that diffusion models can serve as powerful neural rendering engines that can be leveraged for inserting virtual objects into images. However, unlike typical physics-based renderers, these neural rendering engines are limited by the lack of manual control over the lighting, which is often essential for improving or personalizing the desired image outcome. In this paper, we show that precise and controllable lighting can be achieved without any additional training, simply by supplying a coarse shadow hint for the object. Indeed, we show that injecting only the desired shadow of the object into a pre-trained diffusionbased neural renderer enables it to accurately shade the object according to the desired light position, while properly harmonizing the object (and its shadow) within the target background image. Our method, SpotLight, is entirely training-free and leverages existing neural rendering approaches to achieve controllable relighting. We show that SpotLight achieves superior object compositing results, both quantitatively and perceptually, as confirmed by a user study, outperforming existing diffusion-based models specifically designed for relighting. We also demonstrate other applications, such as hand-scribbling shadows and full-image relighting, demonstrating its versatility.

title: MoAngelo: Motion-Aware Neural Surface Reconstruction for Dynamic Scenes
authors: Mohamed Ebbed, Zorah Lähner
abstract: Dynamic scene reconstruction from multi-view videos remains a fundamental challenge in computer vision. While recent neural surface reconstruction methods have achieved remarkable results in static 3D reconstruction, extending these approaches with comparable quality for dynamic scenes introduces significant computational and representational challenges. Existing dynamic methods focus on novel-view synthesis, therefore, their extracted meshes tend to be noisy. Even approaches aiming for geometric fidelity often result in too smooth meshes due to the ill-posedness of the problem. We present a novel framework for highly detailed dynamic reconstruction that extends the static 3D reconstruction method NeuralAngelo to work in dynamic settings. To that end, we start with a high-quality template scene reconstruction from the initial frame using NeuralAngelo, and then jointly optimize deformation fields that track the template and refine it based on the temporal sequence. This flexible template allows updating the geometry to include changes that cannot be modeled with the deformation field, for instance occluded parts or the changes in the topology. We show superior reconstruction accuracy in comparison to previous state-of-the-art methods on the ActorsHQ dataset.

title: Feature-Based Image Selection (FBIS) for Enhancing 3D Reconstruction in Agriculture
authors: Jomana A. Abdelmoaty, Zoltán Istenes
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: The future of technology increasingly relies on artificial intelligence systems, which must be capable of perceiving visual scenes like humans to interpret and understand their surroundings, making 3D reconstruction a fundamental component of this capability. This paper focuses on 3D reconstruction of an apple plantation for monitoring purposes using a mobile robot equipped with an RGB-D RealSense D435 camera. It presents a comparative analysis of two widely used reconstruction tools, COLMAP and Open3D, which utilise different input data types: RGB for COLMAP and RGB-D for Open3D, to identify the most suitable approach for outdoor environments exposed to sunlight. To reduce processing overhead and improve reconstruction efficiency, a Feature-Based Image Selection (FBIS) algorithm is proposed. FBIS leverages feature extraction techniques to eliminate redundant images that lack novel features. By evaluating the performance of COLMAP and Open3D, this work offers insights into their suitability for field conditions and proposes an effective strategy to optimise 3D reconstruction in agricultural settings. The apple plantation datasets are available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17183457.

title: Artificial Neural Networks for Solving Forward and Inverse Kinematics on a 7 Degree-of-Freedom Robotic Manipulator
authors: Aanchal Save
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Traditionally, forward or direct kinematics refers to computing the end-effector pose of a robotic arm given its joint angles, while conversely, inverse kinematics is used to find the joint angles needed to reach a desired end-effector pose. Unlike forward kinematics, inverse kinematics lacks a general deterministic solution and becomes increasingly complex as the number of degrees of freedom (DoF) grows. While Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) have shown promise in solving inverse kinematics for low-DoF systems, their accuracy diminishes in higher-DoF configurations. This study presents a neural network-based approach for both forward and inverse kinematics on a 7-DoF robotic arm, detailing the data generation process, model architecture, and training methodology. The forward kinematics model is used to iteratively validate the inverse kinematics model, a unique solution that allows the model to achieve a median endeffector position error of 9.95 centimeters.

title: Vibration-Based Automatic Fault Detection for Rotary-Percussive Drilling Via IT2 T-S Fuzzy Modeling and Adaptive Observer
authors: Satyam Paul, Magnus Löfstrand
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: This paper presents a vibration–based, observer–driven fault detection scheme for rotary–percussive drilling that is both physics–grounded and robust to operating–regime variability. A compact axial–torsional plant with single–cutter bit–rock interaction captures loading/unloading asymmetry and frictional torque coupling. To compensate for salient nonlinearities and premise uncertainty (e.g., intermittent contact, rate effects), we embed the dynamics in an interval type–2 Takagi–Sugeno (IT2–T–S) fuzzy framework with explicitly defined IF–THEN rules and type reduction, yielding a convex blend of local linear models suitable for analysis and synthesis. An adaptive Luenberger observer is then designed to (i) reconstruct the nominal vibration response, (ii) generate a residual sensitive to faults yet tolerant to modelling errors and measurement noise, and (iii) deliver an online estimate of an unknown axial fault input. A Lyapunov function with vertex LMI conditions guarantees exponential convergence in the fault–free case and uniform ultimate boundedness under bounded faults; the fault–estimation update law is derived to ensure closed–loop stability. Simulations with percussion–style axial forcing demonstrate three key outcomes on a short time horizon: residuals remain within a noise–based threshold pre–fault and cross the band at the fault onset; the estimated fault rapidly converges to the true magnitude with negligible steady bias; and the state–error norm decays quickly pre–fault and exhibits a bounded transient post–fault. The results indicate that the proposed IT2–T–S adaptive observer provides an implementation–ready path to reliable, vibration–based fault detection for drilling systems.

title: Harnessing AI for Sustainable Agriculture: Predicting Plant Growth in Greenhouse Systems Under Climate Change Pressures
authors: Arka Dutta, Ashim Dey, Annesha Das
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: In the modern era, climate change is a significant worry for nearly every nation today. This global warming poses a threat to crop production, which is expected to impact food security in the future. Hence, greenhouse cultivation has emerged as a viable solution. It is essential to forecast plant growth rates based on plants' physiological characteristics in a greenhouse setting. To achieve this, A publicly available dataset of 30,000 plant growth rates in greenhouse conditions is analyzed and utilized here. Nine machine learning methods were explored to forecast the growth rate of plants in a greenhouse setting. This research revealed that the highest <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex> value of approximately 99% was obtained from several models, including SVR, Random Forest, CatBoost, KNN, XGBoost, and Decision Tree. However, based on other evaluation metrics, the KNN model achieved the best overall performance, achieving the lowest error values among all models. Rigorous validation confirmed the robustness of the models, and feature analysis highlighted the dominant role of traits in driving plant growth. The results indicate a strong correlation between plant height growth and variables such as temperature, leaf area, and chlorophyll content. This method improves model performance and offers insights for precision farming techniques. It is anticipated that this study will aid the area of greenhouse farming and support the agricultural sector in addressing the issue of global warming. This study also uncovers the relationship between the rate of plant growth and various physiological traits, which may positively impact the solution to the global food scarcity problem.

title: Integrating Deep Feature Extraction with ML for Robust Lung Cancer Classification on CT Images
authors: Sayeeda Khan, Md. Ibne Shihab Shad, Md. Imran Hossain, Akif Mahdi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) are highly effective for image analysis but it requires large amounts of labeled data to get better, generalized, stable and reproducible result. In medical imaging, like lung cancer CT scans for multi-class cancerous image classification, annotated data is usually limited. As a result, training a DNN directly often leads to overfitting and poor generalization. To address this, pre-trained DNNs on medical images were used as feature extractors, and the extracted features were analyzed using principal component analysis (PCA). EfficientNet-B0, pre-trained on medical images, exhibited the highest PCA1 and PCA2 values, indicating strong feature representation. Due to the relatively high PCA2, the third principal component (PCA3) was also included to enhance discriminative power. Classical machine learning (ML) classifiers including support vector machines (SVM), random forests (RF), logistic regression (LR), and gradient boosting (GB) were trained on the reduced features. SVM achieved the best performance with top three PCA, with a validation accuracy of 93 % and crossvalidation accuracy of 91.5 %, validation F 1 -score of 93.4 % and cross-validation validation F1-score of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 1. 9 \%}$</tex>. Five-fold crossvalidation confirmed consistent performance without overfitting. Grad-CAM was applied to visualize the most influential regions, enhancing interpretability. The framework enables six-class lung cancer classification, demonstrating reproducibility and practical utility for limited medical image datasets.

title: An Interpretable Ensemble CNN Framework for MRI-Based Diagnosis of Neurodegenerative Diseases
authors: Ayesha Siddiqua, Atib Mohammad Oni, Abu Saleh Musa Miah, Najmul Hassan, Md Maruf Al Hossain Prince, Jungpil Shin
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: In medical imaging, convolutional neural networks have made major advances, particularly in the classification of neurodegenerative disorders like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease. But their “black-box” nature makes model interpretability difficult, which is crucial in healthcare for confidence and informed decision-making. This research introduces a multiclass multiclass transfer learning-based ensemble classification system that classifies brain MRI images into AD, PD, and healthy controls using EfficientNetB7, ResNetV2-50, and DenseNet121. Soft voting determines categorisation by averaging predictions from all three models and selecting the class with the highest likelihood. Grad-CAM (Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping) visualises brain areas impacting model predictions to improve interpretability. This method increases classification performance and provides transparent insight into decision making, increasing trust in AI-assisted neuroimaging applications.

title: LabEquipVis: Automated Detection of Computer Laboratory Equipment Using YOLOv8 and Transfer Learning
authors: B. M. Shahria Alam, Omar Faruq Shikdar, Fahad Ahammed, Md Parvez Mia, Jannatul Adan Mahi, Nishat Tasnim Niloy
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Computer labs are a crucial component of the modern education system and an essential facility for research. Monitoring these labs to inventory the number and type of digital equipment, especially in large facilities, presents a significant challenge. An automated monitoring system offers an effective solution to this problem. In this research, we created a novel dataset of computer lab environments consisting of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2, 5 8 4}$</tex> images, annotating all present equipment. We deployed deep learning architectures utilizing transfer learning, employing three versions of the YOLO algorithm: YOLOv8, YOLOv9, and YOLOv11. YOLOv8 emerged as the best-performing model, achieving an accuracy of 87.89%. Its mean average precision (mAP) was also highly impressive, exceeding 87% for all but one class. A web app has been developed to showcase the effectiveness of our system.

title: Slot-Rotated Dual-Patch Circularly Polarized Microstrip Antenna for 5.8 GHz WLAN Applications
authors: Fariha Tasnim, Syed Md Hasin Tasnim, Md Faruk Muntasir Banna, Md Aminul Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: A dual-patch circularly polarized microstrip antenna is introduced for WLAN applications, operating at 5.8 GHz. The design includes a patch and a parasitic patch. Circular polarization is achieved through the rotated slot perturbation method, connecting two orthogonal modes with a 90° phase difference. Full-wave simulations show excellent impedance matching and clear polarization, with a return loss of −27.91 dB, an axial ratio of 1.13, a VSWR of 1.083, and a gain of about 7.03 dBi. The compact single-layer design, based on Rogers RT5880, ensures a simple structure. This makes the antenna ideal for indoor WLAN environments that face multipath propagation and random device orientations.

title: Enhancing Dry Eye Disease Classification Using Hybrid Ensemble Learning with XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost
authors: Nusrat Jahan, Refah Tasnia, Md Rakib Mahmud, Ashutosh Roy, Jasmin Akter, Md Mahabube Alahi Atik, Abdul Ahad
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Dry Eye Disease (DED) occurs frequently among patients as it causes substantial life quality deterioration. The management process requires both early diagnosis together with precise disease classification to be successful. The authors developed a multibox ensemble algorithm that utilizes XGBoost and LightGBM and CatBoost to advance DED classification accuracy levels. The database analyzed contains multiple numerical and categorical features which include data on patient age alongside stress scales and symptoms related to eye health. The preprocessing steps included encoding categorical data while standardizing numerical variables in addition to correcting class distribution using balancing techniques. The ensemble classifier utilized multiple single model strengths to generate predictions about DED. The metrics for performance assessment included accuracy, precision and recall and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex>-score. The combination model yielded much better prediction outcomes than when using models separately which indicated better predictive capability and robust performance. The results demonstrate that joining these advanced machine learning algorithms produces stronger DED classification systems which face down challenges from class imbalance and feature complexity. The proposed approach shows great potential for early detection of Dry Eye Disease because it leads to improved clinical management and customized therapies.

title: IoT-Enabled Insulator Crack Detection Using a YOLOv11n Algorithm on Raspberry Pi 5
authors: Fauzia Fariha Trona, Farhan Ferdous, Ahanaf Tahmid Romit, Riad Bin Islam, Bishwajit Banik Pathik
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Insulators are essential for reliable power transmission. They often develop cracks due to stress, weather, or aging. If these fissures are not found in time, they may spread and cause failures. Traditional inspection techniques are less efficient because they are primarily manual and take a long time. In this research, a lightweight YOLOv11n deep learning model on a Raspberry Pi 5 is used to create an Internet of Things-enabled system for real-time insulator crack detection. This uses a Pi camera Module to take pictures, detects cracks on the gadget, and uses lowpower wireless communication to send out alarms. According to experimental findings, the method offers precise, quick, and contact-free detection, which makes it an effective choice for condition-based tracking and improving grid reliability.

title: A Simulation Framework for Pre-Deployment Optimization of Digital Twin Architectures in Resource-Constrained Precision Agriculture
authors: Sourour Meddeb, Selma Batti, Khaled Bousetta, Habib Fathallah
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Physical deployment of IoT-based precision agriculture systems in remote regions incurs substantial costs ($400-600/tree) with uncertain performance outcomes. This paper presents a simulation-driven framework for systematic evaluation and optimization of digital twin (DT) architectures before hardware investment. We propose a methodological approach integrating: (1) a five-layer DT architecture adapted for oasis agroecosystems addressing coupled groundwater-salinity-microclimate dynamics; (2) a simulation methodology combining NS-3 network modeling with crop physiological models; (3) standardized test scenarios for comparative architectural analysis across energy management, network topology, and sampling strategies; (4) multi-objective evaluation criteria balancing autonomy, reliability, and decision quality. Applied to olive cultivation in North African oases, the framework enables systematic comparison of configuration alternatives through scenarios spanning heat waves, extended cloud cover, and infrastructure failures. This methodology provides a reproducible approach for agricultural IoT design in resource-constrained contexts, with framework validation through case study application and physical deployment identified as essential future work.

title: Preemptive, Buffered or Guided? Empirical Studies on Human-AI Interaction Strategies for Software Test Case Development
authors: Billy Shi, Per Ola Kristensson
abstract: None

title: Accurate phase modulation of Φ-OTDR system based on arbitrary phase-shifting technique
authors: Xianglei Pan, Ke Cui, Haojie Huang, Zhongjie Ren, Rihong Zhu
abstract: Distributed acoustic sensing (DAS) based on phase-sensitive optical time-domain reflectometry (Φ-OTDR) enables the continuous, real-time detection of vibrations and acoustic fields along optical fibers. The phase extraction method plays a pivotal role in determining the performance of the Φ-OTDR system. In this study, we propose a novel three-step phase-shifting demodulation model that is suitable for arbitrary phase-shifting angles. Building upon this model, a piezoelectric ceramic transducer (PZT) within an unbalanced Michelson interferometer (MI) is employed as the phase modulation carrier. A sinusoidal signal serves as the phase modulation source. By controlling the relative timing between the probe pulses and the modulation signal, three adjacent Rayleigh backscattering (RBS) traces are encoded with distinct phase-shifting angles. Phase recovery is then performed using the proposed demodulation model. Experimental results demonstrate the successful demodulation of signals across a frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 1 kHz over a sensing length of 9.85 km, achieving a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of 41.6 dB. This approach provides a cost-effective system configuration and high computational efficiency, offering a promising solution for the large-scale engineering application of DAS.

title: Harnessing Knowledge From Pretrained VLMs for Unsupervised Person Search_supp1-3686858.pdf
authors: Shanshan Zhang
abstract: Person search is a unified task that includes the subtasks of pedestrian detection and re-identification (re-ID). It is expensive to label pedestrian bounding boxes and person identities for training. Purely unsupervised (US) person search is more practical for real-world situations. However, it is difficult to obtain accurate pseudo-IDs from low-quality pseudo-boxes, which brings a new challenge. To address this issue, we propose FMUPS, a novel method that leverages semantic information to produce reliable pseudo-labels. Semantic representations, particularly from vision-language models (VLMs), provide clear and interpretable guidance, reducing noise caused by background disturbances during pseudo-label extraction. Despite their advantages, VLM-generated pseudo-boxes often suffer from poor alignment with person regions and misclassification of other objects as people, adversely affecting the re-ID task. To overcome these issues, we introduce an anti-bbox-noise re-ID loss that not only alleviates the above localization and classification noises but also helps to acquire effective re-ID features. In addition, we propose a CLIP ID labeler, which exploits text–image alignment capabilities of VLMs to generate pseudo-IDs based on our predefined attributes and iteratively refines them using prior knowledge of person search. The experimental results on two typical benchmarks, CUHK-SYSU and PRW, demonstrate the effectiveness of our method; in particular, we outperform some previous fully and weakly supervised (WS) methods.

title: Deep Contrastive Hashing with Proxy Guidance for Multi-Label Micro-Video Retrieval
authors: Yun Li, Rui Chen, Haoyi Sun, Ningyuan Zhao, Peiguang Jing, Xingyu Gao
abstract: The explosive growth of micro-videos has intensified the demand for efficient hash-based retrieval techniques, where the inherent co-occurrence of semantic concepts necessitates effective modeling of multi-label correlations. However, label confusion caused by high-density information, along with the complex entanglement of multi-label dependencies, still poses significant challenges to generating robust hash codes. To address these issues, we propose a proxy-guided contrastive hashing network (DCPH), which integrates contrastive learning with proxy guidance to generate semantic-constrained hash representations for micro-videos. Specifically, to handle the frequent yet tricky visual differences, we design a heterogeneity-aware visual-to-semantic projection (HAVSP) component. HAVSP aggregates overall visual representations in the shared space via cross-modal cyclic projection, and introduces dual-constrained contrastive alignment to alleviate semantic ambiguity between visual features and textual prompts. To explicitly model multi-label dependencies, we develop a proxy-guided decompositional hashing (PGDH) component. PGDH dynamically decomposes label semantics into sequential binary codes via serial hash mappings, and incorporates multi-label proxy-center learning to optimize the commonality and mutual exclusivity among entangled micro-videos. Comprehensive experiments on a large-scale benchmark dataset demonstrate that DCPH achieves state-of-the-art retrieval performance, verifying its efficiency and accuracy in diverse micro-video retrieval.
